name: DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES

topics.title: Interactive Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interactive Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Definition of an Interactive System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> An **Interactive System** is a computer-based system that supports the *iterative, bidirectional communication* between a **human user** and a **machine**, where input from the user causes a perceivable change in the system output, and that output, in turn, influences the user's subsequent input.

In simpler words, it is any software/hardware setup that "talks back" to a person. A user gives a *command* (input), the system processes it, *responds* (output), and the user reacts to that response — forming a continuous **loop of communication**.

> [!NOTE]
> **HCI Discipline Definition (ACM, 1992)**
> *"Human-Computer Interaction is a discipline concerned with the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use, and with the study of major phenomena surrounding them."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Café Counter" Intuition

Imagine ordering coffee at a self-service counter:

| Step | You (User) | The Café System |
|------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | You **see** the menu (Output) | System displays options |
| 2 | You **press** a button "Cappuccino" (Input) | System accepts order |
| 3 | You **hear** the buzzer (Feedback) | System signals processing |
| 4 | You **pick up** the cup (Output) | System delivers result |

This **see → press → hear → pick** cycle is *exactly* how an interactive computer system works. The user is constantly *interpreting* system output and *generating* new input. The quality of this loop is what HCI tries to optimize.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter?**
> A bad interactive system is like a café counter where the menu is in a foreign language, the buzzer is silent, and the cup appears at the wrong window. Users will *leave frustrated*. HCI prevents this.

---

## 1.3 Why "Interactive" — Not Just "Computational"

A **non-interactive** system runs a script and exits (e.g., a cron job). An **interactive** system maintains a *dialogue*. Key properties that distinguish an interactive system:

- **Bidirectionality** — both user and system influence each other's state.
- **Incrementality** — tasks are broken into small, repeatable exchanges.
- **Responsiveness** — feedback is provided within perceptible time limits (typically **< 1 second** for user attention).
- **Goal-orientation** — exchanges aim at completing user goals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEPET861 – Module 1)**
> The term *"interactive systems"* is used in a *broader* sense than just graphical interfaces — it includes voice assistants, brain-computer interfaces, gesture-based controls, kiosks, mobile apps, and even tactile/haptic devices. Always define systems **by their interaction loop**, not by their input/output device.

---

## 1.4 Scope of Interactive Systems

Interactive systems span across:

1. **Desktop GUIs** — Windows, macOS, Linux desktop apps.
2. **Web & Mobile Apps** — Browsers, iOS/Android apps.
3. **Embedded & Ubiquitous Systems** — Smart watches, IoT devices.
4. **Multimodal Systems** — Voice + touch + gesture (e.g., Alexa, Kinect).
5. **Virtual & Augmented Reality** — VR headsets, AR glasses.
6. **Critical Systems** — Air traffic control, medical devices, industrial control.

---

## 1.5 Key Roles in the Interactive System Ecosystem

| Role | Responsibility |
|------|----------------|
| **Designer** | Decides *what* the interaction should be |
| **Developer** | Implements the interaction in code |
| **User** | Performs tasks using the system |
| **Evaluator** | Tests whether the interaction meets goals |
| **Domain Expert** | Provides contextual knowledge (e.g., doctors for medical systems) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of an Interactive System

Every interactive system can be deconstructed into three orthogonal dimensions:

1. **The User (Human)** — cognition, perception, motor skills, emotions, cultural background.
2. **The Computer (Machine)** — input/output devices, software, processing, storage, network.
3. **The Interaction (Bridge)** — the *dialogue* that connects them — dialog styles, feedback, error handling.

> [!NOTE]
> A good interactive system is one in which **the bridge is built according to the strengths and limitations of both the human and the machine** — not just one of them.

---

## 2.2 Goals of Interactive System Design (KTU High-Yield)

KTU examiners frequently ask these as **short-answer and 14-mark questions**. Master the two-tier classification:

### A. Usability Goals (Performance-oriented, measurable)

| Goal | Meaning | Typical Target Metric |
|------|---------|------------------------|
| **Effectiveness** | Can the user complete the goal? | Success rate ≥ **95%** |
| **Efficiency** | How quickly can the goal be completed? | Task time ≤ benchmark |
| **Safety** | Can the user recover from errors? | Error recovery rate ≥ **90%** |
| **Utility** | Does the system provide the right functions? | Function coverage |
| **Learnability** | How fast can a new user start productive work? | Time-to-first-task |
| **Memorability** | Can a casual user resume without relearning? | Relearning time |

### B. User Experience Goals (Satisfaction-oriented, subjective)

- **Satisfaction** — emotional response, trust, comfort.
- **Enjoyability** — fun, engagement, motivation.
- **Aesthetics** — visual appeal, style.
- **Accessibility** — usable by people with disabilities.
- **Trustworthiness** — belief that the system behaves correctly.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Trick**: If asked *"Why can't we just maximize usability?"* — answer: because pure usability neglects the *human* side — beauty, fun, accessibility, emotion. This is why **UX goals complement usability goals**.

---

## 2.3 The Norman's Execution–Evaluation Cycle (KTU ★★★★★)

Donald Norman's classic 7-stage model from *"The Design of Everyday Things"*. This is a **guaranteed 14-mark question**.

| Stage | User Action | System Response |
|-------|-------------|------------------|
| 1. **Forming the Goal** | Decide what to achieve | — |
| 2. **Forming the Intention** | Decide *what* to do | — |
| 3. **Specifying an Action** | Plan the sequence | — |
| 4. **Executing the Action** | Perform the action | System input received |
| 5. **Perceiving the System State** | — | System output produced |
| 6. **Interpreting the State** | Make sense of output | — |
| 7. **Evaluating the Outcome** | Compare with goal | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Gulf of Execution** = the gap between the user's *intention* and the *actions allowed by the system*.
> **The Gulf of Evaluation** = the gap between the *system's physical state* and the *user's ability to interpret it*.
> Good HCI design **minimizes both gulfs**.

---

## 2.4 Abowd & Beale Framework (Interaction Framework ★★★★)

A more formal triangle (covered in Module 1):

- **User** ⇄ **Input** ⇄ **System** ⇄ **Output** ⇄ **User**
- Four key components: **User, Input, Output, System**.
- Four translations: **User → Input (articulation), Input → System (performance), System → Output (presentation), Output → User (observation)**.

---

## 2.5 Normative vs Descriptive Theories

| Aspect | Normative | Descriptive |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **Purpose** | Prescribes *how* users *should* behave | Explains *how* users *actually* behave |
| **Approach** | Sets rules, principles (e.g., Nielsens 10 heuristics) | Models reality (e.g., ACT-R, GOMS) |
| **Use** | Design guidance | Predictions, simulation |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Usability Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Units | Notes |
|--------|---------|-------|-------|
| **Effectiveness** | $E = \dfrac{G_{achieved}}{G_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | $G$ = number of goals |
| **Efficiency (Time)** | $E_t = \dfrac{T_{target}}{T_{actual}}$ | ratio | Higher = better |
| **Efficiency (Errors)** | $E_e = \dfrac{1}{E_{count}}$ | ratio | Inverse of error count |
| **Satisfaction Score** | $S = \dfrac{\sum q_{score}}{q_{max}}$ | 0 to 1 | From Likert questionnaires |
| **Time on Task** | $T = t_{end} - t_{start}$ | seconds | Average across users |
| **Learnability** | $L = \dfrac{T_{first}}{T_{n-th}}$ | ratio | Ratio of task time, $n^{th}$ use to $1^{st}$ use |
| **KLM (Keystroke-Level Model)** | $T_{task} = T_k + n \cdot T_{BB} + \dots$ | seconds | $T_k$ = keystroke time, $T_{BB}$ = button press, etc. |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation, **do not use `|` (vertical pipe) inside markdown tables** — it breaks rendering. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of Interactive Systems Study

| Domain | Use Case | Key HCI Concern |
|--------|----------|------------------|
| **Aviation** | Cockpit displays | Safety, real-time feedback |
| **Medicine** | Surgical robots | Precision, error recovery |
| **Banking** | ATMs, mobile banking | Trust, accessibility |
| **Gaming** | VR headsets | Immersion, motion sickness |
| **E-Governance** | Citizen portals | Learnability for non-tech users |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effectiveness Percentage from Task Data

> **Problem Context**: 50 users were given a goal on a website. 43 successfully completed it. Calculate effectiveness.

**Step 1** — Identify the variables.

$$G_{achieved} = 43$$

$$G_{total} = 50$$

**Step 2** — Apply the effectiveness formula.

$$E = \frac{G_{achieved}}{G_{total}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3** — Substitute.

$$E = \frac{43}{50} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4** — Simplify the fraction.

$$E = 0.86 \times 100\%$$

**Step 5** — Final result.

$$E = 86\%$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU benchmark for a *good* system is **≥ 95%**. 86% is acceptable for *complex tasks* but indicates room for improvement for *simple* tasks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Time-Based Efficiency

> **Problem Context**: An expert performs a task in **28 seconds**; a novice takes **112 seconds**. The benchmark target is **40 seconds**. Compute efficiency for both.

**Step 1** — State the formula.

$$E_t = \frac{T_{target}}{T_{actual}}$$

**Step 2** — Expert case.

$$E_{t,\text{expert}} = \frac{40}{28} = 1.428$$

This means the expert is **~43% faster** than the target.

**Step 3** — Novice case.

$$E_{t,\text{novice}} = \frac{40}{112} = 0.357$$

The novice is **~64% slower** than the target.

**Step 4** — Interpretation.

A ratio **> 1** means *better than target*; **< 1** means *worse*. The gap between expert and novice reflects the **learnability** of the system.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: KLM (Keystroke-Level Model) — Card, Moran & Newell

The KLM predicts task time by summing operator times.

**Step 1** — List of KLM operators (canonical values, in seconds):

| Operator | Symbol | Time (s) |
|----------|--------|----------|
| Keystroke | $T_k$ | 0.28 |
| Button press (mouse) | $T_{BB}$ | 0.20 |
| Point with mouse | $T_p$ | 1.10 |
| Hand to/from keyboard | $T_h$ | 0.40 |
| Mental preparation | $T_m$ | 1.35 |
| System response | $T_r$ | Variable |

**Step 2** — General formula.

$$T_{task} = T_r + n_m \cdot T_m + n_k \cdot T_k + n_p \cdot T_p + n_b \cdot T_{BB} + n_h \cdot T_h$$

where $n_*$ are the number of occurrences of each operator.

**Step 3** — Worked Example.

> **Task**: Type the URL `www.ktu.edu` in a browser, then press Enter.
> - System response (load): $T_r = 1.0$ s
> - Mental preparation (1 time): $T_m = 1.35$ s
> - Keystrokes for URL (12 chars): $n_k = 12$, $T_k = 0.28$ s
> - Enter key (1 keystroke): $n_k = 1$, $T_k = 0.28$ s
> - Point to browser bar (0): not used
> - Hand to keyboard (1): $T_h = 0.40$ s

**Step 4** — Substitution.

$$T_{task} = 1.0 + (1)(1.35) + (13)(0.28) + (0)(1.10) + (0)(0.20) + (1)(0.40)$$

**Step 5** — Evaluate each term.

$$T_{task} = 1.0 + 1.35 + 3.64 + 0 + 0 + 0.40$$

**Step 6** — Final sum.

$$T_{task} = 6.39 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KLM is **descriptive**, not normative. It predicts *expert* time without errors. KTU frequently asks to *"estimate time for a given task using KLM"* — memorize the operator values.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Learnability Index

> **Problem Context**: A new user took 120 s to complete a task on first attempt. After 5 trials, time dropped to 60 s. Compute the learnability index.

**Step 1** — Formula.

$$L = \frac{T_{first}}{T_{n-th}}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute $T_{first} = 120$ s, $T_{5^{th}} = 60$ s.

$$L = \frac{120}{60} = 2.0$$

**Step 3** — Interpretation.

$$L = 2.0 \implies \text{User is } 2 \times \text{ faster after 5 trials}$$

> An $L \geq 1.5$ is considered good. Below $1.2$ suggests a system with poor learnability.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Interaction Models

| Model | Inventor | Focus | Key Strength | Limitation |
|-------|----------|-------|--------------|------------|
| **Execution–Evaluation Cycle** | Norman (1988) | User cognition | Explains gulfs | High-level, not testable |
| **Abowd & Beale** | Abowd & Beale (1991) | Framework | Formalizes components | No detailed stages |
| **GOMS** | Card, Moran & Newell (1983) | Predicts time | Quantifiable | Assumes expert users |
| **KLM** | Card, Moran & Newell (1983) | Low-level prediction | Granular | No error modeling |
| **Norman's Action Cycle** | Norman (1988) | Emotional design | Considers affect | Subjective to evaluate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Norman's Seven-Stage Execution–Evaluation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stage 1: Forming the Goal"] --> B["Stage 2: Forming the Intention"]
    B --> C["Stage 3: Specifying an Action Sequence"]
    C --> D["Stage 4: Executing the Action"]
    D --> E["Stage 5: Perceiving the System State"]
    E --> F["Stage 6: Interpreting the State"]
    F --> G["Stage 7: Evaluating the Outcome"]
    G -->|Mismatch| A
    G -->|Goal Met| H["Task Complete"]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **Mismatch path** in the diagram is the most important — it shows that interactive systems must support *iteration* when the user is unsatisfied. A non-iterative system forces the user to start over.

---

## 4.2 Abowd & Beale Interaction Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U["User"] -->|"Observation"| O["Output"]
    O -->|"Presentation"| S["System"]
    S -->|"Performance"| I["Input"]
    I -->|"Articulation"| U
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **four translations** — *Observation, Presentation, Performance, Articulation* — are the formal names of the arrow labels. KTU examiners often test these as 3-mark definitions.

---

## 4.3 Components of an Interactive System (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "USER SIDE"
        U1["Human Cognition"]
        U2["Perception & Motor Skills"]
        U3["User Goals & Intentions"]
    end

    subgraph "INTERACTION LAYER"
        I1["Input Devices"]
        I2["Output Devices"]
        I3["Dialogue Controller"]
    end

    subgraph "COMPUTER SIDE"
        C1["Operating System"]
        C2["Application Logic"]
        C3["Database / Storage"]
    end

    U3 --> I1
    I1 --> I3
    I3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C1
    C1 --> I3
    I3 --> I2
    I2 --> U2
    U2 --> U1
    U1 --> U3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Dialogue Controller** (also called the *UI Manager* or *Interaction Layer*) is what distinguishes an interactive system from a batch program. It mediates between user inputs and system responses in real time.

---

## 4.4 Hierarchical Goals of Interactive Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Interactive System Goals"] --> B["Usability Goals"]
    A --> C["User Experience Goals"]

    B --> B1["Effectiveness"]
    B --> B2["Efficiency"]
    B --> B3["Safety"]
    B --> B4["Utility"]
    B --> B5["Learnability"]
    B --> B6["Memorability"]

    C --> C1["Satisfaction"]
    C --> C2["Enjoyability"]
    C --> C3["Accessibility"]
    C --> C4["Aesthetics"]
    C --> C5["Trustworthiness"]
```

---

## 4.5 Normative vs Descriptive Theories

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["HCI Theories"] --> B["Normative: How things SHOULD be done"]
    A --> C["Descriptive: How things ARE actually done"]

    B --> B1["Nielsen 10 Heuristics"]
    B --> B2["Shneiderman 8 Golden Rules"]
    B --> B3["Normans Design Principles"]

    C --> C1["GOMS Model"]
    C --> C2["ACT-R Cognitive Model"]
    C --> C3["KLM Keystroke Model"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an interactive system. List any four properties that distinguish it from a non-interactive system.**

**Model Answer:**

> An **interactive system** is a computer-based system that allows a continuous, bidirectional dialogue between a user and a machine, where the user's input causes a perceivable change in the system output, and that output in turn influences the user's subsequent input.

**Four distinguishing properties:**
1. **Bidirectionality** — both user and system influence each other.
2. **Incrementality** — tasks are performed in small, repeatable exchanges.
3. **Responsiveness** — feedback is provided within perceivable time (typically < 1 s).
4. **Goal-orientation** — exchanges support user objectives.

*(3 marks — definition 1.5 + properties 1.5)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the difference between Usability Goals and User Experience (UX) Goals with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Usability Goals | User Experience Goals |
|--------|------------------|------------------------|
| Nature | Objective, measurable | Subjective, perceptual |
| Focus | Task completion efficiency | Emotional satisfaction |
| Example 1 | Effectiveness (95% task success) | Enjoyment while using app |
| Example 2 | Learnability (new user in 2 min) | Aesthetic appeal of interface |

Usability is **about getting the job done**; UX is **about how it feels while doing it**. Both are essential. *(3 marks — 1 for distinction + 2 for examples)*

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the Norman's Execution–Evaluation cycle in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss the **Gulfs of Execution and Evaluation** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Abowd & Beale interaction framework**. List the four key translations. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — Norman's Execution–Evaluation Cycle:**

Norman's model, proposed in *"The Design of Everyday Things"* (1988), describes the **7 stages** a user passes through when interacting with a system:

| Stage | User Activity | System Activity |
|-------|---------------|------------------|
| 1. Forming the Goal | Decides what to achieve | — |
| 2. Forming the Intention | Decides what to do | — |
| 3. Specifying an Action | Plans the sequence | — |
| 4. Executing the Action | Performs the action | Receives input |
| 5. Perceiving the System State | — | Produces output |
| 6. Interpreting the State | Decodes output | — |
| 7. Evaluating the Outcome | Compares with goal | — |

**The Two Gulfs:**

**1. Gulf of Execution** — The gap between the user's *intentions* and the *actions allowed by the system*.

- *Example*: A user wants to save a file. The system has a tiny floppy-disk icon in the menu bar with no tooltip. The user does not know how to "save" → **large gulf of execution**.

**2. Gulf of Evaluation** — The gap between the *system's physical state* and the *user's ability to interpret it*.

- *Example*: A download finishes silently in the background. The user has no clue whether the file saved successfully → **large gulf of evaluation**.

**Bridge strategies:**

- **Mappings** — natural relationships between controls and effects (e.g., volume slider).
- **Affordances** — visual cues suggesting use (e.g., buttons look pressable).
- **Feedback** — immediate response to every action.
- **Constraints** — preventing invalid actions.

*(7 marks: 3 for stages + 2 for gulfs + 1 for example + 1 for diagram)*

---

**Solution (b) — Abowd & Beale Framework:**

The framework formalizes the interaction loop into **four components** and **four translations**.

**Components:**
1. **User** — the human interacting with the system.
2. **Input** — the means by which user actions enter the system (keyboard, mouse, voice).
3. **System** — the computational engine that processes input and produces output.
4. **Output** — the means by which the system communicates with the user (screen, speaker, haptic).

**Four Translations (the labels of the loop arrows):**

1. **Articulation** — User $\rightarrow$ Input (how the user expresses the action).
2. **Performance** — Input $\rightarrow$ System (how the system processes).
3. **Presentation** — System $\rightarrow$ Output (how the result is displayed).
4. **Observation** — Output $\rightarrow$ User (how the user perceives).

**Block Diagram (re-rendered for clarity):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U["USER"] -->|Articulation| I["INPUT"]
    I -->|Performance| S["SYSTEM"]
    S -->|Presentation| O["OUTPUT"]
    O -->|Observation| U
```

**Advantage:** This framework is *formal* and *complete* — every interaction in any system can be mapped onto these four components and translations.

*(7 marks: 2 for components + 2 for translations + 2 for diagram + 1 for explanation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a)** Define HCI as a discipline. Explain the **goals of interactive system design** with a clear classification into **usability goals** and **user experience goals**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the following for the given scenario, showing all steps:
- 30 users attempted a task. 24 succeeded. Compute **effectiveness**.
- Expert time = 20 s, target time = 25 s. Compute **time-based efficiency**.
- First trial = 90 s, fifth trial = 30 s. Compute **learnability index**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution (a):**

**Definition of HCI (ACM 1992):**
> *"Human-Computer Interaction is a discipline concerned with the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use and the study of major phenomena surrounding them."*

**Goals of Interactive System Design:**

**Tier 1 — Usability Goals (Performance-Oriented):**
- **Effectiveness** — the system supports the user in completing their goals.
- **Efficiency** — the system supports users in completing goals with minimal wasted effort.
- **Safety** — the system protects users from hazardous conditions and allows recovery from errors.
- **Utility** — the system provides the right functionality for the user's needs.
- **Learnability** — the system is easy to learn.
- **Memorability** — the system is easy to remember.

**Tier 2 — User Experience Goals (Satisfaction-Oriented):**
- **Satisfaction** — pleasant to use.
- **Enjoyability** — fun, engaging, entertaining.
- **Aesthetics** — visually pleasing.
- **Accessibility** — usable by people with disabilities (WCAG compliance).
- **Trustworthiness** — inspires confidence.

**Why both?** A system with high usability but poor UX (e.g., a hospital system that works but feels cold) will be rejected by users. Conversely, a beautiful app that fails tasks (e.g., a buggy game) is unusable. **Both tiers are necessary.**

*(7 marks: 2 for definition + 3 for usability goals + 2 for UX goals)*

---

**Solution (b):**

**(i) Effectiveness Calculation:**

**Step 1** — Identify variables.

$$G_{achieved} = 24, \quad G_{total} = 30$$

**Step 2** — Apply formula.

$$E = \frac{24}{30} \times 100\% = 80\%$$

✅ **Effectiveness = 80%** — Acceptable for complex tasks, but below the KTU benchmark of **≥ 95%** for simple tasks.

---

**(ii) Time-based Efficiency:**

**Step 1** — State formula.

$$E_t = \frac{T_{target}}{T_{actual}}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute $T_{target} = 25$ s, $T_{actual} = 20$ s.

$$E_t = \frac{25}{20} = 1.25$$

✅ **Efficiency = 1.25** — The expert is **25% faster** than target (ratio > 1 indicates better-than-target performance).

---

**(iii) Learnability Index:**

**Step 1** — State formula.

$$L = \frac{T_{first}}{T_{5^{th}}}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute $T_{first} = 90$ s, $T_{5^{th}} = 30$ s.

$$L = \frac{90}{30} = 3.0$$

✅ **Learnability = 3.0** — The user is **3× faster** after 5 trials, indicating **excellent learnability** (KTU benchmark: $L \geq 1.5$ is good).

*(7 marks: 3 for effectiveness + 2 for efficiency + 2 for learnability)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up "Gulfs"** — many students confuse the Gulf of Execution with the Gulf of Evaluation. **Memorize this line**: *Execution = user → system; Evaluation = system → user.*
> 2. **Skipping diagrams in 14-mark questions** — a missing diagram costs **2 to 3 marks** even if the text is correct. Always draw a labeled block diagram.
> 3. **Confusing Usability vs UX** — these are *complementary*, not *interchangeable*. A 7-mark answer that lumps them together loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Not writing units in numeric answers** — always write "**80%**", "**1.25 (ratio)**", "**6.39 seconds**" — naked numbers lose marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to list the 4 translations in Abowd & Beale** — *Articulation, Performance, Presentation, Observation* — these are examiner favorites.
> 6. **Using `|` in tables** — breaks markdown. Use $\vert$ in LaTeX for absolute value inside formulas.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Interactive Systems**

✅ **Definition** — Interactive System = bidirectional, iterative dialogue between user and machine.

✅ **Three pillars** — User, Computer, Interaction (the bridge).

✅ **Discipline name** — HCI (Human-Computer Interaction) — ACM 1992 definition.

✅ **Goals** — Two tiers: **Usability** (Effectiveness, Efficiency, Safety, Utility, Learnability, Memorability) and **UX** (Satisfaction, Enjoyability, Aesthetics, Accessibility, Trustworthiness).

✅ **Norman's 7-stage cycle** — Goal → Intention → Specifying → Execution → Perception → Interpretation → Evaluation.

✅ **Two Gulfs** — Execution (user→system) and Evaluation (system→user). Good design **minimizes** both.

✅ **Abowd & Beale** — 4 components (User, Input, System, Output) + 4 translations (Articulation, Performance, Presentation, Observation).

✅ **Normative vs Descriptive** — Normative = *should* (heuristics, principles); Descriptive = *actual* (GOMS, KLM, ACT-R).

✅ **Key Formulas** —
$$E = \frac{G_{achieved}}{G_{total}} \times 100\%$$
$$E_t = \frac{T_{target}}{T_{actual}}$$
$$L = \frac{T_{first}}{T_{n-th}}$$
$$T_{task} = T_r + n_m T_m + n_k T_k + n_p T_p + n_b T_{BB} + n_h T_h$$

✅ **KLM Operator Times** — $T_k = 0.28$ s, $T_{BB} = 0.20$ s, $T_p = 1.10$ s, $T_h = 0.40$ s, $T_m = 1.35$ s.

✅ **Benchmarks** — Effectiveness ≥ 95% (good), Efficiency > 1 (better than target), Learnability ≥ 1.5 (good).

✅ **Scope** — Covers GUIs, web, mobile, multimodal, VR/AR, and critical systems.

✅ **Roles** — Designer, Developer, User, Evaluator, Domain Expert — all collaborate in the design loop.

✅ **Real-world domains** — Aviation (safety), Medicine (precision), Banking (trust), Gaming (immersion), E-Governance (learnability).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Usability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Usability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Usability (ISO 9241-11 Standard Definition)**
> *Usability is the extent to which a specified user can achieve specified goals with effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction in a specified context of use.*

The **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** formally defines usability in ISO 9241-11 as a **quality attribute** that assesses how easy user interfaces are to use. It is *not* a single property of a product — it is a *multi-dimensional construct* describing the user–system interaction.

### The Three ISO Sub-Components
| Component | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Effectiveness** | Accuracy and completeness with which users achieve specified goals |
| **Efficiency** | Resources expended (time, effort, cognitive load) in relation to effectiveness |
| **Satisfaction** | Freedom from discomfort, and positive attitudes towards the use of the product |

## 1.2 Usability vs. Utility — The Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Utility** = *what* the system does (functionality)
> **Usability** = *how well* users can use those functions

A system can be highly **useful** (great utility) but utterly **unusable** (terrible usability). Conversely, a beautifully usable system that does nothing useful has zero value. KTU examiners frequently test this conceptual contrast.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Don Norman's "Door Handle" Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Door**
>
> Imagine approaching a door. A **flat push plate** with a handle-shaped indentation is a *Norman "bad design"* — it forces the user to stop, read the sign, experiment, and fail. This is a **utility problem made worse by a usability problem**.
>
> Now imagine a door with a **simple horizontal bar on the pull side** and a **flat plate on the push side**. No instructions needed. This is **good usability** — the *affordance* (what the object invites you to do) matches the *mapping* (the relationship between control and effect).

This is the essence of usability engineering: **designing interactions that match user mental models**, expectations, and physical/cognitive capabilities.

## 1.4 Why Usability Matters (KTU Industrial Perspective)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per industry studies (cited in KTU HCI syllabus references):
> - **Forrester Research** estimates that **~50% of development effort** in software projects is wasted due to inadequate usability requirements.
> - Poor usability costs the US economy an estimated **$100 billion+ annually** in lost productivity.
> - Every **\$1 invested in usability** returns roughly **\$10–\$100** in benefits (ROI from usability engineering).

---

## 1.5 The Five Quality Components of Usability (Nielsen's Framework)

Jakob Nielsen (1993) decomposed usability into **five measurable quality components** — these are *high-yield* for KTU 14-mark questions:

1. **Learnability** — How easy is it for first-time users to accomplish basic tasks?
2. **Efficiency** — Once learned, how fast can users perform tasks?
3. **Memorability** — When returning, how easily can users re-establish proficiency?
4. **Errors** — How many errors do users make, how severe are they, and how do they recover?
5. **Satisfaction** — How pleasant is the design to use?
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of Usability

Usability, in practice, is operationalized through a **layered framework** rather than a single formula. Below is the analytical layering every KTU topper should internalize.

### Layer 1 — Outcome-Level Goals
The five Nielsen components stated above. These are the *user-facing* manifestations of usability.

### Layer 2 — Underlying Design Principles (Nielsen's 10 Heuristics)
These are the *design-time levers* that produce the Layer 1 outcomes:

| # | Heuristic | KTU Board-Phrase Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| H1 | Visibility of system status | "Keep users informed about what is going on" |
| H2 | Match between system and real world | "Speak the users' language" |
| H3 | User control and freedom | "Support undo and redo" |
| H4 | Consistency and standards | "Follow platform conventions" |
| H5 | Error prevention | "Prevent problems from occurring" |
| H6 | Recognition rather than recall | "Minimize memory load" |
| H7 | Flexibility and efficiency of use | "Provide accelerators for experts" |
| H8 | Aesthetic and minimalist design | "Avoid irrelevant information" |
| H9 | Help users recognize, diagnose, recover from errors | "Plain-language error messages" |
| H10 | Help and documentation | "Provide easy-to-search task-oriented help" |

### Layer 3 — Context of Use (ISO 9241-11 Context Triangle)
- **Users** (their characteristics: experience, age, disability, language)
- **Goals/Tasks** (what they want to accomplish)
- **Environment** (physical, social, organizational, technical)
- **Equipment/Platform** (hardware, software, network)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Usability is context-dependent — explain."* Your answer must mention the **Context-of-Use triangle** above and cite that the *same system* may be highly usable for an expert in a quiet office but unusable for a novice in a noisy mobile context.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Usability Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are **tested in Part B (14-mark) questions** under "usability evaluation" sub-parts.

$$
\text{Task Completion Rate (Effectiveness)} \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of tasks completed successfully}}{\text{Total tasks attempted}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Task Time (Efficiency)} \;=\; \frac{\text{Time on task (seconds)}}{\text{Number of tasks completed}}
$$

$$
\text{Error Rate} \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of errors}}{\text{Number of tasks attempted}}
$$

$$
\text{Time-Based Efficiency} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{R_i}{T_i}}{n}
$$

Where $R_i$ is the proportion of task $i$ successfully completed and $T_i$ is the time taken for task $i$, and $n$ is the total number of tasks.

$$
\text{Satisfaction (SUS Score)} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{10} \left( \frac{US_i - 1}{4} \right) \times 2.5
$$

Where $US_i$ is the Likert score (1–5) on the *System Usability Scale* (SUS) item $i$. The result is normalized to a 0–100 scale.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Usability Lever Used |
|---|---|
| **Aviation cockpit design** | Consistency, error prevention, recognition over recall |
| **Medical infusion pumps** | Error prevention, undo support, high visibility |
| **Mobile banking apps** | Efficiency, memorability, minimalist design |
| **E-commerce checkout** | Reduced friction, progress visibility, one-click flows |
| **Assistive technology** | Flexibility, multi-modal input, accessibility (a11y) heuristics |

> [!TIP]
> When answering KTU 14-mark "design critique" questions, **always** anchor your argument to (a) a Nielsen heuristic, (b) a metric, and (c) a real-world consequence. This 3-point anchoring is what differentiates a 10-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

## 2.4 Usability Goals in Interaction Design (Preece, Rogers, Sharp)

> [!NOTE]
> Preece, Rogers & Sharp (the standard KTU-recommended textbook) categorize usability into **four goals** that map directly to Nielsen's five components:
>
> 1. **Effective to use** → Effectiveness
> 2. **Efficient to use** → Efficiency
> 3. **Safe to use** → Errors (low error rate + error recovery)
> 4. **Have good utility** → Functional appropriateness
> 5. **Easy to learn** → Learnability + Memorability
> 6. **Memorable** → Memorability
> 7. **Satisfying to use** → Satisfaction

These are sometimes called the **"Seven usability attributes"** in extended HCI literature.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

## 3.1 The Usability Equation — Worked Derivation

The ISO 9241-11 definition is *qualitative*. To make it operational, usability researchers (e.g., Bevan, Kirakowski) formalized it as a composite **Usability Score (U)**:

### Step 1: Define the Composite Construct
Usability is a *function* of the three ISO sub-components. Symbolically:

$$
U \;=\; f(E_{f},\; E_{c},\; S)
$$

Where $E_{f}$ = Effectiveness, $E_{c}$ = Efficiency, $S$ = Satisfaction.

### Step 2: Choose the Functional Form
The most commonly accepted operational form (per KTU HCI evaluation syllabus) is the **geometric mean of normalized sub-scores** to penalize systems that fail on any one dimension:

$$
U \;=\; \sqrt[3]{\tilde{E}_{f} \cdot \tilde{E}_{c} \cdot \tilde{S}}
$$

The **cube root** ensures dimensional balance and that any sub-score near zero drags the overall score down sharply.

### Step 3: Normalize Each Sub-Score to [0, 1]
Each component is measured on its own scale and then mapped to a unit interval:

$$
\tilde{E}_{f} \;=\; \frac{\text{Completion Rate (\%)}}{100}
$$

$$
\tilde{E}_{c} \;=\; \frac{T_{min}}{T_{actual}}
$$

Where $T_{min}$ is the minimum theoretical task time and $T_{actual}$ is the observed time. Capped at 1.0 when $T_{actual} \le T_{min}$.

$$
\tilde{S} \;=\; \frac{\text{SUS Score}}{100}
$$

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A usability test of a new mobile app yields:
> - Completion Rate = 85%
> - Observed Task Time = 48 s ; Theoretical Minimum = 30 s
> - SUS Score = 72
>
> **Find:** the composite Usability Score $U$.

**Sub-step 4a — Normalize Effectiveness:**

$$
\tilde{E}_{f} \;=\; \frac{85}{100} \;=\; 0.85
$$

**Sub-step 4b — Normalize Efficiency:**

$$
\tilde{E}_{c} \;=\; \frac{30}{48} \;=\; 0.625
$$

**Sub-step 4c — Normalize Satisfaction:**

$$
\tilde{S} \;=\; \frac{72}{100} \;=\; 0.72
$$

**Sub-step 4d — Apply the Geometric Mean Formula:**

$$
U \;=\; \sqrt[3]{0.85 \times 0.625 \times 0.72}
$$

$$
U \;=\; \sqrt[3]{0.3825}
$$

$$
U \;\approx\; 0.7265 \;\;(\text{or } 72.65\% \text{ on a 0–100 scale})
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
| Score Range | Verdict |
|---|---|
| $\vert U - 1.0 \vert < 0.10$ | Excellent usability |
| $0.10 \le \vert U - 1.0 \vert < 0.30$ | Acceptable, needs improvement |
| $\vert U - 1.0 \vert \ge 0.30$ | Poor — major redesign required |

Since $\vert 0.7265 - 1.0 \vert = 0.2735$, this app is in the **"Acceptable, needs improvement"** band.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Principles to Outcomes

| Nielsen Heuristic | Nielsen Component Served | Sample Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Visibility of system status | Learnability, Satisfaction | Time to first successful action |
| Match with real world | Learnability, Memorability | Recognition rate on first attempt |
| User control and freedom | Errors | Undo usage frequency |
| Consistency | Efficiency, Memorability | Cross-task transfer time |
| Error prevention | Errors | Pre-emptive error rate |
| Recognition over recall | Memorability | Free-recall task success |
| Flexibility and efficiency | Efficiency | Expert vs. novice time ratio |
| Aesthetic design | Satisfaction | Subjective SUS scores |
| Error recovery | Errors, Satisfaction | Recovery time, recovery success rate |
| Help & docs | Learnability | Help-search success rate |

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the **right column** (the metric). KTU 14-mark questions on "design a usability test plan" demand a *metric per heuristic* — students who fail to specify a metric lose 3–4 marks.

## 3.3 The Usability Engineering Lifecycle (Analytical Walkthrough)

The lifecycle is **iterative**, not linear. Each loop tightens the design:

$$
D_{k+1} \;=\; D_{k} \;+\; \Delta_{k}^{eval}
$$

Where $D_k$ is the design at iteration $k$ and $\Delta_k^{eval}$ is the change introduced based on the evaluation results at iteration $k$.

> The **convergence condition** is:
> $$\lim_{k \to \infty} \vert D_{k+1} - D_{k} \vert \;=\; 0$$

In practice, this convergence is bounded by **budget and time** — not a mathematical limit. KTU expects you to mention the *iterative* nature explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISO 9241-11 Usability Context Triangle

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *Context of Use* that surrounds every usability judgment. Every node ID is alphanumeric-prefixed to avoid Mermaid parsing errors.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[User Characteristics] --> nodeD[Usability Outcome]
    nodeB[Goals and Tasks] --> nodeD
    nodeC[Environment and Equipment] --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Effectiveness]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Efficiency]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Satisfaction]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Final Usability Verdict]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows entering `nodeD` from three sources — User, Goals, and Environment — visually express the ISO 9241-11 statement that **usability is context-dependent**, not an absolute property of a product.

## 4.2 Nielsen's Five Quality Components — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeRoot[USABILITY]
    nodeRoot --> nodeL[Learnability]
    nodeRoot --> nodeEff[Efficiency]
    nodeRoot --> nodeM[Memorability]
    nodeRoot --> nodeErr[Errors]
    nodeRoot --> nodeSat[Satisfaction]
    nodeL --> nodeL1[First-use success rate]
    nodeL --> nodeL2[Time to learn]
    nodeEff --> nodeE1[Task completion time]
    nodeEff --> nodeE2[Throughput]
    nodeM --> nodeM1[Retention after delay]
    nodeM --> nodeM2[Re-learning time]
    nodeErr --> nodeR1[Error frequency]
    nodeErr --> nodeR2[Error severity]
    nodeErr --> nodeR3[Recovery time]
    nodeSat --> nodeS1[SUS score]
    nodeSat --> nodeS2[Net Promoter Score]
```

## 4.3 Usability Engineering Lifecycle — Iterative Block Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeReq[Requirement Gathering] --> nodeDes[Design Prototype]
    nodeDes --> nodeImp[Implementation]
    nodeImp --> nodeEval[Usability Evaluation]
    nodeEval --> nodeRefine[Design Refinement]
    nodeRefine --> nodeDes
    nodeEval -.-> nodeReq
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The double arrows indicate **iteration**: the evaluation findings feed *back* into both the design (short loop) and the requirements (long loop) when fundamental mismatches are discovered. KTU questions on "user-centered design" require you to draw *this kind of cyclic diagram*, not a linear waterfall.

## 4.4 Heuristic-to-Component Mapping (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    H1[Heuristic 1 Visibility of Status] --> nodeL
    H2[Heuristic 2 Match Real World] --> nodeL
    H2 --> nodeM
    H3[Heuristic 3 User Control] --> nodeErr
    H4[Heuristic 4 Consistency] --> nodeEff
    H4 --> nodeM
    H5[Heuristic 5 Error Prevention] --> nodeErr
    H6[Heuristic 6 Recognition] --> nodeM
    H7[Heuristic 7 Flexibility] --> nodeEff
    H8[Heuristic 8 Minimalist] --> nodeSat
    H9[Heuristic 9 Error Recovery] --> nodeErr
    H9 --> nodeSat
    H10[Heuristic 10 Help Docs] --> nodeL
    nodeL[Learnability]
    nodeEff[Efficiency]
    nodeM[Memorability]
    nodeErr[Errors]
    nodeSat[Satisfaction]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram is the *visual answer key* to the Analytical Matrix in Section 3.2. Many heuristics contribute to *multiple* components — a fact KTU examiners reward when students explicitly state the *one-to-many* mapping.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Q: Define usability as per ISO 9241-11. List its three sub-components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> As per ISO 9241-11, usability is the extent to which a specified user can achieve specified goals with **effectiveness**, **efficiency**, and **satisfaction** in a specified context of use.
>
> The three sub-components are:
> 1. **Effectiveness** — accuracy and completeness of goal achievement.
> 2. **Efficiency** — resources (time, effort) expended relative to effectiveness.
> 3. **Satisfaction** — freedom from discomfort and positive user attitude.
>
> *(1 mark — definition; 1.5 marks — three sub-components with brief explanations; 0.5 mark — context of use mention.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]`
**Q: Differentiate between utility and usability with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Utility** refers to *whether* the system provides the functionality the user needs (i.e., *what* it does). **Usability** refers to *how well* the user can use that functionality (i.e., *how easily and pleasantly* they can do so).
>
> **Example:** A word processor that supports 200 fonts but has no spell-check has *low utility* for a student writing an essay. A spell-checker buried in five nested menus has *low usability* even if its utility is high.
>
> A successful product must have both — high utility **and** high usability.
>
> *(1 mark — definitions of each; 1.5 marks — example with both terms; 0.5 mark — synthesis statement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **five quality components of usability** as proposed by Jakob Nielsen. (7 marks)
**(b)** Discuss **Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics** and explain how each contributes to user experience with a real-world example. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks
Jakob Nielsen (1993) decomposed usability into **five measurable quality components**. Each addresses a *different phase* of user–system interaction.

1. **Learnability (1.4 marks):** The ease with which a *first-time user* can complete basic tasks. A highly learnable system allows novices to become productive quickly. *Example:* A new user can send an email in Gmail within minutes because the "Compose" button is prominently labeled.

2. **Efficiency (1.4 marks):** Once learned, the *speed* at which experienced users can perform tasks. A highly efficient system supports fast, repetitive workflows. *Example:* Keyboard shortcuts in Photoshop (e.g., Ctrl+J to duplicate a layer) boost expert efficiency.

3. **Memorability (1.0 marks):** When a casual user returns after a period of non-use, how readily can they re-establish proficiency? *Example:* A consistent navigation bar across all pages of Amazon ensures users do not have to relearn the layout.

4. **Errors (1.4 marks):** The *frequency, severity, and recoverability* of user errors. A good system prevents errors and supports recovery. *Example:* The "Are you sure you want to delete?" confirmation dialog in Windows prevents accidental data loss.

5. **Satisfaction (1.0 marks):** The *subjective pleasantness* of using the system. Measured via questionnaires like the SUS. *Example:* Apple's macOS is rated highly on satisfaction due to its polished animations and consistent design.

> These five components are not independent — improving *learnability* often improves *memorability* as well.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks
Nielsen's 10 heuristics (1994) are the **design principles** that produce the five quality components.

| # | Heuristic | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| H1 | Visibility of system status | The loading spinner in YouTube shows video buffering progress |
| H2 | Match between system and real world | Google Maps uses "Search nearby restaurants" instead of technical "Query POIs" |
| H3 | User control and freedom | Undo/Redo (Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+Y) in Microsoft Word |
| H4 | Consistency and standards | The same "Save" floppy-disk icon across all Microsoft Office apps |
| H5 | Error prevention | Date-of-birth fields in forms auto-validate the day for the selected month |
| H6 | Recognition rather than recall | Recently-opened files shown in the File menu instead of requiring the user to type paths |
| H7 | Flexibility and efficiency of use | Keyboard shortcuts for power users in Gmail (C for compose, / for search) |
| H8 | Aesthetic and minimalist design | Google's homepage — only the logo, search box, and two buttons |
| H9 | Help users recognize, diagnose, recover from errors | "404 — the page you requested could not be found. Try the home page" instead of cryptic codes |
| H10 | Help and documentation | The F1 help in Visual Studio Code opens context-sensitive documentation |

> These heuristics are *evaluation tools* — designers walk through them while inspecting a prototype, and usability experts use them as a *discount usability inspection* checklist.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is meant by **"Context of Use"** in the ISO 9241-11 definition of usability? Illustrate with an example. (7 marks)
**(b)** A mobile banking app is tested with **30 users** for a money-transfer task. Results: **24 completed successfully**, **average time = 45 seconds**, **6 errors observed**. Compute the **Task Completion Rate, Error Rate, and average task time**. Discuss the usability verdict using the **Acceptable Band** criteria. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks
The **Context of Use** refers to the *conditions* under which a product is used, comprising four interrelated elements:

1. **Users (2 marks):** Their characteristics — age, expertise, language, physical/cognitive abilities, motivation. A banking app used by a 70-year-old retiree has different usability demands than one used by a 25-year-old tech professional.

2. **Goals/Tasks (2 marks):** What users are trying to accomplish. For the banking app, the goal may be "transfer ₹5,000 to a contact in under 30 seconds" — a precise goal enables precise usability measurement.

3. **Environment (1.5 marks):** Physical (lighting, noise), social (privacy needs), organizational (workplace policies), and technical (network reliability, device). Using a banking app in a crowded train with poor 4G coverage is a *very different* context than using it in a quiet office with Wi-Fi.

4. **Equipment/Platform (1.5 marks):** The hardware/software on which the system runs. The same banking app may be usable on a 6.5-inch smartphone but unusable on a smartwatch.

> **Why Context Matters:** A system rated as "highly usable" in a controlled lab test may fail catastrophically in a real-world mobile context. ISO 9241-11 explicitly demands that usability be specified *with respect to a defined context of use* — making it a *relative*, not absolute, attribute.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Given:**
- Number of users $N = 30$
- Successful completions = 24
- Average time $T_{avg} = 45$ s
- Number of errors = 6

**Step 1 — Task Completion Rate (Effectiveness):**
$$
\text{Completion Rate} \;=\; \frac{24}{30} \times 100\% \;=\; 80\%
$$

**Step 2 — Error Rate:**
$$
\text{Error Rate} \;=\; \frac{6}{30} \times 100\% \;=\; 20\%
$$

**Step 3 — Average Task Time:**
$$
T_{avg} \;=\; 45 \text{ seconds (given)}
$$

**Step 4 — Usability Verdict (3 marks for the discussion):**
| Metric | Value | Industry Benchmark | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Completion Rate | 80% | ≥ 90% ideal; ≥ 78% acceptable | Borderline acceptable |
| Error Rate | 20% | ≤ 10% ideal; ≤ 20% acceptable | Borderline acceptable |
| Task Time | 45 s | ≤ 30 s ideal; ≤ 60 s acceptable | Acceptable |

> **Composite Verdict:** The app is **borderline acceptable** — it meets the minimum thresholds for completion and error rate, but **falls short of the ideal benchmarks**. The valuation must also incorporate a **Satisfaction** component (e.g., SUS score) to be complete per ISO 9241-11. The KTU 14-mark answer must mention that **Satisfaction is missing** — that omission is the key *Examiner Pitfall* (see warning below).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Satisfaction component** in the composite verdict. ISO 9241-11 explicitly demands *three* components. An answer that discusses only effectiveness and efficiency loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Utility with Usability.** These are *not* the same. Examiners award 0 marks to a definition that conflates them.
> 3. **Failing to mention Context of Use** in any usability definition. ISO 9241-11 requires the phrase "in a specified context of use" — drop it, lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Listing heuristics without examples.** Each of Nielsen's 10 heuristics *must* be illustrated; a list without examples caps the heuristic sub-part at 4/7 marks.
> 5. **No metric in the test-plan question.** Naming a heuristic is half-credit. Naming a heuristic *and the metric that measures it* is full credit.
> 6. **Stating "usability is subjective" without quantitative support.** KTU 2024 Scheme rewards *operationalization* — always pair qualitative claims with at least one numeric metric.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Memorize Before the Exam**

- [ ] **ISO 9241-11 definition** is the *gold-standard* answer to "Define usability." Always include: *user*, *goals*, *effectiveness*, *efficiency*, *satisfaction*, and *context of use*.
- [ ] **Three ISO sub-components** = Effectiveness, Efficiency, Satisfaction. All three must appear in any composite usability verdict.
- [ ] **Utility ≠ Usability.** Utility is *what* the system does; usability is *how well* the user can do it.
- [ ] **Nielsen's 5 quality components** = Learnability, Efficiency, Memorability, Errors, Satisfaction. (Mnemonic: **L-E-M-E-S**.)
- [ ] **Nielsen's 10 heuristics** = must be known by name and example. Common exam trap: students list the heuristics but cannot provide an example.
- [ ] **Context of Use** = Users + Goals + Environment + Equipment. Always mention this when defining usability.
- [ ] **Usability is *relative*, not absolute.** The same system can be usable in one context and unusable in another.
- [ ] **Key formulas** to memorize: Completion Rate (%), Error Rate (%), Time-Based Efficiency, SUS score normalization, Composite Usability Score (geometric mean).
- [ ] **Usability Engineering Lifecycle** is *iterative* — draw it as a loop, not a waterfall. Evaluation findings feed *back* into design.
- [ ] **Discount usability methods** (heuristic evaluation, cognitive walkthrough) are inspection-based and *cheap*; user testing is empirical and *expensive*. KTU may ask you to compare them.
- [ ] **ROI of usability** ≈ \$10–\$100 per \$1 invested — a frequent "industrial impact" question.
- [ ] **Affordance** (Norman) is the actionable property of an object that suggests how it should be used. Pair it with **mapping** and **feedback** in any design question.
- [ ] **Common heuristic-evaluation mistake:** Evaluators are not users. Always *test with real users* before shipping.

> **End of Notes — Introduction to Usability (Module 1)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engineering for Usability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engineering for Usability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Usability** is formally defined by the **ISO 9241-11** standard as:

> *"The extent to which a product can be used by specified users to achieve specified goals with **effectiveness**, **efficiency**, and **satisfaction** in a specified context of use."*

**Engineering for Usability** (often called **Usability Engineering**) is a systematic, disciplined, and quantifiable approach to building user interfaces. It was formalized by **Jakob Nielsen (1993)** as a structured methodology where usability is treated as a software quality attribute that must be **specified, measured, designed for, and iteratively tested** throughout the development lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Engineering for Usability is the *foundational pillar* of HCI. It shifts the design philosophy from "developer-centric" to "user-centric," demanding empirical evaluation rather than intuition.

## 1.2 The Five Canonical Usability Attributes (Nielsen, 1993)

| # | Attribute | Definition |
|---|-----------|------------|
| 1 | **Learnability** | How easy is it for users to accomplish basic tasks the first time they encounter the design? |
| 2 | **Efficiency** | Once users have learned the design, how quickly can they perform tasks? |
| 3 | **Memorability** | When users return to the design after a period of not using it, how easily can they re-establish proficiency? |
| 4 | **Errors** | How many errors do users make, how severe are these errors, and how easily can they recover? |
| 5 | **Satisfaction** | How pleasant is the design to use? |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The Bridge Builder)

Imagine a **civil engineer** building a bridge. The engineer does not just pour concrete and hope cars can cross — they calculate **load-bearing capacity**, test **wind resistance**, measure **commute time** for users, and ensure **structural safety**. 

**Usability Engineering** does the exact same thing for software:
- **Load-bearing capacity** $\rightarrow$ Can the system handle the user's task load? (**Effectiveness**)
- **Commute time** $\rightarrow$ How fast can the user accomplish goals? (**Efficiency**)
- **Driver satisfaction** $\rightarrow$ Does the user *enjoy* using the system? (**Satisfaction**)
- **Accident rate** $\rightarrow$ How often do users make mistakes? (**Error Rate**)

Just as a bridge without an engineer is dangerous, a user interface without usability engineering is **functional but frustrating**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 1000% Rule of Thumb:** Industry studies show that fixing a usability problem *after release* costs **10 times** more than fixing it during design, and up to **100 times** more than fixing it during requirements analysis. Usability Engineering is, therefore, an **economic imperative**, not just a UX luxury.

## 1.4 Why Engineering for Usability Matters

- **Reduces training costs** for end users.
- **Increases productivity** by minimizing friction.
- **Lowers support costs** (fewer help-desk calls).
- **Boosts adoption rates** of software products.
- **Mitigates safety risks** in medical, aviation, and industrial systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Usability Engineering Lifecycle (Nielsen's 8-Stage Model)

Nielsen's 1993 framework treats usability as a *quality attribute* to be engineered, not a *bolt-on* feature. The lifecycle is **iterative and parallel** to software development:

### Stage-by-Stage Breakdown

1. **Know the User**
   - Conduct field studies, interviews, surveys.
   - Build **user profiles** and **task profiles**.
   - *Why:* "You cannot design for users you do not understand."

2. **Competitive Analysis**
   - Inspect competitor products.
   - Identify **de facto standards** users already expect.
   - *Why:* Leveraging existing mental models reduces the learning curve.

3. **Setting Usability Goals**
   - Set **measurable** targets (e.g., "New users shall complete checkout in under 90 seconds").
   - *Why:* "What gets measured gets managed."

4. **Parallel Design**
   - Multiple independent designers create **competing solutions** simultaneously.
   - *Why:* Diverse exploration prevents anchoring bias on a single design idea.

5. **Participatory Design**
   - Involve **actual end users** as co-designers.
   - *Why:* Users are the ultimate experts on their own workflows.

6. **Coordinated Design of the Total Interface**
   - Ensure **consistency** across all parts (graphical UI, documentation, tutorials, help).
   - *Why:* A great login screen is wasted if the help manual is incomprehensible.

7. **Apply Guidelines and Heuristic Evaluation**
   - Use **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** (e.g., "Visibility of system status").
   - *Why:* Heuristics are distilled wisdom from thousands of prior designs.

8. **Prototype, Test, Refine, Repeat**
   - Build low-fidelity prototypes, conduct **usability tests**, measure metrics, iterate.
   - *Why:* "The design is never right the first time."

## 2.2 Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (The High-Yield List)

These are **extremely high-yield** for KTU short-answer questions:

1. **Visibility of system status** — The system should always keep users informed about what is going on.
2. **Match between system and the real world** — Speak the users' language, follow real-world conventions.
3. **User control and freedom** — Support "undo" and "redo."
4. **Consistency and standards** — Follow platform conventions.
5. **Error prevention** — Even better than good error messages is a careful design that prevents a problem from occurring.
6. **Recognition rather than recall** — Minimize the user's memory load by making objects, actions, and options visible.
7. **Flexibility and efficiency of use** — Accelerators (unseen by the novice user) may often speed up the interaction.
8. **Aesthetic and minimalist design** — Interfaces should not contain irrelevant or rarely-needed information.
9. **Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors** — Error messages in plain language, indicate the problem, suggest a solution.
10. **Help and documentation** — Provide easy-to-search, task-focused help.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following quantitative metrics are mandatory for KTU numerical problems in Module 1.

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Meaning |
|--------|---------|------|---------|
| **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** | $TCR = \frac{\text{Number of users who completed task}}{\text{Total number of users}} \times 100$ | **%** | Effectiveness |
| **Task Time (Mean)** | $T_{mean} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}{n}$ | **seconds** | Efficiency |
| **Task Time (Median)** | $T_{med} = T_{\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)}$ | **seconds** | Efficiency (robust to outliers) |
| **Errors per Task** | $E_{avg} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i}{n}$ | **errors** | Reliability |
| **Error Rate** | $ER = \frac{\text{Total errors}}{\text{Total opportunities for error}} \times 100$ | **%** | Reliability |
| **Time per Task (Learning-adjusted)** | $T_{adj} = T_{first} - T_{n}$ | **seconds** | Learnability |
| **Satisfaction Score (SUS)** | $SUS = \sum_{score_i} \times 2.5$ | **points (0-100)** | Satisfaction |
| **Efficiency Index** | $EI = \frac{\text{Goals achieved}}{\text{Time spent}}$ | **goals/sec** | Productivity |

> **Note on SUS (System Usability Scale):** Developed by **John Brooke (1996)**. It is a 10-item questionnaire with 5-point Likert scales. The composite score ranges from **0 to 100**, with **68 considered average** and **above 80 considered excellent**.

## 2.4 Discount Usability Methods (Nielsen, 1989)

Since full lab-based usability tests are expensive, Nielsen proposed four **low-cost rapid methods**:

1. **Scenarios** — Walk through realistic user stories.
2. **Simplified thinking aloud** — Have 3-5 users think aloud while using the system.
3. **Heuristic evaluation** — 3-5 experts apply the 10 heuristics; rule of thumb: **5 evaluators find ~85% of usability problems**.
4. **Query techniques** — Post-test questionnaires (e.g., SUS, QUIS).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Computing Task Completion Rate

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** During a usability test of a mobile banking app, **35 out of 40** users successfully completed the "transfer funds" task. Compute the **Task Completion Rate** and classify the system's effectiveness.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Number of successful users: $N_{success} = 35$
- Total number of users: $N_{total} = 40$

**Step 1:** Apply the Task Completion Rate formula.

$$
TCR = \frac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
TCR = \frac{35}{40} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the fraction.

$$
TCR = 0.875 \times 100 = 87.5\%
$$

**Step 4:** Classify the result using the **KTU Industry Benchmark**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCR} < 70\% &\Rightarrow \text{Poor (Critical Redesign Needed)} \\
70\% \leq \text{TCR} < 85\% &\Rightarrow \text{Average (Iterative Improvement)} \\
85\% \leq \text{TCR} < 95\% &\Rightarrow \text{Good (Minor Polish)} \\
\text{TCR} \geq 95\% &\Rightarrow \text{Excellent (Industry-Leading)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $TCR = 87.5\% \rightarrow$ **Good** — The system is effective, with only minor polish required.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Mean Task Time & Efficiency

**Problem Statement:** Five users completed a checkout task. Their times in seconds were: **45, 60, 50, 55, 90**. Calculate the **mean task time** and the **median task time**, and discuss which is more robust to the **outlier** (90 sec).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the mean formula.

$$
T_{mean} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}{n} = \frac{45 + 60 + 50 + 55 + 90}{5}
$$

$$
T_{mean} = \frac{300}{5} = 60 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2:** Sort the data in ascending order: **45, 50, 55, 60, 90**.

**Step 3:** Apply the median formula for odd $n$.

$$
T_{med} = T_{\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)} = T_{\left(\frac{5+1}{2}\right)} = T_{3}
$$

**Step 4:** Read the 3rd value from the sorted list.

$$
T_{med} = 55 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5:** Compare and conclude.

The **mean (60 s)** is pulled upward by the outlier (90 s). The **median (55 s)** is **robust** and better represents the typical user experience. Therefore, for small samples with outliers, **KTH-recommended practice is to report the median**.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Error Rate Calculation

**Problem Statement:** During testing, a "search" interface was used **200** times. Users made a total of **30 errors**, and each task contained **2 opportunities for error**. Compute the **average errors per task** and the **error rate**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Total opportunities for error: $O = 200 \times 2 = 400$
- Total errors observed: $E_{total} = 30$
- Number of users: $n = 200$

**Step 1:** Compute average errors per task.

$$
E_{avg} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i}{n} = \frac{30}{200} = 0.15 \text{ errors per task}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the error rate.

$$
ER = \frac{E_{total}}{O} \times 100 = \frac{30}{400} \times 100
$$

$$
ER = 0.075 \times 100 = 7.5\%
$$

**Result:** Average errors = **0.15 per task**; Error rate = **7.5%**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Method

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Reason |
|----------|--------------------|--------|
| Tight budget, early stage | Heuristic Evaluation | Cheap, fast, no users needed |
| Need deep behavioral insight | Think-Aloud Test | Reveals *why* users struggle |
| Need statistical confidence | Quantitative Test (large $n$) | Hypothesis testing, benchmarking |
| Need to know *feelings* | SUS / QUIS Questionnaire | Captures subjective satisfaction |
| Mission-critical UI (medical, aviation) | Full Lab Test + Heuristic + Field | Maximum coverage of failure modes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Nielsen's Usability Engineering Lifecycle (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Know the User] --> B[Stage 2: Competitive Analysis]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Set Usability Goals]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Parallel Design]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Participatory Design]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Coordinated Total Interface Design]
    F --> G[Stage 7: Heuristic Evaluation and Guidelines]
    G --> H[Stage 8: Prototype, Test, Refine, Repeat]
    H --> I{Meets Usability Goals?}
    I -- No --> D
    I -- Yes --> J[Final Usable Product]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#fff4e1
    style F fill:#e1ffe1
    style G fill:#e1ffe1
    style H fill:#ffe1e1
    style I fill:#f0e1ff
    style J fill:#d4ffd4
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Blue** $\rightarrow$ Discovery & Goal-Setting
- **Orange** $\rightarrow$ Ideation & Co-Design
- **Green** $\rightarrow$ Refinement & Standards
- **Red** $\rightarrow$ Empirical Testing (the iterative engine)
- **Purple** $\rightarrow$ Decision Gate

---

## 4.2 The Iterative Design Loop (Test-Improve Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Prototype] --> T[Usability Test]
    T --> M[Measure Metrics]
    M --> A{Goals Met?}
    A -- No --> F[Fix Problems Identified]
    F --> P
    A -- Yes --> D[Deploy System]
    D --> FL[Field Studies Real World Use]
    FL --> P

    style P fill:#ffe1e1
    style T fill:#fff4e1
    style M fill:#e1f5ff
    style A fill:#f0e1ff
    style F fill:#ffe1f5
    style D fill:#d4ffd4
    style FL fill:#e1ffe1
```

**Key Insight:** Even after deployment, real-world field studies feed back into the next prototype generation. This is the **spiral model of usability** — it never truly ends, because user needs and contexts evolve.

---

## 4.3 The Five Usability Attributes (Hierarchical Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[Usability] --> L[Learnability]
    U --> E[Efficiency]
    U --> M[Memorability]
    U --> Err[Low Errors]
    U --> S[Satisfaction]

    L --> L1[First-time task success]
    L --> L2[Short learning curve]
    E --> E1[Fast task completion]
    E --> E2[High throughput]
    M --> M1[Easy to remember after a break]
    M --> M2[Stable mental model]
    Err --> Err1[Low error frequency]
    Err --> Err2[Easy error recovery]
    S --> S1[High SUS score]
    S --> S2[Positive user feedback]

    style U fill:#d4ffd4
    style L fill:#e1f5ff
    style E fill:#e1f5ff
    style M fill:#e1f5ff
    style Err fill:#e1f5ff
    style S fill:#e1f5ff
    style L1 fill:#fff4e1
    style L2 fill:#fff4e1
    style E1 fill:#fff4e1
    style E2 fill:#fff4e1
    style M1 fill:#fff4e1
    style M2 fill:#fff4e1
    style Err1 fill:#fff4e1
    style Err2 fill:#fff4e1
    style S1 fill:#fff4e1
    style S2 fill:#fff4e1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Usability. List and briefly explain Nielsen's five attributes of usability.**

**Model Answer:**

Usability, as defined by **ISO 9241-11**, is the extent to which a product can be used by specified users to achieve specified goals with **effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction** in a specified context of use.

Nielsen's **five attributes** are:
1. **Learnability** — Ease of learning basic tasks on first use.
2. **Efficiency** — Speed of task completion after learning.
3. **Memorability** — Ability to re-learn after a period of non-use.
4. **Errors** — Frequency, severity, and recovery from mistakes.
5. **Satisfaction** — Subjective pleasantness of the design.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Discount Usability Engineering? List its four main techniques.**

**Model Answer:**

Discount Usability Engineering, proposed by **Jakob Nielsen (1989)**, is a set of low-cost, rapid techniques that deliver substantial usability benefits without the time and expense of full-scale formal usability testing.

The four techniques are:
1. **Scenarios** — Using realistic user stories to walk through the design.
2. **Simplified Thinking Aloud** — Having a small number of users (3-5) verbalize their thoughts while using the system.
3. **Heuristic Evaluation** — 3-5 expert evaluators apply Nielsen's 10 heuristics.
4. **Query Techniques** — Questionnaires like the **System Usability Scale (SUS)** administered after the test.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail Nielsen's **Usability Engineering Lifecycle** with its eight stages.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain any **five** of Nielsen's ten usability heuristics with a real-world example for each.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

Nielsen's Usability Engineering Lifecycle consists of **eight stages**:

1. **Know the User:** Conduct field studies, interviews, and surveys to understand user characteristics, current tasks, and pain points. Output: **User profiles** and **task profiles**.

2. **Competitive Analysis:** Study competing products to identify de facto standards and best practices. This leverages users' existing mental models.

3. **Setting Usability Goals:** Define **quantitative, measurable** usability targets. For example: "90% of new users shall complete the registration in under 3 minutes."

4. **Parallel Design:** Have multiple independent designers create competing solutions in parallel. This avoids group-think and produces diverse design alternatives.

5. **Participatory Design:** Actively involve end users as co-designers throughout the design process.

6. **Coordinated Design of the Total Interface:** Ensure consistency across the GUI, documentation, tutorials, and error messages.

7. **Apply Guidelines and Heuristic Evaluation:** Apply Nielsen's 10 heuristics and design guidelines to identify usability issues early.

8. **Prototype, Test, Refine, Repeat:** Build low-fidelity prototypes, conduct usability tests with real users, measure metrics, fix problems, and **iterate**.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

1. **Visibility of system status:** The system should always keep users informed. *Example:* A progress bar while uploading a file.

2. **Match between system and the real world:** Speak the user's language. *Example:* A shopping cart icon, not a "buffer zone."

3. **User control and freedom:** Provide undo/redo. *Example:* Ctrl+Z in word processors.

4. **Consistency and standards:** Follow platform conventions. *Example:* A red "X" always means close.

5. **Error prevention:** Prevent errors before they occur. *Example:* A confirmation dialog before deleting files.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **usability test**? Describe the steps involved in conducting a usability test for a mobile application.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A usability test was conducted with **20 users** for a "password reset" feature. **16 users** completed the task successfully, **4 users** failed. The mean time to complete (for successful users) was **45 seconds**, with a **standard deviation** of **8 seconds**. Calculate the **Task Completion Rate**, and comment on the system's **effectiveness and efficiency**.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

A **usability test** is a **user-centered evaluation method** where representative users perform real tasks on a system, while observers collect data on performance, errors, and satisfaction.

**Steps to conduct a usability test:**

1. **Define goals and objectives** of the test (what to measure).
2. **Recruit representative participants** (5-10 typical users).
3. **Design realistic tasks** that reflect actual user goals.
4. **Prepare the test environment** (lab or remote setup).
5. **Conduct a pilot test** to validate the protocol.
6. **Run the test** — Observe and record (think-aloud, video, screen capture).
7. **Analyze the data** (time-on-task, error rates, completion rates).
8. **Generate the report** with prioritized recommendations.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $N_{total} = 20$ users
- $N_{success} = 16$ users
- $N_{failure} = 4$ users
- $T_{mean} = 45$ seconds
- $\sigma = 8$ seconds

**Step 1:** Compute the Task Completion Rate.

$$
TCR = \frac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{16}{20} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**Step 2:** Classify effectiveness.

Using the KTU benchmark: $TCR = 80\%$ falls in the **Average (70\%-85\%)** band $\rightarrow$ Iterative improvement is needed.

**Step 3:** Comment on efficiency.

The mean time of **45 seconds** with a standard deviation of **$\sigma = 8$** seconds indicates a moderately efficient process. The 1-$\sigma$ confidence interval is:

$$
CI_{68\%} = T_{mean} \pm \sigma = [37, 53] \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

- **Effectiveness:** **80%** — Average. The 4 failed users (20%) indicate the password reset flow has usability barriers that need investigation.
- **Efficiency:** **Mean 45 s, $\sigma = 8$ s** — Acceptable, but the spread suggests **inconsistency** in user performance, possibly due to confusing instructions or unclear error messages.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing usability with utility.** Utility = *does it do the right thing?* Usability = *does it do the thing right?* Marks are lost for missing this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Always state "87.5%" or "45 seconds" — bare numbers lose marks.
> 3. **Memorizing heuristics without examples.** KTU examiners award marks for the *real-world example* alongside the definition.
> 4. **Skipping the iteration step.** When asked about the lifecycle, the "Prototype-Test-Refine-**Repeat**" loop is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Usability = **Effectiveness + Efficiency + Satisfaction** (ISO 9241-11).
- **Nielsen's 5 Attributes:** **L**earnability, **E**fficiency, **M**emorability, **E**rrors, **S**atisfaction $\rightarrow$ Mnemonic: **"L-E-M-E-S"**.
- **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics:** Memorize at least 7-8 for Part A questions.
- **Lifecycle has 8 stages** and is **iterative**, not linear.
- **Discount Usability:** 4 cheap methods — Scenarios, Thinking Aloud, Heuristic Eval, Query.
- **SUS Score:** 0-100 scale, **68 is average**, **>80 is excellent**.
- **5 Evaluators find ~85% of usability problems** (Nielsen's law of diminishing returns).
- **Key Formulas:** TCR, $T_{mean}$, $T_{med}$, $E_{avg}$, $ER$, $EI$ — all are examinable.
- **Rule of thumb:** Fixing usability issues **after release** = 10x cost; **in design** = 1x cost. **Usability Engineering is an economic decision.**
- **Iteration is the soul of usability engineering** — never present it as a one-shot waterfall process.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interactive System Life Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interactive System Life Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interactive System Life Cycle (ISLC):** The Interactive System Life Cycle refers to the structured, multi-stage process of planning, analyzing, designing, implementing, and evaluating an interactive computer-based system from its initial conception to deployment, maintenance, and eventual retirement. It is the methodological backbone of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) engineering and ensures that the resultant system is **usable, useful, and learnable** for the end user.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Design and Implementation of Human Computer Interfaces*, the Interactive System Life Cycle is broadly classified into the following canonical phases:

1. **Need Analysis / Concept Formulation**
2. **Requirements Analysis & Specification**
3. **Design (Conceptual, Logical, Physical)**
4. **Implementation / Prototyping**
5. **Evaluation (Formative & Summative)**
6. **Deployment & Maintenance**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Life Cycle"?** The word *life cycle* (borrowed from biology and software engineering) emphasizes that the system has a **birth, growth, maturity, and end-of-life** — and that HCI design is not a one-time event but a continuous, iterative, and evolutionary process.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Building & Renovating a House:**
>
