The total elapsed time per session is **≈ 2 hours**, but the **debrief** must occur **within an hour** of session end — delaying it destroys recall fidelity.

---

### **Question B — (14 Marks)**
**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the **master–apprentice model** in Contextual Inquiry? Explain the roles and responsibilities of the master and the apprentice during a CI session.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **seven question types** a Contextual Inquiry researcher uses, with one example of each. Which question type is forbidden and why?

#### **Model Solution for B(a):**

The **master–apprentice model** is the conceptual heart of CI, borrowed from medieval craft guilds. It reframes a power-asymmetric interview as a learning-asymmetric apprenticeship.

**Master (the User):**
- Owns the craft — knows the work inside-out.
- Sets the pace — decides which tasks to perform and when.
- Reveals **tacit knowledge** — the unspoken know-how (which shortcut key, which paper register, which workaround).
- **Corrects** the researcher's misunderstandings in real time.
- Is treated with respect; their work is never judged or corrected by the researcher.

**Apprentice (the Researcher):**
- **Follows** the master's work — does not lead, does not redirect.
- Asks short, **in-context probes** triggered by observation.
- Does **NOT** take over the keyboard, mouse, or task.
- Does **NOT** ask hypothetical, leading, or judgmental questions.
- Continuously **interprets** — narrates observations back to the master for validation.
- Captures field notes during the debrief, **not** during the live session.

The model ensures that the researcher captures **work as it is**, not **work as it should be**.

#### **Model Solution for B(b):**

The seven question types are:

| # | Question Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Establishing context** | *"What are you working on today?"* |
| 2 | **Current activity** | *"What are you doing right now?"* |
| 3 | **Probe** | *"Why did you switch tabs just now?"* |
| 4 | **Detail** | *"What does that red icon mean?"* |
| 5 | **Clarification** | *"By 'process' you mean the email or the order?"* |
| 6 | **Comparison** | *"Is this a typical day?"* |
| 7 | **Influence** | *"What changes if the printer is offline?"* |

**Forbidden question form:** **Hypothetical questions** of the form *"What would you do if...?"* are forbidden as primary probes because they drive **speculation** rather than the user's **actual practice**. They may be used **only** in the wrap-up phase to surface variations.

Other discouraged forms include **leading questions** ("Don't you think the menu is confusing?") which bias the user, and **closed yes/no questions** which kill probing depth.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in CI Answers (KTU 2024 ESE):**
> 
> 1. **Defining CI as "just an interview"** — examiner deducts 3 marks for missing the field / context / apprenticeship pillars.
> 2. **Listing only 3 principles** — full marks require **all 4** (Context, Partnership, Interpretation, Focus).
> 3. **Confusing CI phases with the Contextual Design phases** — CI has 4 session phases; Contextual Design has 8 project phases.
> 4. **Mixing observation and interpretation in field notes** — examiner expects raw observation notes **separate** from interpretation notes.
> 5. **Omitting the debrief duration** — examiners specifically look for *"within 60 minutes"* or equivalent wording.
> 6. **Calling CI a usability-testing method** — they are at opposite ends of the UCD lifecycle.
> 7. **Writing "the user is a subject"** — wrong framing; the user is a *master* and *partner*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 1-Page Cheat Sheet for the Night Before the Exam**

- **Contextual Inquiry (CI)** = field-based, qualitative, user-centered research method.
- **Origin:** Karen Holtzblatt & Hugh Beyer, 1998 (Contextual Design methodology).
- **Core stance:** *People do not do what they say they do.*
- **Four Principles (CPFI):** **C**ontext, **P**artnership, **I**nterpretation, **F**ocus.
- **Master–Apprentice Model:** User = master; researcher = apprentice.
- **Four Session Phases:** Transition (3–5 min) → Inquiry (45–90 min) → Wrap-Up (5–10 min) → Debrief (within 60 min).
- **Seven Question Types:** Establishing context, Current activity, Probe, Detail, Clarification, Comparison, Influence.
- **Forbidden Questions:** Hypotheticals, leading questions, closed yes/no as primary probes.
- **3-second rule:** Wait 3 seconds after an interesting event before probing — user often self-narrates.
- **Sample size:** **3–5 users per role** for saturation.
- **Field note format (CIDL++):** ⟨User ID, Role, Activity, Quote, Interpretation, Breakdown Flag⟩.
- **Five Work Models:** Flow, Sequence, Artefact, Cultural, Physical.
- **Synthesis technique:** Affinity Diagram (bottom-up clustering).
- **Output of CI:** Design requirements + user stories + design ideas + testable hypotheses.
- **CI ≠ Usability Testing:** CI is **before** design; UT is **after** prototype.
- **CI ≠ Traditional Interview:** CI observes real work; traditional interview asks about recalled work.
- **Ethical pillar:** Explicit consent before audio/video recording.
- **KTU word to remember for Part A:** *"Contextual Inquiry is a field-based, master–apprentice, semi-structured interview conducted in the user's workplace."* (≈ 15 words — fits a 3-mark definition slot.)
- **KTU phrase to remember for Part B:** *"Four principles (Context, Partnership, Interpretation, Focus) and four session phases (Transition, Inquiry, Wrap-Up, Debrief) producing five work models (Flow, Sequence, Artefact, Cultural, Physical) synthesized via Affinity Diagram."*

> **End of Notes — Contextual Inquiry (Module 1) | KTU PEPET861 | 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Functional Requirements # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the discipline of **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** and software engineering, **Functional Requirements (FRs)** constitute the detailed, prescriptive statements that explicitly define the **specific behaviors, functions, capabilities, and operations** that a system must support in order to enable users to accomplish their intended tasks. They describe **what the system does**, not how it does it. According to the **IEEE Standard 830 / IEEE 29148:2018** specification language adopted within KTU's Software Engineering and HCI curriculum, a functional requirement is defined as: > *"A statement that describes a specific behavior, function, or capability that the system must exhibit under specified conditions, expressed in terms of an input, the behavior, and the corresponding output."* Within the **HCI design lifecycle**, functional requirements form the **bridge between user needs (gathered through user research)** and **system implementation (carried out by developers)**. They translate abstract user goals into concrete, testable, and verifiable system behaviors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Module 1 Anchor Concept** > Functional Requirements in HCI focus on **the user-system interaction loop** — the *inputs the user provides*, the *processing the system performs*, and the *outputs/feedback the system returns*. Every FR must be **traceable to a user need** identified during requirements elicitation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are **designing the cockpit of a commercial aircraft**: - A **pilot** needs to perform tasks: "know the altitude", "communicate with ATC", "change course", "engage autopilot". - Each of these tasks translates into a **functional requirement**: - "The system shall display the current altitude on the Primary Flight Display." - "The system shall allow voice and text communication with Air Traffic Control." - "The system shall permit course modification through the Flight Management System." The functional requirements are the **verbs and actions** of the system — *what buttons, screens, gestures, voice commands, and feedback loops the user will encounter*. They do **NOT** describe *how fast* the display updates (that is a **non-functional requirement** like performance) or *how aesthetically pleasing* the cockpit is (that is **usability**). > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary** > Functional Requirements = **"What the system must DO so that the user can DO what they want to DO."** > In HCI terms, they are the *capabilities* and *user actions* the interface must support. ## 1.3 Core Distinction: Functional vs. Non-Functional Requirements A foundational concept in HCI and software engineering is the **separation of concerns** between *what* the system does and *how well* it does it. The table below formalizes this distinction: | Aspect | Functional Requirements | Non-Functional Requirements | |---|---|---| | **Defines** | What the system does | How the system performs | | **Nature** | Verifiable behaviors, features, actions | Quality attributes, constraints | | **HCI Focus** | Tasks, dialogue, feedback, control | Usability, accessibility, efficiency | | **Example** | "System shall allow password reset via email" | "Password reset must complete within 30 seconds" | | **Tested by** | Functional testing, acceptance testing | Performance, load, usability testing | | **Measured in** | Pass / Fail (binary) | Metrics (e.g., ms, %, SUS score) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In Module 1 questions, students frequently confuse usability requirements (non-functional) with functional ones. **If the requirement describes a quality, attribute, or constraint — it is NON-FUNCTIONAL.** If it describes a behavior, action, or feature — it is FUNCTIONAL. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Functional Requirements in HCI Systems Functional requirements in HCI are classified into **five primary categories**, each representing a distinct aspect of the user-system dialogue: ### 2.1.1 Input Requirements These specify **what data the user can provide** to the system and through which modality (keyboard, mouse, touch, voice, gesture, eye-tracking). - **Why:** Define the user's vocabulary of interaction. - **How:** Enumerated as user actions → system interpretation pairs. - **Example:** "The interface shall accept user input via touchscreen taps, swipe gestures, and voice commands." ### 2.1.2 Output Requirements These specify **what information the system presents** to the user and in what format (visual, auditory, haptic). - **Why:** Define the system's communication vocabulary. - **How:** Defined as system state → user-perceivable representation. - **Example:** "The system shall display a confirmation dialog after successful form submission." ### 2.1.3 Processing Requirements These specify **transformations, calculations, and business logic** the system performs on user inputs. - **Why:** Define the system's intelligence and logic layer. - **How:** Described as input → transformation → output pipelines. - **Example:** "The system shall calculate the total bill including applicable taxes and discounts." ### 2.1.4 Storage / Data Requirements These specify **what data the system persists, retrieves, and manipulates**. - **Why:** Define the system's memory and recall capabilities. - **How:** Defined as data entities, attributes, and relationships. - **Example:** "The system shall store user profile information including name, email, and preferences." ### 2.1.5 Control / Interaction Flow Requirements These specify **the sequence, navigation, and dialogue structure** of user interactions. - **Why:** Define the user journey and screen flow. - **How:** Modeled via use cases, sequence diagrams, state diagrams. - **Example:** "The system shall redirect unauthenticated users to the login page upon session expiry." ## 2.2 Properties of Well-Formed Functional Requirements (KTU High-Yield) Every functional requirement that scores full marks in KTU examinations must exhibit the following **five quality properties**, derived from the **SMART** and **INVEST** criteria: | Property | Description | Example (Good vs. Bad) | |---|---|---| | **Correct** | Accurately reflects user need | ❌ "Login should be fast" → ✅ "Login shall succeed within 2 seconds under 1000 concurrent users" | | **Unambiguous** | Single, clear interpretation | ❌ "User-friendly form" → ✅ "Form shall provide inline validation for all mandatory fields" | | **Complete** | All conditions and exceptions specified | ❌ "System shall allow password change" → ✅ "System shall allow password change with old password verification, meeting complexity rules" | | **Verifiable** | Can be tested objectively | ❌ "Good performance" → ✅ "Page load time ≤ 1.5 s on 4G" | | **Traceable** | Linked to a source user need or use case | Tied to User Story ID: US-014 | > [!NOTE] > **The "Shall" Convention** > KTU-accepted functional requirements **MUST** use the auxiliary verb **"shall"** (never "must", "should", or "will"). This is the IEEE 830 / 29148 normative phrasing and is the **first thing KTU examiners look for** in answer sheets. ## 2.3 The Role of Functional Requirements in the HCI Design Lifecycle Functional requirements are positioned at the **Critical Juncture** between User-Centered Design (UCD) phases: 1. **Requirements Elicitation** (interviews, observations, focus groups) → produces *user needs*. 2. **Requirements Specification** → transforms user needs into *functional requirements*. 3. **Design Phase** → functional requirements inform *information architecture, interaction design, and prototyping*. 4. **Implementation** → developers build *features* matching each FR. 5. **Evaluation** → usability testing verifies that each FR is satisfied. ## 2.4 KTU Formula / Framework Cheat Sheet | # | Framework / Formula | Purpose in HCI | Notation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{FR} : \text{Trigger} \rightarrow \text{Behavior} \rightarrow \text{Output}$ | Canonical FR statement form | $F = (T, B, O)$ | | 2 | $\text{Total FRs} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} FR_i$ | Requirements completeness metric | $n$ = number of use cases | | 3 | $\text{Coverage} = \frac{\vert \text{FRs Implemented} \vert}{\vert \text{FRs Specified} \vert} \times 100\%$ | Traceability / completeness ratio | $0 \leq C \leq 1$ | | 4 | $\text{Ambiguity Index} = \frac{\text{Adjectives/Adverbs}}{\text{Total Words}}$ | Lower is better; ideal ≈ 0 | $AI < 0.05$ | | 5 | $\text{Use Case Coverage} = \frac{\vert \text{FRs linked to Use Cases} \vert}{\vert \text{Total FRs} \vert}$ | FR-to-Use-Case traceability | $UCC \geq 0.95$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No vertical bars `|` inside table cells** — use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. The above table follows this rule. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Functional requirements drive **every** interactive system you interact with daily: - **Automotive HMI** (e.g., Tesla infotainment): FRs define touch, voice, and gesture controls for climate, navigation, and media. - **Medical Devices** (e.g., infusion pumps): FRs define dose entry, alarm acknowledgment, and override protocols — these are **safety-critical** and regulated by **FDA / IEC 62366**. - **Smart Home Interfaces**: FRs define voice command grammar, scene activation rules, and device pairing workflows. - **Assistive Technologies**: FRs define screen reader compatibility, switch input, and alternative interaction modalities. - **Aviation Cockpits**: FRs define Glass Cockpit displays, mode transitions, and alert prioritization — **DO-178C** compliance mandates traceability. In every case, **defective or missing functional requirements** lead to system failures, user frustration, regulatory non-compliance, and — in safety-critical domains — loss of life. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough ## 3.1 Worked Example: Deriving Functional Requirements from User Needs This section demonstrates **the complete transformation chain** from raw user statements to validated, traceable functional requirements — the exact process KTU examiners reward in 14-mark answers. ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup > **Scenario:** A university wants a **Student Portal HCI** that allows students to register for courses, view grades, and receive notifications. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: User Need Elicitation (Raw User Voice)** From user interviews, we capture the following raw needs: - *Student A:* "I want to add courses to my semester." - *Student B:* "I want to check my marks anytime." - *Student C:* "I want to know when my results are published." **Step 2: Categorization into Functional Requirement Domains** We map each raw need to a category from Section 2.1: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Need A} &\rightarrow \text{Input + Control + Storage (Course Registration)} \\ \text{Need B} &\rightarrow \text{Output + Storage (Grade Viewing)} \\ \text{Need C} &\rightarrow \text{Output + Control (Notification System)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Conversion to Formal FR Statements using the IEEE "Shall" Template** Following the canonical **Trigger → Behavior → Output** structure: $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{FR-001:} \quad & \text{The system shall allow an authenticated student to register} \\ & \text{for courses by selecting from the available catalog and submitting} \\ & \text{a registration request, subject to prerequisite and credit-limit checks.} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{FR-002:} \quad & \text{The system shall display the authenticated student's grade} \\ & \text{report for all completed courses, organized by semester, upon} \\ & \text{request from the "My Grades" interface.} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{FR-003:} \quad & \text{The system shall send a real-time push notification to the} \\ & \text{authenticated student's mobile and web interfaces when new} \\ & \text{grades are published by an authorized instructor.} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Verification via the Five Property Checklist** | Property | FR-001 | FR-002 | FR-003 | |---|---|---|---| | Correct | ✓ Aligned with need A | ✓ | ✓ | | Unambiguous | ✓ No vague terms | ✓ | ✓ | | Complete | ✓ Includes constraints | ✓ Specifies view structure | ✓ Specifies channels & trigger | | Verifiable | ✓ Testable via test case | ✓ | ✓ | | Traceable | → Need A, UC-001 | → Need B, UC-002 | → Need C, UC-003 | **Step 5: Coverage Calculation** Suppose the team defined 3 user needs and 3 FRs. The **Coverage Ratio** is: $$ \text{Coverage} = \frac{\vert \text{FRs Specified} \vert}{\vert \text{User Needs} \vert} = \frac{3}{3} = 1.0 \quad (100\%) $$ This is the **ideal traceability** — every need has a corresponding FR, and every FR maps back to a need. **Step 6: Ambiguity Index Check (Optional but high-yield)** For FR-001, suppose the statement contains 42 words with 0 adjectives/adverbs (all verbs and nouns): $$ AI = \frac{0}{42} = 0.00 \quad (\text{Excellent — target } AI < 0.05) $$ If a poorly-worded version said *"The system should provide a nice, user-friendly, fast course registration feature"*, then $AI = 3/11 \approx 0.27$ — **unacceptable**. > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Insight** > The transformation chain $\text{User Need} \rightarrow \text{Category} \rightarrow \text{"Shall" Statement} \rightarrow \text{Verification}$ is the **gold standard answer structure** for KTU 14-mark questions on Functional Requirements. Memorize this flow. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Use Case vs. Functional Requirement A frequent KTU exam pitfall is the conflation of **Use Cases** and **Functional Requirements**. The matrix below resolves this: | Dimension | Use Case | Functional Requirement | |---|---|---| | **Granularity** | High-level user goal | Specific, atomic behavior | | **Scope** | One per user goal | Many per use case | | **Format** | Narrative + diagram | Structured "shall" statement | | **Audience** | Stakeholders, designers | Developers, testers | | **Example** | "Register for Courses" | "FR-001: System shall validate prerequisites before registration" | **Derivation rule:** A single use case typically decomposes into **3–10 functional requirements**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FRs per Use Case} &= \text{Inputs} + \text{Validations} + \text{Processing} + \text{Outputs} + \text{Error Handling} \\ &\approx 3 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 10 \text{ (typical upper bound)} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Common Pitfall: Mixing Functional and Non-Functional Requirements | Statement | Type | Reason | |---|---|---| | "System shall allow login" | **Functional** | Describes a behavior | | "Login shall complete within 2 seconds" | **Non-Functional (Performance)** | Describes a quality | | "System shall be accessible to users with disabilities" | **Non-Functional (Accessibility)** | Quality attribute | | "System shall provide screen reader support for all forms" | **Functional** | Specific behavior | | "Forms shall be usable by novice users" | **Non-Functional (Usability)** | Quality attribute | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Requirement Derivation Pipeline (Mermaid Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[User Research
Interviews, Observations] --> B1[Raw User Needs
Verbatim Statements] B1 --> C1[Categorization
Input, Output, Processing,
Storage, Control] C1 --> D1[Drafting FR Statements
IEEE Shall Format] D1 --> E1[Verification
Correct, Unambiguous,
Complete, Verifiable, Traceable] E1 --> F1[Approved Functional
Requirements Specification] F1 --> G1[Traceability Matrix
Need to FR to Test Case] G1 --> H1[Implementation
by Development Team] E1 -->|Reject| D1 H1 -->|Defect Found| F1 style A1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000000 style B1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000000 style C1 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#000000 style D1 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000000 style E1 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000000 style F1 fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00838F,color:#000000 style G1 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,color:#000000 style H1 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F,color:#000000 ``` **Interpretation:** The diagram traces the **lifecycle of a Functional Requirement** from initial user research to final implementation. The **feedback loop** from verification back to drafting is critical — it represents **iterative refinement**, a core principle of the User-Centered Design (UCD) process. ## 4.2 Five-Category Classification Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR FR[Functional
Requirements] --> IN[Input
Requirements] FR --> OUT[Output
Requirements] FR --> PROC[Processing
Requirements] FR --> STO[Storage
Requirements] FR --> CTRL[Control
Requirements] IN --> INex[User Actions
Gestures, Voice, Touch] OUT --> OUTex[Visual, Audio,
Haptic Feedback] PROC --> PROex[Calculations,
Validations, Logic] STO --> STOex[CRUD Operations,
Data Persistence] CTRL --> CTRLex[Navigation,
Dialogue Flow,
Session Management] style FR fill:#1565C0,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#FFFFFF style IN fill:#4FC3F7,stroke:#0288D1,color:#000000 style OUT fill:#81C784,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000000 style PROC fill:#FFB74D,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000000 style STO fill:#BA68C8,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#000000 style CTRL fill:#E57373,stroke:#D32F2F,color:#000000 ``` **Interpretation:** Functional Requirements decompose into **five orthogonal categories**, each representing a distinct dimension of the user-system dialogue. Every FR in any system can be classified under one or more of these categories. ## 4.3 Use Case Decomposition into Functional Requirements (Mermaid Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD UC1[Use Case 1
Course Registration] --> FR1a[FR-1.1
Display Course Catalog] UC1 --> FR1b[FR-1.2
Validate Prerequisites] UC1 --> FR1c[FR-1.3
Check Credit Limit] UC1 --> FR1d[FR-1.4
Submit Registration] UC1 --> FR1e[FR-1.5
Display Confirmation] UC2[Use Case 2
Grade Viewing] --> FR2a[FR-2.1
Authenticate Student] UC2 --> FR2b[FR-2.2
Retrieve Grade Records] UC2 --> FR2c[FR-2.3
Display Semester-wise Grades] UC2 --> FR2d[FR-2.4
Download Transcript PDF] style UC1 fill:#1976D2,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#FFFFFF style UC2 fill:#388E3C,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#FFFFFF style FR1a fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000000 style FR1b fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000000 style FR1c fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000000 style FR1d fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000000 style FR1e fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000000 style FR2a fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000000 style FR2b fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000000 style FR2c fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000000 style FR2d fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000000 ``` **Interpretation:** Each **Use Case** (a high-level user goal) decomposes into **multiple atomic Functional Requirements**. This is the **primary decomposition pattern** used in HCI requirements engineering and a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU examinations. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define Functional Requirements in the context of HCI. Give two examples.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Functional Requirements are precise statements that describe the **specific behaviors, features, and capabilities** that an interactive system must provide in order to enable users to accomplish their intended tasks. They define **what the system does** in response to user inputs, specifying the data processed, operations performed, and outputs generated. Functional requirements are **distinct from non-functional requirements** (which specify quality attributes like performance and usability). **Examples (1 mark):** 1. *The system shall allow users to reset their password via a registered email address.* 2. *The system shall display a confirmation message upon successful order placement.* --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between Functional Requirements and Non-Functional Requirements with examples.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Functional Requirements | Non-Functional Requirements | |---|---|---| | **Focus** | What the system does | How well the system performs | | **Example 1** | "The system shall allow users to upload a profile picture" | "The uploaded profile picture shall be displayed within 1 second" | | **Example 2** | "The system shall provide a search bar on the home page" | "The search bar shall be accessible to users with screen readers" | Functional requirements are **binary (pass/fail) testable**, while non-functional requirements are **metric-based** (measured against quantitative thresholds). **(1 mark for differentiation + 1 mark per example pair)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### **Question A: Functional Requirements for a Smart Home HMI System** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Q(a). Identify and explain any four categories of Functional Requirements with suitable examples from a Smart Home HMI system (e.g., controlling lights, thermostats, and security cameras).** **Model Solution (7 marks):** The four categories of functional requirements demonstrated in a Smart Home HMI are: **1. Input Requirements (2 marks):** These define the modalities through which the user communicates with the system. - *Example:* "The Smart Home HMI shall accept user commands via touch inputs on a wall-mounted control panel, voice commands through a smart speaker, and mobile app gestures." **2. Output Requirements (1.5 marks):** These define the feedback the system provides to the user. - *Example:* "The system shall display the current room temperature, humidity, and lighting status on the home dashboard in real-time." **3. Processing Requirements (1.5 marks):** These define the logic and transformations. - *Example:* "The system shall automatically adjust the thermostat to 22°C when the user is detected as 'at home' and lower it to 18°C when 'away'." **4. Control / Interaction Flow Requirements (2 marks):** These define the dialogue and navigation structure. - *Example:* "When a security camera detects motion, the system shall display a real-time alert on the user's mobile app and require explicit acknowledgment to dismiss the alarm." --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Q(b). Apply the IEEE "shall" format to derive five Functional Requirements for the Smart Home HMI's "Energy Management" feature. Justify each requirement against the five quality properties of well-formed FRs.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Derived Functional Requirements (5 marks, 1 mark each):** **FR-EM-01:** *The Smart Home HMI shall display the real-time power consumption (in Watts) of all connected appliances on the Energy Dashboard.* **FR-EM-02:** *The system shall allow the user to set a daily energy budget (in kWh) and shall display a warning notification when consumption reaches 80% of the budget.* **FR-EM-03:** *The system shall automatically switch off non-essential appliances when the daily energy budget is exceeded, and shall log each such event in the activity history.* **FR-EM-04:** *The system shall generate a monthly energy consumption report in PDF format, broken down by appliance, and shall email it to the registered user by the 1st of each month.* **FR-EM-05:** *The system shall allow the user to schedule appliance activation/deactivation times (e.g., for the washing machine, water heater) through a 7-day calendar interface.* **Quality Property Justification (2 marks — pick any 2 FRs and justify):** For **FR-EM-02**: - **Correct:** Addresses the genuine user need of budget control. - **Unambiguous:** Specifies both the threshold (80%) and the action (warning notification). - **Complete:** Defines the input (budget), the trigger condition (80% reached), and the output (warning). - **Verifiable:** Can be tested by setting a budget and observing the warning trigger. - **Traceable:** Links to user need "control my electricity bill" and Use Case UC-EM-02. --- ### **Question B: Functional Requirements for a Hospital Patient Monitoring HCI** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Q(a). Define Functional Requirements. List and explain the five properties of well-formed Functional Requirements as per IEEE 830 / 29148 standards.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Functional Requirements are precise, verifiable statements that describe the specific behaviors, features, and operations a system must perform to satisfy user needs. They define *what the system does* in terms of inputs, processing, and outputs, and are documented in a Software Requirements Specification (SRS) document. **Five Properties of Well-Formed FRs (5 marks, 1 mark each):** 1. **Correctness:** The FR must accurately reflect the user's actual need and must not contradict any higher-level requirement or constraint. 2. **Unambiguity:** The FR must have only one possible interpretation by all stakeholders — designers, developers, and testers. 3. **Completeness:** The FR must define all inputs, outputs, conditions, and exception cases — including error handling and boundary scenarios. 4. **Verifiability:** The FR must be testable through inspection, demonstration, or a measurable test. Vague terms like "fast" or "user-friendly" are not allowed. 5. **Traceability:** The FR must be uniquely identified (e.g., FR-PM-001) and linked back to its source user need, use case, and forward to design, code, and test artifacts. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Q(b). For a Hospital Patient Monitoring HCI (displaying heart rate, SpO₂, and body temperature of ICU patients), derive seven well-formed Functional Requirements using the IEEE "shall" format. Classify each requirement into its appropriate category.** **Model Solution (7 marks — 1 mark per FR + classification bonus):** | FR ID | Statement | Category | |---|---|---| | **FR-PM-01** | The system shall acquire heart rate, SpO₂, and body temperature data from connected medical sensors every 1 second. | Input | | **FR-PM-02** | The system shall display each patient's vital signs on a dedicated bedside monitor with color-coded values (green = normal, yellow = borderline, red = critical). | Output | | **FR-PM-03** | The system shall trigger an audible and visual alarm at the central nursing station when any vital sign exceeds the configured threshold. | Output / Control | | **FR-PM-04** | The system shall allow authorized nurses to acknowledge alarms and shall record the acknowledgment timestamp in the patient log. | Control / Storage | | **FR-PM-05** | The system shall calculate the 5-minute moving average of heart rate and shall display it alongside the instantaneous value. | Processing | | **FR-PM-06** | The system shall store all vital sign readings in a secure database with a retention period of 7 years as per hospital policy. | Storage | | **FR-PM-07** | The system shall restrict access to patient data based on role: doctors may view all patients, nurses may view only their assigned ward, and auditors may view logs only. | Control / Security | **Bonus Justification (built into marks):** Each FR uses the **"shall"** verb, has a **unique ID**, is **unambiguous**, is **verifiable** through test cases, and is **traceable** to the ICU patient monitoring use case. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Functional Requirement Questions** > > 1. **Using "should", "must", or "will" instead of "shall".** The IEEE 29148 normative verb is **"shall"** for mandatory requirements. Using any other auxiliary verb costs at least 1 mark in 14-mark answers. > > 2. **Conflating Functional with Non-Functional requirements.** Statements like *"The system should respond quickly"* or *"The interface should be user-friendly"* are **non-functional** (performance/usability) and **do not earn marks** in a Functional Requirements question. > > 3. **Forgetting traceability IDs.** Every FR must have a **unique identifier** (e.g., FR-001, FR-PM-03). Untagged statements are treated as vague prose and marked down. > > 4. **Mixing multiple behaviors in one FR.** A single FR should describe **one atomic behavior**. Combining "allow login AND display dashboard AND send email" into one statement violates the atomicity principle and loses 1–2 marks. > > 5. **Omitting the "what" of the requirement.** FRs must answer: *What does the system do? Under what conditions? What is the output?* Vague statements like "The system shall be good" earn zero marks. > > 6. **Ignoring error/exception conditions.** A well-formed FR includes **normal AND exception paths** (e.g., "shall display an error message if login fails after 3 attempts"). Omitting this loses 1 mark. > > 7. **Not mapping to a use case or user need.** In 14-mark questions, KTU examiners look for **traceability**. A standalone FR without a linked user need or use case is considered incomplete. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Functional Requirements (FRs)** define **what the system does** to support user tasks — inputs accepted, processing performed, outputs presented, data stored, and control flow maintained. - The **canonical form** is the **IEEE "shall" statement**: `The shall .` - The **five categories** are: **Input, Output, Processing, Storage, and Control** (interaction flow). - The **five quality properties** are: **Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Verifiable, and Traceable** (IEEE 830 / 29148). - **FRs are atomic** — one behavior per statement — and **uniquely identified** (e.g., FR-001). - FRs **decompose from Use Cases** (typically 3–10 FRs per use case) and must be **traceable** to user needs and test cases. - The **Coverage Ratio** $C = \frac{\text{FRs Implemented}}{\text{FRs Specified}} \times 100\%$ measures requirements completeness. - **Distinguish strictly from Non-Functional Requirements** (performance, usability, security, reliability, accessibility) — quality attributes are **not** functional. - In safety-critical HCI (medical, aviation, automotive), FRs are **legally mandated** and regulated (FDA, IEC 62366, DO-178C, ISO 26262). - Always include **error/exception handling** in FRs to ensure completeness. - The transformation pipeline is: **User Need → Categorization → "Shall" Statement → Verification → Traceability Matrix → Implementation.**

topics.title: Software Requirements Specifications (SRS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Requirements Specifications (SRS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a comprehensive, formal document that fully describes the **external behaviour**, **functional capabilities**, **performance constraints**, and **design limitations** of a software system intended for development. As per the **IEEE Standard 830-1998** (superseded by **IEEE 29148:2011**), the SRS serves as the *single source of truth* that bridges the gap between the user's informal needs and the developer's technical implementation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** SRS is the foundational document for any Human-Computer Interface (HCI) project. It defines WHAT the system must do (functional) and HOW WELL it must do it (non-functional/quality attributes) before a single screen or interaction is designed.

**Formal Definition (IEEE 29148:2011):**
> *"A Software Requirements Specification (SRS) is a structured collection of requirements — statements that describe the essential capabilities, characteristics, and constraints of a system — that enables designers to design the system and testers to verify that the system meets those requirements."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Architectural Blueprint" Analogy for SRS:**
>
> Imagine you want to build a dream house. Before any brick is laid, you need a **blueprint**. This blueprint doesn't tell the mason *how* to mix cement (that's the implementation), but it tells *everyone* — the client, the engineer, the contractor — exactly:
> - **How many rooms** are required (Functional Requirements)
> - **What load** the foundation must bear (Performance Requirements)
> - **What material** the walls should be (Design Constraints)
> - **Safety standards** for earthquakes (Non-Functional Requirements)
> - **How to verify** the house is ready (Acceptance Criteria)
>
> The **SRS is the blueprint of software**. Without it, the developers (architects) and the users (clients) speak different languages, leading to costly rework, missed deadlines, and unusable products. In the HCI context, the SRS is what ensures the interface we design *truly* meets the user's needs.

---

## 1.3 Why SRS is Critical in HCI Design

In the specific context of **Design and Implementation of Human-Computer Interfaces**, the SRS plays three pivotal roles:

1. **Usability Baseline Definition:** It quantifies the *usability goals* (e.g., "A novice user must complete checkout in ≤ 3 clicks").
2. **Stakeholder Alignment:** It aligns UI designers, interaction designers, and backend developers on a unified set of acceptance criteria.
3. **Traceability Foundation:** Every screen, button, and animation in the final interface can be traced back to a documented requirement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Metric:** Industry studies by the **Standish Group (CHAOS Report)** consistently show that **~70% of software project failures** are due to poor or missing requirements specifications. The SRS directly mitigates this risk.

---

## 1.4 Core Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Requirement** | A condition or capability that the system must possess |
| **Specification** | The document that records the requirements formally |
| **Stakeholder** | Any person (user, manager, regulator) affected by the system |
| **Functional Requirement (FR)** | What the system *does* (features, actions) |
| **Non-Functional Requirement (NFR)** | How the system *performs* (speed, security, usability) |
| **Constraint** | A limitation imposed on the design (e.g., "must run on Android 9+") |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of SRS Quality (IEEE 29148)

A high-quality SRS document must satisfy **three mandatory pillars**. The following analytical breakdown explains the *Why* and *How* of each:

### Pillar 1: Content (What must be inside)
- **Step 1 — Identify the Purpose:** State *why* the system is being built.
- **Step 2 — Define the Scope:** State *what is in* and *what is out* of the system.
- **Step 3 — List Functional Requirements:** Every user action the system must support.
- **Step 4 — List Non-Functional Requirements:** Performance, security, usability, reliability.
- **Step 5 — Specify External Interfaces:** Hardware, software, user, and communication interfaces.

### Pillar 2: Quality (How well it is written)
- **Correctness:** Every requirement must accurately represent the stakeholder's need.
- **Unambiguity:** Only one possible interpretation. Use **"shall"** for mandatory items.
- **Completeness:** All possible scenarios, including error states, are covered.
- **Consistency:** No two requirements contradict each other.
- **Rankability:** Each requirement is prioritized (High / Medium / Low) and stability-tagged.
- **Verifiability:** There must exist a finite, cost-effective way to test it.
- **Modifiability:** Structure must allow easy change without losing consistency.
- **Traceability:** Every requirement has a unique ID (e.g., `FR-001`, `NFR-007`) and can be traced forward (to design/code) and backward (to origin/stakeholder).

### Pillar 3: Presentation (How it is delivered)
- Standard template adherence (IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148).
- Cross-referencing using a **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)**.
- Use of structured language, numbered sections, and consistent terminology.

---

## 2.2 The IEEE 830 Standard Structure of SRS

The **IEEE 830-1998** standard prescribes the following hierarchical structure. This is **high-yield** for KTU 14-mark questions.

| Section # | Section Name | Description | Typical Weightage |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | **Introduction** | Purpose, Scope, Definitions, References, Overview | 2 marks |
| 1.1 | Purpose | Why this document exists | — |
| 1.2 | Scope | Product name, benefits, objectives | — |
| 1.3 | Definitions & Abbreviations | Glossary of all terms | — |
| 1.4 | References | Bibliography of related docs | — |
| 1.5 | Overview | How the rest of the document is organised | — |
| 2 | **Overall Description** | Product perspective, user characteristics, assumptions, constraints | 4 marks |
| 3 | **Specific Requirements** | Detailed FRs, NFRs, interfaces, design constraints | 8 marks |
| 3.1 | External Interface Requirements | User, hardware, software, communication interfaces | — |
| 3.2 | Functional Requirements | Detailed feature-by-feature behaviour | — |
| 3.3 | Performance Requirements | Speed, accuracy, response time | — |
| 3.4 | Design Constraints | Standards, languages, platforms | — |
| 3.5 | Software System Attributes | Reliability, security, maintainability, portability | — |
| 3.6 | Other Requirements | Database, legal, etc. | — |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Heuristic Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula, heuristic, and key metric a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the SRS topic. **Memorize the columns — they appear in almost every 14-mark question.**

| # | Heuristic / Formula | Expression | Description / Application |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Requirement Stability** | $S = \dfrac{\text{Number of unchanged requirements}}{\text{Total number of requirements}}$ | Measures how stable the SRS is during development. Higher $S$ = lower risk. |
| 2 | **Volatility Index** | $V = 1 - S = \dfrac{\text{Number of changed requirements}}{\text{Total requirements}}$ | A high $V$ signals unstable requirements and project risk. |
| 3 | **Requirements-to-Code Traceability** | $T = \dfrac{\text{Requirements traced to code}}{\text{Total requirements}} \times 100\%$ | Used in the **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)**. |
| 4 | **Goal–Question–Metric (GQM)** | $Goal \rightarrow Questions \rightarrow Metrics$ | A top-down approach to derive measurable NFRs from business goals. |
| 5 | **Function Point (FP) Estimate** | $FP = UFP \times VAF$ | Where $UFP$ = Unadjusted Function Points, $VAF$ = Value Adjustment Factor (0.65–1.35). Used to size the project from SRS. |
| 6 | **Eisenhower Prioritization (Eisenhower Matrix)** | Quadrant: $U \times I$ | $U$ = Urgency, $I$ = Importance. Used to rank requirements in the SRS. |
| 7 | **MoSCoW Method** | Must / Should / Could / Won't | Prioritization scheme for requirements. **Must-have** = critical. |
| 8 | **The "Shall" Rule** | Each requirement statement must use **"shall"** | Ensures mandatory, verifiable phrasing in SRS. |
| 9 | **NFR Sub-Classification (ISO 25010)** | 8 Quality Characteristics | Functional Suitability, Performance, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, Portability. |
| 10 | **SMART Requirements Test** | Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound | Quality-check filter for every requirement statement. |

---

## 2.4 Requirements Engineering Process (Predecessor to SRS)

The SRS is the **output** of a larger process called **Requirements Engineering (RE)**. The four sequential sub-processes are:

1. **Elicitation** — Discover requirements via interviews, questionnaires, observation, prototyping, and workshops.
2. **Analysis** — Negotiate, prioritise, and resolve conflicts among stakeholder requirements.
3. **Specification** — Document the agreed requirements into the **SRS** (this is our focus topic).
4. **Validation** — Review, inspect, and prototype-test the SRS for correctness and completeness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In the automotive industry, an SRS is legally binding. The **ISO 26262** standard for automotive functional safety mandates an SRS that is traceable, unambiguous, and verifiable — exactly the qualities prescribed by IEEE 830. In HCI design, the SRS is what allows UX researchers to define usability testable criteria *before* any Figma mockup is drawn.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Functional vs Non-Functional Requirements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the most frequently tested analytical table in KTU Module 1. Memorize the contrasting columns.

| Dimension | Functional Requirements (FR) | Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Specify *what the system does* — its observable behaviour and features. | Specify *how well* the system performs — the quality attributes and constraints. |
| **Focus** | Features, functions, data processing. | Performance, security, usability, scalability, reliability. |
| **Measurability** | Verified by **pass/fail tests** (binary). | Verified by **quantitative metrics** (e.g., response time ≤ 2 s). |
| **Examples** | "The system shall allow users to reset their password." | "The password reset page shall load within 1.5 seconds on 3G." |
| **Identifier Prefix** | `FR-XXX` | `NFR-XXX` |
| **Volatility** | Generally **stable** once signed off. | **Highly volatile** — may change with deployment environment. |
| **KTU 2024 Weight** | ~60% of marks in 14-mark questions. | ~40% of marks. |
| **Origin** | Derived from user tasks and use-cases. | Derived from stakeholder quality expectations and constraints. |
| **Standard Source** | Use-case specifications. | **ISO/IEC 25010** quality model. |
| **Example Domain** | "Search for a train." | "Search results must appear in ≤ 1 second for 95% of queries." |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction of an SRS Document

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a **14-mark-style structured derivation** showing how a real SRS is built. Follow each numbered step in your exam answer to score full marks.

**Step 1: Identify the Project Charter and Scope**

Before writing a single requirement, the SRS author must:
- Identify the *product name* and *version* (e.g., "IRCTC Mobile App v2.0").
- Define the *boundaries* — what is in scope and what is out of scope.
- Identify *stakeholders* (passengers, ticket agents, system admins, DBAs).

**Step 2: Elicit Raw Requirements**

Use a combination of techniques:
- **Interviews** (1-on-1 with domain experts).
- **Questionnaires** (for a large, distributed user base).
- **Observation / Ethnography** (watch users in their natural environment).
- **Focus Groups** (5–10 users discussing needs).
- **Brainstorming** (creative generation sessions).
- **Prototyping** (build a throwaway UI to elicit reactions).

**Step 3: Classify and Prioritize Requirements**

Apply the **MoSCoW** method to every requirement:

$$
\text{Priority}(R) = \begin{cases} \text{Must} & \text{if } R \text{ is critical to system operation} \\ \text{Should} & \text{if } R \text{ is important but not vital} \\ \text{Could} & \text{if } R \text{ is desirable if time permits} \\ \text{Won't} & \text{if } R \text{ is explicitly excluded from this release} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the SMART Filter to Each Requirement**

Every requirement statement must pass the SMART test:
- **S** — Specific (clearly defined).
- **M** — Measurable (can be verified quantitatively).
- **A** — Achievable (technically and financially feasible).
- **R** — Relevant (aligned with the system goal).
- **T** — Time-bound (has a deadline or response time).

**Example SMART Transformation:**

> ❌ **Bad:** "The login should be fast."
> ✅ **Good:** "The login page **shall** load and authenticate the user within **2 seconds** at the **95th percentile** under a load of **1000 concurrent users**."

**Step 5: Assign a Unique Identifier and Traceability Link**

Each requirement is tagged as follows:

$$
\text{ID Format} = \underbrace{\text{[FR} \vert \text{ NFR}}_{\text{Type}}]\underbrace{-\text{XXX}}_{\text{Sequence}} -\underbrace{\text{[V1]}}_{\text{Version}}
$$

**Example:** `FR-007-V1` — "The system shall allow the user to upload a profile picture of size ≤ 2 MB."

**Step 6: Populate the IEEE 830 Template**

Insert all classified, prioritized, SMART-validated, ID-tagged requirements into the **IEEE 830 template** (refer to Section 2.2 of these notes).

**Step 7: Build the Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)**

The RTM is a cross-reference table ensuring that:
- Every requirement is traced to its source (a stakeholder, a use case, a regulation).
- Every requirement is traced forward to design, code, and test cases.

$$
\text{RTM Coverage} = \frac{\vert \text{Requirements with both source and test case} \vert}{\vert \text{Total requirements} \vert} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 8: Validate the SRS**

Validation activities include:
- **Reviews** (formal walk-through with stakeholders).
- **Inspections** (defect-detection using Fagan's method).
- **Prototyping** (build a throwaway interface to verify usability requirements).
- **Acceptance Test Design** (write test cases directly from NFRs).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Function Point Estimation from SRS

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **complete, KTU-typical numerical problem** worth 7 marks. Learn the steps.

**Problem:** A software company has identified the following information from the SRS of a new Inventory Management System:

- **5 External Inputs (EI)**
- **3 External Outputs (EO)**
- **2 External Inquiries (EQ)**
- **1 Internal Logical File (ILF)**
- **1 External Interface File (EIF)**

The complexity weights (standard) are:

| Component | Low | Average | High |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| External Inputs (EI) | 3 | 4 | 6 |
| External Outputs (EO) | 4 | 5 | 7 |
| External Inquiries (EQ) | 3 | 4 | 6 |
| Internal Logical Files (ILF) | 7 | 10 | 15 |
| External Interface Files (EIF) | 5 | 7 | 10 |

Assume **average complexity** for all components and the **Value Adjustment Factor (VAF) = 1.05**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the Unadjusted Function Point (UFP) for each component.**

$$
UFP_{EI} = 5 \times 4 = 20
$$

$$
UFP_{EO} = 3 \times 5 = 15
$$

$$
UFP_{EQ} = 2 \times 4 = 8
$$

$$
UFP_{ILF} = 1 \times 10 = 10
$$

$$
UFP_{EIF} = 1 \times 7 = 7
$$

**Step 2: Sum the UFPs.**

$$
UFP = UFP_{EI} + UFP_{EO} + UFP_{EQ} + UFP_{ILF} + UFP_{EIF}
$$

$$
UFP = 20 + 15 + 8 + 10 + 7 = 60
$$

**Step 3: Apply the VAF to get the Function Point (FP).**

$$
FP = UFP \times VAF = 60 \times 1.05 = 63 \text{ FP}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** The SRS for the Inventory Management System has a functional size of **63 Function Points**, which categorizes it as a **medium-scale project** (typical FP ranges: Small < 100, Medium 100–500, Large 500–1000, Very Large > 1000).

---

## 3.4 Logical Derivation — Why "Shall" is Mandatory

The use of the modal verb **"shall"** in SRS statements is not a stylistic choice — it is a **logical necessity** derived from contract law and verification theory.

**Step 1:** A requirement is a *commitment* by the developer to the stakeholder.
**Step 2:** In logic, commitments are expressed using **deontic operators** (must, shall, ought).
**Step 3:** Among English modal verbs, only **"shall"** carries the unambiguous *obligation-on-the-developer* semantics in international technical standards (IEEE, ISO, IEC).
**Step 4:** A requirement using "should" or "may" is interpreted as a *recommendation* and is **not contractually enforceable** — it cannot be verified as pass/fail.

$$
\text{Requirement}(R) = \begin{cases} \text{Verifiable (Pass/Fail)} & \text{if } R \text{ contains "shall"} \\ \text{Ambiguous / Unverifiable} & \text{if } R \text{ contains "should", "may", "might"} \end{cases}
$$

**Conclusion:** Every KTU-marked requirement statement must use **"shall"** for the verb linking subject to the action.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SRS Document Hierarchy (IEEE 830 Tree)

> [!NOTE]
> This **Mermaid Tree Diagram** depicts the formal hierarchy of an IEEE 830-compliant SRS document. It is a frequently requested 7-mark illustration in KTU exams.

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRS_ROOT["SRS Document"]
    SEC1["1. Introduction"]
    SEC2["2. Overall Description"]
    SEC3["3. Specific Requirements"]
    SEC4["4. Appendices"]
    
    SEC1_SUB1["1.1 Purpose"]
    SEC1_SUB2["1.2 Scope"]
    SEC1_SUB3["1.3 Definitions and Abbreviations"]
    SEC1_SUB4["1.4 References"]
    SEC1_SUB5["1.5 Overview"]
    
    SEC2_SUB1["2.1 Product Perspective"]
    SEC2_SUB2["2.2 Product Functions"]
    SEC2_SUB3["2.3 User Characteristics"]
    SEC2_SUB4["2.4 Constraints"]
    SEC2_SUB5["2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies"]
    
    SEC3_SUB1["3.1 External Interface Requirements"]
    SEC3_SUB2["3.2 Functional Requirements"]
    SEC3_SUB3["3.3 Performance Requirements"]
    SEC3_SUB4["3.4 Design Constraints"]
    SEC3_SUB5["3.5 Software Quality Attributes"]
    SEC3_SUB6["3.6 Other Requirements"]
    
    SEC4_SUB1["4.1 Glossary"]
    SEC4_SUB2["4.2 Analysis Models"]
    SEC4_SUB3["4.3 Requirements Traceability Matrix"]
    SEC4_SUB4["4.4 To Be Determined (TBD) List"]
    
    SRS_ROOT --> SEC1
    SRS_ROOT --> SEC2
    SRS_ROOT --> SEC3
    SRS_ROOT --> SEC4
    
    SEC1 --> SEC1_SUB1
    SEC1 --> SEC1_SUB2
    SEC1 --> SEC1_SUB3
    SEC1 --> SEC1_SUB4
    SEC1 --> SEC1_SUB5
    
    SEC2 --> SEC2_SUB1
    SEC2 --> SEC2_SUB2
    SEC2 --> SEC2_SUB3
    SEC2 --> SEC2_SUB4
    SEC2 --> SEC2_SUB5
    
    SEC3 --> SEC3_SUB1
    SEC3 --> SEC3_SUB2
    SEC3 --> SEC3_SUB3
    SEC3 --> SEC3_SUB4
    SEC3 --> SEC3_SUB5
    SEC3 --> SEC3_SUB6
    
    SEC4 --> SEC4_SUB1
    SEC4 --> SEC4_SUB2
    SEC4 --> SEC4_SUB3
    SEC4 --> SEC4_SUB4
```

---

## 4.2 Requirements Engineering Process Flow (Predecessor to SRS)

> [!NOTE]
> This **process flow diagram** shows the **four sequential phases** that produce the SRS as output. The arrows indicate the iterative, non-linear nature of requirements engineering (backtracking is common).

```mermaid
graph LR
    STAKE["Stakeholder Needs and Goals"]
    ELIC["Phase 1: Elicitation"]
    ANAL["Phase 2: Analysis and Negotiation"]
    SPEC["Phase 3: Specification - SRS Document"]
    VAL["Phase 4: Validation and Review"]
    DESIGN["Next Stage: System and HCI Design"]
    
    STAKE --> ELIC
    ELIC --> ANAL
    ANAL --> SPEC
    SPEC --> VAL
    VAL --> DESIGN
    
    FEED1["Feedback Loop"] -.-> ANAL
    FEED2["Defect Loop"] -.-> SPEC
    FEED3["Change Request"] -.-> ELIC
    
    VAL -.-> FEED1
    ANAL -.-> FEED2
    SPEC -.-> FEED3
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Requirements Classification

> [!NOTE]
> This **functional block diagram** classifies all requirement types inside an SRS. Use this diagram to structure your 14-mark answer on "Explain the components of an SRS."

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRS_CORE["Software Requirements Specification SRS"]
    FR_BLOCK["Functional Requirements Block"]
    NFR_BLOCK["Non-Functional Requirements Block"]
    INT_BLOCK["External Interface Requirements Block"]
    CON_BLOCK["Design Constraints Block"]
    
    FR1["Use Case 1"]
    FR2["Use Case 2"]
    FR3["Use Case 3"]
    FR4["Data Processing Rules"]
    FR5["Business Logic Rules"]
    
    NFR1["Performance: Response Time and Throughput"]
    NFR2["Security: Authentication and Authorization"]
    NFR3["Usability: Learnability and Efficiency"]
    NFR4["Reliability: MTBF and Availability"]
    NFR5["Portability: Platform Support"]
    NFR6["Maintainability: Modularity"]
    
    INT1["User Interface"]
    INT2["Hardware Interface"]
    INT3["Software Interface"]
    INT4["Communication Interface"]
    
    CON1["Standards Compliance"]
    CON2["Implementation Language"]
    CON3["Platform Limitations"]
    CON4["Resource Budget"]
    
    SRS_CORE --> FR_BLOCK
    SRS_CORE --> NFR_BLOCK
    SRS_CORE --> INT_BLOCK
    SRS_CORE --> CON_BLOCK
    
    FR_BLOCK --> FR1
    FR_BLOCK --> FR2
    FR_BLOCK --> FR3
    FR_BLOCK --> FR4
    FR_BLOCK --> FR5
    
    NFR_BLOCK --> NFR1
    NFR_BLOCK --> NFR2
    NFR_BLOCK --> NFR3
    NFR_BLOCK --> NFR4
    NFR_BLOCK --> NFR5
    NFR_BLOCK --> NFR6
    
    INT_BLOCK --> INT1
    INT_BLOCK --> INT2
    INT_BLOCK --> INT3
    INT_BLOCK --> INT4
    
    CON_BLOCK --> CON1
    CON_BLOCK --> CON2
    CON_BLOCK --> CON3
    CON_BLOCK --> CON4
```

---

## 4.4 Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) — Visual Model

> [!NOTE]
> This **bipartite mapping diagram** illustrates how a requirement is traced *forward* (to design and test) and *backward* (to origin). The RTM is the single most important **quality-assurance artefact** linked to the SRS.

```mermaid
graph LR
    REQ_A["FR-001: User Login"]
    REQ_B["FR-002: Password Reset"]
    REQ_C["NFR-005: Page Load ≤ 2s"]
    REQ_D["NFR-006: WCAG 2.1 AA Compliance"]
    
    SRC_1["Origin: Stakeholder Interview"]
    SRC_2["Origin: Use Case UC-03"]
    SRC_3["Origin: SLA Contract"]
    SRC_4["Origin: Govt. Regulation"]
    
    DES_1["Design: LoginScreen.png"]
    DES_2["Design: ResetScreen.png"]
    DES_3["Design: Performance Spec"]
    DES_4["Design: Accessibility Audit"]
    
    TEST_1["Test Case: TC_LOGIN_01"]
    TEST_2["Test Case: TC_RESET_02"]
    TEST_3["Test Case: TC_PERF_05"]
    TEST_4["Test Case: TC_A11Y_06"]
    
    SRC_1 --> REQ_A
    SRC_2 --> REQ_B
    SRC_3 --> REQ_C
    SRC_4 --> REQ_D
    
    REQ_A --> DES_1
    REQ_B --> DES_2
    REQ_C --> DES_3
    REQ_D --> DES_4
    
    DES_1 --> TEST_1
    DES_2 --> TEST_2
    DES_3 --> TEST_3
    DES_4 --> TEST_4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Software Requirements Specification (SRS). List any four characteristics of a good SRS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A Software Requirements Specification (SRS) is a formal document that completely describes the external behaviour, functional capabilities, performance constraints, and design limitations of a software system intended for development. As per **IEEE Standard 830**, the SRS serves as the single source of truth for designers, developers, and testers throughout the software lifecycle.

**Four Characteristics of a Good SRS (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks):**

1. **Correct** — Every requirement accurately represents a stakeholder's need.
2. **Unambiguous** — Only one possible interpretation exists for each statement.
3. **Complete** — All possible scenarios, including error states, are documented.
4. **Verifiable** — There exists a finite, cost-effective way to test the requirement (pass/fail).
5. **Consistent** — No two requirements contradict each other.
6. **Modifiable** — Structure permits easy revision without loss of integrity.
7. **Traceable** — Every requirement has a unique ID and is linked to its source and design.
8. **Ranked** — Requirements are prioritized by importance and stability.

*(Any four are accepted.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Functional Requirements and Non-Functional Requirements with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Dimension | Functional Requirements (FR) | Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition (1 mark)** | Describe *what* the system does — its features and behaviour. | Describe *how well* the system performs — quality attributes. |
| **Examples (2 marks)** | (i) The system shall allow users to register using email and password. (ii) The system shall generate a monthly sales report in PDF format. | (i) The system shall respond to search queries within 1 second at the 95th percentile. (ii) The system shall comply with WCAG 2.1 Level AA accessibility standards. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**Q: (a)** Explain the IEEE 830 standard structure of an SRS with a neat block diagram. List the eight essential quality characteristics of a good SRS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the SRS for the following system: *"Online Railway Reservation System"* — covering its purpose, scope, at least four functional requirements, and at least three non-functional requirements. Use proper "shall" statements. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1** (the IEEE 830 Tree) of these notes. The standard has four top-level sections:
1. **Introduction** (Purpose, Scope, Definitions, References, Overview)
2. **Overall Description** (Product perspective, user characteristics, constraints, assumptions)
3. **Specific Requirements** (External interface, functional, performance, design constraints, software attributes)
4. **Appendices** (Glossary, analysis models, RTM, TBD list)

**Eight Quality Characteristics (4 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Correct** — Represents the true stakeholder need.
2. **Unambiguous** — Single, clear interpretation.
3. **Complete** — All scenarios, including error paths, are covered.
4. **Consistent** — Internally non-contradictory.
5. **Ranked for Importance/Stability** — Prioritised.
6. **Verifiable** — Testable in a finite, cost-effective manner.
7. **Modifiable** — Easy to change while preserving integrity.
8. **Traceable** — Origin and downstream links are recorded.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — 7 Marks

**Online Railway Reservation System — Sample SRS Excerpt**

**1. Purpose (0.5 mark):**
The purpose of this document is to define the functional and non-functional requirements of the Online Railway Reservation System (ORRS), a web and mobile platform that allows passengers to search, book, pay for, and cancel train tickets in real time.

**2. Scope (0.5 mark):**
The ORRS shall serve passengers across India, integrate with the IRCTC payment gateway, and operate on browsers (Chrome, Edge, Firefox) and mobile OS (Android 9+, iOS 14+). Out of scope: catering, tour packages, and corporate bookings.

**3. Functional Requirements (4 marks — 1 each):**

- **FR-001:** The system shall allow registered users to search for trains by entering source station, destination station, and date of journey.
- **FR-002:** The system shall display available trains along with departure time, arrival time, duration, and seat availability in a sortable list.
- **FR-003:** The system shall allow the user to select a seat from an interactive seat map and proceed to payment.
- **FR-004:** The system shall generate a unique 10-digit PNR number upon successful booking and send a confirmation via email and SMS.

**4. Non-Functional Requirements (2 marks — distribute):**

- **NFR-001 (Performance):** The search results page shall load within **2 seconds** at the **95th percentile** for **5000 concurrent users** on a 4G connection.
- **NFR-002 (Security):** All payment transactions shall be encrypted using **TLS 1.3** and shall comply with **PCI-DSS** standards.
- **NFR-003 (Usability):** A first-time user shall be able to complete a booking within **5 minutes** as measured by usability testing with at least 20 participants.
- **NFR-004 (Availability):** The system shall maintain **99.9% uptime** measured monthly, excluding scheduled maintenance windows.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**

**Q: (a)** Explain the Requirements Engineering process in detail. How does it lead to the creation of an SRS? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A software company has identified the following Function Point components from the SRS of a new Library Management System: 6 External Inputs, 4 External Outputs, 3 External Inquiries, 2 Internal Logical Files, 1 External Interface File. Assuming **average complexity** and **VAF = 1.10**, compute the **Unadjusted Function Point (UFP)** and the **Adjusted Function Point (FP)**. Use standard FP weights. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — 7 Marks

The **Requirements Engineering (RE) Process** is the systematic, iterative set of activities that produces the SRS. It has four phases:

**1. Requirements Elicitation (1.5 marks):**
This is the discovery phase, where stakeholder needs are uncovered. Techniques include:
- **Interviews** (one-on-one, structured or unstructured)
- **Questionnaires** (distributed to a large user base)
- **Observation / Ethnography** (watching users in context)
- **Focus Groups** (5–10 users in guided discussion)
- **Brainstorming** (creative idea generation)
- **Prototyping** (throwaway UIs to stimulate feedback)

**2. Requirements Analysis and Negotiation (1.5 marks):**
Raw requirements are classified, prioritised (using **MoSCoW**: Must/Should/Could/Won't), and conflicts between stakeholders are negotiated. The SMART filter is applied.

**3. Requirements Specification (2 marks):**
The negotiated, prioritised, and validated requirements are formally documented in the **SRS** following a standard (e.g., **IEEE 830**). Each requirement:
- Receives a unique ID (e.g., `FR-007`)
- Uses the modal verb **"shall"**
- Is linked to a use case
- Is tagged with priority and stability

**4. Requirements Validation (2 marks):**
The SRS undergoes:
- **Reviews** (formal walk-throughs with stakeholders)
- **Inspections** (Fagan-style defect detection)
- **Prototyping** (verify usability)
- **Acceptance test design** (write test cases directly from NFRs)

**Linkage to SRS:** The SRS is the **deliverable output** of phases 1–3, and phase 4 ensures its quality. The RTM is created here.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — 7 Marks

**Given (1 mark):**
- 6 External Inputs (EI)
- 4 External Outputs (EO)
- 3 External Inquiries (EQ)
- 2 Internal Logical Files (ILF)
- 1 External Interface File (EIF)
- Average complexity, VAF = 1.10

**Step 1: Recall Average Complexity Weights (1 mark):**

| Component | Weight |
|---|:---:|
| EI | 4 |
| EO | 5 |
| EQ | 4 |
| ILF | 10 |
| EIF | 7 |

**Step 2: Compute UFP per Component (3 marks — 0.6 each):**

$$
UFP_{EI} = 6 \times 4 = 24
$$

$$
UFP_{EO} = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

$$
UFP_{EQ} = 3 \times 4 = 12
$$

$$
UFP_{ILF} = 2 \times 10 = 20
$$

$$
UFP_{EIF} = 1 \times 7 = 7
$$

**Step 3: Sum the UFPs (1 mark):**

$$
UFP = 24 + 20 + 12 + 20 + 7 = 83
$$

**Step 4: Compute Adjusted FP (1 mark):**

$$
FP = UFP \times VAF = 83 \times 1.10 = 91.3 \approx 91 \text{ FP}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation (0.5 mark):** A Library Management System of **91 FP** falls in the **Small-to-Medium** project category.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 SRS Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Using "should" / "may" / "might" instead of "shall":** This is the **#1 mistake**. Every functional/non-functional requirement **must** use the verb **"shall"**. Using softer modals makes the requirement unverifiable and you will lose **at least 1 mark per occurrence**.
> 
> 2. **Writing "fast" or "user-friendly" without metrics:** Vague quality terms (e.g., "fast", "intuitive", "easy") will cost you **0.5 to 1 mark**. Always quantify: "≤ 2 seconds", "≤ 3 clicks", "WCAG 2.1 AA".
> 
> 3. **Omitting the unique requirement ID:** Every requirement **must** carry a unique identifier (e.g., `FR-001`, `NFR-002`). Missing IDs indicate non-traceability and lose **0.5 marks each**.
> 
> 4. **Confusing FR and NFR:** A common error is placing "The system shall be fast" under Functional Requirements. Speed is a *quality attribute* and belongs to **NFR**.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the "Out of Scope" sub-section:** Most students define *what* the system does but forget to state *what it does not do*. Examiners explicitly allocate **0.5 marks** for this.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the IEEE 830 numbering:** When asked to write an SRS, follow the **1.x, 2.x, 3.x** hierarchy. Disorganised answers are docked **1 mark** for "lack of structure."
> 
> 7. **In FP numericals, forgetting to multiply by VAF:** The most common arithmetic error. Many students compute UFP and stop. Always show the **$FP = UFP \times VAF$** step explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for SRS (Module 1)**

- **SRS = Software Requirements Specification.** It is a **formal, structured document** that fully describes a software system's behaviour, capabilities, and constraints.
- The SRS is governed by the **IEEE 830-1998** standard (now superseded by **IEEE 29148:2011**).
- The **IEEE 830 structure** has **4 main sections**: Introduction, Overall Description, Specific Requirements, Appendices.
- **Eight quality characteristics** of a good SRS: Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable.
- **Functional Requirements (FR)** describe *what* the system does. **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)** describe *how well* it does it.
- The **NFR sub-classification** uses the **ISO/IEC 25010** standard with **8 quality characteristics**: Functional Suitability, Performance Efficiency, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, Portability.
- **"Shall" is mandatory** in every requirement statement. No other modal verb (should, may, might) is acceptable.
- The **SMART test** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) is the quality filter for every requirement.
- **MoSCoW prioritization** is the industry-standard scheme: Must, Should, Could, Won't.
- The **Requirements Engineering process** has **4 phases**: Elicitation → Analysis → Specification → Validation.
- The **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** links each requirement to its source (backward) and to its design/test (forward).
- **Function Point (FP)** estimation sizes the project: $FP = UFP \times VAF$. Weights are EI=4, EO=5, EQ=4, ILF=10, EIF=7 (average).
- **Volatility Index** $V = 1 - S$ where $S$ is the requirement stability.
- For HCI design, the SRS is the **blueprint** that defines usability, learnability, and efficiency targets **before** any screen is drawn.
- **Out-of-Scope declaration** is mandatory; forgetting it costs marks.
- A **unique ID** is mandatory for every requirement (e.g., `FR-001`, `NFR-002`).
- The SRS bridges the **user's informal needs** and the **developer's technical implementation** — it is the **single source of truth** for the entire project lifecycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Usability and Non-Functional Requirements in SRS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Usability and Non-Functional Requirements in SRS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a formal document that describes **what** the system must do (functional) and **how well** it must do it (non-functional). As per the **IEEE 29148:2018** standard (which has superseded IEEE 830-1998 in modern KTU-recommended practice), an SRS is a structured representation of requirements that serves as the contract between stakeholders, designers, and validators.

Within the SRS, requirements are partitioned into:

| # | Requirement Type | What it Captures |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Functional Requirements (FR)** | Specific behaviors, inputs/outputs, business rules |
| 2 | **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)** | Quality attributes, constraints, performance, **usability**, reliability, security |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim worth memorising):**
> *Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs)* — also called **quality attributes**, **quality of service (QoS) requirements**, or **constraints** — specify *how* the system performs its functions rather than *what* the functions are. They are the **"–ilities"** (usability, reliability, scalability, maintainability…) that govern the *user experience* and *operational fitness* of the product.

### 1.2 Usability — The Crown Jewel of Human-Computer Interaction

According to **ISO 9241-11:2018**, the *de facto* international standard adopted by KTU syllabus and HCI literature:

$$\text{Usability} = f(\text{Effectiveness}, \text{Efficiency}, \text{Satisfaction})$$

More formally:

> **Usability:** *The extent to which a product can be used by specified users to achieve specified goals with **effectiveness**, **efficiency**, and **satisfaction** in a specified context of use.*

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Building a House (Architectural Blueprint vs. Interior Design)**
>
> Imagine you are commissioning a home.
> - The **blueprint** (number of rooms, plumbing layout, electrical wiring) = **Functional Requirements**.
> - The **interior finishing** (warm lighting, smooth door handles, slip-resistant tiles, ambient acoustics, ergonomic kitchen height) = **Non-Functional / Usability Requirements**.
>
> Without usability specs, you may get a house with all rooms (functions complete), but the doors are too heavy for elderly residents, the light switches are behind the doors, and the staircase is dangerously steep. *Functionally correct, but unusable.* This is exactly the failure mode an HCI engineer prevents by writing **usability requirements in the SRS**.

### 1.4 Why Usability is a *Non-Functional* Requirement (and not a feature)

- A user cannot "buy" usability as a discrete button or menu item.
- It is a **cross-cutting quality attribute** that pervades every function.
- It is **measurable** (task time, error count, satisfaction score) and hence testable — making it a legitimate SRS entry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of Non-Functional Requirements (FURPS+ Model)

The **FURPS+** model (Grady, Caswell, Bustamante — adopted in KTU reference texts) classifies NFRs into five core categories plus auxiliary constraints:

1. **F — Functional** (feature sets, capabilities)
2. **U — Usability** (human factors, aesthetics, consistency, help, documentation)
3. **R — Reliability** (availability, MTBF, fault tolerance, recoverability)
4. **P — Performance** (response time, throughput, accuracy, resource usage)
5. **S — Supportability** (maintainability, adaptability, testability, configurability)
6. **+ Constraints** (design, implementation, interface, physical, regulatory)

### 2.2 Nielsen's Five Usability Attributes (1993)

For a concrete decomposition of the abstract word "usability", Jakob Nielsen's model — heavily quoted in KTU examinations — gives:

| Attribute | Operational Meaning | Measurement Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Learnability** | How quickly a first-time user accomplishes basic tasks | Time-to-first-success, error count on Day 1 |
| **Efficiency** | Speed of task execution once learned | Task completion time after training |
| **Memorability** | Return-to-competence after period of non-use | Re-test time gap analysis |
| **Errors** | Frequency and severity of user errors | Error rate, recovery time, near-misses |
| **Satisfaction** | Subjective pleasantness of use | SUS, UMUX, NASA-TLX, post-task ratings |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Usability Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All metrics below are *measurable* and therefore must appear in a well-written SRS as **acceptance criteria**.

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit / Range | Acceptance Threshold (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** | $TCR = \dfrac{N_{\text{success}}}{N_{\text{attempts}}} \times 100\%$ | % | $\geq 90\%$ |
| 2 | **Mean Task Time (MTT)** | $MTT = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n}$ | seconds | $\leq$ baseline |
| 3 | **Error Rate (ER)** | $ER = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{tasks}}}$ | errors/task | $\leq 0.10$ |
| 4 | **Efficiency Score** | $Eff = \dfrac{T_{\text{optimal}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}$ | ratio $\in [0,1]$ | $\geq 0.80$ |
| 5 | **System Usability Scale (SUS)** | $SUS = 2.5 \times \sum_{k=1}^{10} \left(S_{k,\text{adj}}\right)$ | 0–100 | $\geq 68$ (avg) |
| 6 | **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $NPS = \%P - \%D$ | $-100$ to $+100$ | $\geq 30$ |
| 7 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | ratio | $\geq 0.999$ (three 9s) |
| 8 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{\text{operational}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | hours | domain specific |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Verification & Validation:** Concrete NFRs enable test-case generation (e.g., "95% of novice users shall complete checkout in ≤ 90 seconds").
- **Contractual binding:** SRS NFRs become SLA terms in B2B software.
- **Design driver:** Forces architects to choose interaction patterns (e.g., progressive disclosure for learnability).
- **Risk mitigation:** Catches "functionally correct, but unusable" failures before release — the #1 cause of post-deployment reworks in KTU case studies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: SUS (System Usability Scale) Score
