The SUS is a 10-item Likert questionnaire (5-point scale). Each odd item contributes positively; each even item contributes negatively. Brooke (1996) defined the scoring algorithm that KTU examiners expect you to reproduce.

**Step 1 — Raw Scoring per Item ( $S_{k,\text{adj}}$ ):**

For each of the 10 items with raw score $x_k$ (where $x_k \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$):

$$
S_{k,\text{adj}} =
\begin{cases}
x_k - 1, & \text{if } k \text{ is odd (positively phrased)} \\
5 - x_k, & \text{if } k \text{ is even (negatively phrased)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the Adjusted Scores:**

$$
S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{10} S_{k,\text{adj}}
$$

**Step 3 — Scale to 0–100:**

$$
SUS = 2.5 \times S_{\text{total}}
$$

#### Worked Numerical Example

A user submits responses: 4, 2, 4, 1, 4, 2, 5, 1, 4, 2.

| Item $k$ | Raw $x_k$ | Type | Adjustment | $S_{k,\text{adj}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | Odd | $4 - 1$ | **3** |
| 2 | 2 | Even | $5 - 2$ | **3** |
| 3 | 4 | Odd | $4 - 1$ | **3** |
| 4 | 1 | Even | $5 - 1$ | **4** |
| 5 | 4 | Odd | $4 - 1$ | **3** |
| 6 | 2 | Even | $5 - 2$ | **3** |
| 7 | 5 | Odd | $5 - 1$ | **4** |
| 8 | 1 | Even | $5 - 1$ | **4** |
| 9 | 4 | Odd | $4 - 1$ | **3** |
| 10 | 2 | Even | $5 - 2$ | **3** |

Sum: $S_{\text{total}} = 3+3+3+4+3+3+4+4+3+3 = 33$.

Therefore:

$$
SUS = 2.5 \times 33 = 82.5
$$

**Interpretation:** $SUS = 82.5 > 68$ (industry average) $\Rightarrow$ **above-average usability**, grade B+ on Sauro-Lewis curved grading.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Writing "Good" vs "Bad" Usability Requirements

A KTU-favourite exam question. The matrix below contrasts common student mistakes with the examiner-expected phrasing.

| Dimension | ❌ BAD (Vague, untestable) | ✅ GOOD (Specific, measurable, SRS-grade) |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject** | "The system shall be user-friendly." | "The system shall enable $\geq 90\%$ of first-time users to complete *Task T1* (new booking) within $\leq 120$ seconds without assistance." |
| **Metric** | "Login should be fast." | "Login response time shall be $\leq 1.5$ s on 4G mobile networks (95th percentile)." |
| **Error tolerance** | "Errors must be minimised." | "Users shall be able to recover from any single erroneous data entry in $\leq 2$ corrective actions, with a recoverable error rate of $\geq 99\%$." |
| **Accessibility** | "App should be accessible." | "The interface shall conform to WCAG 2.1 Level AA for all interactive controls." |
| **Satisfaction** | "Users will like it." | "Mean System Usability Scale score $\geq 75$ across a panel of $\geq 20$ representative users." |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "SMART-NFR":**
> Each usability requirement must be **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, **T**raceable — plus traceable to a **N**amed user group and a **R**ealistic context of use.

### 3.3 Derivation: Mean Time To First Success (Learnability Metric)

For a learnability study with $m$ novice users and one common task:

$$
L = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} t_{j,\text{first-success}}
$$

Where $t_{j,\text{first-success}}$ is the time taken by user $j$ to complete the task on first attempt. The SRS would specify an upper bound $L \leq L_{\max}$.

**Example:** 5 users complete a first-time login in 45, 60, 38, 52, 70 s respectively.

$$
L = \frac{45 + 60 + 38 + 52 + 70}{5} = \frac{265}{5} = 53 \text{ seconds}
$$

If the SRS specifies $L_{\max} = 60$ s, then **53 s ≤ 60 s** ⇒ learnability requirement **PASSES**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SRS Document Hierarchy (IEEE 29148 inspired)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SRS Document] --> B[Section 1 Introduction]
    A --> C[Section 2 Overall Description]
    A --> D[Section 3 Specific Requirements]
    A --> E[Section 4 Appendices]

    B --> B1[Purpose]
    B --> B2[Scope]
    B --> B3[Definitions and Acronyms]
    B --> B4[References]
    B --> B5[Overview]

    C --> C1[Product Perspective]
    C --> C2[User Classes and Characteristics]
    C --> C3[Operating Environment]
    C --> C4[Design and Implementation Constraints]
    C --> C5[Assumptions and Dependencies]

    D --> D1[Functional Requirements]
    D --> D2[Non Functional Requirements]
    D --> D3[External Interface Requirements]

    D2 --> D2a[Usability]
    D2 --> D2b[Reliability]
    D2 --> D2c[Performance]
    D2 --> D2d[Security]
    D2 --> D2e[Maintainability]
    D2 --> D2f[Portability]

    D2a --> D2a1[Learnability]
    D2a --> D2a2[Efficiency]
    D2a --> D2a3[Memorability]
    D2a --> D2a4[Error Tolerance]
    D2a --> D2a5[Satisfaction]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D2 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D2a fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 FURPS+ Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    F[Functional] --> F1[Use Cases]
    F --> F2[Business Rules]
    U[Usability] --> U1[Learnability]
    U --> U2[Efficiency]
    U --> U3[Satisfaction]
    R[Reliability] --> R1[Availability]
    R --> R2[MTBF]
    R --> R3[Fault Tolerance]
    P[Performance] --> P1[Response Time]
    P --> P2[Throughput]
    P --> P3[Resource Usage]
    S[Supportability] --> S1[Maintainability]
    S --> S2[Testability]
    S --> S3[Configurability]
    X[Constraints] --> X1[Regulatory]
    X --> X2[Hardware Limits]
    X --> X3[Standards]

    style F fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style U fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style X fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Usability Engineering Workflow (Nielsen's Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1 Know the User] --> S2[Stage 2 Competitive Analysis]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Setting Usability Goals]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Parallel Design]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Participatory Design]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 Coordinated Design]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7 Heuristic Evaluation]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8 User Testing]
    S8 --> S9[Stage 9 Iterative Design]
    S9 --> S10[Stage 10 Collect Field Data]
    S10 --> S1

    S3 -.-> M1[Quantitative Metrics e.g. SUS]
    S8 -.-> M2[TCR MTT ER]
    S10 -.-> M3[SUS NPS Logs]

    style S1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S3 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S7 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S8 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S10 fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Mapping Nielsen Heuristics to SRS NFRs

```mermaid
graph TD
    H[10 Nielsen Heuristics] --> H1[Visibility of System Status]
    H --> H2[Match Real World]
    H --> H3[User Control and Freedom]
    H --> H4[Consistency and Standards]
    H --> H5[Error Prevention]
    H --> H6[Recognition rather than Recall]
    H --> H7[Flexibility and Efficiency]
    H --> H8[Aesthetic Minimalist Design]
    H --> H9[Help Users Recognise Diagnose Recover Errors]
    H --> H10[Help and Documentation]

    H1 --> NFR1[NFR Loading Indicators]
    H5 --> NFR2[NFR Undo Redo Affordances]
    H4 --> NFR3[NFR UI Style Guide Conformance]
    H9 --> NFR4[NFR Plain Language Error Messages]
    H10 --> NFR5[NFR Context Sensitive Help within 3 Clicks]

    style H fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style NFR1 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style NFR2 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style NFR3 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style NFR4 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style NFR5 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Functional Requirements and Non-Functional Requirements in an SRS. Give two examples of each from a hospital-management system.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Style):**

| Aspect | Functional Requirements | Non-Functional Requirements |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Specify *what* the system does | Specify *how well* the system does it |
| Nature | Verifiable by input-output mapping | Verifiable by quality measurement |
| Focus | Features and behaviour | Quality attributes, constraints |
| Test method | Unit / functional tests | Performance, load, usability tests |
| Hospital Example 1 | "The system shall allow a doctor to view a patient's medical history." | "The medical history page shall load within 2 seconds on 4G." |
| Hospital Example 2 | "The system shall generate an e-prescription for a diagnosed patient." | "The system shall conform to HL7 FHIR for interoperability." |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often give only *one* example. The rubric explicitly allocates **0.5 marks per example × 2 examples = 1 mark**. Always give **at least two** crisp pairs.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain the three components of usability as defined by ISO 9241-11.**

**Model Answer:**

As per ISO 9241-11:2018, usability is the *extent to which a product can be used by specified users to achieve specified goals with*:

1. **Effectiveness** — the accuracy and completeness with which users achieve specified goals.
   *Example metric:* Task Completion Rate, Number of errors.

2. **Efficiency** — the resources expended in relation to the accuracy and completeness of the goals achieved.
   *Example metric:* Time per task, clicks per task, learnability quotient.

3. **Satisfaction** — the extent to which the user's physical, cognitive, and emotional responses resulting from the use of a product meet the user's needs and expectations.
   *Example metric:* System Usability Scale (SUS), Net Promoter Score (NPS), post-task ratings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do NOT write only "effectiveness, efficiency, satisfaction" without the *one-line operational definition*. KTU 2024 rubric allocates **1 mark per component**.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **FURPS+ model** for classifying non-functional requirements. Discuss each category with two examples relevant to a **mobile banking application**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write **five measurable usability requirements** (SRS-grade) for the same mobile banking app, ensuring each conforms to the **SMART-NFR** criteria. Justify the metric chosen for each. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) FURPS+ for Mobile Banking — Stepwise Explanation (7 marks):**

The FURPS+ model, developed by Grady and broadened by Booch, is a *de facto* industry standard for partitioning NFRs.

**1. Functional (F) — 0.5 mark**

Define the discrete capabilities.
- *Example 1:* The system shall allow users to transfer funds between two linked accounts.
- *Example 2:* The system shall allow users to view last 90 days of transaction history.

**2. Usability (U) — 1.5 marks**

Defines the human-factor quality of the interface.
- *Example 1:* A first-time user shall complete a money transfer in ≤ 60 seconds without training.
- *Example 2:* The interface shall be usable by a person with 20/40 vision and mild arthritis (WCAG 2.1 AA).

**3. Reliability (R) — 1.5 marks**

Defines uptime and fault behaviour.
- *Example 1:* The system shall maintain $\geq 99.95\%$ availability (four 9s) measured monthly.
- *Example 2:* The system shall recover from a network drop within 5 seconds without data loss (auto-resume).

**4. Performance (P) — 1.5 marks**

Defines speed and throughput.
- *Example 1:* Account balance enquiry shall complete within ≤ 1.5 s on a 4G network at 95th percentile.
- *Example 2:* The system shall support ≥ 50,000 concurrent transactions with ≤ 2% degradation.

**5. Supportability (S) — 1 mark**

Defines maintainability, testability, adaptability.
- *Example 1:* All UI text shall be externalised in i18n resource files supporting English, Hindi, Malayalam.
- *Example 2:* Unit test coverage for transaction core shall be ≥ 90%.

**6. + Constraints — 1 mark**

Design and regulatory constraints.
- *Example 1:* The system shall comply with **RBI Cybersecurity Framework 2023**.
- *Example 2:* The mobile app shall run on Android 10+ and iOS 15+.

---

**(b) Five SRS-Grade Usability Requirements (7 marks):**

| # | Requirement | Metric | Threshold | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| UR-1 | A first-time user shall complete first fund transfer in ≤ 90 s with no help. | Mean Time to First Success | $\leq 90$ s | Captures **learnability** objectively |
| UR-2 | ≥ 95% of users shall successfully complete transfer without error. | Task Completion Rate | $\geq 95\%$ | Captures **effectiveness** |
| UR-3 | Mean SUS score across ≥ 30 users shall be ≥ 78. | SUS | $\geq 78$ | Captures **satisfaction** reliably |
| UR-4 | User shall require ≤ 2 corrective attempts to recover from any single input error. | Recovery Path Length | $\leq 2$ | Captures **error tolerance** |
| UR-5 | App shall be operable one-handed on a 6.1-inch phone for all critical tasks. | Touch Reach Heuristic | 100% within thumb arc | Captures **ergonomic efficiency** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — 2 marks deducted if:**
> - Requirement uses vague adjectives like *"easy"*, *"fast"*, *"user-friendly"* without a numeric bound.
> - No *user class* is named (must say *"first-time users"*, *"visually impaired"*, etc.).
> - No *context of use* is stated (mobile vs. desktop, network conditions).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define **usability** as per ISO 9241-11. Differentiate between **Nielsen's 5 usability attributes** and **ISO 9241-11's 3-component model**. Show how they map onto each other. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A startup runs a usability test on its new e-commerce checkout with **20 users**. Compute the **System Usability Scale (SUS)** score from the data below and interpret the result. Also state whether the product clears a typical industry acceptance threshold of **SUS ≥ 68**. *(7 marks)*

**Raw user responses (1–5 Likert) across 10 items:**

| User | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Q5 | Q6 | Q7 | Q8 | Q9 | Q10 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 4 | 2 |
| 2 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| 3 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 |
| 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 |
| 5 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 |

---

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and Mapping (7 marks):**

**ISO 9241-11 Definition — 2 marks**

> *"Usability is the extent to which a product can be used by specified users to achieve specified goals with effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction in a specified context of use."*

**Nielsen's 5 Attributes — 1 mark**

Learnability, Efficiency, Memorability, Errors, Satisfaction.

**Comparison & Mapping — 4 marks**

| ISO 9241-11 Component | Closest Nielsen Attribute(s) | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Effectiveness** | *Errors* (partially) + completion rate | Measures whether the goal was achieved — both talk about success and recovery from errors |
| **Efficiency** | *Efficiency* | Same concept — resources (time) per goal |
| **Satisfaction** | *Satisfaction* | Direct 1-to-1 match |
| *(Implicit in ISO: context of use)* | *Learnability* + *Memorability* | Nielsen expands "context of use" into learnability (new users) and memorability (returning users) |

**Conclusion:** ISO 9241-11 is the **abstract canonical definition**; Nielsen's model is an **operational decomposition** giving measurable engineering handles. They are **complementary**, not competing.

---

**(b) SUS Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute per-user SUS using the standard formula.**

Per the SUS formula:

$$
SUS = 2.5 \times \sum_{k=1}^{10} S_{k,\text{adj}}
$$

| User | Sum of $S_{k,\text{adj}}$ | SUS |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | **75.0** |
| 2 | 20 | **50.0** |
| 3 | 40 | **100.0** |
| 4 | 10 | **25.0** |
| 5 | 30 | **75.0** |

*Detailed calculation for User 1 (illustrative):*
- Odd items (1,3,5,7,9): raw = 4+4+4+4+4 = 20 → adj = (4-1)×5 = 15
- Even items (2,4,6,8,10): raw = 2+1+2+1+2 = 8 → adj = (5-2)+(5-1)+(5-2)+(5-1)+(5-2) = 15
- Sum = 30 → SUS = 75.

**Step 2 — Mean SUS across the 5 users (sample calculation):**

$$
\overline{SUS} = \frac{75 + 50 + 100 + 25 + 75}{5} = \frac{325}{5} = 65.0
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

| Range | Grade | Adjective Rating |
|---|---|---|
| $\geq 80.3$ | A | Excellent |
| 68 – 80.2 | B | Good |
| 68 (industry avg) | C | OK |
| 51 – 67.9 | D | Poor |
| $\leq 51$ | F | Awful |

With $\overline{SUS} = 65.0$, the product is in the **D-band (Poor)**.

**Step 4 — Acceptance Verdict:**

Required threshold: $SUS \geq 68$. Observed: $65.0 < 68$.

$$\boxed{\text{FAIL — usability requirement NOT satisfied.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — 3 marks lost if:**
> - Student forgets to *alternate* odd/even adjustment rules (writes $x - 1$ for all items).
> - Student forgets the *2.5 multiplier* and reports a raw sum out of 40.
> - Student only computes *one* user and generalises — examiners demand *aggregate* SUS.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SRS = Functional + Non-Functional Requirements.** KTU board expects IEEE 29148-style structure (Intro, Overall Description, Specific Requirements, Appendices).
- ✅ **Non-Functional Requirements = the "–ilities".** Remember **FURPS+** (Functional, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Supportability, +Constraints).
- ✅ **Usability (ISO 9241-11) = Effectiveness + Efficiency + Satisfaction** — memorise verbatim.
- ✅ **Nielsen's 5 attributes** = Learnability, Efficiency, Memorability, Errors, Satisfaction — they *decompose* ISO's abstract model.
- ✅ **Every usability NFR must be SMART-NFR** — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Traceable, plus *Named user group* and *Realistic context*.
- ✅ **SUS Formula** = $SUS = 2.5 \times \sum S_{k,\text{adj}}$; odd items subtract 1, even items use $5-x$. Industry average = **68**; passing band **≥ 75**; world-class **≥ 85**.
- ✅ **TCR** = Successes/Attempts; **MTT** = Mean task time; **ER** = Errors/Tasks. Always quote the unit and threshold.
- ✅ **Availability** = $\dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$; three-9s = 99.9%, four-9s = 99.99%.
- ✅ **Heuristic Evaluation** uses Nielsen's 10 heuristics — each heuristic maps to a measurable NFR (e.g., *Error Prevention* → *Undo/Redo*).
- ✅ **Common exam traps:** vague adjectives ("fast", "easy"); missing user-class; missing metric; missing threshold; confusing FR with NFR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Golden Rule for HCI / SRS Questions:**
> *“If you cannot measure it, you cannot write it as a requirement.”* Every usability NFR you write in the KTU exam MUST end with a **number**, a **user class**, and a **context**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shneiderman's Golden Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shneiderman's Golden Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shneiderman's Golden Rules of Interface Design** (originally formulated in 1987, refined in 1998 by Ben Shneiderman and Catherine Plaisant in *Designing the User Interface*) constitute a set of **eight (8) high-level heuristic design principles** that serve as a normative framework for crafting usable, learnable, and user-satisfying interactive systems. These rules are cornerstone heuristics in the KTU 2024 Scheme HCI syllabus under the broader umbrella of *User Interface Design Principles* and are explicitly aligned with the revised Nielsen heuristics adopted in Module 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme — PEPET861):**
> Shneiderman's Golden Rules must be studied alongside Norman's Seven Stages of Action, Norman's Design Principles, and Nielsen's Ten Heuristics. They are tested as both **direct 3-mark short-answer items** and as **applied 14-mark design case studies** in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

In formal terms, Shneiderman's framework operationalizes the **GOMS** (Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules) cognitive model and the **seven stages of action** by prescribing what an interface *should do* to minimize the *gulf of execution* (user → system) and the *gulf of evaluation* (system → user).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Well-Trained Restaurant" Metaphor**
> Imagine walking into a well-managed restaurant:
> - The **menu** is consistently formatted across all branches (consistent typography, layout) — *Rule 1: Consistency*
> - The restaurant has a **ramp, braille menu, and multilingual staff** so everyone can dine — *Rule 2: Universal Usability*
> - The waiter says, *"Your pasta will be ready in 8 minutes"* — *Rule 3: Informative Feedback*
> - At the end, you receive a **bill and a thank-you card** — *Rule 4: Closure*
> - The chef ensures the **dish is prepared correctly the first time**, so no complaints occur — *Rule 5: Error Prevention*
> - If you don't like the dish, you can **return it for a replacement or refund** — *Rule 6: Easy Reversal*
> - You choose **your own table, music volume, and spice level** — *Rule 7: User Control*
> - The menu uses **clear symbols (🌶️, 🌱) instead of long descriptions** so you don't have to memorize — *Rule 8: Reduce Short-Term Memory Load*

Just as a great restaurant anticipates diner needs, Shneiderman's rules help an interface designer **anticipate and respect the cognitive, perceptual, and motor limitations** of the human user. Each rule is empirically grounded in **human factors psychology** and validated by decades of usability studies at the **University of Maryland's Human-Computer Interaction Laboratory (HCIL)**.

## 1.3 The Eight Golden Rules — The Canonical Enumeration

The eight rules, in Shneiderman's canonical ordering, are:

1. **Strive for consistency**
2. **Cater to universal usability**
3. **Offer informative feedback**
4. **Design dialogs to yield closure**
5. **Prevent errors**
6. **Permit easy reversal of actions**
7. **Support internal locus of control**
8. **Reduce short-term memory load**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Box:**
> - **Heuristic** (from Greek *heuriskein* = "to discover"): A rule-of-thumb that is **empirically derived**, not formally proven.
> - **Internal Locus of Control**: A psychological concept from Rotter (1966) where the user feels they are the **origin** of actions, not a passive victim of the system.
> - **Closure**: Gestalt psychology principle — the human mind **craves a sense of completion** in any action sequence.
> - **Universal Usability**: Going beyond *accessibility* (for people with disabilities) to include **all skill levels, ages, cultures, and contexts of use**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Decomposition of Each Rule

### Rule 1 — Strive for Consistency
Consistency operates at **four distinct levels** in HCI design:

- **Consistency of sequence of actions** → same keystrokes/menu paths in similar situations
- **Consistency of terminology** → same words, icons, prompts across screens
- **Consistency of color, layout, and typography** → visual coherence
- **Consistency of platform conventions** → adheres to OS-level guidelines (e.g., Windows HIG, Apple HIG, Material Design)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** Cognitive Load Theory (Sweller, 1988) shows that inconsistent interfaces force the user's **working memory** to perform unnecessary *element interactivity*, degrading performance by **30–50%** in menu-navigation tasks.

### Rule 2 — Cater to Universal Usability
This rule evolved from Shneiderman's original 1987 formulation. It expands *accessibility* (a subset, for users with disabilities — WCAG 2.1/2.2 compliance) into a broader **universal usability** mandate covering:

- Novice → Expert users (**progressive disclosure**)
- Young → Elderly users (age-related motor and perceptual decline)
- Local → Global users (i18n, l10n, RTL scripts)
- Calm → Stressful contexts of use (in-vehicle, surgical, emergency)

### Rule 3 — Offer Informative Feedback
Feedback must be:
- **Continuous** (not just at the end)
- **Specific** (not just "OK" — but "Saved 3 of 3 photos to /Downloads")
- **Appropriately timed** (per Miller's 2008 research, **< 100 ms** for keystroke echo, **< 1 s** for completion of an action, **< 10 s** for complex operations with progress bar)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Nielsen Response-Time Thresholds (instructive for KTU answers):**
> - **< 100 ms** → feels instantaneous (perceptual limit)
> - **100 ms – 1 s** → noticeable but flow is maintained
> - **1 s – 10 s** → user starts to lose attention; show progress indicator
> - **> 10 s** → user will switch tasks; show determinate progress bar with ETA

### Rule 4 — Design Dialogs to Yield Closure
A *dialog* is any unit of interaction (e.g., a form, a checkout flow, a file-save operation). Closure means:
- Clear **beginning, middle, and end**
- A **definitive "done" signal** (e.g., "Order placed — tracking #12345")
- **Grouping** of actions into logical **chunks** (Miller's *The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two* → $7 \pm 2$ chunks)

### Rule 5 — Prevent Errors
Error prevention is more effective than error handling. Strategies include:
- **Constraint enforcement** (e.g., date pickers that disallow invalid dates)
- **Smart defaults** (pre-selecting the most likely option)
- **Confirmation dialogs** for destructive actions
- **Destructive-by-default opt-in** (e.g., "Enable experimental features")

### Rule 6 — Permit Easy Reversal of Actions
Reversibility is the "**undo**" mandate:
- **Single-level Undo** (Ctrl+Z) for actions
- **Multi-level Undo/Redo** (Photoshop: 100+ steps; GIMP: unlimited)
- **Reversible exploration** — users can try options without penalty, encouraging learning-by-doing

### Rule 7 — Support Internal Locus of Control
The user must feel **in command**, not manipulated by the system:
- **Predictable interfaces** — actions have the consequences the user expects
- **No surprise behavior** — system doesn't jump dialogs, change settings, or auto-submit forms
- **Customizable shortcuts and preferences**

### Rule 8 — Reduce Short-Term Memory Load
Short-term (working) memory holds **$7 \pm 2$ chunks** for **15–30 seconds** without rehearsal (Miller, 1956). Design tactics:
- **Recognition over recall** (icons + labels, not just icons)
- **Externalized cognition** (let the system remember, not the user)
- **Chunking** (phone numbers: 555-123-4567)
- **Breadcrumbs, history lists, and recently used files**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Apply in Design Case Studies**

| Rule # | Name | Core Design Tactic | Cognitive Principle Invoked | Quantitative Threshold / Metric |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Consistency | Reuse same icons, menus, layouts | Reduced learning cost | $\leq 3$ unique visual variants per concept |
| 2 | Universal Usability | WCAG 2.2 + progressive disclosure | User diversity accommodation | $\geq 95\%$ task success across all user groups |
| 3 | Informative Feedback | Per-action confirmation messages | Continuous loop closure | Response time $\leq 1$ s for sub-tasks |
| 4 | Closure | Explicit "Done" signal per dialog | Gestalt completion | $7 \pm 2$ chunks per dialog |
| 5 | Error Prevention | Constraint-based inputs, defaults | Pre-attentive validation | Target $\leq 0.5\%$ error rate |
| 6 | Easy Reversal | Multi-level Undo/Redo | Safe exploration | $\geq 20$ undo levels |
| 7 | User Control | Customizable shortcuts, settings | Internal locus of control | No "trapped" dialogs |
| 8 | Reduce STM Load | Recognition over recall | Miller's $7 \pm 2$ | $\leq 7$ items per menu, icons + text labels |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shneiderman's rules are operationalized in **safety-critical** and **commercial-grade** systems:

- **Avionics (DO-178C compliance)**: Rule 3 (Feedback) and Rule 5 (Error Prevention) drive cockpit display design in Boeing 787 and Airbus A350.
- **Medical devices (IEC 62366-1)**: Usability engineering mandates Rule 2 (Universal Usability) and Rule 5 (Error Prevention) — surgical robotics (da Vinci Xi) requires **unambiguous feedback** to the surgeon.
- **Automotive HMI (ISO 15005)**: Rule 8 (Reduce STM Load) caps the number of in-vehicle infotainment interactions to $\leq 23$ seconds of eyes-off-road time per task (NHTSA guideline).
- **Consumer software (Google Material You, Apple HIG)**: All 8 rules are embedded in platform design guidelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to Module 1:** These rules complement Norman's **Seven Stages of Action** by prescribing *how* the gulfs of execution and evaluation should be closed. They also map closely to Nielsen's 10 heuristics (1994); e.g., Nielsen's "Visibility of system status" $\equiv$ Rule 3 (Informative Feedback).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Mapping: From Cognitive Principle → Design Heuristic

The derivation of Shneiderman's rules is **inductive** — they were synthesized from empirical observations of expert designers and validated usability studies. Below is a rigorous step-by-step derivation of the **cognitive-to-design mapping** that KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers.

### Step 1 — Define the User's Cognitive Architecture
We begin with the standard model of human information processing (Card, Moran & Newell, 1983 — the **Model Human Processor**):

$$
\text{Perceptual Processor} \xrightarrow{\text{Early visual storage (250 ms)}} \text{Working Memory} \xrightarrow{\text{Cognitive Processor (70 ms cycles)}} \text{Long-Term Memory}
$$

### Step 2 — Quantify Working Memory Constraints
Miller's (1956) result is restated as:

$$
C_{\text{STM}} = 7 \pm 2 \text{ chunks}
$$

where $C_{\text{STM}}$ is the working memory capacity in discrete chunks. Cowan (2001) refined this to:

$$
C_{\text{STM}} = 4 \pm 1 \text{ chunks} \quad \text{(for adults, focused attention)}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Design Drivers
For each cognitive limitation, a corresponding design heuristic must be generated:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } C_{\text{STM}} &\leq 7 \pm 2 \\
\Rightarrow \text{Design tactic} &= \text{Recognition over recall} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Heuristic} &= \text{Rule 8: Reduce Short-Term Memory Load}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Feedback Loop Closure (Norman's Loop)
For an action to be "complete" in the user's mind, the **gulf of evaluation** must be closed. Mathematically, the perceived system state $\hat{S}$ must converge to the actual system state $S$:

$$
\lim_{t \to t_{\text{feedback}}} \vert \hat{S}(t) - S(t) \vert = 0
$$

where $t_{\text{feedback}}$ is the time at which feedback is delivered. This implies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{feedback}} &\leq 100 \text{ ms (perceptual echo)} \\
t_{\text{feedback}} &\leq 1 \text{ s (action confirmation)} \\
t_{\text{feedback}} &\leq 10 \text{ s (with progress indicator)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This derivation **directly motivates Rule 3: Offer Informative Feedback**.

### Step 5 — Map to All Eight Rules (Analytical Matrix)

The complete mapping from cognitive principle to design rule is summarized in the **analytical matrix** below. This matrix is the **single most important artifact** for writing a 14-mark KTU answer.

| Cognitive Limitation | Quantitative Bound | Resulting Design Tactic | Shneiderman Rule |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Limited STM capacity | $C_{\text{STM}} = 7 \pm 2$ | Chunking, recognition over recall | Rule 8 |
| Slow perceptual processing | $\sim 100$ ms reaction | Continuous feedback | Rule 3 |
| Need for completion | Gestalt closure | Explicit "Done" indicators | Rule 4 |
| Motor slip errors | $1$–$5\%$ baseline error | Constraints, confirmations | Rule 5 |
| Fear of commitment | Psychological aversion to loss | Undo, Redo, history | Rule 6 |
| Desire for autonomy | Locus of control (Rotter) | Customization, predictability | Rule 7 |
| Diverse user population | Demographic variance | Progressive disclosure, i18n | Rule 2 |
| Learning cost | $\propto 1/\text{consistency}$ | Reuse patterns, conventions | Rule 1 |

### Step 6 — Worked Example: Applying All 8 Rules to a Real Interface
**Scenario:** Design a **mobile banking fund-transfer screen** for a KTU student.

| Rule | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 — Consistency | "Confirm" button color (blue) is identical to all other "confirm" actions in the app |
| 2 — Universal Usability | Large-font toggle, screen-reader compatible, supports regional languages |
| 3 — Informative Feedback | "Validating beneficiary…", "Transfer of ₹2,000 successful at 14:32 IST" |
| 4 — Closure | "Transaction complete — Receipt ID: TXN9821" with a green checkmark and shareable PDF |
| 5 — Error Prevention | IFSC code auto-validates; amount capped at daily limit; beneficiary name shown for confirmation |
| 6 — Easy Reversal | "Reverse this transfer" option available for 24 hours (with cooling period for fraud prevention) |
| 7 — User Control | User can save beneficiary as draft before submitting; cancel any time |
| 8 — Reduce STM Load | Recent beneficiaries shown by photo + name (recognition, not recall); no need to remember account numbers |

### Step 7 — Validation Metric
A design following all 8 rules should achieve the following on **standardized usability testing** (per ISO 9241-11):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effectiveness (E)} &= \frac{\text{Tasks completed successfully}}{\text{Tasks attempted}} \geq 0.90 \\
\text{Efficiency (F)} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{task}}} \quad \text{where } T_{\text{task}} \text{ is mean completion time} \\
\text{Satisfaction (S)} &= \text{SUS Score (System Usability Scale, Brooke 1996)} \geq 68
\end{aligned}
$$

These are the **KTU-recommended metrics** for validating a design case study answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchical View of the 8 Golden Rules

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1["SHNEIDERMANS 8 GOLDEN RULES OF INTERFACE DESIGN"] --> grp1["CONCEPTUAL CLUSTER 1: CONSISTENCY AND ACCESSIBILITY"]
    root1 --> grp2["CONCEPTUAL CLUSTER 2: FEEDBACK AND CLOSURE"]
    root1 --> grp3["CONCEPTUAL CLUSTER 3: ERROR HANDLING AND CONTROL"]
    root1 --> grp4["CONCEPTUAL CLUSTER 4: COGNITIVE EFFICIENCY"]

    grp1 --> r1["Rule 1: Strive for Consistency"]
    grp1 --> r2["Rule 2: Cater to Universal Usability"]

    grp2 --> r3["Rule 3: Offer Informative Feedback"]
    grp2 --> r4["Rule 4: Design Dialogs to Yield Closure"]

    grp3 --> r5["Rule 5: Prevent Errors"]
    grp3 --> r6["Rule 6: Permit Easy Reversal of Actions"]
    grp3 --> r7["Rule 7: Support Internal Locus of Control"]

    grp4 --> r8["Rule 8: Reduce Short-Term Memory Load"]

    r3 -.Cognitive principle.-> principle1["Normans Action Loop Closure"]
    r5 -.Cognitive principle.-> principle2["Normans Seven Stages of Action"]
    r8 -.Cognitive principle.-> principle3["Miller 7 plus or minus 2"]
    r1 -.Cognitive principle.-> principle4["Gestalt Laws of Perception"]
    r7 -.Cognitive principle.-> principle5["Rotter Locus of Control 1966"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Mapping Rules to Norman's Action Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user1["USER"] -->|"Goal Formation"| plan1["Intention"]
    plan1 -->|"Specify Action Sequence"| spec1["Specification"]
    spec1 -->|"Execute Action"| sysA["INTERFACE"]

    sysA -->|"Rule 3 Feedback"| eval1["Perception"]
    eval1 -->|"Rule 8 Reduce STM Load"| interp1["Interpretation"]
    interp1 -->|"Rule 4 Closure"| eval2["Evaluation"]
    eval2 -->|"Rule 1 Consistency + Rule 2 Universal Usability"| goalchk["Goal Achieved?"]

    goalchk -->|"Yes"| done1["Action Complete"]
    goalchk -->|"No"| r5error["Rule 5 Error Prevention Active"]
    r5error -->|"Rule 6 Reversal Available"| retrymod["Modify and Retry"]
    retrymod --> plan1
    done1 --> user1

    r7ctrl["Rule 7: User Control — Customization Layer"] -.wraps.-> sysA
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Rule-Compliant Interface

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["INPUT LAYER - Perceptual Processor Side"]
        in1["Visual Input - Icons and Labels - Rule 1 and Rule 8"]
        in2["Audio Input - Screen Reader - Rule 2"]
        in3["Motor Input - Tap, Swipe, Type - Rule 7"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING_LAYER["PROCESSING LAYER - Cognitive Processor Side"]
        proc1["Action Validator - Rule 5 Error Prevention"]
        proc2["State Manager - Maintains Undo Stack - Rule 6"]
        proc3["Dialog Controller - Manages Dialogs and Closure - Rule 4"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["OUTPUT LAYER - Effectors Side"]
        out1["Visual Display - Consistent Theme - Rule 1"]
        out2["Tactile and Audio Feedback - Rule 3"]
        out3["Haptic and Animation Confirmations - Rule 4"]
    end

    subgraph MEMORY_AID_LAYER["MEMORY AID LAYER"]
        mem1["Recent Items - Recognition - Rule 8"]
        mem2["Auto-complete Suggestions - Rule 8"]
        mem3["Breadcrumb History - Rule 8"]
    end

    in1 --> proc1
    in2 --> proc1
    in3 --> proc1
    proc1 --> proc2
    proc2 --> proc3
    proc3 --> out1
    proc3 --> out2
    proc3 --> out3
    mem1 --> in1
    mem2 --> in1
    mem3 --> proc3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU 2024 Examiners:**
> The diagrams above visually separate the **8 rules** into 4 conceptual clusters (consistency/accessibility, feedback/closure, error/control, cognitive efficiency). This cluster structure is the recommended answer template for 14-mark design questions in the ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define Shneiderman's Golden Rules of Interface Design. List any three of them with one-line explanations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — Rubric Breakdown):**
- **1 mark** — Definition: A set of 8 empirically-derived heuristic design principles formulated by Ben Shneiderman (1987, refined 1998) for designing usable, learnable, and satisfying user interfaces.
- **2 marks** — Any 3 rules correctly named with concise (one-line) explanations. Award ⅔ mark per rule, deduct ½ mark for each missing or vague explanation.

**Sample correct answer:**
1. **Strive for Consistency** — Identical terminology, sequences, and visual styling should be used in similar situations.
2. **Offer Informative Feedback** — Every user action should produce a clear, timely, and specific system response.
3. **Reduce Short-Term Memory Load** — Interfaces should promote recognition over recall, respecting Miller's $7 \pm 2$ working memory limit.

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary Exam]`
**Differentiate between "Recognition" and "Recall" in interface design, and explain how Shneiderman's 8th rule applies this principle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — Rubric Breakdown):**
- **1 mark** — Definitions: Recall = retrieving information from memory without cues (hard); Recognition = identifying information when presented (easier).
- **1 mark** — Examples: Recall = typing a phone number from memory; Recognition = selecting a contact from a photo grid.
- **1 mark** — Application of Rule 8: The rule mandates recognition-based UI (icons with labels, dropdowns, autocomplete, breadcrumbs) to minimize working memory load and align with Miller's $7 \pm 2$ chunk limit.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B carries 14 marks per question, with two sub-parts typically of 7 marks each. The internal choice is between two completely independent questions, both drawn from the same module.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Choice A]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain any **four** of Shneiderman's Golden Rules in detail, with one real-world example for each.

**(b) [7 marks]** Design the user interface of a **university online exam registration portal** (KTU students) by applying **at least six** of Shneiderman's Golden Rules. Justify each application with the underlying cognitive principle.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (a) [7 marks]

**1. Strive for Consistency (1½ marks)**
- *Explanation:* Use identical terminology, sequences of actions, color schemes, and layouts across all screens. Consistency operates at the level of words, situations, and actions.
- *Example:* In Microsoft Office, the keyboard shortcut **Ctrl + S** (Save) works identically across Word, Excel, and PowerPoint, regardless of the active document type.
- *Cognitive basis:* Consistency reduces **gulf of execution** (Norman, 1988) by enabling users to transfer learning from one screen to another.

**2. Offer Informative Feedback (1½ marks)**
- *Explanation:* Every user action must be acknowledged with a clear, timely, and specific system response within the perceptual time window.
- *Example:* When uploading a file to Google Drive, the system shows a real-time progress bar with percentage complete, file size, and estimated time remaining, and concludes with a "Upload complete" toast notification.
- *Cognitive basis:* Feedback closes the **perception–cognition–action** loop (Norman's seven stages). Nielsen's threshold: **$\leq 100$ ms** for keystroke echo, **$\leq 1$ s** for action confirmation, **$\leq 10$ s** with progress bar.

**3. Prevent Errors (1½ marks)**
- *Explanation:* The interface should anticipate and block erroneous actions before they occur, rather than relying on error messages after the fact.
- *Example:* Date pickers in flight-booking apps (e.g., MakeMyTrip) disable past dates and dates beyond the airline's booking window (typically 365 days), preventing logically invalid selections.
- *Cognitive basis:* Errors originate in Norman's **gulf of execution**. Pre-emptive constraint enforcement reduces the user's **slip rate** from a baseline 1–5% to below 0.5% (Reason, 1990 — Human Error theory).

**4. Reduce Short-Term Memory Load (1½ marks)**
- *Explanation:* The interface should leverage recognition over recall and present information in chunks bounded by Miller's $7 \pm 2$ limit.
- *Example:* Phone numbers are formatted as **+91 555 123 4567** (3 chunks) rather than as a 13-digit ungrouped string; the WhatsApp chat list shows contact names with profile photos and last-message previews.
- *Cognitive basis:* Cowan (2001) refined Miller's result to $4 \pm 1$ chunks for focused attention. UI design that exceeds this bound causes **cognitive overload** and measurable performance degradation.

**Conclusion (½ mark):**
The four rules — Consistency, Feedback, Error Prevention, and Reduced STM Load — together form a **defense-in-depth** strategy against the cognitive and motor limitations of human users. Modern HCI platforms (Material Design 3, Apple HIG, Microsoft Fluent) embed these rules as **codified design tokens** rather than informal guidelines.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (b) [7 marks — Design Justification Table]

| Shneiderman Rule | Application in KTU Exam Registration Portal | Cognitive Principle |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Consistency** | All form fields use the same label position (above field), same blue "Continue" button, and identical font (Roboto Regular 16pt) on every page | Reduces learning cost; leverages Gestalt similarity |
| **2. Universal Usability** | Language toggle (English / Malayalam), high-contrast mode, screen-reader (NVDA/JAWS) compatibility, large-font toggle, mobile-responsive layout | WCAG 2.2 AA compliance; supports users with diverse abilities |
| **3. Informative Feedback** | After clicking "Register": "Validating… → Seat availability checked → Payment gateway loading (3s) → Registration #KTU2024EX98765 confirmed at 11:45 IST" | Closes Norman's perception–cognition loop |
| **4. Closure** | Final screen shows: "Registration Complete", a downloadable hall ticket, calendar invite, and an SMS/email confirmation — a clear "end" to the dialog | Gestalt principle of closure; provides cognitive peace |
| **5. Error Prevention** | Field-level inline validation (e.g., Register Number must match KTU format `KTU\d{8}`), prevent double-submission by disabling button after one click, show real-time fee calculation | Pre-attentive validation; reduces slips (Reason, 1990) |
| **6. Easy Reversal** | "Withdraw Registration" option visible until 48 hours before the exam; full refund initiated with a single click; "Undo Last Action" on multi-step form | Permits safe exploration; reduces learner anxiety |
| **7. User Control** | "Save as Draft" at every step; ability to edit any section before final submission; no forced dialogs; explicit "Cancel" option on every page | Internal locus of control (Rotter, 1966) |
| **8. Reduce STM Load** | All registered courses shown in a visual list (icon + name + code), no need to remember subject codes; breadcrumbs ("Home > Registration > Payment > Confirmation") | Miller's $7 \pm 2$; recognition over recall |

**Validation Metrics (½ mark):**
- Effectiveness $E \geq 0.90$ on first attempt
- SUS (System Usability Scale) score $\geq 75$ (Grade B or higher)
- Mean task completion time $T_{\text{task}} \leq 4$ minutes

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Choice B]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast **Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules** with **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** for usability. Identify at least **three** areas of overlap and **two** key differences.

**(b) [7 marks]** A regional bank in Kerala is redesigning its **ATM interface** to serve users aged 18–80, including first-time users and elderly customers. Apply **Shneiderman's Golden Rules** to redesign the **cash withdrawal flow** and justify each design decision with a usability metric.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules (1987, refined 1998) and Nielsen's 10 Heuristics (1994) are the **two most widely cited heuristic frameworks** in HCI. Both were derived empirically from expert practice, but differ in scope, granularity, and intended audience.

**Three Areas of Overlap (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| # | Shneiderman Rule | Corresponding Nielsen Heuristic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Rule 3: Offer Informative Feedback | H1: Visibility of System Status |
| 2 | Rule 5: Prevent Errors | H5: Error Prevention |
| 3 | Rule 6: Permit Easy Reversal | H3: User Control and Freedom |

**Two Key Differences (2 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Aspect | Shneiderman | Nielsen |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of rules** | Exactly 8 (compact, memorable) | Exactly 10 (more granular) |
| **Emphasis** | Strong on **cognitive support** (Rule 8) and **universal accessibility** (Rule 2) | Strong on **aesthetic and minimalist design** (H8) and **help & documentation** (H10) |
| **Origin** | Synthesized from 30+ years of HCI lab work at University of Maryland | Synthesized from discount usability engineering (factor analysis of 249 usability problems) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The two frameworks are **complementary, not competing**. Nielsen's heuristics are best used for **heuristic evaluation** (expert walkthroughs), while Shneiderman's rules serve better as **prescriptive design guidelines** at the architecture phase. A complete HCI design process (and a complete KTU answer) should reference both.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 marks]

**Redesign of Cash Withdrawal Flow for Kerala Bank ATM**

**Step 1 — Original Flow (Problem Statement):**
Current flow: `Insert Card → Enter PIN → Select "Withdraw" → Enter Amount → Confirm → Cash Dispensed` — a 5-step linear dialog with no closure feedback, no error prevention, and no support for elderly users.

**Step 2 — Redesigned Flow with Shneiderman's Rules Applied:**

| Step in Flow | Interface Element | Rule Applied | Justification with Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Welcome screen | "Welcome, Sri. Ravi Kumar" with ATM card photo | Rule 2 — Universal Usability | Personalization supports first-time users; SUS score gain of +12 points (Bangor et al., 2009) |
| PIN entry | On-screen keypad with shuffled layout, large 30mm × 30mm buttons, tactile audio cues, Braille labels | Rule 2 + Rule 8 | Recognition over recall; accessible to visually impaired; reduces STM load |
| Main menu | "Cash Withdrawal" button is the largest, top-left, in green (high saliency) | Rule 1 + Rule 5 | Consistency with all SBI/SBT ATM flows (Hick's Law: $\text{RT} = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)$ — fewer prominent options = faster selection) |
| Amount selection | Quick-pick buttons: ₹500, ₹1000, ₹2000, ₹5000 + "Other Amount" with on-screen numeric pad | Rule 8 | Recognition, not recall; quick-picks reduce typing errors by 78% (Nielsen, 2004) |
| Confirmation | "You are withdrawing ₹2000 from A/C ****5678. Proceed?" with large "Yes / No" buttons | Rule 3 + Rule 4 | Informative feedback with closure; prevents muscle-memory slip errors |
| Cash dispensing | "Please collect your cash and card. Thank you, Sri. Ravi!" + receipt with audio confirmation | Rule 4 | Explicit closure signal; addresses Norman's gulf of evaluation |
| Error handling (e.g., insufficient funds) | Plain-language message in English + Malayalam: "Balance is low. Please try a smaller amount." | Rule 5 + Rule 2 | Prevents confusion, supports universal usability |
| Undo / Cancel | "Cancel" button visible on every screen, returns user to main menu without ejecting card prematurely | Rule 6 + Rule 7 | Permits reversal; supports user control |
| Help | "Press the help button for a live agent" available at every screen, with a 60s average response time | Rule 2 | Universal usability for elderly and novice users |

**Validation (½ mark):**
- Target task completion time for elderly users (60+): $T_{\text{task}} \leq 45$ seconds (from baseline 90+ s).
- Error rate reduction: From baseline $\sim 8\%$ to $\leq 1\%$.
- User satisfaction measured via After-Scenario Questionnaire (ASQ, Lewis 1991): mean score $\leq 1.7$ on a 7-point scale.

**Conclusion (½ mark):**
The redesigned ATM flow applies all 8 of Shneiderman's Golden Rules systematically, transforming a frustrating, error-prone experience into an inclusive, learnable one. The redesign is particularly relevant to Kerala's demographic profile, which includes a high proportion of elderly and first-time ATM users in rural districts.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (per KTU 2024 Valuation Key):**
> 1. **Listing rules without application** — A 14-mark answer that merely *lists* the 8 rules without applying them to a specific interface scores at most **5–6 marks**. KTU examiners expect a **design case study** with concrete UI elements.
> 2. **Confusing Shneiderman with Nielsen** — These are two distinct frameworks. Mixing them up or attributing the wrong rules to the wrong author costs **1–2 marks** per instance.
> 3. **Missing cognitive justification** — Each design decision must be justified with a *cognitive principle* (e.g., Miller's $7 \pm 2$, Norman's gulfs, Gestalt closure). Answers that lack this justification lose **1 mark per sub-part**.
> 4. **Forgetting the "closure" and "reversal" rules** — Students often omit Rules 4 and 6. Examiners note this as a recurring gap.
> 5. **No mention of universal usability in modern context** — Rule 2 is often treated as a synonym for "accessibility" (disability-only). KTU 2024 specifically expects the **broader universal usability** interpretation, including age, culture, and context-of-use diversity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Prep)**

- [x] Shneiderman's Golden Rules are **exactly 8** in number (not 7, not 10 — those are Norman and Nielsen respectively).
- [x] Author: **Ben Shneiderman** (with Catherine Plaisant in the 1998 refinement). First proposed in **1987**.
- [x] The rules cover: **Consistency, Universal Usability, Feedback, Closure, Error Prevention, Reversal, User Control, Memory Load Reduction**.
- [x] Miller's **$7 \pm 2$** chunks is the key cognitive basis for Rule 8.
- [x] Norman's **gulfs of execution and evaluation** underpin Rules 3, 5, and 6.
- [x] Gestalt **closure** principle is the basis for Rule 4.
- [x] **Rotter's (1966) internal locus of control** is the basis for Rule 7.
- [x] Nielsen's response-time thresholds: **$\leq 100$ ms** (echo), **$\leq 1$ s** (confirmation), **$\leq 10$ s** (with progress).
- [x] For 14-mark KTU answers, **always** present a **design case study** with at least 6 rules applied.
- [x] **Universal usability $\supset$ accessibility** — Rule 2 is broader than WCAG compliance.
- [x] For 3-mark questions, the **definition + 3 rules with one-line explanations** is the safest format.
- [x] Standard KTU validation metrics to cite: **SUS score $\geq 68$**, **task success rate $\geq 90\%$**, **task time $\leq 5$ minutes** for typical flows.
- [x] Always end a 14-mark answer with a **conclusion** linking the design back to **user-centered design philosophy**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Norman's Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — NORMAN'S PRINCIPLES OF INTERACTION DESIGN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Norman's Principles of Design** are a foundational framework in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) formulated by cognitive scientist **Donald Arthur Norman** (1988) in his seminal work *The Design of Everyday Things*. These principles define the psychological and cognitive criteria that distinguish a *usable*, *learnable*, and *error-tolerant* interface from a frustrating one. They are categorised into **seven core design principles** and a **seven-stage model of action** that together address the *Gulf of Execution* and *Gulf of Evaluation*.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, Norman's Principles are positioned under **Module 2 (Interaction Design)** and are examinable as direct short-answer theory (Part A — 2 marks) and as applied design-analysis questions (Part B — 14 marks).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Norman Door"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Push Door vs. Pull Door**
> Imagine walking up to a glass door with a flat metal handle. Without thinking, you *push* — but it is meant to be *pulled*. You push harder, look confused, and eventually someone walks by and pulls it open. This is what Norman calls a **"Norman Door"** — a door whose design fails to communicate how it works.
> - The door has **no visible affordance** (you cannot tell whether to push or pull).
> - It provides **no immediate feedback** (no swing direction, no signs).
> - It violates **mapping** (handle suggests pulling, but only one side works).
> - It breaks **discoverability** and **constraints**.
>
> Replace this door with a flat plate (you must push) or a vertical handle (you must pull) — and the problem disappears. **Good design is invisible; bad design is everywhere.**

### 1.3 Why Norman's Principles Matter in HCI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These principles are not arbitrary rules — they are derived from **human cognitive architecture**: perception, memory, attention, motor control, and problem-solving. Any interface (web, mobile, embedded, industrial) that respects these principles will be **learnable in minutes**, **memorable**, and **low in user error rate**.

| Cognitive Limit | Engineering Implication | Norman's Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Limited short-term memory (**7 ± 2 items**) | Avoid overloading menus | **Visibility & Simplicity** |
| Slow pattern recognition | Use familiar icons | **Consistency & Standards** |
| Attention bottlenecks | Reduce visual clutter | **Affordances** |
| High error cost in critical systems | Prevent mistakes proactively | **Constraints & Recovery** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Seven Principles

## 2.1 The Seven Principles of Design (Norman)

Each principle addresses a specific failure mode of poorly designed interfaces.

### Principle 1 — Discoverability
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: The user can readily determine what actions are possible and the current state of the system simply by *looking at it*.
> - **Why it matters**: Users should never have to "hunt" for functionality.
> - **Engineering example**: A smartphone's home screen showing all installed apps through icons.

### Principle 2 — Feedback
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: After every user action, the system must provide **immediate, clear, and unambiguous** information about what has happened and what has changed.
> - **Modes of feedback**: visual, auditory, haptic, textual.
> - **Engineering example**: A button that depresses, animates, and emits a "click" sound on press.

### Principle 3 — Affordance
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: A perceived and actual property of an object that suggests how it can be used.
> - **Real affordance** (physical): a handle affords *grasping*.
> - **Perceived affordance** (psychological): a 3D-shaded button on a touchscreen affords *pressing*.
> - **Anti-pattern**: a flat, grey rectangle labelled "Submit" affords nothing.

### Principle 4 — Mapping
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: The relationship between a control and its effect must follow a **natural, spatial, or cultural** logic.
> - **Best mapping**: spatial correspondence (steering wheel ↔ car wheels).
> - **Worst mapping**: arbitrary layout (gas pedal on left, brake on right in a left-hand-drive car would be fatal).

### Principle 5 — Constraints
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: Restricting the set of possible user actions to prevent errors before they occur.
> - **Types**: physical (only USB-C fits), semantic (only numbers in a phone field), cultural (red = stop), logical (greyed-out menu items).

### Principle 6 — Consistency
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: Similar operations should look and behave similarly across the interface, and the system should behave the same way in similar contexts.
> - **Internal consistency**: same button does the same thing on every screen.
> - **External consistency**: same colour scheme as the platform (e.g., Material Design on Android).

### Principle 7 — Visibility (and Perceived Accessibility)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: Important functions and controls should be **visible to the user** so they can be discovered without learning.
> - **Counter-Principle — Invisible Functionality**: If a feature is invisible, users will assume it does not exist.

## 2.2 Norman's Seven Stages of Action

Norman further proposed that every interaction consists of **seven cognitive stages**, grouped into two major activities: **Execution** (doing) and **Evaluation** (checking).

| Stage # | Stage Name | Activity | Bridging Concept |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Forming the Goal | Execution | — |
| 2 | Forming the Intention | Execution | — |
| 3 | Specifying the Action | Execution | **Gulf of Execution** |
| 4 | Executing the Action | Execution | **Gulf of Execution** |
| 5 | Perceiving the System State | Evaluation | **Gulf of Evaluation** |
| 6 | Interpreting the System State | Evaluation | **Gulf of Evaluation** |
| 7 | Evaluating the Outcome | Evaluation | — |

## 2.3 The Two Gulfs

> [!NOTE]
> **Gulf of Execution**: The gap between the *user's intention* (what they want to do) and the *physical actions allowed by the system*. A wide gulf means the user cannot easily make the system do what they want.
>
> **Gulf of Evaluation**: The gap between the *system's physical state* and the *user's perception/understanding* of that state. A wide gulf means the user cannot easily tell what the system just did.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Principle | One-Line Definition | Common Anti-Pattern | Best Fix |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Discoverability | What can I do? | Hidden menus | Visible labels & icons |
| 2 | Feedback | What just happened? | No response on click | Animation + sound |
| 3 | Affordance | How do I use it? | Flat grey rectangle button | 3D shadow + label |
| 4 | Mapping | Where will this take me? | Reversed scroll direction | Natural mapping |
| 5 | Constraints | What's allowed? | Free-text for phone | Numeric keypad |
| 6 | Consistency | Is this familiar? | Different icon for save | Same icon everywhere |
| 7 | Visibility | Where is it? | Required feature hidden | Always visible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: In **safety-critical systems** (aviation cockpits, medical devices, industrial SCADA, automotive infotainment), Norman's principles are *non-negotiable*. The FDA, EASA, and ISO 9241-210 explicitly reference them in human-factors certification standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Applying Norman's Principles

Because Norman's Principles are a **qualitative framework**, the "derivation" below is an **analytical procedure** for applying them rigorously to any interface.

## 3.1 The N.A.P. Analysis Method (Norman's Analytical Procedure)

This is the **systematic procedure** examiners expect when you are given an interface to evaluate in the KTU exam.

**Step 1 — Inventory the Interface Elements**
List every interactive component (buttons, sliders, menus, gestures).

**Step 2 — Test Discoverability**
For each element, ask: *"Can a first-time user identify what this does without instruction?"*
- If **No** → flag as a **Discoverability Violation**.

**Step 3 — Test Affordance**
For each element, ask: *"Does its appearance suggest how to operate it?"*
- If **No** → flag as an **Affordance Violation**.

**Step 4 — Test Feedback**
For each interactive element, perform the action. Ask: *"Did the system respond within 100 ms with a clear, multi-modal cue?"*
- If **No** → flag as a **Feedback Violation**.

**Step 5 — Test Mapping**
For each control, ask: *"Does its position/label/intuitively map to its result?"*
- If **No** → flag as a **Mapping Violation**.

**Step 6 — Test Constraints**
For each input field, ask: *"Are illegal inputs prevented at the UI level?"*
- If **No** → flag as a **Constraint Violation**.

**Step 7 — Test Consistency**
Compare each element to its peers in the same app and across the OS.
- If **No** → flag as a **Consistency Violation**.

**Step 8 — Test Visibility**
Ask: *"Are all critical functions reachable in ≤ 2 taps/clicks from the home screen?"*
- If **No** → flag as a **Visibility Violation**.

**Step 9 — Compute the **N.A.P. Compliance Score (NCS)****

$$
\text{NCS} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{7} w_{i}\,\cdot\,\text{Pass}_{i}}{\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{7} w_{i}} \,\times\, 100\,\%
$$

Where:
- $\text{Pass}_{i} \in \{0, 1\}$ = whether principle $i$ passed.
- $w_i$ = weight of principle $i$ (default = 1; in safety-critical systems, **Affordance** and **Feedback** may be weighted 2×).
- **NCS ≥ 85 %** → *Usable*, **NCS 60–84 %** → *Refinements needed*, **NCS < 60 %** → *Redesign required*.

**Step 10 — Bridge the Two Gulfs**

For the **Gulf of Execution**, apply *Visibility + Affordance + Mapping + Constraints*.

$$
\text{Gulf of Execution Width} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\text{Visibility} + \text{Affordance} + \text{Mapping} + \text{Constraints}}
$$

For the **Gulf of Evaluation**, apply *Feedback + Visibility + Consistency*.

$$
\text{Gulf of Evaluation Width} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\text{Feedback} + \text{Visibility} + \text{Consistency}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of the Derivation**: An interface that **minimises both gulfs** is one where the user can (a) easily figure out *what to do* and *how to do it*, and (b) easily figure out *what just happened* and *whether it was successful*.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Elevator Button Panel

| Component | Discoverability | Affordance | Feedback | Mapping | Constraints | Consistency | Visibility | Verdict |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Floor buttons (1–10) | ✓ | ✓ (push-able) | ✓ (light + beep) | ✓ (number = floor) | ✓ (only one lit) | ✓ (all identical) | ✓ | **Pass** |
| Emergency button | ✓ (red) | ✗ (looks flat) | ✓ (alarm) | ✓ (red = danger) | ✓ (recessed) | ✓ | ✓ | **Borderline** |
| "Door Close" button | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (no child-lock) | ✓ | ✓ | **Fail (constraints)** |
| Braille next to floor # | ✗ (often missing) | n/a | n/a | ✓ | n/a | ✓ | ✓ | **Fail (discoverability for visually impaired)** |

**Analyst Conclusion**: The conventional elevator panel **fails 2 of 7 principles** for accessibility. The recommended redesign: tactile ridges, audible floor announcement, and child-lock on "Door Close".

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Norman's Seven Stages of Action — Cognitive Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stage 1: Forming the Goal"] --> B["Stage 2: Forming the Intention"]
    B --> C["Stage 3: Specifying the Action"]
    C --> D["Stage 4: Executing the Action"]
    D --> E["Stage 5: Perceiving the System State"]
    E --> F["Stage 6: Interpreting the System State"]
    F --> G["Stage 7: Evaluating the Outcome"]

    C -.- X["GULF OF EXECUTION"]
    D -.- X
    E -.- Y["GULF OF EVALUATION"]
    F -.- Y

    G --> H{"Goal Achieved?"}
    H -->|Yes| I["STOP - Task Complete"]
    H -->|No| J["Re-form Goal - Iterate"]
    J --> A
```

## 4.2 The Two Gulfs — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Exec["EXECUTION SIDE"]
        U1["User Intention"] --> GE["Gulf of Execution"]
        GE --> S1["System Action Layer"]
    end

    subgraph Eval["EVALUATION SIDE"]
        S2["System State Output"] --> GV["Gulf of Evaluation"]
        GV --> U2["User Perception"]
    end

    S1 --> S2
    U2 --> U1

    GE -.- M1["Bridge with: Visibility, Affordance, Mapping, Constraints"]
    GV -.- M2["Bridge with: Feedback, Visibility, Consistency"]
```

## 4.3 The Seven Principles — Hierarchical Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NORM["Normans Seven Principles of Design"]

    NORM --> P1["Principle 1: Discoverability"]
    NORM --> P2["Principle 2: Feedback"]
    NORM --> P3["Principle 3: Affordance"]
    NORM --> P4["Principle 4: Mapping"]
    NORM --> P5["Principle 5: Constraints"]
    NORM --> P6["Principle 6: Consistency"]
    NORM --> P7["Principle 7: Visibility"]

    P1 --> A1["User can see what actions are possible"]
    P2 --> A2["System shows results of every action"]
    P3 --> A3["Appearance suggests usage"]
    P4 --> A4["Control-to-effect relationship is natural"]
    P5 --> A5["Illegal actions are prevented"]
    P6 --> A6["Same action behaves same way everywhere"]
    P7 --> A7["Critical functions stay visible"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q:** Define **Affordance** in the context of interface design. Give one example.

> **Model Answer (2 marks):**
> **Affordance** is a perceived and actual property of an object that suggests how it can be used (Norman, 1988). It communicates the possible actions a user can perform with a control simply by *looking* at it.
> **Example (1 mark):** A 3D-shaded rectangular button with rounded corners affords *pressing*; a door handle affords *pulling*; a slider affords *sliding*.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q:** What is the **Gulf of Evaluation**? How can it be reduced?

> **Model Answer (2 marks):**
> The **Gulf of Evaluation** is the gap between the system's physical state and the user's perception/understanding of that state (1 mark). It is reduced by providing **immediate, clear feedback**, keeping critical information **visible**, and ensuring **consistency** so users can correctly interpret what the system has done (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Do **NOT** list the 7 principles in a single bullet list and stop — that scores ≤ 4 of 14 marks.
> - For full marks you must **explain each principle** (1 mark), **give a real interface example** (½ mark), and **state what design failure occurs if it is violated** (½ mark).
> - When drawing the **Stages of Action**, label the **two gulfs explicitly** — students lose 2 marks for omitting them.
> - Use **diagrams wherever possible**; a well-drawn mermaid/flowchart earns 1–2 extra marks under "Presentation".

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain Norman's **Seven Principles of Design** with a suitable interface example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe Norman's **Seven Stages of Action** model. Explain the **Gulf of Execution** and **Gulf of Evaluation**. How does an interface designer minimise these two gulfs? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 Marks]

The **Seven Principles of Design** proposed by Donald Norman (1988) are fundamental guidelines for usable interface design:

**1. Discoverability (1 mark):** The user should be able to determine what actions are possible simply by perceiving the interface. *Example:* A smartphone home screen with visible app icons. *Failure:* Hidden gesture-only menus.

**2. Feedback (1 mark):** Every user action must produce an immediate, visible response. *Example:* A "like" button that turns red and increments a counter. *Failure:* Buttons that appear unresponsive.

**3. Affordance (1 mark):** The visual appearance of a control must suggest its operation. *Example:* A slider thumb affords *dragging*. *Failure:* A flat grey rectangle that does not afford clicking.

**4. Mapping (1 mark):** The relationship between a control and its result must be intuitive. *Example:* Volume up button on the right side of a remote maps to increased speaker output. *Failure:* Reversed temperature controls in hotel showers.

**5. Constraints (1 mark):** The interface should restrict illegal actions. *Example:* A numeric keypad appearing for a phone-number field. *Failure:* Allowing letters in a date field.

**6. Consistency (1 mark):** Similar operations should look and behave similarly. *Example:* "Save" is always a floppy-disk icon across Microsoft Office. *Failure:* "Delete" being a trash icon on one screen and an "X" on another.

**7. Visibility (1 mark):** Important controls must be visible. *Example:* Critical "Emergency Stop" button on industrial machinery. *Failure:* Hiding a required confirmation dialog behind a settings menu.

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 Marks]

Refer to **Section 4.1 (Stages of Action diagram)** for the required schematic. The seven stages are grouped into two phases:

**Execution Phase (Stages 1–4):** The user forms a goal, forms an intention, specifies an action, and physically executes it. The **Gulf of Execution** lies between *specifying the action* and *executing it* — this is the gap between what the user wants and what the system allows. (2 marks)

**Evaluation Phase (Stages 5–7):** The user perceives the system's response, interprets it, and compares it to the original goal. The **Gulf of Evaluation** lies between *perceiving* and *interpreting* — the gap between the system's actual state and the user's understanding. (2 marks)

**Bridging the Gulfs (3 marks):**

| Gulf | Bridges to Apply |
|---|---|
| **Gulf of Execution** | **Visibility** (so user knows options), **Affordance** (so user knows how), **Mapping** (so user knows where), **Constraints** (so user knows what's allowed) |
| **Gulf of Evaluation** | **Feedback** (immediate response), **Visibility** (state always shown), **Consistency** (interpretation matches expectation) |

A well-designed interface keeps both gulfs **narrow**, enabling seamless two-way communication between user and system.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` *(Internal Choice for Q.A)*

**(a)** Differentiate between **Gulf of Execution** and **Gulf of Evaluation**. Explain how Norman proposes to bridge each gulf. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply Norman's principles to evaluate the **user interface of a Microwave Oven**. Identify at least **four principles** with specific examples of how each is satisfied or violated, and suggest redesigns. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 Marks]

| Basis | Gulf of Execution | Gulf of Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition (2 marks)** | Gap between user's intention and the physical actions the system permits. | Gap between the system's physical state and the user's perception of that state. |
| **Direction (1 mark)** | User → System | System → User |
| **Question it answers** | *"How do I make the system do what I want?"* | *"What did the system just do?"* |
| **Located in (Stages)** | Stages 3 → 4 of Norman's model | Stages 5 → 6 of Norman's model |
| **Bridged by (2 marks)** | Visibility, Affordance, Mapping, Constraints | Feedback, Visibility, Consistency |
| **Symptom of wide gulf** | User does not know *how* to operate the system. | User does not know *what* the system did or whether it succeeded. |

(2 marks for diagram showing both gulfs in the Stages of Action — refer to Section 4.1.)

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 Marks]

| # | Principle | Evaluation of Microwave Oven | Verdict | Suggested Redesign |
|---|---|---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Affordance (2 marks)** | The start button is a plain rectangle identical to the stop button; users cannot tell which is which by touch. | ✗ Violated | Use **distinct shapes** — start = raised round button; stop = large flat red recessed button. |
| 2 | **Feedback (2 marks)** | The beeper sounds once when cooking ends but does not repeat — users miss it. No visual countdown on the door for hearing-impaired users. | ✗ Violated | Add a **flashing light**, **repeating beep every 30 s**, and a **large LCD countdown** visible through the door. |
| 3 | **Mapping (1 mark)** | Power-level knob has numbers (1–10) but no indication of what each means (watts? %?). | ✗ Violated | Replace numbers with **descriptive labels** — "Defrost", "Warm", "Reheat", "High". |
| 4 | **Constraints (1 mark)** | The door can be opened while the microwave is running — minor safety concern. | ✗ Violated | Add a **physical interlock** that pauses cooking when the door opens. |
| 5 | **Consistency (1 mark)** | Start and Stop buttons use the same icon style but different colours — inconsistent with industry norms (green/red). | ✗ Violated | Adopt **green for start**, **red for stop** universally. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The microwave oven satisfies **Visibility and Discoverability** (labels visible) but **violates Affordance, Feedback, Mapping, Constraints, and Consistency** — making it frustrating especially for elderly or first-time users. A redesign following Norman's principles would drastically reduce user errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 12 Things to Remember for the KTU Exam on Norman's Principles**

- [x] Norman authored *The Design of Everyday Things* (originally *The Psychology of Everyday Things*, 1988).
- [x] There are **exactly 7 Principles**: Discoverability, Feedback, Affordance, Mapping, Constraints, Consistency, Visibility.
- [x] There are **exactly 7 Stages of Action**: Goal → Intention → Specification → Execution → Perception → Interpretation → Evaluation.
- [x] The **Gulf of Execution** lies between user intention and system action (Stages 3 → 4).
- [x] The **Gulf of Evaluation** lies between system state and user perception (Stages 5 → 6).
- [x] **Affordance** is *perceived* + *actual* — both must be present.
- [x] **Mapping** is best when **spatial** (steering wheel ↔ wheels).
- [x] **Constraints** can be physical, semantic, cultural, or logical.
- [x] **Consistency** has two flavours: **internal** (within an app) and **external** (with platform conventions).
- [x] **Visibility** is the opposite of *hidden menus*; the rule is "important things must be seen".
- [x] For full marks in 14-mark questions, always **diagram the Stages of Action** with the **two gulfs labelled**.
- [x] Apply the **N.A.P. Analysis Method** (10-step procedure in Section 3.1) for any interface evaluation question.

> [!WARNING]
> **One-Line Mantra for the Exam Hall:**
> *"Good design is invisible — Norman."* — Memorise this quote; it earns easy marks in any descriptive answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prototyping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prototyping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Prototyping** in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) is an iterative, user-centered design activity in which an incomplete, simulated, or partial version of a future interactive system is built early in the design lifecycle to enable **concrete experience, stakeholder evaluation, and requirement refinement** before committing to full-scale development.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework aligned with NEP 2020, prototyping is positioned as a **bridge artifact** between the conceptual design phase and the implementation phase. It transforms abstract specifications into tangible representations that can be **inspected, manipulated, and critiqued** by users, designers, and developers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> A *prototype* is a concrete, working artifact (physical, digital, or hybrid) that represents a subset of the final system's functionality, fidelity, or content, created specifically to support **early evaluation, communication, and iterative refinement** in the user-centered design (UCD) process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The House Architect's Model**
> When an architect designs a house for a client, they do not immediately pour the concrete foundation. Instead, they build a **scale model** — sometimes a cardboard mockup, sometimes a 3D-printed miniature, sometimes a virtual walkthrough in software. This model lets the client *see, touch, and critique* the design before a single brick is laid. The client might say: *"The kitchen feels cramped — can we move the wall?"* The architect changes the model in hours, not months. This is **prototyping**.
> In HCI, the "house" is the interactive software system, the "scale model" is the prototype, and the "client" is the end-user. Prototyping allows designers to **fail fast, learn cheaply, and converge on a design that genuinely serves the user** before expensive, hard-to-change code is written.

### 1.3 Why Prototyping is Mandatory in KTU 2024 HCI Curriculum

| Reason | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Requirements Uncertainty** | Users cannot fully articulate needs until they *see* something concrete |
