- **Cardinalities (binary):** 1:1, 1:N, M:N — determined by business rules in the narrative.
- **Total participation** = double line / `min = 1` → use phrase cues like "must," "every," "always."
- **Partial participation** = single line / `min = 0` → use phrase cues like "may," "can," "optional."
- **Look-Here (Min-Max) notation:** ordered pair $(\min, \max)$ placed next to each entity on the relationship line.
- **7-Stage Mapping Algorithm:**
  1. Strong entities → own relation with PK.
  2. Weak entities → new relation with owner FK in composite PK.
  3. 1:1 relationships → FK on the side with total participation.
  4. 1:N relationships → FK on the **N-side**.
  5. M:N relationships → **new relation** with composite PK.
  6. Multivalued attributes → **new relation** with composite PK.
  7. n-ary relationships (n ≥ 3) → **new relation** with all participating PKs.
- **Derived attributes are NEVER stored** — compute at query time.
- **Crow's Foot vs Chen:** Crow's Foot is industry standard; Chen is academic standard (default for KTU exams).
- **Min-Max notation** is most rigorous for specifying exact participation bounds.
- **ERD is DBMS-independent** — lives at the **conceptual layer** of the three-schema architecture.
- **HCI relevance:** ERD directly drives UI form design, navigation menus, and view composition.
- **Validation principle:** Mapping must preserve information without allowing spurious tuples.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Case Study in HCI?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Case Study** in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) is a *detailed, in-depth, empirical investigation* of a real-world interactive system, application, or interface where the designer/researcher applies established HCI principles (heuristics, usability guidelines, design heuristics, cognitive models) to **analyze, evaluate, and redesign** the system. It bridges theoretical HCI concepts with practical interface design through observational and evaluative study of existing products.

In simple terms — a **Case Study is a "post-mortem + prescription" of an existing interface**. You dissect how a real product (like WhatsApp, an ATM, or Google Search) was designed, where it succeeds, where it fails user expectations, and how it could be improved using HCI theory.

## 1.2 Why Are Case Studies Critical in HCI?

Case studies are the **bridge between textbook HCI theory and real-world product engineering**. The KTU 2024 scheme places them in Module 2 because they consolidate all design principles learned earlier into a single evaluative exercise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Primary Goals of an HCI Case Study:**
> 1. **Diagnose** — Identify usability problems in an existing system.
> 2. **Validate** — Test whether design heuristics (e.g., Nielsen's 10 heuristics) hold in practice.
> 3. **Redesign** — Propose a better interface using learned HCI principles.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Diagnosis" View

Think of an HCI case study like a **doctor examining a patient**:
- The **system** (e.g., an ATM) is the *patient*.
- The **usability heuristics & user tests** are the *medical instruments* (stethoscope, MRI).
- The **usability problems** discovered are the *symptoms*.
- The **redesign proposal** is the *treatment plan*.

Just as no two patients are identical, no two case studies follow the exact same format — but the diagnostic **process** (observation → heuristic walkthrough → user testing → redesign) is universally applicable.

## 1.4 Types of Case Studies Used in HCI

| Type | Purpose | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Illustrative Case Study** | Describes the use of a technique or principle | Showing how Fitts's Law applies to button placement in iOS |
| **Evaluative Case Study** | Judges the merit/quality of an existing interface | Heuristic evaluation of IRCTC website |
| **Exploratory Case Study** | Identifies new design principles | Studying how first-time users interact with voice assistants |
| **Comparative Case Study** | Compares two systems | WhatsApp vs. Telegram UI walkthrough |
| **Redesign Case Study** | Takes an existing system and improves it | Redesigning a college ERP portal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** Module 2 typically asks students to perform a **Redesign Case Study** — choose a known interface, identify 5–10 usability problems, and propose a redesigned solution.

## 1.5 Standard HCI Case Study Methodology

The widely accepted process followed in KTU-evaluated case studies consists of **6 stages**:

1. **Selection of System** — Pick a familiar interactive system.
2. **Domain Understanding** — Identify target users, tasks, and environment.
3. **User Research / Personas** — Define user profiles and use-case scenarios.
4. **Heuristic Evaluation** — Apply Nielsen's 10 heuristics and identify violations.
5. **Usability Testing** — Observe real users performing representative tasks.
6. **Redesign Proposal** — Present a low-fi/high-fi prototype with justifications.

## 1.6 Standard Metrics in Case Studies

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-Standard Usability Metrics (must know for KTU):**
> - **Effectiveness** — Task success rate (target: ≥ **78%**)
> - **Efficiency** — Time to complete a task (seconds)
> - **Learnability** — Time for first-time users to reach expert performance
> - **Memorability** — Retention after period of non-use
> - **Errors** — Frequency and severity of mistakes
> - **Satisfaction** — Measured via SUS (System Usability Scale), target score ≥ **68**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation of an HCI Case Study

Every strong HCI case study rests on **three theoretical pillars** that must be explicitly cited in any KTU answer:

### Pillar 1: Norman's Seven Stages of Action (Donald Norman, 1988)
This model explains *why* users make mistakes in any interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Norman's Seven Stages (Memorize This!):**
> 1. **Forming the Goal** — What does the user want?
> 2. **Forming the Intention** — What action will achieve it?
> 3. **Specifying the Action** — Translating intention to a sequence of actions.
> 4. **Executing the Action** — Physical performance (click, tap, type).
> 5. **Perceiving the System State** — What happened?
> 6. **Interpreting the State** — What does it mean?
> 7. **Evaluating the Outcome** — Did the goal succeed?

**Gulfs Caused by Design Failure:**
- **Gulf of Execution** — Gap between user intention and system action (Stages 2→4).
- **Gulf of Evaluation** — Gap between system state and user understanding (Stages 5→7).

### Pillar 2: Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (1994)
These are the *gold standard* checklist applied in **every HCI case study**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics (KTU Board Favorite — Memorize All 10):**
> 1. **Visibility of System Status** — System should keep users informed (e.g., loading spinners).
> 2. **Match Between System and Real World** — Use familiar language, not jargon.
> 3. **User Control and Freedom** — Provide undo/redo, cancel options.
> 4. **Consistency and Standards** — Same words/actions mean the same thing.
> 5. **Error Prevention** — Design to prevent problems before they occur.
> 6. **Recognition Rather than Recall** — Minimize memory load (show options, don't make user remember).
> 7. **Flexibility and Efficiency of Use** — Accelerators for experts, simple for novices.
> 8. **Aesthetic and Minimalist Design** — No irrelevant information.
> 9. **Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors** — Plain-language error messages.
> 10. **Help and Documentation** — Easy-to-search, task-focused help.

### Pillar 3: Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules (1986)
A complementary, simpler checklist often cited in KTU answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules:**
> 1. Strive for **consistency**.
> 2. Enable frequent users to use **shortcuts**.
> 3. Offer **informative feedback**.
> 4. Design **dialogs to yield closure**.
> 5. Provide **simple error handling**.
> 6. Permit easy **reversal of actions**.
> 7. Support an **internal locus of control**.
> 8. Reduce **short-term memory load**.

## 2.2 Case Study Evaluation Framework

A complete case study must address each of these dimensions:

| Dimension | What to Investigate | Method |
|-----------|---------------------|--------|
| **Functional** | Does it do what users need? | Task analysis |
| **Usability** | Is it easy to learn, efficient, satisfying? | Heuristic walkthrough |
| **Accessibility** | Can people with disabilities use it? | WCAG 2.1 guidelines |
| **Aesthetic** | Is the visual design appealing? | Heuristic #8 evaluation |
| **Information Architecture** | Is the content logically organized? | Card sorting |
| **Cognitive Load** | Does it overload the user? | Fitts's Law, Hick's Law |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Mathematical Laws Applied in Case Studies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These are the only quantitative formulas used in HCI case studies. Master them.**

| Law / Formula | Expression | Meaning | Engineering Application |
|---------------|-----------|---------|------------------------|
| **Fitts's Law** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Time to move to a target | $T$ = movement time, $D$ = distance, $W$ = target width, $a,b$ = empirical constants. **Larger targets, closer → faster.** |
| **Hick's Law** | $T = b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | Time to choose among $n$ options | $T$ = decision time, $n$ = number of choices. **More choices → slower decisions.** |
| **Index of Difficulty (ID)** | $ID = \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Difficulty of a pointing task | Lower ID = easier interaction. |
| **Throughput (TP)** | $TP = \frac{ID_{avg}}{MT_{avg}}$ bits/s | User's information-processing rate | Typical user ≈ **4–10 bits/s** (excellent ≈ 25 bits/s). |
| **Steering Law** | $T = a + b \cdot \frac{A}{W}$ | Time to traverse a tunnel | $A$ = tunnel length, $W$ = tunnel width. **Used in gesture design.** |
| **Fitts's Law Index of Performance** | $IP = \frac{1}{b}$ bits/s | Empirical constant | Lower $b$ = better performance. |
| **Sterner's Task Load** | $TLX = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{6} W_i \cdot R_i}{15}$ | Subjective workload (0–100) | $W_i$ = weight, $R_i$ = rating per dimension. |
| **SUS Score** | $SUS = \sum_{i=1}^{10} s_i \times 2.5$ | System Usability Scale (0–100) | Score ≥ **68** = above average. |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of HCI Case Studies

Case studies are the **primary training tool** for:
- **UX Designers** — Portfolio work for job applications.
- **Product Managers** — Diagnosing why features fail.
- **HCI Researchers** — Building new design guidelines.
- **Software QA Teams** — Identifying usability defects pre-release.
- **Government / E-Governance** — Redesigning citizen-facing portals (e.g., DigiLocker, UMANG app).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always link a discovered usability problem to a *specific principle*. Saying "the design is bad" loses marks. Saying "the design violates **Heuristic 4 (Consistency)** because the 'Back' button is placed inconsistently across screens" scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Case Study Analysis

Below are **three complete case studies** — each modeled on the structure expected in KTU 14-mark exam answers.

---

## 3.1 Case Study 1: The ATM (Automated Teller Machine) Interface

### Step 1 — System Description
A standalone touchscreen ATM used by **novice users** (elderly, first-time users) to perform **cash withdrawal, balance inquiry, and PIN change**.

### Step 2 — Task Analysis
**Primary Task:** Withdraw ₹5,000.
- Insert card → Enter PIN → Select "Withdrawal" → Enter amount → Confirm → Collect cash → Take card → Leave.

### Step 3 — Heuristic Evaluation

| # | Heuristic | Existing ATM Problem | Severity (0–4) |
|---|-----------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Visibility of system status | Screen goes blank after 20 s of inactivity; no countdown warning. | **3 (Major)** |
| 2 | Match with real world | Uses jargon like "**Enter Auth Code**" instead of "Enter PIN". | 2 |
| 3 | User control & freedom | Once PIN is entered, **no Cancel button** until next screen. | 4 (Catastrophic) |
| 4 | Consistency | Card slot, cash slot, and receipt slot are **not color-coded**; users confuse them. | 3 |
| 5 | Error prevention | ATM dispenses cash **before** writing back account balance — power failure leads to phantom withdrawals. | 4 |
| 6 | Recognition vs recall | Users must remember 4-digit PIN; no biometric option. | 2 |
| 7 | Flexibility | No **quick-withdraw buttons** (₹500, ₹1000, ₹2000, ₹5000) — all users type amount every time. | 3 |
| 8 | Aesthetic design | Cluttered screens with small fonts; poor contrast for low-vision users. | 3 |
| 9 | Error messages | "Transaction Failed. Code 0xC7" — cryptic, no recovery hint. | 4 |
| 10 | Help & documentation | No contextual help; only a call-center number buried in fine print. | 2 |

### Step 4 — Redesign Proposal
- **Add countdown timer** with progress bar (Heuristic 1).
- **Replace "Auth Code"** with "PIN" (Heuristic 2).
- **Add "Cancel"** button on every screen (Heuristic 3).
- **Color-code slots**: card=blue, cash=green, receipt=yellow (Heuristic 4).
- **Quick-withdraw preset buttons** for ₹500, ₹1000, ₹2000, ₹5000 (Heuristic 7).
- **Biometric fallback** using Aadhaar-based fingerprint (Heuristic 6).
- **Plain-language errors** with suggested next steps (Heuristic 9).

### Step 5 — Apply Fitts's Law
For the PIN keypad buttons, current buttons are $W = 1.0$ cm wide, distance $D = 0.5$ cm.

$$
T_{old} = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{0.5}{1.0} + 1\right) = a + b \cdot \log_2(1.5)
$$

For the redesigned keypad, enlarge buttons to $W = 1.5$ cm (and maintain same spacing).

$$
T_{new} = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{0.5}{1.5} + 1\right) = a + b \cdot \log_2(1.333)
$$

Computing the ratio of difficulties:

$$
\frac{ID_{new}}{ID_{old}} = \frac{\log_2(1.333)}{\log_2(1.5)} = \frac{0.4150}{0.5850} \approx 0.709
$$

> **Interpretation:** The redesigned keypad requires only **~71%** of the original movement time — a **~29% efficiency gain**, all from a 50% increase in button size.

---

## 3.2 Case Study 2: WhatsApp Messaging Interface

### Step 1 — System Description
A cross-platform instant messaging app used by **2 billion+ users** ranging from teenagers to non-tech-savvy elderly individuals.

### Step 2 — User Personas
- **Persona A — Priya, 22, College Student:** Sends 100+ messages/day, uses voice notes heavily.
- **Persona B — Ramesh, 65, Retired Banker:** Uses WhatsApp once a week mainly for video calls with grandchildren.

### Step 3 — Heuristic Walkthrough

| # | Heuristic | Observation | Severity |
|---|-----------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | Visibility of status | **Ticks** (✓ sent, ✓✓ delivered, blue ✓✓ read) provide excellent feedback. | ✅ Excellent |
| 3 | User control | "Delete for Everyone" within 1 hour — supports freedom. | ✅ Good |
| 4 | Consistency | Camera icon, voice icon, attachment icon — all consistently placed across chats. | ✅ Excellent |
| 5 | Error prevention | Auto-confirmation before deleting messages. | ✅ Good |
| 6 | Recognition vs recall | Recent emojis & stickers are pre-shown. | ✅ Good |
| 7 | Flexibility | **Swipe to reply**, keyboard shortcuts on web, voice notes — all accelerate expert use. | ✅ Excellent |
| 9 | Error messages | "Couldn't send. Tap to retry" — plain language. | ✅ Good |

### Step 4 — Usability Problems Identified

| Problem | Heuristic Violated | Proposed Fix |
|---------|---------------------|--------------|
| Older users confuse **archived** chats with **deleted** chats. | H2 (Match with real world) | Add tooltip: "Archived chats are hidden but not deleted". |
| **Status uploads** disappear after 24 h with no warning. | H1 (Visibility of status) | Add countdown overlay on status updates. |
| Long press is **not discoverable** for novice users. | H6 (Recognition) | Add a tutorial card on first use. |
| No **undo** for "Delete for Everyone". | H3 (User control) | Add 5-second undo toast. |

### Step 5 — Apply Hick's Law
The home screen exposes 5 navigation choices: **Chats, Status, Calls, Communities, Settings**.

$$
T_{base} = b \cdot \log_2(5 + 1) = b \cdot \log_2(6) \approx 2.585 \cdot b
$$

If a designer proposed adding **2 more tabs** (e.g., "Channels", "Shop"):

$$
T_{new} = b \cdot \log_2(7 + 1) = b \cdot \log_2(8) = 3b
$$

Penalty:

$$
\Delta T = \frac{3b - 2.585b}{2.585b} \approx 16\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Adding 2 tabs slows down navigation by ~16%. This is why WhatsApp is conservative in adding new tabs.

---

## 3.3 Case Study 3: IRCTC Railway Booking Website

### Step 1 — System Description
India's national railway booking platform handling **millions of concurrent users** with a notoriously poor user experience.

### Step 2 — Heuristic Evaluation

| Heuristic | Violation | Severity |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| H1 (Visibility of status) | CAPTCHA refresh on slow networks; no queue position feedback. | 4 |
| H2 (Match with real world) | Uses terms like "**PNR**", "**Class**", "**Quota**", "**Tatkal**" — opaque to first-time users. | 4 |
| H3 (User control) | Cannot easily cancel partially-filled forms. | 3 |
| H4 (Consistency) | Multiple pages use different layouts; date pickers behave inconsistently. | 3 |
| H5 (Error prevention) | Users can easily book wrong date/class; no preview before payment. | 4 |
| H6 (Recognition vs recall) | Train names with 5-digit numbers — no autocomplete. | 4 |
| H8 (Aesthetic) | Visual layout resembles a 2005-era design. | 3 |

### Step 3 — Redesign Proposal
- **Real-time queue display**: "You are 2,341st in queue. Estimated wait: 3 min."
- **Plain-language tooltips** for PNR, Quota, Tatkal.
- **Auto-save** partially-filled forms to user account.
- **Smart autocomplete** for train numbers and station names.
- **Confirmation modal** with journey summary before payment.
- **Mobile-first redesign** with Material Design 3 components.

### Step 4 — SUS Score Estimation
- Pre-redesign SUS: **~38** (well below average).
- Post-redesign SUS: **~72** (above industry average — net improvement of **+34 points**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** IRCTC is the *most cited* real-world example in HCI textbooks for the gap between engineering success and usability failure. Use it in your answer for maximum impact.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 6-Stage HCI Case Study Methodology (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: System Selection] --> B[Stage 2: Domain Understanding]
    B --> C[Stage 3: User Research and Personas]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Heuristic Evaluation]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Usability Testing]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Redesign Proposal]
    F --> G[Validated HCI Solution]
    D --> H[Apply Fitts Law]
    D --> I[Apply Hick Law]
    E --> J[Task Completion Metrics]
    E --> K[SUS Score Survey]
    H --> F
    I --> F
    J --> F
    K --> F
```

## 4.2 Norman's Gulf of Execution & Evaluation Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Forms Goal] --> B[Gulf of Execution]
    B --> C[System Performs Action]
    C --> D[Gulf of Evaluation]
    D --> E[User Interprets Result]
    E --> F{Goal Achieved?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Task Complete]
    F -- No --> A
```

## 4.3 Case Study Evaluation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input[Input Phase]
        I1[Selected Interactive System]
        I2[Defined User Personas]
        I3[Task Scenarios]
    end
    
    subgraph Process[Evaluation Process]
        P1[Heuristic Walkthrough]
        P2[User Testing]
        P3[Cognitive Walkthrough]
    end
    
    subgraph Output[Output Deliverables]
        O1[List of Usability Problems]
        O2[Severity Ratings 0 to 4]
        O3[Redesign Wireframes]
        O4[Improvement Metrics]
    end
    
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I3 --> P3
    P1 --> O1
    P2 --> O1
    P3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
```

## 4.4 Mapping Heuristics to Identified Problems (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Nielsen[Nielsens 10 Heuristics]
        H1[H1 Visibility]
        H2[H2 Real World Match]
        H3[H3 User Control]
        H4[H4 Consistency]
        H5[H5 Error Prevention]
        H6[H6 Recognition Recall]
        H7[H7 Flexibility]
        H8[H8 Minimalist Design]
        H9[H9 Error Recovery]
        H10[H10 Help Docs]
    end
    
    subgraph Problems[Identified Problems in ATM Case]
        P1[Blank screen timeout]
        P2[Jargon Auth Code]
        P3[No Cancel button]
        P4[Confusing slot colors]
        P5[Phantom withdrawals]
        P6[Must remember PIN]
        P7[No quick withdraw]
        P8[Small fonts]
        P9[Cryptic error codes]
        P10[No contextual help]
    end
    
    H1 --> P1
    H2 --> P2
    H3 --> P3
    H4 --> P4
    H5 --> P5
    H6 --> P6
    H7 --> P7
    H8 --> P8
    H9 --> P9
    H10 --> P10
```

## 4.5 Severity Classification Scheme (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Usability Problem Detected] --> B{Severity Rating}
    B -- 0 --> S0[Not a usability issue]
    B -- 1 --> S1[Cosmetic only]
    B -- 2 --> S2[Minor usability issue]
    B -- 3 --> S3[Major usability issue]
    B -- 4 --> S4[Catastrophic must fix]
    S3 --> F1[Fix before release]
    S4 --> F2[Fix immediately]
    S2 --> F3[Fix in next sprint]
    S1 --> F4[Backlog item]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Define a Case Study in HCI. List any 4 usability metrics used to evaluate an interactive system.**

**Model Answer:**
A Case Study in HCI is an empirical, in-depth investigation of a real-world interactive system to identify usability problems and propose redesigns based on established HCI principles.

Four usability metrics:
1. **Effectiveness** (task success rate)
2. **Efficiency** (time-to-task completion)
3. **Learnability** (time to expert performance)
4. **Satisfaction** (measured via SUS score)

*(Other acceptable: Memorability, Errors, Cognitive load via NASA-TLX.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Mention any 5 of Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Visibility of system status** — system should always inform users of what is going on.
2. **Match between system and the real world** — use familiar language.
3. **User control and freedom** — provide undo, redo, cancel.
4. **Consistency and standards** — same words/actions mean the same thing.
5. **Recognition rather than recall** — minimize memory load.

---

## 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Perform a complete HCI case study on the Google Search Engine homepage. Your answer must include:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** Identify 6 usability problems by applying **Nielsen's heuristics**, citing the heuristic number for each.
- **(b) [7 Marks]** Propose a **redesign** with justifications linked to specific heuristics and compute the **decision-time improvement** using **Hick's Law** if the redesign reduces visible navigation choices from 11 to 6.

#### 📋 Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

| # | Identified Problem | Heuristic Violated |
|---|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | The "I'm Feeling Lucky" button is hidden under the search bar — no clear indication of its effect. | H1 (Visibility of system status) |
| 2 | Advanced search uses technical terms like "**filetype:pdf**" without explanation. | H2 (Match with real world) |
| 3 | No way to undo a previous search query accidentally entered. | H3 (User control & freedom) |
| 4 | Search button and "I'm Feeling Lucky" button look identical, but behave very differently. | H4 (Consistency & standards) |
| 5 | Auto-complete suggestions can lead users to wrong queries. | H5 (Error prevention) |
| 6 | No option to clear recent search history quickly from the homepage. | H6 (Recognition vs recall) |

*(Each problem + heuristic = ~1.1 marks; pair them with brief justifications.)*

#### 📋 Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Redesign Proposals:**

| Problem | Redesign | Heuristic Addressed |
|---------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Add subtle text hint under "I'm Feeling Lucky" — "Takes you to the first result." | H1, H2 |
| 2 | Replace "filetype:pdf" with plain "**PDF documents only**" toggle. | H2 |
| 3 | Add "**Clear last search**" icon next to the search bar. | H3 |
| 4 | Color-code the two buttons (blue = Search, white = Lucky). | H4 |
| 5 | Add confirmation tooltip before submitting ambiguous queries. | H5 |
| 6 | Add "**View search history**" shortcut icon. | H6 |

**Hick's Law Computation:**

For the **current homepage with 11 navigation links** in the top-right menu (Gmail, Images, Apps icon, Account, Search, etc.):

$$
T_{old} = b \cdot \log_2(11 + 1) = b \cdot \log_2(12) \approx 3.585 \cdot b
$$

For the **redesigned homepage with 6 navigation links** (consolidated Apps drawer):

$$
T_{new} = b \cdot \log_2(6 + 1) = b \cdot \log_2(7) \approx 2.807 \cdot b
$$

**Percentage improvement in decision time:**

$$
\Delta T \% = \frac{T_{old} - T_{new}}{T_{old}} \times 100 = \frac{3.585 - 2.807}{3.585} \times 100 \approx 21.7\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** Reducing visible choices from 11 to 6 yields a **~22% faster decision time**, directly demonstrating Hick's Law in practice.

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Consider the Smart TV Remote Control as an interactive system. Your answer must include:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** Identify user personas, perform task analysis for the "Watch Netflix" scenario, and list 5 usability problems using Nielsen's heuristics.
- **(b) [7 Marks]** Propose a redesigned interface, apply **Fitts's Law** to justify a button-size increase, and explain how the redesign addresses the original problems.

#### 📋 Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**User Personas:**

| Persona | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **A — Anita, 70, Retired Teacher** | First-time smart TV user; wears reading glasses; watches news & serials. |
| **B — Karthik, 30, Software Engineer** | Power user; uses streaming apps, gaming, screen-mirroring. |

**Task Analysis for "Watch Netflix":**

1. Pick up remote.
2. Press "Home" button.
3. Navigate to Netflix tile in app grid.
4. Press "OK".
5. Wait for Netflix to load.
6. Enter profile (password typing via on-screen keyboard).
7. Search for a show.
8. Press play.

**5 Usability Problems with Heuristic Mapping:**

| # | Problem | Heuristic |
|---|---------|-----------|
| 1 | 50+ tiny buttons on the remote — overwhelming for Anita. | H8 (Aesthetic & minimalist) |
| 2 | "Input" and "Source" buttons are interchangeable but behave differently across TV brands. | H4 (Consistency) |
| 3 | Typing passwords on the on-screen keyboard with arrow keys is painfully slow. | H7 (Flexibility) |
| 4 | No haptic/auditory feedback confirms key-presses — Anita often double-clicks. | H1 (Visibility of status) |
| 5 | Netflix icon is buried in the app drawer; takes 6+ clicks to launch. | H6 (Recognition) |

#### 📋 Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Redesign Proposal:**

- **Reduce buttons** from 50+ to **12 essentials** (numbers, d-pad, Home, Back, Vol, Mute, Power) — and add a **touchscreen** at the top of the remote for app shortcuts (H8, H6).
- **Standardize "Source"** label across all TV brands (H4).
- **Voice input** as a primary alternative to on-screen typing (H7).
- **Add LED + vibration** confirmation on each key press (H1).
- **Pin Netflix** to the home screen as a shortcut button (H6).

**Fitts's Law Justification:**

Current OK button width: $W_{old} = 0.8$ cm, distance from user's thumb: $D = 4$ cm.

$$
ID_{old} = \log_2\left(\frac{4}{0.8} + 1\right) = \log_2(6) \approx 2.585
$$

Redesigned OK button (larger, thumb-rest indented): $W_{new} = 1.6$ cm (double the size).

$$
ID_{new} = \log_2\left(\frac{4}{1.6} + 1\right) = \log_2(3.5) \approx 1.807
$$

**Performance gain:**

$$
\Delta ID \% = \frac{ID_{old} - ID_{new}}{ID_{old}} \times 100 = \frac{2.585 - 1.807}{2.585} \times 100 \approx 30.1\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** Doubling the OK button width reduces the pointing difficulty by **~30%**, which is consistent with Fitts's Law and improves both efficiency and error rate for elderly users.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't just list heuristics — APPLY them.** Writing "Heuristic 1, 2, 3…" without linking each to a *specific real problem* in the system will lose **at least 4 marks** out of 14.
> 2. **Avoid subjective language.** Never write *"the design is ugly"*. Write *"violates Heuristic 8 (Aesthetic & Minimalist Design) due to cluttered layout"*.
> 3. **Numbers matter.** If the question allows a quantitative approach, **show the Fitts/Hick calculation** — it's worth **2–3 bonus marks**.
> 4. **Personas and task analysis are mandatory** for full marks in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Redesign = Justification.** Every redesign proposal must be linked to a heuristic or a quantitative law.
> 6. **Don't skip the conclusion.** Always end with a measurable benefit (e.g., "SUS score improves from 38 to 72").

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Case Studies**
> - ✅ A **case study** is empirical, in-depth analysis of a real interactive system.
> - ✅ Three pillars: **Norman's 7 stages**, **Nielsen's 10 heuristics**, **Shneiderman's 8 rules**.
> - ✅ **6-stage methodology**: Selection → Domain → Personas → Heuristic evaluation → Testing → Redesign.
> - ✅ **Nielsen's 10 heuristics** are the most-cited framework — memorize all 10.
> - ✅ **Norman's Gulfs**: Execution (user→system) and Evaluation (system→user).
> - ✅ **Fitts's Law** governs target acquisition; **Hick's Law** governs decision time.
> - ✅ Always quantify: compute **ID, TP, or SUS** score wherever possible.
> - ✅ **SUS score** is the industry-standard satisfaction metric (≥68 = above average).
> - ✅ Real-world exemplars: **ATM, IRCTC, WhatsApp, Google Search, Smart TV Remote**.
> - ✅ Every usability problem must be linked to a **named principle** — never be vague.
> - ✅ **Severity rating** (0–4) is essential for prioritization in redesign.
> - ✅ **Redesign justifications** must reference heuristics/laws — no design without reasoning.
> - ✅ Best exam answer = **Heuristic application + Quantitative law + Persona + Clear conclusion**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unified Modelling Language (UML)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unified Modelling Language (UML)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Unified Modelling Language (UML)** is a **standardized, general-purpose, visual modelling language** maintained by the **Object Management Group (OMG)** that provides software engineers, analysts, and designers with an integrated set of notations, diagrams, and semantic rules to **specify, visualize, construct, document, and communicate the artefacts of complex software-intensive systems**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 HCI curriculum, UML is treated as the **canonical bridge between Human-Computer Interface (HCI) design and software architecture**, because every screen-flow, dialog state, user-task model, and prototype artefact must eventually be translated into a UML diagram before coding begins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim expected in 2-mark answers):**
> *"UML is a standard language for specifying, visualizing, constructing, and documenting the artefacts of software systems. It combines the best practices of object-oriented modelling (Booch, OMT, Objectory) under a single unified notation."*

### 1.2 Historical Lineage

| Era | Contributor | Notation Contributed | Merged Into UML |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1991 | Grady Booch | Booch Method | Yes |
| 1991 | James Rumbaugh | OMT (Object Modelling Technique) | Yes |
| 1995 | Ivar Jacobson | OOSE / Objectory (Use Cases) | Yes |
| 1996 | Rational Software | **UML 0.9** | Birth |
| 1997 | OMG Adopted | **UML 1.0** | Standard |
| 2005 | OMG | **UML 2.0** | Major revision |
| 2017 | OMG | **UML 2.5.1** | Current stable |
| 2024 | OMG | **UML 2.5.2** (latest) | In-force |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Architectural Blueprint

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Building a House**
> Imagine you want to build a house. You do not start hammering nails the first day. Instead, an **architect** draws:
> 1. A **floor plan** (structure) — where the walls, doors, and rooms go.
> 2. A **wiring diagram** (behaviour) — how electricity flows.
> 3. A **plumbing schematic** (interaction) — how water moves between fixtures.
>
> These drawings are **NOT the house itself**; they are the *language* the engineer, contractor, and client all speak.
>
> **UML plays exactly this role for software.** Instead of floor plans we have **Class Diagrams** (structure), instead of wiring diagrams we have **Activity / State Machine Diagrams** (behaviour), and instead of plumbing schematics we have **Sequence / Communication Diagrams** (interaction). Anyone who knows UML — even a new developer on day one — can "read the blueprints" of an unfamiliar system.

### 1.4 The Three Amigos & Goals of UML

UML was created by three methodologists at Rational Software — affectionately called the **Three Amigos**:

1. **Grady Booch** (Booch method)
2. **James Rumbaugh** (OMT)
3. **Ivar Jacobson** (Objectory)

The four explicit goals of UML are:

- **First** — Provide users with a ready-to-use, expressive visual modelling language for developing and exchanging meaningful models.
- **Second** — Provide extensibility and specialization mechanisms to extend the core concepts.
- **Third** — Be independent of any specific programming language or development process.
- **Fourth** — Provide a formal basis for understanding the language (via the OMG meta-model).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 14 Official UML 2.5 Diagrams — Classification

UML 2.5 organizes its diagrams into **three top-level groups**, totalling **14 diagram types**. This classification is a **guaranteed KTU question** (table-filling or two-marker).

| # | Category | Diagram | Primary Purpose | HCI Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Structure | **Class Diagram** | Static classes, attributes, methods, relationships | Domain model of UI entities (User, Session, Form) |
| 2 | Structure | **Object Diagram** | Snapshot of class instances at run-time | Quick screens displaying live data |
| 3 | Structure | **Component Diagram** | Modular software components & dependencies | Plug-in architecture of UI modules |
| 4 | Structure | **Composite Structure Diagram** | Internal structure of a class | Inner widgets of a composite dialog |
| 5 | Structure | **Deployment Diagram** | Physical hardware & software mapping | Server / Client / Browser topology |
| 6 | Structure | **Package Diagram** | Grouping of model elements | Module-wise file organisation |
| 7 | Structure | **Profile Diagram** | Stereotype customisation | Extending UML for HCI-specific tags |
| 8 | Behaviour | **Use Case Diagram** | Functional requirements & actors | Requirement capture of user goals |
| 9 | Behaviour | **Activity Diagram** | Workflow / process flow | User task-flow analysis |
| 10 | Behaviour | **State Machine Diagram** | Lifecycle of an object | Dialog box state transitions |
| 11 | Interaction | **Sequence Diagram** | Time-ordered message exchange | Mouse/keyboard event sequencing |
| 12 | Interaction | **Communication Diagram** | Object links with numbered messages | UI object collaborations |
| 13 | Interaction | **Timing Diagram** | State changes vs. time | Animation / loading state behaviour |
| 14 | Interaction | **Interaction Overview** | High-level activity of interactions | Connecting use-cases to sequences |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "Seven-Seven" Rule (KTU Favourite):**
> UML has **7 Structure Diagrams** + **7 Behaviour + Interaction Diagrams** = **14 diagrams**.
> This split is the most-asked theory question in KTU exams.

### 2.2 Core Vocabulary (The 4 + 1 Things That Connect Everything)

Every UML diagram is built from the same **five building-blocks** (the "**Things**"):

1. **Structural Things** — nouns of the model (Class, Object, Use Case, Actor, Component, Node).
2. **Behavioural Things** — verbs of the model (Interaction, State Machine).
3. **Grouping Things** — organisational boxes (Package, Subsystem).
4. **Annotational Things** — explanatory notes (Note / Comment).
5. **Relationships** — the "glue" (Dependency, Association, Aggregation, Composition, Generalization, Realization).

### 2.3 The Six Relationship Types (THE Most-Asked KTU Topic)

| # | Relationship | UML Notation | Meaning | Plain-English |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dependency** | `---->` (dashed arrow) | "uses" temporarily | Class A uses Class B occasionally |
| 2 | **Association** | `——` (solid line) | "knows / has" | A *uses* B regularly |
| 3 | **Directed Association** | `——>` (solid arrow) | "uses / flows to" | A drives B |
| 4 | **Aggregation** | `◇——` (hollow diamond) | "weak has-a" | Department has Teachers (teachers survive) |
| 5 | **Composition** | `◆——` (filled diamond) | "strong has-a" | House has Rooms (rooms die with house) |
| 6 | **Generalization** | `▷——` (hollow triangle) | "is-a" inheritance | Car **is-a** Vehicle |
| 7 | **Realization** | `▷ - - -` (dashed triangle) | "implements" | Class implements Interface |

> [!NOTE]
> **Aggregation vs. Composition (Examiner's favourite trap):**
> - **Aggregation (hollow ◇):** Child can exist independently of the parent. *Destroying the Library does NOT destroy the Books.*
> - **Composition (filled ◆):** Child's lifetime is owned by the parent. *Destroying the House destroys the Rooms.*

### 2.4 Multiplicity Notation (Cardinality)

$$ multiplicity = \{ lowerBound .. upperBound \} $$

| Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `1` | Exactly one |
| `0..1` | Zero or one |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many |
| `1..*` | One or many |
| `n..m` | Between n and m |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — UML Visibility Markers

$$ Visibility = \{ +, \ \#, \ -, \ \sim \} $$

| Symbol | Visibility (Scope) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `+` | **public** | Accessible to all classes |
| `-` | **private** | Accessible within the class only |
| `#` | **protected** | Accessible to subclasses |
| `~` | **package** | Accessible within the same package |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Software Industry:** IBM Rational Rose, StarUML, Visual Paradigm, Enterprise Architect all export/import UML.
- **HCI Design:** Translates user-task models into Class and Sequence diagrams before GUI prototyping.
- **Embedded Systems:** Deployment diagrams model micro-controller ↔ sensor networks.
- **Web Engineering:** Component diagrams model SPA module dependencies.
- **Banking & ERP:** Use-case diagrams are a regulatory requirement for documenting business workflows.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Deriving the Class Diagram of a Use Case (KTU Analytical Pattern)

Given a natural-language use case, follow this deterministic **6-step algorithm** to derive the corresponding Class Diagram. This pattern appears every year as a 7-mark Part-B sub-question.

**Input Use Case (English):**
> *"A registered Customer places an Order on an Online Shopping website. Each Order contains one or more LineItems, and each LineItem references exactly one Product. Payment is processed by a PaymentGateway service."*

**Step 1 — Identify the Candidate Nouns (Heuristic):**
Filter the nouns; ignore pronouns, articles, generic verbs.

$$ Nouns = \{ Customer, Order, LineItem, Product, PaymentGateway, Website, Service \} $$

**Step 2 — Eliminate Non-Domain Nouns:**
Discard *Website* and *Service* (they are infrastructural, not domain objects).

$$ DomainCandidates = \{ Customer, Order, LineItem, Product, PaymentGateway \} $$

**Step 3 — Resolve Verbs into Relationships:**
Scan verbs of the use-case text.

| Verb Phrase | Resolved Relationship |
|---|---|
| "Customer **places** Order" | Association (1 → 0..*) |
| "Order **contains** LineItems" | Composition (1 → 1..*) |
| "LineItem **references** Product" | Association (1 → 1) |
| "Payment **is processed by** PaymentGateway" | Dependency |

**Step 4 — Compute Multiplicities from Cardinality Phrases:**

$$ multiplicity(Places) = \{ 1 \, Customer \rightarrow 0..* \, Orders \} $$

$$ multiplicity(Contains) = \{ 1 \, Order \rightarrow 1..* \, LineItems \} $$

$$ multiplicity(References) = \{ 1..* \, LineItems \rightarrow 1 \, Product \} $$

**Step 5 — Identify Attributes and Operations:**
For each class, list fields and methods derived from the use-case verbs.

| Class | Attributes ( $-$ private ) | Operations ( $+$ public ) |
|---|---|---|
| Customer | $-customerId$, $-name$, $-email$ | $+placeOrder()$, $+register()$ |
| Order | $-orderId$, $-orderDate$, $-total$ | $+addLineItem()$, $+calculateTotal()$ |
| LineItem | $-quantity$, $-unitPrice$ | $+getSubTotal()$ |
| Product | $-productId$, $-name$, $-price$ | $+updateStock()$ |
| PaymentGateway | $-gatewayId$ | $+processPayment()$ |

**Step 6 — Assemble the Final Class Diagram:** (rendered in SECTION 4).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Diagram (Decision Table)

Use this matrix during KTU "Compare & Choose" questions.

$$ \text{Diagram}^{*} = \underset{d \in UML14}{\arg\max} \; Score(d) $$

where

$$ Score(d) = w_1 \cdot GoalFit + w_2 \cdot TimeFit + w_3 \cdot AudienceFit $$

| If the question asks about… | Choose… | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Static system structure | Class Diagram | Shows classes & relations |
| User goals & system boundary | Use Case Diagram | Actors + goals |
| Order of messages over time | Sequence Diagram | Time axis vertical |
| Object-to-object links | Communication Diagram | Numbered messages on links |
| Lifecycle of an object | State Machine Diagram | States + transitions |
| Workflow / business process | Activity Diagram | Swim-lanes + decision nodes |
| Hardware layout | Deployment Diagram | Nodes & artifacts |
| File / module structure | Package Diagram | Folders of code |

### 3.3 Worked Derivation — Visibility Default in UML

**Question:** In a UML class, what is the default visibility if no symbol is written?

$$ \text{Visibility}_{\text{default}} = \begin{cases} \text{Public} & \text{if declared in interface} \\ \text{Private} & \text{if declared in class body without symbol} \end{cases} $$

**Derivation steps:**
1. The UML specification states that attributes without an explicit symbol MAY be treated as implementation-private.
2. However, the convention adopted by all major CASE tools (Rational Rose, StarUML, Enterprise Architect) is that **omitted symbol = Package visibility**.
3. For KTU exams, the *accepted* answer is **Package ( $\sim$ )**.

$$ \boxed{\text{Default Visibility} = \sim \; (\text{Package Scope})} $$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All four diagrams below are the **canonical KTU answers** for a 7-mark "Draw and explain the … diagram" question.

### 4.1 Class Diagram — Online Shopping System (Derived in Section 3)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Customer {
      -customerId : String
      -name : String
      -email : String
      +register()
      +placeOrder() Order
    }
    class Order {
      -orderId : String
      -orderDate : Date
      -total : double
      +addLineItem()
      +calculateTotal() double
    }
    class LineItem {
      -quantity : int
      -unitPrice : double
      +getSubTotal() double
    }
    class Product {
      -productId : String
      -name : String
      -price : double
      +updateStock()
    }
    class PaymentGateway {
      -gatewayId : String
      +processPayment() boolean
    }
    Customer "1" --> "0..*" Order : places
    Order "1" *-- "1..*" LineItem : contains
    LineItem "*" --> "1" Product : references
    Order ..> PaymentGateway : depends on
```

### 4.2 Use Case Diagram — ATM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    actor1((Customer))
    actor2((Bank Server))
    usecase1([Withdraw Cash])
    usecase2([Check Balance])
    usecase3([Deposit Cash])
    usecase4([Print Receipt])
    usecase5([Validate PIN])
    usecase6([Settle Transaction])

    actor1 --- usecase1
    actor1 --- usecase2
    actor1 --- usecase3
    usecase1 -.extends.-> usecase4
    usecase1 -- includes --> usecase5
    usecase3 -- includes --> usecase5
    usecase1 -- includes --> usecase6
    usecase2 -- includes --> usecase6
    actor2 --- usecase6
    actor2 --- usecase5
```

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram — User Login Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant L as LoginForm
    participant A as AuthController
    participant D as Database

    U ->> L : enterCredentials(username, password)
    activate L
    L ->> A : validateCredentials(username, password)
    activate A
    A ->> D : fetchUserRecord(username)
    activate D
    D -->> A : UserRecord
    deactivate D
    alt credentials valid
        A -->> L : authSuccess(sessionToken)
        L -->> U : displayDashboard()
    else credentials invalid
        A -->> L : authFailure(errorCode)
        L -->> U : showErrorMessage()
    end
    deactivate A
    deactivate L
```

### 4.4 Activity Diagram — Online Order Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Start])
    nodeCart[Browse Products]
    nodeAdd[Add to Cart]
    nodeCheckout[Proceed to Checkout]
    nodePay{Payment Success?}
    nodeConfirm[Confirm Order]
    nodeNotify[Send Email]
    nodeLog[Log Failure]
    nodeEnd([End])

    nodeStart --> nodeCart
    nodeCart --> nodeAdd
    nodeAdd --> nodeCheckout
    nodeCheckout --> nodePay
    nodePay -- Yes --> nodeConfirm
    nodeConfirm --> nodeNotify
    nodeNotify --> nodeEnd
    nodePay -- No --> nodeLog
    nodeLog --> nodeEnd
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — UML Tool Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1 [Modelling Layer]
        tool1[StarUML]
        tool2[Enterprise Architect]
        tool3[Visual Paradigm]
    end
    subgraph S2 [Notation Layer]
        std1[UML 2.5.1 Specification]
        std2[OMG Meta-Model]
    end
    subgraph S3 [Export Layer]
        exp1[XMI - XML Metadata Interchange]
        exp2[SVG Image]
        exp3[PDF Report]
    end
    subgraph S4 [Downstream Layer]
        ds1[Java / C++ Source Code]
        ds2[Database DDL]
        ds3[Test Cases]
    end

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define UML. List any FOUR diagrams used in UML." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
UML (Unified Modelling Language) is a standard, general-purpose visual modelling language defined by the Object Management Group (OMG). It is used to specify, visualize, construct, and document the artefacts of software systems.

Four diagrams:
1. **Class Diagram** — static structure
2. **Use Case Diagram** — functional requirements
3. **Sequence Diagram** — time-ordered interaction
4. **Activity Diagram** — workflow / process flow

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between Aggregation and Composition with suitable examples." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Aggregation (◇) | Composition (◆) |
|---|---|---|
| Strength | Weak "has-a" | Strong "has-a" |
| Lifetime | Child survives parent | Child dies with parent |
| Symbol | Hollow diamond | Filled diamond |
| Example | Department ◇— Teachers | House ◆— Rooms |

Example: If the Department closes, the Teachers still exist (Aggregation). If the House is demolished, the Rooms cease to exist (Composition).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Total 14

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Modified]**
**"Design a UML Use Case diagram and Class Diagram for a Hospital Management System. Patients book appointments with Doctors; each Doctor belongs to a Department. Prescriptions contain one or more Medicines. Billing is handled by a separate Billing module." (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Use Case Diagram + Explanation **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Actors:**
- Patient (primary)
- Doctor (secondary)
- Billing System (external)

**Use Cases Identified (Noun-Verb analysis):**
- Register Patient
- Book Appointment
- View Medical History
- Generate Prescription
- Generate Bill

**Relationships:**
- *Book Appointment* **includes** → *Verify Patient*.
- *Generate Prescription* **extends** → *Print Prescription*.
- *Generate Bill* **includes** → *Fetch Treatment Record*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    patient((Patient))
    doctor((Doctor))
    billing((Billing System))
    uc1([Register Patient])
    uc2([Book Appointment])
    uc3([View Medical History])
    uc4([Generate Prescription])
    uc5([Generate Bill])
    uc6([Verify Patient])
    uc7([Print Prescription])
    uc8([Fetch Treatment Record])

    patient --- uc1
    patient --- uc2
    patient --- uc3
    doctor --- uc4
    billing --- uc5
    uc2 -. includes .-> uc6
    uc4 -. extends .-> uc7
    uc5 -. includes .-> uc8
```

**Explanation:**
- *Book Appointment* is a primary use case executed by the Patient.
- *Verify Patient* is reused across multiple use cases (Registration and Booking), hence modelled with `<<include>>`.
- *Print Prescription* is optional — the system may or may not print, hence `<<extends>>`.
- The Billing System is a **secondary actor** (system actor) because the billing module is an external service.

#### Part (b) — Class Diagram with Attributes, Methods, Relationships **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Patient {
      -patientId : String
      -name : String
      -dob : Date
      -contact : String
      +register()
      +bookAppointment() Appointment
    }
    class Doctor {
      -doctorId : String
      -name : String
      -specialization : String
      +prescribe() Prescription
    }
    class Department {
      -deptId : String
      -deptName : String
    }
    class Appointment {
      -appointmentId : String
      -appointmentDate : Date
      +reschedule()
    }
    class Prescription {
      -prescriptionId : String
      -date : Date
    }
    class Medicine {
      -medicineId : String
      -name : String
      -dosage : String
    }
    class Bill {
      -billId : String
      -amount : double
      +generateBill()
    }

    Doctor "*" --> "1" Department : belongs to
    Patient "1" --> "0..*" Appointment : books
    Doctor "1" --> "0..*" Appointment : attends
    Appointment "1" --> "1" Prescription : generates
    Prescription "1" *-- "1..*" Medicine : contains
    Appointment "1" --> "1" Bill : produces
    Bill ..> BillingModule : processed by
    class BillingModule {
      +processPayment() boolean
    }
```

**Explanation of Relationships:**
- *Doctor belongs to Department* — Aggregation (doctors survive departmental change).
- *Patient books Appointment* — Association (1 to 0..*).
- *Prescription contains Medicines* — **Composition** (medicines cease to exist when the prescription is deleted).
- *Bill processed by BillingModule* — Dependency.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"With a suitable example, explain the components of a UML Class Diagram. Also draw a State Machine Diagram for an ATM card that transitions through Idle → Card Inserted → PIN Entered → Transaction → Eject states." (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Components of a Class Diagram **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Class Diagram consists of **three compartments**:

1. **Name Compartment** — Class name in bold (e.g., `Account`).
2. **Attribute Compartment** — fields with visibility and type.
   Format: `visibility name : type`
3. **Operation Compartment** — methods.
   Format: `visibility name(params) : returnType`

**Example Class — `BankAccount`:**

| Compartment | Contents |
|---|---|
| Name | **BankAccount** |
| Attributes | `+accountNo : String`, `-balance : double`, `#interestRate : float` |
| Operations | `+deposit(amount:double)`, `-validatePin():boolean`, `+getBalance():double` |

**Relationships Demonstrated with Code-Like Explanation:**

| Relationship | UML Notation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Association | ——— | Customer *uses* Account |
| Aggregation | ◇——— | Library ◇— Books |
| Composition | ◆——— | Order ◆— OrderItems |
| Generalization | ▷——— | SavingsAccount ▷— BankAccount |
| Realization | ▷ - - - | PrintableAccount ▷ - - - Printable (interface) |
| Dependency | - - -> | Order - -> PaymentGateway |

#### Part (b) — State Machine Diagram for ATM Card **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> CardInserted : insertCard
    CardInserted --> PinEntered : enterPin
    PinEntered --> PinInvalid : wrongPin entered three times
    PinInvalid --> CardBlocked : block
    CardBlocked --> [*]
    PinEntered --> Transaction : selectOption
    Transaction --> Eject : complete
    Eject --> Idle : cardRemoved
    Eject --> [*] : timeout
```

**Explanation of States and Transitions:**

| State | Description |
|---|---|
| **Idle** | ATM waiting for user interaction |
| **CardInserted** | Card has been read; awaiting PIN |
| **PinEntered** | PIN validated; awaiting transaction type |
| **Transaction** | Withdrawal/Deposit/Balance-check in progress |
| **Eject** | Card returned to user |
| **CardBlocked** | Security state after three failed attempts |

**Trigger Events:** `insertCard`, `enterPin`, `wrongPin`, `selectOption`, `complete`, `cardRemoved`, `timeout`.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse stereotype notations.** `<<include>>` and `<<extends>>` are *NOT* interchangeable. `<<include>>` means the use case **MUST** always call the included one; `<<extends>>` is **optional / conditional**.
> 2. **Aggregation vs Composition trap:** Many students draw a *filled* diamond for aggregation. The *filled* diamond is **Composition**; the *hollow* diamond is Aggregation.
> 3. **Multiplicity position error:** The multiplicity at the *target* end belongs to the *source* class. For example, `Customer "1" --> "0..*" Order` means **one Customer** has *zero-to-many* Orders, not the other way round.
> 4. **Forgetting to mark `<-u>` vs `<-u>` direction** in Sequence Diagrams loses 2 marks.
> 5. **State names must be nouns; transitions must be verbs.** "Click Submit" is an event (verb), not a state.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **UML = Unified Modelling Language**, standardised by OMG, version 2.5.x (current).

✅ **14 diagrams** split as **7 Structure + 3 Behaviour + 4 Interaction**.

✅ **Five building blocks:** Structural, Behavioural, Grouping, Annotational, and Relationships.

✅ **Six (technically 7 with Realization) relationships:**
Dependency → Association → Directed Association → Aggregation → Composition → Generalization → Realization.

✅ **Visibility Symbols:** `+ public`, `- private`, `# protected`, `~ package`.

✅ **Multiplicity placeholders:** `1`, `0..1`, `*`, `1..*`, `n..m`.

✅ **Aggregation = hollow ◇, Composition = filled ◆** — diamond shape encodes lifetime ownership.

✅ **`<<include>>` is mandatory reuse; `<<extends>>` is optional addition.**

✅ **Sequence Diagrams = time-ordered; Communication Diagrams = structure-focused.**

✅ **Class diagrams for static structure, Use Case diagrams for functional scope, Activity diagrams for workflows, State Machine diagrams for lifecycle.**

✅ **Default visibility if omitted = Package ( `~` ).**

✅ **Actor is always OUTSIDE the system boundary rectangle; Use Cases are INSIDE.**

✅ **KTU always expects *naming convention*, *visibility*, *multiplicity*, and *stereotype* on every drawn diagram — missing any one costs 1–2 marks.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coding Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Coding Basics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)**, *Coding Basics* refers to the foundational programming constructs, scripting languages, and markup techniques that practitioners use to **translate interface designs (wireframes, mockups, prototypes) into functional, interactive digital artifacts**. It encompasses the syntax, semantics, and logical structures required to implement user interfaces, handle user input, and manage dynamic content rendering on the client (front-end) or server (back-end) side. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *Design and Implementation of Human Computer Interfaces*, coding basics is positioned as the bridge between conceptual HCI design and the working prototype. The topic covers: - **Markup Languages:** HTML5, XML for structural definition. - **Styling Languages:** CSS3, SCSS, Tailwind for visual presentation. - **Scripting Languages:** JavaScript (ES6+), TypeScript for behavior. - **Programming Constructs:** Variables, data types, operators, control flow, functions, events, DOM manipulation. - **Event-Driven Programming Paradigm:** The dominant model in interactive UI development. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Coding basics is *not* a deep computer science course on algorithms. It is an **applied, design-oriented coding foundation** focused on building usable, accessible, and responsive interfaces. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you are an **architect designing a dream house**. - **HTML** is the *brick-and-mortar skeleton* — the walls, the doors, the windows, the rooms. It defines **what exists** in the structure. - **CSS** is the *interior decoration* — the paint, the wallpaper, the furniture arrangement, the lighting. It defines **how things look and feel**. - **JavaScript** is the *electricity and plumbing* — the light switches that respond to your touch, the faucet that pours water when you turn the handle, the automatic garage door. It defines **how things behave and react** to you, the user. Just as an architect must understand materials, load-bearing walls, and ergonomics to build a livable house, an HCI designer must understand coding basics to build a *usable, accessible, and interactive* interface. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** In HCI, the goal of coding is not to write the most efficient algorithm, but to write code that produces an interface that is **usable, accessible, responsive, and delightful** for the human at the other end. ### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics While coding is largely abstract, certain HCI standards are quantitative: - **WCAG 2.1 Minimum Contrast Ratio:** **4.5:1** for normal text, **3:1** for large text. - **Touch Target Minimum Size:** **44 × 44 CSS pixels** (Apple HIG) or **48 × 48 dp** (Google Material Design). - **Frame Rate Target:** **60 FPS** (frames per second) for smooth UI animations. - **Response Latency Threshold:** **100 ms** for instant feel, **1 s** for uninterrupted flow, **10 s** for user attention limit (Jakob Nielsen's response time limits). - **Color Depth Standards:** sRGB, Display P3, Rec. 2020. > [!TIP] > These standards will appear in your KTU viva and design-essay questions. Memorize the **60 FPS** and **100 ms** thresholds — they are favorite examiner questions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three-Layer Web Architecture (Foundation) Modern interface coding follows a strict three-layer separation principle: | Layer | Language | Responsibility | HCI Concern | |-------|----------|----------------|-------------| | **Structure** | HTML / JSX / XML | *What* the content is (semantic tags, ARIA roles) | Accessibility, screen-reader compatibility | | **Presentation** | CSS / SCSS / Tailwind | *How* the content looks (colors, layout, typography) | Visual design, responsive breakpoints | | **Behavior** | JavaScript / TypeScript | *How* the content responds (events, state, DOM updates) | Interactivity, feedback, real-time updates | This separation aligns with the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** pattern, where: - **Model** = Data / State (held in JavaScript variables or a database). - **View** = HTML + CSS rendering. - **Controller** = JavaScript event handlers and logic. ### 2.2 Core Programming Constructs (Structured Bullets) **A. Variables & Data Types** - `var`, `let`, `const` for declaration scope. - Primitive types: `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `null`, `undefined`, `symbol`. - Composite types: `object`, `array`, `function`. - HCI use case: Storing user input, form state, theme preferences. **B. Control Flow** - Conditional: `if...else if...else`, `switch`, ternary operator. - Loops: `for`, `for...of`, `for...in`, `while`, `do...while`. - HCI use case: Validating forms, iterating over lists of UI components. **C. Functions** - Declarations, expressions, arrow functions (`=>`). - Callbacks, Promises, `async/await` for asynchronous operations. - HCI use case: Submitting forms, fetching data, animation tick handlers. **D. The Document Object Model (DOM)** - A tree-structured API representation of the HTML document. - Each element is a **node**; the browser exposes methods to traverse, read, and mutate this tree. - HCI use case: Dynamic UI updates without page reload (Single Page Applications). **E. Event-Driven Programming Model** - The **core paradigm** of interactive HCI coding. - Flow: **User Action → Event Fired → Event Listener Triggered → Handler Function Executed → DOM Updated → User Sees Feedback**. - Common events: `click`, `mouseover`, `keydown`, `submit`, `focus`, `blur`, `scroll`, `touchstart`, `resize`. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---------|----------------|----------------| | Contrast Ratio (WCAG) | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05}$ | $L_1$ = lighter relative luminance, $L_2$ = darker. Min **4.5:1** for normal text. | | Relative Luminance | $L = 0.2126R + 0.7152G + 0.0722B$ | After gamma correction of sRGB channels. | | Frame Budget (60 FPS) | $T_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ ms}}{60} \approx 16.67 \text{ ms}$ | Total render budget per frame in milliseconds. | | Touch Target Hit Area | $A_{\text{target}} \geq 44 \times 44$ | CSS pixels (Apple HIG) or dp (Material). | | Animation Duration Sweet Spot | $d \in [200 \text{ ms},\, 500 \text{ ms}]$ | Below 200 ms feels abrupt; above 500 ms feels sluggish. | | Fitts's Law (Pointing) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $T$ = move time, $D$ = distance, $W$ = target width. **Wider + closer = faster.** | | Hick's Law (Decision) | $T = b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $T$ = decision time, $n$ = number of choices. **Fewer choices = faster decisions.** | | Responsive Breakpoint Rule | $\text{BP}_n = 320 + 160n$ | $n = 0,1,2,\dots$ Common BPs: 320, 480, 768, 1024, 1280 px. | | Nielsen's Response Limits | $\leq 0.1 \text{ s}$ instant; $\leq 1 \text{ s}$ uninterrupted; $\leq 10 \text{ s}$ attention | Defines perceived user experience thresholds. | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and division symbols use `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\dfrac` — never the vertical pipe `\vert` character — to preserve the markdown table structure. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility - **Front-End Development:** Every website (Google, Amazon, KTU portal) is built on this exact stack. - **Mobile App Development:** React Native, Flutter, SwiftUI all follow the structure-presentation-behavior separation. - **Accessibility Engineering:** Coding basics empower the implementation of ARIA attributes, keyboard navigation, and screen-reader semantics. - **Prototyping Tools:** Figma plugins, Framer, and Webflow all export the same HTML/CSS/JS triad. - **IoT & Embedded HCI:** Even Arduino and Raspberry Pi UI dashboards rely on web technologies. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From a Design Mockup to Working Code **Problem:** Convert a simple HCI mockup — *"A centered button that, when clicked, displays an alert saying 'Hello, User!'"* — into a complete, working code artifact. This derivation demonstrates the **event-driven programming flow** in action. **Step 1: Identify the three layers from the mockup.** - *Structure layer:* A ` ``` *Explanation:* The `` declaration tells the browser to use HTML5. The `lang="en"` attribute is an **accessibility best practice** for screen readers. The CSS file is linked in ``, and the JS file is placed at the **end of ``** so the DOM is fully parsed before scripts run. **Step 3: Write the CSS (Presentation).** ```css body { margin: 0; font-family: system-ui, sans-serif; display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center; min-height: 100vh; background-color: #f5f5f5; } .primary-btn { padding: 12px 24px; font-size: 16px; background-color: #0066cc; color: #ffffff; border: none; border-radius: 8px; cursor: pointer; min-width: 120px; min-height: 44px; } .primary-btn:hover { background-color: #004c99; } .primary-btn:focus { outline: 3px solid #ffbf00; outline-offset: 2px; } ``` *Explanation:* The `min-height: 44px` enforces the **44×44 touch-target standard**. The `:hover` state provides **visual feedback**, and `:focus` adds a high-contrast outline (a **WCAG keyboard accessibility requirement**). **Step 4: Write the JavaScript (Behavior).** ```javascript // 1. Wait until the DOM is fully loaded document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", function () { // 2. Select the target element from the DOM const greetBtn = document.getElementById("greetBtn"); // 3. Attach an event listener for the "click" event greetBtn.addEventListener("click", function (event) { // 4. Execute the handler logic window.alert("Hello, User!"); }); }); ``` *Explanation:* The `DOMContentLoaded` listener ensures the script runs *only after* the HTML tree is ready. `document.getElementById` returns a reference to the button node. `addEventListener("click", handler)` registers a callback that fires whenever the user clicks the button. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Event Flow Derivation The event-driven flow can be expressed as a formal chain of operations: $$ \text{User}_{\text{action}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Event}_{\text{fired}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Listener}_{\text{triggered}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Handler}_{\text{executed}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{DOM}_{\text{updated}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{User}_{\text{feedback}} $$ Mapping to our concrete example: $$ \text{Click} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{click}_{\text{event}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{addEventListener} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{alert("Hello, User!")} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Modal displayed} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{User reads message} $$ Timing analysis (using the **100 ms Nielsen threshold**): $$ T_{\text{handler}} = T_{\text{parse}} + T_{\text{DOM}_{\text{query}}} + T_{\text{listener}_{\text{lookup}}} + T_{\text{callback}_{\text{exec}}} \ll 100 \text{ ms} $$ For a simple `alert`, all four sub-totals are sub-millisecond, so the user perceives the response as **instantaneous** — well below the **0.1 s** perception threshold. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Touch-Target Size Compliance Check Given a button with `width = 120px` and `height = 44px`, verify HCI compliance: $$ A_{\text{target}} = w \times h = 120 \text{ px} \times 44 \text{ px} = 5280 \text{ px}^2 $$ $$ A_{\text{required}} \geq 44 \text{ px} \times 44 \text{ px} = 1936 \text{ px}^2 $$ Since $5280 \geq 1936$, the design **passes** the touch-target compliance check. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Contrast Ratio Verification (WCAG) Given text color `#ffffff` (white) on background `#0066cc` (blue): *Step 1: Convert hex to normalized RGB.* $$ R = \frac{255}{255} = 1.0, \quad G = \frac{255}{255} = 1.0, \quad B = \frac{204}{255} = 0.8 $$ *Step 2: Apply sRGB gamma correction.* Since each channel $\leq 0.03928$? No — apply the upper branch $((c + 0.055) / 1.055)^{2.4}$: $$ R' = \left(\frac{1.0 + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} = 1.0 $$ $$ G' = 1.0 $$ $$ B' = \left(\frac{0.8 + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} = \left(0.8104\right)^{2.4} \approx 0.6038 $$ *Step 3: Compute relative luminance.* $$ L_1 = 0.2126(1.0) + 0.7152(1.0) + 0.0722(0.6038) \approx 0.971 $$ For the blue background `#0066cc` (R=0, G=102, B=204): $$ R_2 = 0, \quad G_2 = \frac{102}{255} = 0.4, \quad B_2 = \frac{204}{255} = 0.8 $$ After gamma correction: $$ G_2' = \left(\frac{0.4 + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} \approx 0.1329 $$ $$ B_2' = 0.6038 $$ $$ L_2 = 0.2126(0) + 0.7152(0.1329) + 0.0722(0.6038) \approx 0.1386 $$ *Step 4: Compute contrast ratio.* $$ \text{CR} = \frac{0.971 + 0.05}{0.1386 + 0.05} = \frac{1.021}{0.1886} \approx 5.41 $$ *Step 5: Compare to WCAG minimum.* $$ 5.41 \geq 4.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{AAA not yet met, AA PASSED for normal text.} $$ > [!TIP] > If the question requires **WCAG AAA (7:1 for normal text)**, you would need a darker background. The derivation is fully reproducible by the examiner. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Event-Driven Programming Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant B as Browser DOM participant L as Event Listener participant H as Handler Function participant V as View (CSS Render) U->>B: 1. Performs physical action (click, tap, keypress) B->>L: 2. Event object dispatched to registered listener L->>H: 3. Invoke callback function with event payload H->>H: 4. Execute business logic / state update H->>B: 5. Mutate DOM (innerText, classList, style) B->>V: 6. Browser re-renders affected elements V->>U: 7. Visual feedback perceived by user U->>U: 8. Cognitive closure - task complete ``` ### 4.2 Three-Layer Web Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STRUCT["STRUCTURE LAYER - HTML5"] A1["Semantic tags header nav main footer"] A2["ARIA roles and accessibility attributes"] A3["Form elements input button select textarea"] end subgraph PRESENT["PRESENTATION LAYER - CSS3"] B1["Layout system Flexbox and Grid"] B2["Responsive media queries"] B3["Animations transitions and transforms"] B4["Theming variables and custom properties"] end subgraph BEHAVIOR["BEHAVIOR LAYER - JavaScript"] C1["DOM querying and manipulation"] C2["Event registration and handling"] C3["State management and async operations"] C4["API calls and data binding"] end STRUCT --> PRESENT PRESENT --> BEHAVIOR BEHAVIOR -->|re-render| PRESENT BEHAVIOR -->|DOM update| STRUCT ``` ### 4.3 HCI Coding Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Design Mockup"] --> B["Translate to HTML structure"] B --> C["Style with CSS following design system"] C --> D["Wire up JS event handlers"] D --> E["Test usability with users"] E --> F{"Meets WCAG and Nielsen thresholds?"} F -->|Yes| G["Deploy production interface"] F -->|No| H["Iterate on design and code"] H --> A ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Alternative Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB INPUT["User Input Layer\nKeyboard, Mouse, Touch, Voice"] --> PARSE["Browser Parser\nHTML and CSS engine"] PARSE --> RENDER["Render Tree Construction\nLayout and Paint phases"] RENDER --> DISPLAY["Display Surface\nPixels on screen"] DISPLAY --> PERCEIVE["User Perception\nSensory and cognitive processing"] PERCEIVE --> INPUT ``` > [!NOTE] > The four diagrams above represent the **complete event loop** of an interactive interface: from physical user action, through browser processing, to visual feedback, and back to the user's next action. This closed loop is the foundation of all HCI coding. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the Document Object Model (DOM). Why is it fundamental to event-driven programming in HCI?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Document Object Model (DOM)** is a programming interface (API) provided by the browser that represents an HTML or XML document as a **tree structure of objects** (nodes). Each element, attribute, and text fragment is a node that can be accessed and manipulated programmatically. The DOM is fundamental to event-driven programming in HCI because it serves as the **bridge between the static HTML structure and the dynamic JavaScript behavior layer**. When a user interacts with an element (e.g., clicks a button), the browser dispatches an event object that references the corresponding DOM node. JavaScript event listeners can then query, traverse, and mutate the DOM tree in real time, enabling **dynamic, responsive interfaces without full page reloads**. This is the technical foundation of modern Single Page Applications (SPAs) and is critical for delivering the sub-100 ms feedback that users expect. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for tree structure, 1 mark for HCI relevance.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *List and briefly explain any three standard HCI metrics that a coder should respect while implementing an interface.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **60 FPS Frame Rate (≈16.67 ms/frame):** All UI animations and scrolling must complete rendering within a 16.67 ms budget to appear smooth. Coder must use `requestAnimationFrame`, avoid layout thrashing, and prefer `transform`/`opacity` for GPU-accelerated changes. 2. **44 × 44 CSS Pixel Touch Target:** All interactive elements (buttons, links, form controls) must have a minimum hit area of 44 × 44 CSS pixels to accommodate finger-tap accuracy, especially on mobile devices. 3. **WCAG 2.1 Contrast Ratio of 4.5:1:** Body text must maintain a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 against its background to be legible for users with low vision. Coder must compute this using the relative-luminance formula. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark each. Naming without explanation = 0.5 marks only.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]** *(a)* Explain the three-layer architecture of web-based interface coding in detail. Discuss how each layer contributes to usability and accessibility. **(7 marks)** *(b)* With a suitable code example, describe the event-driven programming model. Show how the *click* event flows from the user to the screen feedback. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Three-Layer Architecture (7 marks):** Modern HCI interface coding is structured around three loosely coupled layers, each serving a distinct purpose: **1. Structure Layer (HTML5) – 2 marks:** - Provides **semantic meaning** to content (e.g., `
`, `
`, `
`, `
`, `
`). - Enables **screen-reader navigation** because assistive technologies parse these tags. - Embeds **ARIA attributes** (`role`, `aria-label`, `aria-live`) to declare dynamic regions and state. - Contributes to **accessibility (WCAG Perceivable and Robust principles)** and **SEO**. **2. Presentation Layer (CSS3) – 2 marks:** - Controls the **visual design**: colors, typography, spacing, layout (Flexbox, Grid). - Implements **responsive design** via media queries, ensuring usability across devices. - Provides **visual feedback** through `:hover`, `:focus`, `:active`, `:disabled` pseudo-classes. - Enforces **aesthetic-usability effect** and **consistency** through design tokens and variables. **3. Behavior Layer (JavaScript) – 2 marks:** - Implements **interactivity**: event handlers, form validation, dynamic content updates. - Manages **client-side state** and asynchronous data fetching (Promises, `async/await`). - Enables **real-time feedback** below the 100 ms Nielsen threshold. - Supports **progressive enhancement** by adding behavior only when JS is available. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The separation of concerns allows designers (CSS), content authors (HTML), and engineers (JS) to collaborate without conflicts, while also enabling **graceful degradation** when one layer fails (e.g., CSS-off or JS-off modes). **(b) Event-Driven Programming Model (7 marks):** **Code Example (4 marks):** ```html

0 likes

``` ```javascript // app.js let count = 0; const btn = document.getElementById("likeBtn"); const counter = document.getElementById("counter"); btn.addEventListener("click", () => { count += 1; counter.textContent = count + (count === 1 ? " like" : " likes"); }); ``` **Flow Explanation (3 marks):** 1. **User Action:** User physically clicks (or taps, or presses Enter/Space when focused) the button. 2. **Event Fired:** The browser dispatches a `MouseEvent` (or `KeyboardEvent`) of type `click` to the target DOM node. 3. **Listener Triggered:** The `addEventListener("click", ...)` callback registered on `btn` is invoked with the event object as its argument. 4. **Handler Executed:** The arrow function increments `count` and updates `counter.textContent`, mutating the DOM. 5. **DOM Updated:** The browser's rendering engine detects the text change, recalculates layout, repaints the affected node. 6. **User Feedback:** Within <16 ms, the user sees the updated count, completing the perception-action loop. This is the **defining pattern** of HCI coding: every interactive element is a node in a continuous feedback loop. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]** *(a)* Define and compare the terms *markup language*, *styling language*, and *scripting language*. Give one example of each as used in HCI interface implementation. **(7 marks)** *(b)* A designer creates a button with text "Submit" on a yellow `#ffeb3b` background. Verify whether the contrast ratio meets WCAG 2.1 AA standards for normal text. Use the standard relative-luminance formula. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison Table (7 marks = 2 + 2 + 2 + 1):** | Term | Definition | HCI Example | Purpose | |------|------------|-------------|---------| | **Markup Language** | A declarative system for annotating text with tags that describe structure and semantics, not behavior. | HTML5 (`
`, ``) | Defines *what* the content means. | | **Styling Language** | A declarative system for specifying visual presentation, layout, and aesthetics via rule-based selectors. | CSS3 (`color: #fff; padding: 1rem;`) | Defines *how* the content appears. | | **Scripting Language** | An imperative, Turing-complete language that executes logic, manipulates state, and responds to events. | JavaScript (`element.addEventListener(...)`) | Defines *how* the content behaves. | **Conclusion (1 mark):** All three are required for a complete HCI implementation; none alone produces an interactive interface.
