The trio maps directly to the **semantic, perceptual, and behavioral** dimensions of human cognition, which is why this separation is pedagogically elegant and practically essential. **(b) Contrast Ratio Verification (7 marks):** Given: Text color = `#000000` (black), Background = `#ffeb3b` (yellow). **Step 1: Convert hex to normalized RGB (0–1).** (1 mark) - Black: $R_1 = 0$, $G_1 = 0$, $B_1 = 0$ - Yellow: $R_2 = 255/255 = 1.0$, $G_2 = 235/255 \approx 0.922$, $B_2 = 59/255 \approx 0.231$ **Step 2: Apply gamma correction for $R_2, G_2, B_2$.** (1 mark) - Since all channels > 0.03928: $c' = ((c + 0.055) / 1.055)^{2.4}$ - $R_2' = 1.0$ - $G_2' = ((0.922 + 0.055)/1.055)^{2.4} = (0.926)^{2.4} \approx 0.832$ - $B_2' = ((0.231 + 0.055)/1.055)^{2.4} = (0.271)^{2.4} \approx 0.0506$ **Step 3: Compute relative luminance $L_1$ and $L_2$.** (1 mark) - $L_1 = 0.2126(0) + 0.7152(0) + 0.0722(0) = 0$ - $L_2 = 0.2126(1.0) + 0.7152(0.832) + 0.0722(0.0506) \approx 0.2126 + 0.5951 + 0.00365 \approx 0.811$ **Step 4: Compute contrast ratio.** (1 mark) - $\text{CR} = (L_1 + 0.05) / (L_2 + 0.05) = (0 + 0.05) / (0.811 + 0.05) = 0.05 / 0.861 \approx 0.058$ **Step 5: Verification of result (note: this is inverted — yellow is light, black is dark).** (1 mark) The correct ordering requires $L_{\text{light}}$ in numerator. Since $L_2 > L_1$, we swap: - $\text{CR} = (L_2 + 0.05) / (L_1 + 0.05) = (0.811 + 0.05) / (0 + 0.05) = 0.861 / 0.05 \approx 17.22$ **Step 6: Compare to WCAG AA standard (4.5:1 for normal text).** (1 mark) - $17.22 \geq 4.5$ → **PASS** (easily meets WCAG AA and even AAA which requires 7:1) **Conclusion:** The yellow-on-black combination provides excellent contrast and is fully accessible. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Pipe symbol in tables breaks markdown:** Students writing `(L_1 + 0.05) / (L_2 + 0.05)` inside a markdown table cell using `|` for absolute value will *destroy* the table render. Always use `\vert` or `\dfrac` in LaTeX. > 2. **Forgetting gamma correction:** A common mistake is to plug raw RGB values (0–255 or 0–1) directly into the luminance formula. WCAG mandates the **sRGB gamma transform first**. Skipping this costs full marks on Part (b). > 3. **Reversing $L_1$ and $L_2$:** The contrast ratio formula always places the **lighter** color's luminance in the numerator. Inverting the ratio gives a value < 1, which is mathematically valid but semantically wrong and earns 0. > 4. **Writing code without semantic HTML:** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you use `
` and `` everywhere instead of semantic `

topics.title: Code Testing Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Code Testing Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Code Testing Basics — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Code Testing** is the systematic process of evaluating and verifying that a software application (specifically its Human-Computer Interface components) meets the specified requirements, behaves as expected, and is free from defects. In the context of HCI, code testing extends beyond mere functionality verification to encompass **usability**, **accessibility**, **interaction reliability**, and **user experience fidelity**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (CO mapping for Module 3), testing in HCI involves two parallel dimensions:

1. **Software Engineering Dimension** — Does the code work correctly?
2. **Human Factors Dimension** — Does the software work *for humans* correctly?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Code testing in HCI is not merely bug-hunting. It is a structured verification activity that validates the *contract* between user intentions and system responses at every interaction point.

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of Testing in HCI

| Pillar | Focus | Primary Question |
|--------|-------|------------------|
| **Functional Testing** | Code correctness | "Does the system do what it should?" |
| **Usability Testing** | User interaction quality | "Does the system do it *well enough* for real users?" |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Inspection

Imagine you are opening a new restaurant (the software). Before serving customers (users):

- **Code Testing** is like the chef tasting every dish before it leaves the kitchen (does the recipe work, is the food cooked properly, are ingredients fresh?). This is **functional verification**.
- **Usability Testing** is like inviting friends to dine — do they find the menu readable? Can they use the cutlery? Is the lighting comfortable? This is **user-centered verification**.
- **Integration Testing** is like ensuring the kitchen can prepare a *complete meal* — appetizer, main course, and dessert arrive together, hot and coordinated.
- **Acceptance Testing** is like a food critic's final verdict — does the overall dining experience justify opening to the public?

Just as a restaurant cannot survive with great food alone (if the service is poor), an HCI system cannot succeed with bug-free code alone (if users find it confusing).

## 1.4 Why Code Testing is Non-Negotiable in HCI

> [!NOTE]
> **The 1:10:100 Rule (Industry Standard Metric):**
> - Fixing a defect during **design** costs **1 unit**
> - Fixing it during **coding/testing** costs **10 units**
> - Fixing it after **deployment** costs **100 units**

In HCI, post-deployment defects are particularly catastrophic because they directly damage **user trust**, **brand reputation**, and **accessibility compliance**.

## 1.5 Core Terminology Quick-Reference

- **Defect (Bug)** — A flaw in the system that causes incorrect behavior.
- **Error** — A human mistake that produces a defect.
- **Failure** — The observable manifestation of a defect during execution.
- **Test Case** — A set of conditions, inputs, and expected outputs used to validate a specific feature.
- **Test Suite** — A collection of test cases grouped for execution.
- **Coverage** — The percentage of code paths or requirements exercised by tests.
- **Regression** — Re-introduction of a previously fixed defect after a change.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Testing (The V-Model Mapping)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes a layered testing approach. Each level corresponds to a phase in the development lifecycle:

### Level 1: **Unit Testing**
- **Scope:** Individual functions, methods, or interface widgets (e.g., a single button click handler).
- **Performed by:** Developers.
- **Goal:** Verify the smallest testable parts in isolation.

### Level 2: **Integration Testing**
- **Scope:** Interaction between modules (e.g., login form + authentication backend).
- **Performed by:** Developers or QA team.
- **Goal:** Expose faults in the interaction between integrated units.

### Level 3: **System Testing**
- **Scope:** The complete, integrated HCI application.
- **Performed by:** Independent QA team.
- **Goal:** Validate compliance with specified requirements.

### Level 4: **Acceptance Testing (User Acceptance Testing — UAT)**
- **Scope:** End-to-end business/user workflow.
- **Performed by:** Actual end-users or representatives.
- **Goal:** Confirm the system is fit for deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** Module 3 specifically emphasizes *Unit Testing* and *Integration Testing* as they relate directly to interface code implementation.

## 2.2 The Two Fundamental Testing Strategies

### 2.2.1 Black-Box Testing
- The tester has **no knowledge** of internal code structure.
- Focuses on **inputs and outputs** only.
- Techniques: Equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis, decision tables.
- **HCI Relevance:** Perfect for usability testing — users never see the code.

### 2.2.2 White-Box Testing (Glass-Box Testing)
- The tester has **full knowledge** of internal code structure.
- Focuses on **code paths, branches, and conditions**.
- Techniques: Statement coverage, branch coverage, path coverage.
- **HCI Relevance:** Used by developers to test interface logic (e.g., event handlers).

### 2.2.3 Gray-Box Testing
- A hybrid approach — partial knowledge of internals combined with black-box techniques.
- **HCI Relevance:** Ideal for testing UI/API interactions where testers understand the contract but not implementation.

## 2.3 Test Case Design Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

### Technique A: **Equivalence Partitioning**
Inputs are divided into groups (partitions) that should behave equivalently. You test one representative from each group.

**Example:** For an age field accepting 18–60:
- Invalid partition: < 18
- Valid partition: 18–60
- Invalid partition: > 60

### Technique B: **Boundary Value Analysis**
Errors often occur at the **edges** of input ranges. Test values just inside, at, and just outside boundaries.

**Example:** For an age field 18–60:
- Test 17, 18, 19, 59, 60, 61

### Technique C: **Statement Coverage**
$$\text{Statement Coverage} = \frac{\text{Number of Executed Statements}}{\text{Total Number of Statements}} \times 100\%$$

### Technique D: **Branch (Decision) Coverage**
$$\text{Branch Coverage} = \frac{\text{Number of Executed Branches}}{\text{Total Number of Branches}} \times 100\%$$

### Technique E: **Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe's Metric)**
$$M = E - N + 2P$$

Where:
- $M$ = Cyclomatic complexity (number of independent paths)
- $E$ = Number of edges in the control flow graph
- $N$ = Number of nodes
- $P$ = Number of connected components (typically 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Rule:** A cyclomatic complexity $\vert M \vert \leq 10$ is considered testable and maintainable. $\vert M \vert > 15$ indicates high-risk code requiring refactoring.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|-------------------|-----------|--------------|
| Statement Coverage | $\text{SC} = \frac{\text{Executed Statements}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| Branch Coverage | $\text{BC} = \frac{\text{Executed Branches}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| Path Coverage | $\text{PC} = \frac{\text{Executed Paths}}{\text{Total Paths}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| Cyclomatic Complexity | $M = E - N + 2P$ | Unitless (integer) |
| Defect Density | $\text{DD} = \frac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{Size (KLOC)}}$ | Defects per 1000 Lines of Code |
| Test Effectiveness | $\text{TE} = \frac{\text{Defects Found by Test}}{\text{Defects Found in Total}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 1:10:100 Rule | Cost multiplier for defect fix timing | Ratio |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the HCI industry, these testing formulas underpin:
- **Automated CI/CD pipelines** that report coverage metrics on every commit.
- **Quality gates** in tools like SonarQube, JaCoCo, and Istanbul.
- **Regression test suites** in Selenium, Cypress, and Playwright for UI testing.
- **Accessibility audits** that use boundary-value principles to test screen reader compatibility across WCAG compliance levels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Cyclomatic Complexity from First Principles

Cyclomatic complexity, developed by **Thomas J. McCabe (1976)**, measures the number of linearly independent paths through a program's source code.

### Step 1: Graph Representation
A program is converted into a **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**:
- **Nodes** represent sequential statements or decision points.
- **Edges** represent the flow of control between nodes.

### Step 2: The Three Equivalent Formulations

**Formulation A — Graph-Theoretic:**
$$M = E - N + 2P$$

**Formulation B — Region Counting:**
$$M = R + 1$$

Where $R$ is the number of enclosed regions in the planar graph representation (including the outer region).

**Formulation C — Predicate Counting:**
$$M = \pi + 1$$

Where $\pi$ is the number of predicate (decision) nodes in the flow graph (e.g., `if`, `while`, `for`, `case`).

### Step 3: Worked Example — Derivation of M for a Sample Interface Module

Consider a login button handler with the following logical structure:

```
IF (username is empty)            // Decision 1
    Show error
ELSE IF (password is empty)       // Decision 2
    Show error
ELSE IF (credentials are valid)    // Decision 3
    Navigate to dashboard
ELSE
    Show login failure
```

**Predicate Count Method:**
- Decision 1: 1 predicate
- Decision 2: 1 predicate
- Decision 3: 1 predicate
- Total predicates $\pi = 3$

Applying the formula:
$$M = \pi + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$$

**Independent Paths Enumerated:**
1. Path 1: username empty → show error
2. Path 2: username present, password empty → show error
3. Path 3: username and password present, credentials valid → navigate to dashboard
4. Path 4: username and password present, credentials invalid → show login failure

Thus, exactly **4 test cases** are required to achieve **100% branch coverage**.

### Step 4: Verification via Graph-Theoretic Method

Building the CFG:
- Node 1: Entry
- Node 2: Decision 1 (username empty?)
- Node 3: Decision 2 (password empty?)
- Node 4: Decision 3 (credentials valid?)
- Node 5: Exit

Counting:
- $N = 5$ nodes
- $E = 7$ edges (1→2, 2→3, 2→error1, 3→4, 3→error2, 4→dashboard, 4→failure, all converging to exit — counted distinctly)
- $P = 1$ connected component

$$M = E - N + 2P = 7 - 5 + 2(1) = 4$$

**Confirmed:** Both methods yield $M = 4$. ✅

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Statement Coverage Calculation

Consider a simple interface validation function that screens user input across three sequential statements:

- **Statement S1:** Trim whitespace from input
- **Statement S2:** Check if length > 0
- **Statement S3:** Display the cleaned input

A test case that exercises only S1 and S2 (because S3 is inside a branch that was not taken) yields:

$$\text{SC} = \frac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100 = \frac{2}{3} \times 100 = 66.67\%$$

To achieve **100% statement coverage**, at least one test case must execute every statement. This often requires a *second* test case targeting the alternate branch.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Testing Techniques Comparison

| Technique | Knowledge Required | Best Used For | HCI-Specific Example |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Black-Box (Equivalence Partitioning)** | None | Validating input ranges | Testing form fields for valid/invalid age ranges |
| **Black-Box (BVA)** | None | Catching off-by-one errors | Testing text fields at min/max character limits |
| **White-Box (Statement Coverage)** | Full | Ensuring all code runs | Verifying all event listeners fire correctly |
| **White-Box (Branch Coverage)** | Full | Ensuring all decisions tested | Verifying if/else paths in click handlers |
| **Gray-Box** | Partial | API + UI contract testing | Validating backend response formats in UI display |
| **Usability Testing** | User perspective | Real-world interaction quality | Measuring task completion time for novice users |

## 3.4 The Testing Pyramid (HCI Application)

The Testing Pyramid, originally from Mike Cohn, is highly relevant to HCI:

```
            ▲
           /  \         End-to-End / UI Tests (Few)
          /────\        - Selenium, Cypress
         /      \       - Slow, expensive, brittle
        /────────\      
       /          \     Integration Tests (Some)
      /────────────\    - API + UI component
     /              \   - Moderate speed/cost
    /────────────────\  
   /                  \  Unit Tests (Many)
  /____________________\ - Fast, cheap, isolated
                          - Functions, widgets, validators
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** A well-balanced HCI project typically follows the ratio **70% unit : 20% integration : 10% end-to-end** in terms of test count.

## 3.5 Regression Testing — The Maintenance Imperative

Every code change risks breaking existing functionality. **Regression testing** re-runs the existing test suite to ensure no previously working feature has been compromised.

**Common regression strategies:**
1. **Selective Re-testing** — Re-run only tests related to the change.
2. **Full Re-test** — Re-run the entire test suite (used before major releases).
3. **Test Case Prioritization** — Run high-risk tests first to catch critical failures early.

The mathematical foundation for prioritization uses **fault-detection effectiveness**:
$$FDE_i = \frac{\text{Faults Detected by Test}_i}{\text{Total Faults Detected in Session}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Code Testing Workflow in HCI Development

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Interface Code Complete] --> planA[Step 1: Develop Test Plan]
    planA --> designA[Step 2: Design Test Cases]
    designA --> unitA[Step 3: Execute Unit Tests]
    unitA --> decisionA{All Unit Tests Pass?}
    decisionA -->|No| fixA[Debug and Fix Code]
    fixA --> unitA
    decisionA -->|Yes| integA[Step 4: Execute Integration Tests]
    integA --> decisionB{All Integration Tests Pass?}
    decisionB -->|No| fixB[Debug and Fix Integration]
    fixB --> integA
    decisionB -->|Yes| sysA[Step 5: Execute System Tests]
    sysA --> decisionC{All System Tests Pass?}
    decisionC -->|No| fixC[Debug and Fix System]
    fixC --> sysA
    decisionC -->|Yes| uatA[Step 6: User Acceptance Testing]
    uatA --> decisionD{Users Approve?}
    decisionD -->|No| refineA[Refine Based on Feedback]
    refineA --> planA
    decisionD -->|Yes| deployA[Step 7: Deploy to Production]
    deployA --> monitorA[Step 8: Monitor in Production]
    monitorA --> startA
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Test Case Design Flowchart (Boundary Value Analysis)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input Range Identified e.g. 18 to 60] --> bvaA[Test Below Boundary 17]
    inputA --> bvaB[Test At Minimum 18]
    inputA --> bvaC[Test Just Above Minimum 19]
    inputA --> bvaD[Test At Maximum 60]
    inputA --> bvaE[Test Just Above Maximum 61]
    bvaA --> resultA[Should Reject]
    bvaB --> resultB[Should Accept]
    bvaC --> resultB
    bvaD --> resultB
    bvaE --> resultA
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Black-Box vs White-Box vs Gray-Box Testing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Testing Strategy Selection] --> bb[Black-Box Testing]
    root --> wb[White-Box Testing]
    root --> gb[Gray-Box Testing]
    bb --> bb1[Tester has No Code Knowledge]
    bb1 --> bb2[Focus on Input Output Behavior]
    bb2 --> bb3[Techniques: Equivalence Partitioning, BVA, Decision Tables]
    wb --> wb1[Tester Has Full Code Knowledge]
    wb1 --> wb2[Focus on Code Paths and Branches]
    wb2 --> wb3[Techniques: Statement Coverage, Branch Coverage, Path Coverage]
    gb --> gb1[Tester Has Partial Knowledge]
    gb1 --> gb2[Focus on Integration Contracts]
    gb2 --> gb3[Techniques: API Testing, UI API Validation]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Cyclomatic Complexity Application Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    codeA[Step 1: Source Code] --> cfgA[Step 2: Construct Control Flow Graph]
    cfgA --> countA[Step 3: Count Nodes N and Edges E]
    countA --> applyA[Step 4: Apply M = E minus N plus 2P]
    applyA --> interpretA{Step 5: Interpret M}
    interpretA -->|M le 10| easyA[Low Risk Testable Code]
    interpretA -->|M 11 to 20| mediumA[Moderate Risk Refactor Recommended]
    interpretA -->|M gt 20| hardA[High Risk Mandatory Refactor]
    hardA --> refactorA[Refactor into Smaller Functions]
    refactorA --> codeA
    easyA --> designA[Step 6: Design M Independent Test Paths]
    mediumA --> designA
    designA --> executeA[Step 7: Execute Test Suite]
    executeA --> coverA{Step 8: Coverage Target Met?}
    coverA -->|No| designA
    coverA -->|Yes| doneA[Testing Complete]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Defect Lifecycle in HCI Development

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> newState : Defect Discovered
    newState --> assigned : Assign to Developer
    assigned --> inProgress : Work Begins
    inProgress --> resolved : Fix Implemented
    resolved --> verified : QA Re-tests
    verified --> closed : Verified Fix
    verified --> reopened : Still Failing
    reopened --> assigned : Reassign
    closed --> [*]
```

## 4.6 Diagram 6: V-Model Mapping of Testing to Development

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    req[Requirements Specification] --> acceptTest[Acceptance Testing]
    design[System Design] --> sysTest[System Testing]
    arch[Architecture Design] --> integTest[Integration Testing]
    module[Module Design] --> unitTest[Unit Testing]
    code[Code Implementation] --> unitTest
    unitTest --> integTest
    integTest --> sysTest
    sysTest --> acceptTest
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Unit Testing. Why is it considered the foundation of the testing pyramid?
**Model Answer:**
Unit Testing is the lowest level of software testing where individual units or components of a software application — such as functions, methods, or interface widgets — are tested in isolation to verify that each performs as designed. It is the foundation of the testing pyramid because: (i) it is fast to execute and inexpensive to maintain, (ii) defects caught at this level are cheapest to fix (per the 1:10:100 rule), (iii) it provides a safety net for integration testing, and (iv) it enables rapid feedback during iterative HCI development cycles.

### Question 2: Distinguish between Black-Box Testing and White-Box Testing with a suitable HCI example.
**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Black-Box Testing | White-Box Testing |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Knowledge of internals | None | Complete |
| Focus | Input-output behavior | Code structure & paths |
| Performed by | QA testers, end-users | Developers |
| HCI Example | Testing a login form by entering valid/invalid credentials | Inspecting the click-handler code to ensure all `if-else` branches execute |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the different levels of software testing with a focus on HCI applications. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The software testing process follows a hierarchical structure known as the testing pyramid. Each level addresses a specific scope and objective:

**1. Unit Testing (Lowest Level)**
- **Definition:** Tests individual components such as a single button's event handler or a validation function.
- **HCI Application:** Testing whether a "Submit" button correctly triggers form validation logic.
- **Tool Examples:** JUnit (Java), pytest (Python), Jest (JavaScript).

**2. Integration Testing**
- **Definition:** Tests the interaction between two or more combined units.
- **HCI Application:** Verifying that when a user clicks "Login", the UI correctly communicates with the authentication backend and displays appropriate feedback.
- **Approaches:** Top-down, bottom-up, sandwich.

**3. System Testing**
- **Definition:** Tests the complete integrated system against requirements.
- **HCI Application:** Validating that the entire application — navigation, forms, dashboards — functions cohesively.
- **Types:** Functional, performance, security, compatibility.

**4. Acceptance Testing (Highest Level)**
- **Definition:** Conducted by end-users to determine fitness for use.
- **HCI Application:** Real users performing real tasks (e.g., booking a ticket) to confirm the interface is usable and effective.
- **Variants:** Alpha testing (in-house), Beta testing (limited external users).

#### (b) Compute the Cyclomatic Complexity of the following interface code snippet and identify the number of independent test paths required. (7 Marks)

```
FUNCTION validateRegistration(username, password, age)
    IF length(username) >= 5 THEN
        IF length(password) >= 8 THEN
            IF age >= 18 AND age <= 100 THEN
                RETURN "Registration Successful"
            ELSE
                RETURN "Invalid Age"
            END IF
        ELSE
            RETURN "Password Too Short"
        END IF
    ELSE
        RETURN "Username Too Short"
    END IF
END FUNCTION
```

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify decision points (predicates):**
- Predicate P1: `length(username) >= 5`
- Predicate P2: `length(password) >= 8`
- Predicate P3: `age >= 18 AND age <= 100`
- Total $\pi = 3$ predicates

**Step 2: Apply the cyclomatic complexity formula:**
$$M = \pi + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$$

**Step 3: Enumerate the 4 independent test paths:**

| Path | Conditions | Expected Output |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| Path 1 | username too short | "Username Too Short" |
| Path 2 | username OK, password too short | "Password Too Short" |
| Path 3 | username & password OK, invalid age | "Invalid Age" |
| Path 4 | username & password OK, valid age | "Registration Successful" |

**Step 4: Verification using the graph formula:**
- Nodes $N = 6$ (entry, P1, P2, P3, success, exit)
- Edges $E = 8$
- Components $P = 1$

$$M = E - N + 2P = 8 - 6 + 2(1) = 4 \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The function has cyclomatic complexity $M = 4$, which is well within the safe limit ($\vert M \vert \leq 10$). Exactly **4 independent test cases** are required to achieve 100% branch coverage.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Describe Black-Box and White-Box Testing techniques. Apply Equivalence Partitioning and Boundary Value Analysis to test a "Quantity" input field that accepts values from 1 to 10. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Black-Box Testing** is a technique where the tester examines the functionality of the software without peering into its internal structures. The tester is oblivious to the system internals and possesses no knowledge of implementation. Black-box testing is also known as behavioral testing. **White-Box Testing** is the opposite — the tester has full visibility into the internal code structure and tests internal logic, code paths, and structures.

**Application to a Quantity Field (Range 1–10):**

**Equivalence Partitioning:**
| Partition | Class | Representative Value |
|-----------|-------|---------------------|
| Invalid (below) | $x < 1$ | 0 |
| Valid | $1 \leq x \leq 10$ | 5 |
| Invalid (above) | $x > 10$ | 15 |

Three test cases suffice to validate behavior across all input classes.

**Boundary Value Analysis:**
| Test Value | Partition | Expected Result |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| 0 | Just below minimum | Reject |
| 1 | At minimum | Accept |
| 2 | Just above minimum | Accept |
| 9 | Just below maximum | Accept |
| 10 | At maximum | Accept |
| 11 | Just above maximum | Reject |

This yields 6 test cases targeting the boundary edges where defects are most likely.

#### (b) Explain Regression Testing and the concept of Test Coverage metrics. Compute Statement Coverage and Branch Coverage for the code below given that Test Case T1 executes the TRUE branch of the IF statement and Test Case T2 executes the FALSE branch. (7 Marks)

```
1. FUNCTION checkDiscount(price)
2.     IF price > 1000 THEN
3.         DISCOUNT = 20 percent
4.     ELSE
5.         DISCOUNT = 0 percent
6.     END IF
7.     RETURN DISCOUNT
8. END FUNCTION
```

**Model Solution:**

**Regression Testing** is the process of re-running existing test cases after a code change to ensure that previously working functionality has not been broken. It is essential during HCI development because interface modifications (e.g., changing a button's behavior) often unintentionally affect other parts of the system.

**Test Coverage Metrics:**
Test coverage is a quantitative measure of the degree to which the source code is exercised by a test suite. It is expressed as a percentage.

**Step 1: Count total statements and branches.**

- Total statements = 8 (lines 1 through 8, including both IF and ELSE bodies and RETURN)
- Total branches = 2 (TRUE branch: line 3; FALSE branch: line 5)

**Step 2: Compute Statement Coverage for T1 alone.**

Test case T1 executes the TRUE branch. It executes: line 1, line 2, line 3, line 6, line 7, line 8 = **6 statements**.

$$\text{SC}_{T1} = \frac{6}{8} \times 100 = 75\%$$

Test case T1 does **not** execute lines 4 and 5 (the FALSE branch body).

**Step 3: Compute Statement Coverage for T1 + T2 together.**

Combined test suite executes: line 1, line 2, line 3, line 4, line 5, line 6, line 7, line 8 = **8 statements**.

$$\text{SC}_{T1+T2} = \frac{8}{8} \times 100 = 100\%$$

**Step 4: Compute Branch Coverage for T1 + T2.**

- TRUE branch executed by T1 ✓
- FALSE branch executed by T2 ✓

$$\text{BC}_{T1+T2} = \frac{2}{2} \times 100 = 100\%$$

**Step 5: Conclusion.**

| Metric | T1 Only | T1 + T2 Combined |
|--------|---------|------------------|
| Statement Coverage | 75% | 100% |
| Branch Coverage | 50% (1 of 2) | 100% (2 of 2) |

To achieve **full coverage**, both T1 and T2 are mandatory — neither alone is sufficient.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently confuse *Statement Coverage* with *Branch Coverage*. A test suite may achieve 100% statement coverage yet only 50% branch coverage if alternate branches are never exercised. **Always enumerate branches explicitly before computing coverage** to avoid losing 2–3 marks on a 7-mark question.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Code Testing Basics (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- ☐ **Definition:** Code testing verifies correctness, completeness, and usability of software.
- ☐ **Testing Levels:** Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance (in increasing scope).
- ☐ **Unit Testing:** Tests the smallest components (functions, widgets); performed by developers.
- ☐ **Integration Testing:** Tests interactions between combined modules.
- ☐ **System Testing:** Tests the complete integrated application against requirements.
- ☐ **Acceptance Testing (UAT):** End-user validation for fitness for deployment.
- ☐ **Black-Box Testing:** No knowledge of internals; focuses on inputs and outputs.
- ☐ **White-Box Testing:** Full knowledge of internals; focuses on code paths and branches.
- ☐ **Gray-Box Testing:** Hybrid approach for API + UI contract validation.
- ☐ **Equivalence Partitioning:** Divide inputs into valid/invalid classes; test one representative each.
- ☐ **Boundary Value Analysis:** Test at the edges of input ranges (min, min+1, max, max+1).
- ☐ **Statement Coverage Formula:** $\text{SC} = \frac{\text{Executed Statements}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100\%$
- ☐ **Branch Coverage Formula:** $\text{BC} = \frac{\text{Executed Branches}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100\%$
- ☐ **Cyclomatic Complexity:** $M = E - N + 2P = \pi + 1 = R + 1$
- ☐ **Safe Complexity Threshold:** $\vert M \vert \leq 10$ (testable); $\vert M \vert > 15$ (refactor required).
- ☐ **Independent Paths:** Equal to $M$; represents minimum test cases for full branch coverage.
- ☐ **Regression Testing:** Re-run existing tests after code changes to prevent re-introduced defects.
- ☐ **Testing Pyramid Ratio:** 70% unit : 20% integration : 10% end-to-end (HCI best practice).
- ☐ **1:10:100 Rule:** Cost of fixing defects grows exponentially across the development lifecycle.
- ☐ **Defect Density:** Defects per KLOC (thousand lines of code) — a quality indicator.
- ☐ **Test Effectiveness:** Ratio of defects found by tests to total defects — measures test suite quality.
- ☐ **Alpha Testing:** In-house pre-release testing; **Beta Testing:** Limited external user testing.
- ☐ **HCI-Specific Testing:** Always validate accessibility (WCAG), responsiveness, and user task success.
- ☐ **Examiner Tip:** Always show full derivation steps for cyclomatic complexity — list predicates, apply the formula, enumerate paths, and verify with the graph method.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Review-Based Code Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Review-Based Code Testing # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!NOTE] > **Review-Based Code Testing (RBCT)** is a **static verification technique** in the software engineering life cycle where software artifacts (requirements, design models, source code, test plans) are systematically examined by a team of qualified personnel to identify defects, violations of standards, and other problems **without executing the program**. According to the **IEEE Standard 1028-2008** (the canonical reference for software reviews adopted universally in KTU-affiliated curricula), review is defined as: > *"A systematic evaluation of a software product by one or more individuals who work together to identify defects, recommend improvements, and confirm compliance with specifications and standards."* In the context of **Human-Computer Interface (HCI) Design and Implementation**, Review-Based Code Testing is critically applied to: - **UI Component Code** (React, Flutter, Swing, Qt, etc.) - **Event-handling logic** and **input validation routines** - **Accessibility compliance code** (WCAG 2.1 / 2.2 checkers) - **Interaction-model implementations** (state machines for UI flows) - **Cross-platform consistency** scripts ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Editorial Desk" Metaphor Imagine you are a **chief editor at a leading newspaper**. Before tomorrow's edition goes to print, you do **not** wait for readers to call in with complaints. Instead, you perform a **proofreading session** with your editorial team — a sub-editor checks grammar, a fact-checker verifies data, a senior editor evaluates the headline. They never "run" the article; they only **read, mark, and discuss**. This is precisely what Review-Based Code Testing does: - The **code** is the article. - The **reviewers** are the editorial team. - The **defects (bugs)** are the typos, factual errors, and awkward sentences. - The **comments/annotations** are the proofreader's red ink. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Review-Based Testing is a **STATIC** technique. The program is **NEVER executed**. This is the single most distinguishing feature from **Dynamic Testing** (unit testing, integration testing, system testing). ## 1.3 Why RBCT is a High-Yield Module-3 Topic for KTU - It appears almost every year as a **Part-A (3 marks)** conceptual question and frequently in **Part-B (14 marks)** as a long-answer on "types of software reviews" or "Fagan Inspection." - It is the **only static V&V technique** that combines human judgment with structured process discipline. - It is the foundation of modern **DevOps code review** practices (GitHub Pull Requests, Gerrit, CodeCollaborator). --- ## 1.4 Foundational Vocabulary (KTU Must-Know Terms) > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the following eight terms — they are examiner favourites: | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Static Testing** | Testing without code execution. | | **Dynamic Testing** | Testing by executing the program. | | **Defect** | A flaw in software that may cause it to behave incorrectly. | | **Verification** | "Are we building the product RIGHT?" (Reviews, inspections). | | **Validation** | "Are we building the RIGHT product?" (User testing, acceptance). | | **Author** | The person who created the work product under review. | | **Moderator/Leader** | The person who plans and leads the review. | | **Scribe/Recorder** | The person who logs all defects and decisions. | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Review-Based Testing (The Master Hierarchy) Review-Based Code Testing is broadly classified into **three formal categories** (per IEEE 1028) and several **lightweight variants**. Understanding the hierarchy is critical for KTU long-answer questions. ### 2.1.1 Tier-1: Informal Reviews These are the most relaxed forms: 1. **Desk Checking** — The author steps through their own code line-by-line mentally, possibly with printouts. Useful but limited by the author's own blind spots. 2. **Pair Programming** — Two developers work at one workstation; one writes code, the other reviews in real time. An extreme form of continuous review. 3. **Peer Review** — A colleague casually reviews the code. Low formality, no documented process, no metrics. ### 2.1.2 Tier-2: Walkthroughs - **Author-driven**: The author narrates the code to the review team. - The author prepares sample inputs, test cases, and questions in advance. - Goal: **Knowledge transfer** + defect detection. - Often used in academia to teach junior developers. - **No formal defect logging** is mandated. ### 2.1.3 Tier-3: Technical Reviews (TR) - A **peer group** of technical experts examines the product. - Led by a **Moderator** (not the author). - Focused on **technical correctness, design conformance, and standards compliance**. - Outcomes are formally **documented** as a Technical Review Report (TRR). - **No management participation**. ### 2.1.4 Tier-4: Inspections (Fagan Inspections) — THE MOST IMPORTANT This is the **gold standard** of review-based testing, invented by **Michael Fagan** at IBM in 1976. It is a **highly structured, rigidly defined process** with the following six steps: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Six Stages of a Fagan Inspection** (Memorize verbatim for KTU 14-mark questions): 1. **Planning** — Moderator forms the inspection team, distributes materials, and schedules the inspection meeting. 2. **Overview (Optional)** — The author presents the code to the inspection team so they understand the context. This stage is sometimes skipped for experienced teams. 3. **Individual Preparation** — Each inspector studies the code independently, marking potential defects. This is where **most defects are found** (empirically ~75%). 4. **Inspection Meeting** — The team meets; the Moderator leads, the Scribe logs defects, the Author answers questions but does not defend. The meeting is **time-boxed**. 5. **Rework** — The author fixes all logged defects. 6. **Follow-up** — The Moderator verifies that every defect has been properly addressed, either by re-inspection or by the moderator alone. ## 2.2 Roles & Responsibilities (Fagan Inspection) — KTU Favourite Question | Role | Responsibility | |------|----------------| | **Moderator** | Plans, leads, mediates, and signs off. Never the author. | | **Author** | Produces the work product and performs the rework. Cannot be the moderator or reader. | | **Reader** | Walks the team through the code logic (paraphrasing, not reading verbatim). | | **Scribe / Recorder** | Logs every defect, decision, and action item in the Log Book. | | **Inspector / Reviewer** | Identifies defects in their assigned area (e.g., one inspector focuses on security, another on UI state transitions). | > [!NOTE] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **Reader** with **Author**. The Reader paraphrases the code; the Author answers questions. The Author must **never** paraphrase their own code. ## 2.3 Entry and Exit Criteria A properly defined review must have: - **Entry Criteria** (what must exist *before* the review starts): reviewed document, distribution list, agenda, time-box, prepared materials. - **Exit Criteria** (what must be true *before* the review ends): all defects logged, rework assigned, follow-up scheduled, **preparation rate and inspection rate within bounds** (see formulas below). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following five formulas are **guaranteed** to appear in KTU numericals on Review-Based Testing. Memorize units and definitions. | # | Metric | Formula | Unit / Typical Range | |---|--------|---------|----------------------| | 1 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{D}{S}$ | Defects per KLOC or per FP. Typical: $0.5$–$5$ per KLOC. | | 2 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \dfrac{D_{before}}{D_{before} + D_{after}} \times 100\,\%$ | Percentage. Inspection typically achieves **85–95%**. | | 3 | **Review / Inspection Rate** | $R_{rate} = \dfrac{L}{T_{review}}$ | LOC/hour or Pages/hour. Typical: $100$–$200$ LOC/hr. | | 4 | **Preparation Rate** | $P_{rate} = \dfrac{L}{T_{prep}}$ | LOC/hour. Typical: $200$–$500$ LOC/hr. | | 5 | **Reviewer Yield (Defects/Hour)** | $Y = \dfrac{D_{found}}{T_{total}}$ | Defects per hour. Typical: $3$–$8$ def/hr. | > **Symbol Legend:** > - $D$ = Number of defects found > - $S$ = Software size (KLOC = Thousand Lines of Code, or FP = Function Points) > - $D_{before}$ = Defects removed before release > - $D_{after}$ = Defects found after release (in field) > - $L$ = Lines of code or pages reviewed > - $T_{review}$ = Time spent in the review meeting > - $T_{prep}$ = Time spent in individual preparation > - $T_{total}$ = Total time spent across all activities > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pipe-Symbol Trap:** In your answer sheet, write $D_{before}$ as a subscript, **never** as $\vert D_{before} \vert$. Markdown tables break with vertical bars — and so does the examiner's auto-grader. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial adoption:** Google uses **Mondrian** (its web-based code review tool); Microsoft uses **CodeFlow**; open-source uses **GitHub Pull Requests** (a lightweight descendant of inspections). - **Safety-critical industries:** DO-178C (avionics), IEC 62304 (medical devices), ISO 26262 (automotive) **mandate** inspections as a primary V&V activity. - **In HCI Engineering:** RBCT catches **interaction-flow defects** (e.g., a button that should disable when the form is invalid but doesn't), **accessibility omissions** (missing `alt` text, wrong `aria-role`), and **cross-modal inconsistencies** (keyboard shortcut works on Chrome but not Firefox). # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) from First Principles The DRE metric answers the engineer's most pressing question: *"Of all the defects that existed in the system, how many did we actually catch before shipping?"* **Step 1: Define the total defect population.** Let $D_{total}$ be the total number of defects that existed in the software over its entire lifetime. This includes both the defects caught by reviews/testing **and** those that escaped into the field. $$D_{total} = D_{before} + D_{after}$$ where $D_{before}$ = defects caught and removed **before** release, and $D_{after}$ = defects discovered **after** release by users in the field. **Step 2: Define the proportion caught before release.** We want the ratio of defects caught to the total population: $$\text{Proportion} = \dfrac{D_{before}}{D_{total}}$$ **Step 3: Substitute and simplify.** Substituting Step 1 into Step 2: $$\text{Proportion} = \dfrac{D_{before}}{D_{before} + D_{after}}$$ **Step 4: Express as a percentage.** $$DRE = \dfrac{D_{before}}{D_{before} + D_{after}} \times 100\,\%$$ **Step 5: Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical).** > A team performed Fagan Inspections on a 12 KLOC HCI module. They found **180 defects before release**. After deployment, users reported **20 additional defects**. Calculate the DRE. > **Solution:** > - $D_{before} = 180$ > - $D_{after} = 20$ > - $D_{total} = 180 + 20 = 200$ > - $DRE = \dfrac{180}{200} \times 100 = 90\,\%$ > **Interpretation:** A DRE of $90\%$ is excellent and meets industry benchmarks for safety-critical HCI systems (typical threshold: $DRE \geq 85\%$). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Defect Density Normalization Defect counts alone are misleading because larger systems naturally contain more defects. We must **normalize by size**. **Step 1: Express size in KLOC.** If the project has $N$ lines of code, the size in KLOC is: $$S_{KLOC} = \dfrac{N}{1000}$$ **Step 2: Define raw defect count.** Let $D$ be the number of defects found in a review. Then: $$D = DD \times S_{KLOC}$$ **Step 3: Rearrange to solve for Defect Density.** $$DD = \dfrac{D}{S_{KLOC}}$$ **Step 4: Worked Example.** > A 5 KLOC UI component review uncovered **25 defects**. The same team's previous 8 KLOC project had a $DD = 4$ defects/KLOC. Compare. > **Solution:** > - Current project: $DD = \dfrac{25}{5} = 5$ defects/KLOC. > - Previous project: $DD = 4$ defects/KLOC. > - **Conclusion:** The current project is **$25\%$ more defect-prone** per KLOC than the previous project, signalling a need for process improvement (perhaps a stricter coding standard or better unit testing). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Review Cost Estimation The total effort $E_{total}$ (in person-hours) for a Fagan Inspection is: $$E_{total} = E_{prep} + E_{meeting} + E_{rework} + E_{follow\text{-}up}$$ Expanding each term using the rate formulas: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{total} &= N_{rev} \times T_{prep} + N_{rev} \times T_{meeting} + T_{rework} + T_{follow\text{-}up} \\ &= N_{rev} \left( \dfrac{L}{P_{rate}} + T_{meeting} \right) + T_{rework} + T_{follow\text{-}up} \end{aligned} $$ where: - $N_{rev}$ = number of reviewers - $T_{meeting}$ = meeting duration (typically $2$ hours, hard-capped) - $T_{rework}$ = time for the author to fix defects (estimated as $D \times$ avg-fix-time) - $T_{follow\text{-}up}$ = Moderator's verification time (usually $30$ min) **Step 4: Worked Example.** > 4 reviewers each spent 3 hours preparing for a 2-hour inspection meeting of a 1000 LOC module. Preparation rate = 200 LOC/hr. Rework took 6 hours; follow-up took 1 hour. Calculate total review effort. > **Solution:** > - $E_{prep} = 4 \times 3 = 12$ person-hours > - $E_{meeting} = 4 \times 2 = 8$ person-hours > - $E_{rework} = 6$ hours > - $E_{follow\text{-}up} = 1$ hour > - $E_{total} = 12 + 8 + 6 + 1 = 27$ person-hours > **Cost Interpretation:** At a blended rate of ₹500/hr, the review cost is ₹13,500. Catching a defect at this stage costs a fraction (typically $10\times$ cheaper) of catching it after release. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Review Types > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix is the **most common 14-mark question** in KTU exams. Memorize the full table. | Attribute | Informal Review | Walkthrough | Technical Review | Fagan Inspection | |-----------|-----------------|-------------|------------------|------------------| | **Formality** | Very Low | Low | Medium | Very High | | **Leader** | None | Author | Moderator | Moderator (not author) | | **Documented** | No | Sometimes | Yes (TRR) | Yes (Log + Reports) | | **Metrics Collected** | No | No | Limited | Yes (DRE, rates, yield) | | **Defects Found/Hour** | $1$–$2$ | $2$–$4$ | $4$–$6$ | $6$–$10$ | | **DRE Achieved** | $20$–$40\,\%$ | $40$–$60\,\%$ | $60$–$80\,\%$ | $85$–$95\,\%$ | | **Best For** | Quick sanity check | Knowledge transfer | Design correctness | Safety-critical HCI | | **Cost per Defect** | Low | Low | Medium | Lowest ($10\times$ cheaper than dynamic) | --- ## 3.5 Integration with HCI Development Lifecycle In the **HCI implementation phase** (the focus of PEPET861), Review-Based Code Testing is applied at three distinct checkpoints: 1. **Widget/Component Code Review** — Each UI component (button, slider, modal) is inspected for accessibility, state correctness, and event-handling. 2. **Interaction Logic Review** — State machines governing screen transitions are inspected for unreachable states and deadlocks. 3. **Integration Glue Review** — The code that binds UI to business logic is inspected for tight coupling, race conditions, and threading defects. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Six-Stage Fagan Inspection Process Flow The following Mermaid diagram renders the canonical Fagan Inspection lifecycle. It is exactly what KTU examiners expect you to draw (or describe) in the exam hall. ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Stage 1: PLANNING
Moderator forms team
Distributes materials
Schedules meeting"] node2["Stage 2: OVERVIEW
Author presents context
Optional for expert teams"] node3["Stage 3: INDIVIDUAL PREPARATION
Each inspector studies code
Marks potential defects
75 percent of defects found here"] node4["Stage 4: INSPECTION MEETING
Moderator leads
Scribe logs defects
Author answers questions
Time-boxed 2 hours max"] node5["Stage 5: REWORK
Author fixes all logged defects
Re-runs unit tests"] node6{"Stage 6: FOLLOW-UP
All defects verified closed?"} node7["Return to STAGE 3 for re-inspection
if critical defects remain"] node8["REVIEW COMPLETE
Sign-off by Moderator
TRR filed in repository"] node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5 --> node6 node6 -- No --> node7 node6 -- Yes --> node8 node7 --> node3 ``` ## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Review Techniques ```mermaid flowchart TD root["REVIEW-BASED CODE TESTING
Static Verification Techniques"] informal["INFORMAL REVIEWS
Low formality, no metrics"] semiformal["SEMI-FORMAL REVIEWS
Documented but flexible"] formal["FORMAL REVIEWS
IEEE 1028 standard, metrics-driven"] root --> informal root --> semiformal root --> formal informal --> desk["Desk Checking
Self-review by author"] informal --> pair["Pair Programming
Continuous real-time review"] informal --> peer["Peer Review
Casual colleague check"] semiformal --> walk["Walkthrough
Author-led narration"] semiformal --> tech["Technical Review
Peer-group expert review"] formal --> fagan["Fagan Inspection
Six-stage rigid process"] formal --> active["Active Design Review
Fagan variant for design"] ``` ## 4.3 Roles & Information Flow During an Inspection Meeting ```mermaid flowchart LR moderator["MODERATOR
Leads meeting
Enforces time-box
Mediates disputes"] author["AUTHOR
Answers questions
Performs rework"] reader["READER
Paraphrases code
Walks through logic"] scribe["SCRIBE
Logs every defect
Maintains Log Book"] inspector1["INSPECTOR A
UI Accessibility"] inspector2["INSPECTOR B
Event handling"] inspector3["INSPECTOR C
State machine logic"] reader --> moderator inspector1 --> scribe inspector2 --> scribe inspector3 --> scribe author --> moderator moderator --> scribe ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: RBCT in an HCI Toolchain ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DEV["DEVELOPMENT ENVIRONMENT"] code["UI Source Code
Components Events State"] commit["Git Commit
Triggers PR"] end subgraph RBCT["REVIEW-BASED TESTING LAYER"] static["Static Analysis
Linter Formatter
SonarQube"] human["Human Inspection
Fagan Process
GitHub PR Review"] metrics["Metrics Engine
DRE Defect Density
Review Rate"] end subgraph OUT["VERIFICATION OUTPUT"] signoff["Sign-off Artifact
TRR Inspection Log"] quality["Quality Gate
Pass Fail Decision"] end commit --> static static --> human human --> metrics metrics --> signoff metrics --> quality quality --> signoff ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part-A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Differentiate between static testing and dynamic testing. Give one example of each as applied to a Human-Computer Interface (HCI) project.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Static Testing | Dynamic Testing | |--------|----------------|-----------------| | **Execution** | Code is **not executed** | Code **is executed** | | **Technique** | Reviews, inspections, walkthroughs, static analysis tools | Unit, integration, system, acceptance testing | | **When** | Early in the SDLC, on artifacts | After code is compiled and runnable | | **Defect type found** | Coding standards violations, missing accessibility attributes, logic flaws in design | Runtime crashes, memory leaks, performance bottlenecks | | **HCI Example** | Inspecting a React component for missing `aria-label` on an icon button | Running a Selenium script to verify the button triggers the correct API call when clicked | > **Examiner Tip (1 mark):** Mention that static testing is also called **verification**, while dynamic testing is called **validation**. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]** **List the six stages of a Fagan Inspection in their correct order. Who leads the inspection meeting?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The six stages of a Fagan Inspection are: 1. **Planning** 2. **Overview** (optional) 3. **Individual Preparation** 4. **Inspection Meeting** 5. **Rework** 6. **Follow-up** > **Examiner Tip (1 mark):** The **Moderator** (also called the Inspection Leader) leads the meeting. The Author **does not** lead and **does not** defend their own code. --- ## 5.2 Part-B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 1** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, End Semester Evaluation]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of software reviews used in review-based code testing. Compare their formality and effectiveness using a suitable tabular representation. **(b) [7 Marks]** A Fagan Inspection was conducted on a 15 KLOC HCI module by 5 reviewers. The team spent a total of 40 person-hours across all stages and found 240 defects before release. Post-release, the field reported 60 additional defects. Calculate: - (i) Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) - (ii) Defect Density (DD) - (iii) Reviewer Yield (defects per hour) Interpret each result in the context of the HCI project. --- #### ⭐ Model Solution for Question A **(a) [7 Marks — Detailed Model Answer]** Software reviews are classified into four main categories based on formality: **1. Informal Reviews:** - **Desk Checking:** Author reviews their own code on paper. Lowest formality. - **Pair Programming:** Two developers, one writes code, the other reviews in real time. - **Peer Review:** Casual colleague review, no structured process, no documented output. **2. Walkthroughs:** - Author-driven session; author narrates code to a small team. - Goal: knowledge transfer and defect identification. - No formal defect log, no metrics. - DRE: $40$–$60\,\%$. **3. Technical Reviews:** - Conducted by a group of technical experts led by a **Moderator** (not the author). - Focused on technical correctness, standards compliance, and design conformance. - Outputs a **Technical Review Report (TRR)**. - DRE: $60$–$80\,\%$. **4. Fagan Inspections:** - Highly structured, six-stage process with strict roles. - Uses the **Log Book** for formal defect recording. - Achieves the highest DRE ($85$–$95\,\%$). - Mandated in safety-critical domains (avionics, medical, automotive). **Comparison Table (must be drawn in exam):** | Attribute | Informal | Walkthrough | Technical Review | Fagan Inspection | |-----------|----------|-------------|------------------|------------------| | Formality | Very Low | Low | Medium | Very High | | Leader | None | Author | Moderator | Moderator (≠ Author) | | DRE | $20$–$40\,\%$ | $40$–$60\,\%$ | $60$–$80\,\%$ | $85$–$95\,\%$ | | Documented | No | Partial | Yes (TRR) | Yes (Log + Reports) | **(b) [7 Marks — Numerical Solution]** **Given:** - $S = 15$ KLOC - $N_{rev} = 5$ reviewers - $T_{total} = 40$ person-hours - $D_{before} = 240$ - $D_{after} = 60$ **(i) Defect Removal Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} DRE &= \dfrac{D_{before}}{D_{before} + D_{after}} \times 100\,\% \\ &= \dfrac{240}{240 + 60} \times 100\,\% \\ &= \dfrac{240}{300} \times 100\,\% \\ &= 80\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** DRE of $80\%$ is **good but below the safety-critical threshold of $85\%$**. The team should increase preparation time, add a UI-accessibility specialist, and consider a second inspection pass on critical components (e.g., the authentication flow). **(ii) Defect Density:** $$ \begin{aligned} DD &= \dfrac{D_{before}}{S} \\ &= \dfrac{240}{15} \\ &= 16 \text{ defects per KLOC} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** A DD of $16$ defects/KLOC is **higher than the industry average of $0.5$–$5$/KLOC**. This suggests the module is either (a) unusually complex, (b) developed under tight time pressure, or (c) has an immature coding standard. Recommended actions: conduct a root-cause analysis on the top 5 defect categories and create a coding checklist. **(iii) Reviewer Yield:** $$ \begin{aligned} Y &= \dfrac{D_{before}}{T_{total}} \\ &= \dfrac{240}{40} \\ &= 6 \text{ defects per person-hour} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** A yield of $6$ defects/hour is **above average** (typical: $3$–$8$ def/hr) and indicates a well-prepared, focused inspection team. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 2 (ALTERNATIVE)** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, End Semester Evaluation]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Define Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE). Why is it a critical metric for HCI code reviews? Derive the formula and explain what a DRE of less than $50\%$ indicates about the review process. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **roles and responsibilities** in a Fagan Inspection. With the help of a neat diagram, illustrate the six-stage inspection process and explain why the **Individual Preparation** stage is considered the most critical. --- #### ⭐ Model Solution for Question B **(a) [7 Marks — Detailed Model Answer]** **Definition:** Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) is a static-testing metric that quantifies the **percentage of total defects removed before the software is released to users**. **Derivation:** Let $D_{before}$ = defects caught in reviews before release, and $D_{after}$ = defects discovered post-release. The total defect population is: $$D_{total} = D_{before} + D_{after}$$ The proportion removed before release is: $$DRE = \dfrac{D_{before}}{D_{total}} = \dfrac{D_{before}}{D_{before} + D_{after}} \times 100\,\%$$ **Why DRE is critical for HCI code reviews:** - HCI defects (e.g., broken keyboard navigation, inaccessible forms) directly impact **usability, accessibility, and user trust**. - A low DRE means HCI flaws reach end users, leading to **negative user experiences, support costs, and reputational damage**. - Regulatory compliance (e.g., WCAG 2.2, Section 508) demands a documented DRE of $\geq 85\,\%$. **Interpretation of DRE $< 50\%$:** - The review process is **catching fewer than half** of the existing defects. - Likely causes: insufficient preparation, unqualified reviewers, time-boxed meetings being rushed, or the author "defending" their code instead of listening. - **Action required:** Pause releases, retrain reviewers, enforce strict entry/exit criteria, and consider adding tool-based static analysis (e.g., ESLint, axe-core) as a complement to human review. **(b) [7 Marks — Detailed Model Answer]** **Roles in a Fagan Inspection:** | Role | Responsibility | |------|----------------| | **Moderator** | Plans the inspection, selects the team, leads the meeting, ensures time-box, signs off on completion. Must **not** be the author. | | **Author** | Created the work product. Answers questions, performs rework. Cannot defend. | | **Reader** | Paraphrases the code during the meeting (walks the team through the logic, not reading it verbatim). | | **Scribe / Recorder** | Logs every defect, suggestion, and decision in the **Log Book**. | | **Inspector** | Subject-matter expert who identifies defects in their assigned area. | **Six-Stage Process Diagram (must be drawn):** ``` [Planning] → [Overview] → [Individual Preparation] → [Inspection Meeting] → [Rework] → [Follow-up] ↺ (re-inspect if critical defects remain) ``` **Why Individual Preparation is the most critical stage:** - **Empirical evidence** (Fagan's original IBM studies, later confirmed by Humphrey and others) shows that **$70$–$80\%$ of all defects are found during individual preparation**, *before* the meeting ever starts. - During the meeting, participants are time-pressured and group-think can suppress dissenting opinions. Without rigorous individual study, the meeting degenerates into a discussion rather than a defect-logging session. - The meeting's role is **verification and clarification** of defects already found, not discovery. - Therefore, **skipping or rushing preparation invalidates the entire inspection** — the meeting becomes theatre, not engineering. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** write "code review is same as code testing." They are related but distinct: testing implies execution, review does not. (Lose 1 mark) > - **Do NOT** confuse Walkthrough with Inspection. In a walkthrough, the author leads; in an inspection, the moderator (≠ author) leads. (Lose 2 marks) > - **Do NOT** forget the **Scribe/Recorder** role. Many students omit it. (Lose 1 mark) > - **Do NOT** confuse the **Reader** (paraphraser) with the **Author**. (Lose 1 mark) > - **In numericals:** Always show the **substitution step explicitly**. Examiners award partial credit for the formula but the bulk of marks come from correct substitution. (Lose up to 2 marks if you skip steps) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > Final 60-second revision before entering the exam hall: - ✅ RBCT is a **STATIC** technique — code is **never executed**. - ✅ The four-tier hierarchy: **Informal → Walkthrough → Technical Review → Fagan Inspection**. - ✅ Fagan Inspection has **six stages**: **P-O-P-I-R-F** (Planning, Overview, Preparation, Inspection meeting, Rework, Follow-up). - ✅ **Moderator leads** the inspection meeting; **Author answers questions but does not defend**. - ✅ Five key metrics: **DD, DRE, Review Rate, Preparation Rate, Reviewer Yield**. - ✅ DRE formula: $DRE = \dfrac{D_{before}}{D_{before} + D_{after}} \times 100\,\%$. - ✅ Fagan Inspections achieve **DRE of $85$–$95\%$** — the highest among all review types. - ✅ **$70$–$80\%$ of defects are found in Individual Preparation** — not in the meeting. - ✅ Review-based testing is **$10\times$ cheaper** per defect than dynamic testing. - ✅ In HCI context: RBCT catches **accessibility omissions, event-handling flaws, and state-machine errors** before users encounter them. - ✅ IEEE 1028 is the **standard** governing software reviews (cite this in answers for full marks). - ✅ Modern equivalents: **GitHub Pull Requests, Gerrit, Phabricator, CodeCollaborator** are lightweight descendants of Fagan Inspections. - ✅ Always show **formula → substitution → result → interpretation** in numericals; examiners reward all four. - ✅ Use `\vert` or `\mid`, never `|`, in LaTeX-table contexts to avoid rendering errors. **End of Notes — Module 3, Review-Based Code Testing.**

topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — HCI Case Study**
> A **Case Study** in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) is a structured, empirical investigation of a real-world interactive system, interface, or design incident that is analyzed retrospectively (or contemporaneously) using established HCI theory, heuristics, and usability principles to extract **transferable design lessons**, identify **failure patterns**, and inform **future interface engineering decisions**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, case studies sit at the intersection of *User-Centered Design (UCD)*, *Usability Engineering*, and *Interaction Design Reflection*. They are classified into three canonical types:

| Type | Description | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **Success Case Study** | A well-designed system whose adoption is high and user satisfaction measurable (e.g., Apple iPhone, Google Search). | High |
| **Failure Case Study** | A system where poor HCI led to user rejection, accidents, or financial loss (e.g., Healthcare.gov, Therac-25). | Very High |
| **Iterative/Longitudinal Case Study** | A system tracked across multiple design cycles (e.g., NASA Cockpit evolution). | Medium |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Medical School Analogy**
> Imagine a medical student who has never seen a patient. Textbooks teach symptoms, but **real learning happens when a doctor presents "Case 47: A 54-year-old with chest pain"** — and the student must reason through history, tests, diagnosis, and treatment. **HCI Case Studies function identically**: they are the "clinical rounds" of interaction designers. Instead of "what does the textbook say about visibility of system status?", the student examines *"Why did 60% of users abandon the UK NHS COVID-19 booking portal in 2020?"* — and reverse-engineers the missing design principle.
> 
> **One-liner intuition:** *A case study is a "post-mortem" or "biopsy" of an interface — you dissect a finished product to discover the design DNA (good or bad) inside it.*

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in HCI Case Studies

> [!NOTE]
> The following standardized metrics are universally cited across KTU reference texts when evaluating case studies. Memorize them as **bolded constants of the discipline**:

- **System Usability Scale (SUS) Score Range: 0 to 100** (industry-acceptable threshold: **> 68**)
- **Nielsen Severity Rating Scale: 0 (no problem) to 4 (catastrophic)**
- **Nielsen's "5 Users" Rule — 5 test users uncover ~85% of usability issues**
- **Don Norman's 7 Stages of Action**
- **Jakob Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Case Study Analytical Framework — Why and How

A KTU-quality case study analysis must follow a **structured 5-stage reasoning ladder**. This is how the Board examiner thinks when grading your answer:

1. **Contextualization** — *What was the system, who were the users, what was the environment?*
   - *Why:* Without context, you cannot judge whether a design was appropriate for its target population (e.g., a smartphone UI for rural Indian farmers has different constraints than one for Silicon Valley engineers).

2. **Affordance Mapping** — *What actions does the interface suggest are possible?*
   - *Why:* Don Norman's theory states that **perceived affordances** drive user behavior. A successful case study shows clear, learnable affordances; a failed one shows ambiguous or misleading ones.

3. **Heuristic Violation / Compliance Audit** — *Apply Nielsen's 10 Heuristics line-by-line.*
   - *How:* For each heuristic, mark it **✓ (Compliant)**, **✗ (Violated)**, or **N/A (Not Applicable)**. Count violations.

4. **Quantification of Impact** — *What was the measurable damage or benefit?*
   - *Why:* KTU examiners award full marks only when you quantify (e.g., "Task failure rate of **47%**" rather than "many users had trouble").

5. **Lesson Extraction & Generalization** — *What transferable design principle emerges?*
   - *Why:* A case study that does not produce a generalized principle is just an anecdote.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quantitative Toolkit for Case Study Analysis**

| Metric / Formula | Equation | Normalization / Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** | $\text{TCR} = \dfrac{\text{Completed Tasks}}{\text{Attempted Tasks}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | Measures interface learnability. |
| **Error Rate (ER)** | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Errors}}{\text{Total User Actions}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | Detects confusing affordances. |
| **Time on Task (ToT)** | $\text{ToT} = T_{\text{end}} - T_{\text{start}}$ | Seconds / Minutes | Efficiency benchmark. |
| **SUS Score** | $\text{SUS} = \left[\sum_{\text{odd}} (R_i - 1) + \sum_{\text{even}} (5 - R_i)\right] \times 2.5$ | Score 0–100 | Standardized usability index. |
| **Nielsen Severity Rating** | $\text{Severity} = \text{Frequency} \times \text{Impact} \times \text{Persistence}$ | Scale 0–4 | Prioritizes bug fixes. |
| **Discount Usability (Quick & Dirty)** | $\text{Cost}_{\text{Quick}} \approx 10\% \text{ of} \;\text{Cost}_{\text{Full Study}}$ | Monetary / Hours | When full studies are infeasible. |
| **5-User Rule Coverage** | $\text{Coverage}(N) = 1 - (1-p)^N$ | Probability 0–1 | With $N=5$, coverage ≈ **85%** if $p \approx 0.31$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** In KTU valuation, writing the SUS formula as `|x|` in a markdown table breaks the table. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` if you need absolute value, OR keep formulas outside the table as a centered equation.

### 2.3 Don Norman's 7 Stages of Action (Essential for Case Study Analysis)

Every successful HCI case study analysis must reference Norman's stages. When a user interacts with any interface, they traverse these **7 cognitive stages**:

1. **Forming the Goal** — What does the user want?
2. **Forming the Intention** — What action will satisfy it?
3. **Specifying an Action** — Concrete sequence of moves.
4. **Executing the Action** — Physical/mental performance.
5. **Perceiving the System State** — Sensory feedback.
6. **Interpreting the State** — Meaning extraction.
7. **Evaluating the Outcome** — Goal achieved?

> **Engineering Utility:** When analyzing a failure case study, identify **which stage had the "Gulf of Execution"** (user cannot figure out how to act) or **"Gulf of Evaluation"** (user cannot interpret results). This is the gold-standard KTU answer structure.

### 2.4 Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (Board-Examiner Favorites)

These appear in **nearly every KTU HCI question** involving case studies. Memorize the list verbatim:

1. Visibility of system status
2. Match between system and the real world
3. User control and freedom
4. Consistency and standards
5. Error prevention
6. Recognition rather than recall
7. Flexibility and efficiency of use
8. Aesthetic and minimalist design
9. Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors
10. Help and documentation

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The "5-User Rule" Mathematical Justification

A KTU examiner may ask: *"Why does Jakob Nielsen recommend 5 test users?"* Here is the rigorous derivation.

**Step 1 — Define the single-user detection probability.**
Let $p$ be the probability that a single randomly chosen user will encounter a specific usability problem. Empirically, Nielsen observed that on average each user catches about $p \approx 0.31$ of the total problem set.

**Step 2 — Probability that N users all miss the problem.**
If one user fails to detect a given issue with probability $(1 - p)$, then for $N$ independent users:

$$
P(\text{all miss}) = (1 - p)^N
$$

**Step 3 — Probability that at least one user catches it.**

$$
P(\text{at least one detects}) = 1 - (1 - p)^N
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $p = 0.31$ and $N = 5$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{detection at } N=5) &= 1 - (1 - 0.31)^5 \\
&= 1 - (0.69)^5 \\
&= 1 - 0.1564 \\
&\approx 0.8436 \\
&\approx \mathbf{84.36\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic Explained:**
- $(0.69)$ is the chance a single user *misses* the issue.
- $(0.69)^5$ is the chance *all 5* users miss it.
- Subtracting from 1 gives the chance that *someone* among the five catches it.
- The result $\approx 85\%$ is the basis for the famous rule.

**Step 5 — Why not 10 or 20 users?**
Diminishing returns: $N = 10$ yields $\approx 96.7\%$, but cost roughly doubles. $N = 15$ yields $\approx 99.2\%$, but at $\times 3$ cost. Hence **5 is the engineering sweet spot** for early-stage iterative testing in case study evaluations.

### 3.2 Derivation: SUS Score Computation

The System Usability Scale uses 10 Likert items rated 1–5. Steps for a single respondent:

**Step 1 — Separate odd and even items.**
- Odd items (1, 3, 5, 7, 9): subtract 1 from each response.
- Even items (2, 4, 6, 8, 10): subtract each response from 5.

**Step 2 — Sum the two adjusted totals.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{\text{odd}} &= \sum_{i \in \{1,3,5,7,9\}} (R_i - 1) \\
X_{\text{even}} &= \sum_{i \in \{2,4,6,8,10\}} (5 - R_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the total by 2.5 to normalize to 0–100.**

$$
\text{SUS} = (X_{\text{odd}} + X_{\text{even}}) \times 2.5
$$

**Conversion Logic Explained:**
- The subtraction centers each item around 0 instead of 1.
- Multiplication by 2.5 rescales a maximum raw score of 40 (10 items × 4) into 100.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Computing Task Completion Rate

Suppose a case study reports: **42 out of 50 users successfully booked a flight** using a redesigned airline app.

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{42}{50} \times 100\% = \mathbf{84\%}
$$

> **Interpretation benchmark:** A TCR of **< 78%** is considered a usability failure in aviation booking systems (per ISO 9241-11).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Case Studies to HCI Theories

| Case Study | Domain | Primary Failure / Success | Applicable Heuristic | Norman's Gulf |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Therac-25** (1985) | Medical / Radiation Therapy | Race condition in UI led to overdoses | Error Prevention (#5) | Gulf of Evaluation |
| **Healthcare.gov** (2013) | Government / Web | Overloaded architecture, no UCD | Visibility of System Status (#1) | Gulf of Execution |
| **Apple iPhone (2007)** | Mobile | First true multi-touch consumer interface | Match with Real World (#2) | Both closed |
| **London Ambulance (1992)** | Emergency Dispatch | GUI overwhelmed dispatchers under load | Consistency & Standards (#4) | Gulf of Execution |
| **Boeing 737 MAX** | Aviation | MCAS override contradicted pilot intuition | User Control & Freedom (#3) | Gulf of Evaluation |
| **Google Search Homepage** | Web | Radical minimalist aesthetic | Aesthetic & Minimalist (#8) | Gulf closed |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The KTU-Recommended Case Study Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Case Study Selected] --> B[Stage 1: Contextualize System]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Identify Target Users and Environment]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Map User Goals to Norman 7 Stages]
    D --> E{Stage 4: Audit Against Nielsen 10 Heuristics}
    E --> F[List Violations and Compliances]
    F --> G[Stage 5: Quantify Impact using SUS / TCR / ER]
    G --> H{Impact Above Acceptable Threshold?}
    H -->|Yes, Negative| I[Mark as Failure Case]
    H -->|Yes, Positive| J[Mark as Success Case]
    H -->|Mixed| K[Mark as Iterative Case]
    I --> L[Stage 6: Identify Gulf of Execution or Evaluation]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Stage 7: Extract Transferable Design Principle]
    M --> N[End: Lessons Documented]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Failure-Pattern Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Poor HCI Case Study] --> B[Root Cause Category 1]
    A --> C[Root Cause Category 2]
    A --> D[Root Cause Category 3]
    B --> B1[Ignoring User Mental Model]
    B --> B2[Poor Affordance Visibility]
    C --> C1[Inadequate Feedback Loops]
    C --> C2[No Error Prevention Layer]
    D --> D1[Inconsistency Across Modules]
    D --> D2[Overloaded Cognitive Demand]
```

### 4.3 Heuristic-to-Failure Mapping Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Heuristic 1: Visibility of System Status] --> F1[Healthcare.gov Dropdown Silence]
    H2[Heuristic 2: Match Real World] --> F2[Therac 25 Cursor Behavior Jargon]
    H3[Heuristic 3: User Control Freedom] --> F3[Boeing 737 MAX MCAS Override Conflict]
    H4[Heuristic 4: Consistency] --> F4[London Ambulance Dispatch UI Inconsistency]
    H5[Heuristic 5: Error Prevention] --> F5[Therac 25 Race Condition]
    H6[Heuristic 6: Recognition over Recall] --> F6[Excel Ribbon Hidden Formulas]
    H7[Heuristic 7: Flexibility Efficiency] --> F7[Photoshop Default Workspace Cluttered]
    H8[Heuristic 8: Aesthetic Minimalist] --> F8[Google Search vs Yahoo 1999]
    H9[Heuristic 9: Error Recovery Help] --> F9[Windows Vista Cancel Cancel Dialog]
    H10[Heuristic 10: Help Documentation] --> F10[Autodesk Maya Default Empty Docs]
```

### 4.4 Don Norman's Gulf Model (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UserSide[USER SIDE]
        G1[Forming Goal]
        G2[Forming Intention]
        G3[Specifying Action]
    end
    subgraph SystemSide[SYSTEM SIDE]
        P1[Perceived State]
        P2[Interpreted State]
        P3[Evaluated Outcome]
    end
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 -->|Gulf of Execution| SysAct[Executing Action on System]
    SysAct --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 -->|Gulf of Evaluation| G1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define an HCI Case Study. List any THREE types of case studies with one real-world example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks distribution):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An HCI case study is an empirical investigation of a real-world interactive system analyzed using established usability heuristics, design principles, and user-centered metrics to extract transferable design lessons.
>
> **Three Types (2 marks):**
> 1. **Success Case Study** — Example: *Apple iPhone (2007)* — demonstrated first successful multi-touch consumer interface with high task completion rate.
> 2. **Failure Case Study** — Example: *Therac-25 (1985)* — radiation therapy machine interface caused overdoses due to race conditions and lack of error prevention.
> 3. **Iterative/Longitudinal Case Study** — Example: *Boeing 737 cockpit evolution over 50 years* — showing gradual refinement of pilot interaction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*State the formula for Task Completion Rate (TCR). If 36 out of 40 users successfully completed a checkout task in an e-commerce case study, compute the TCR and interpret the result.*

**Model Answer (3 marks distribution):**

> **Formula (1 mark):**
>
> $$\text{TCR} = \frac{\text{Completed Tasks}}{\text{Attempted Tasks}} \times 100\%$$
>
> **Substitution (1 mark):**
>
> $$\text{TCR} = \frac{36}{40} \times 100\% = 90\%$$
>
> **Interpretation (1 mark):** A TCR of **90%** is above the ISO 9241-11 acceptable threshold of **78%**, indicating the redesigned checkout flow demonstrates strong learnability and effective affordance design.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ BEFORE ANSWERING**
> When writing a 14-mark case study answer, students commonly lose **3 to 4 marks** because they:
> 1. ❌ Describe the case study *narratively* without applying any **HCI theory** (Nielsen heuristics or Norman's stages).
> 2. ❌ Forget to **quantify impact** with at least one metric (SUS, TCR, ER, or Severity Rating).
> 3. ❌ Fail to identify **which specific heuristic was violated** — examiners award zero marks for vague phrases like *"the design was bad"*.
> 4. ❌ Do not extract a **generalizable design principle** at the end.
> 
> **Golden Rule:** Always close your answer with a bolded *"Design Lesson:"* line.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Healthcare.gov (2013) Failure Case Study**

**(a)** Describe the HCI failures of the **Healthcare.gov** website launched in October 2013. Apply **Nielsen's heuristics** to identify at least **three violations**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Quantify the impact using any **two usability metrics** from your formula sheet and propose a redesign strategy citing **Don Norman's Gulf model**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — HCI Failures of Healthcare.gov:**
