> **Context (1 mark):** Launched October 1, 2013, by the U.S. government as the ACA enrollment portal. Within 2 weeks, only **~1%** of users successfully enrolled. Reported error rate of system: **~30–60% drop-off** at the application creation stage.

> **Violation 1 — Heuristic #1: Visibility of System Status (2 marks):**
> The site provided no real-time feedback during multi-step form filling. Users could not tell whether the page was loading, frozen, or had rejected their input. Many users clicked "Submit" repeatedly, creating duplicate applications and further overloading the system.

> **Violation 2 — Heuristic #5: Error Prevention (2 marks):**
> The system required simultaneous verification with the IRS, Social Security, and Homeland Security databases. When any of these failed, users saw generic *"Server Error"* messages with no guidance on what to do next.

> **Violation 3 — Heuristic #3: User Control and Freedom (2 marks):**
> Users had no way to save partial progress and return later. A 45-minute application had to be completed in one session, leading to massive abandonment.

**(b) — Quantification & Redesign:**

> **Metric 1 — Task Completion Rate (TCR) Calculation (2 marks):**
> If 271,000 of the intended 27 million users completed enrollment by October 31, 2013:
>
> $$\text{TCR} = \frac{271{,}000}{27{,}000{,}000} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{1.00\%}$$
>
> This is catastrophically below the **78%** industry threshold.

> **Metric 2 — Estimated Error Rate (2 marks):**
> If each user encountered on average 1.7 errors (per GAO report) across 8 steps:
>
> $$\text{ER} = \frac{1.7}{8} \times 100\% = \mathbf{21.25\%}$$
>
> This indicates severe feedback breakdown.

> **Redesign via Norman's Gulf Model (3 marks):**
> - **Close the Gulf of Execution:** Add visible progress bars (Heuristic #1), explicit "Save & Continue Later" buttons (Heuristic #3).
> - **Close the Gulf of Evaluation:** Replace generic error messages with **plain-language diagnostics** ("Your IRS record is still verifying — this usually takes 2 minutes").
> - **Apply User-Centered Design:** Run **5-user discount usability tests** (per Nielsen) before public launch.

> **Design Lesson:** A technically functional backend is not a usable interface. **User-visible feedback loops and progress indicators are non-negotiable for any multi-step transactional system.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Apple iPhone (2007) Success Case Study**

**(a)** Analyze why the **Apple iPhone (2007)** is considered a landmark HCI success. Apply **Don Norman's 7 Stages of Action** and **Nielsen's heuristics** to justify your answer. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Quantify its usability advantage using **SUS score** and **TCR** benchmarks. Compare with the dominant smartphone (BlackBerry or Nokia) of that era and extract a generalizable design principle. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — HCI Success Analysis:**

> **Context (1 mark):** Before iPhone, smartphones (BlackBerry, Nokia Symbian) used physical keypads, stylus input, and layered menus. Steve Jobs unveiled the iPhone on Jan 9, 2007 — full multi-touch capacitive screen, single Home button, and direct manipulation.

> **Norman 7 Stages Mapping (3 marks):**
> - *Goal formation:* User sees an icon — clear and immediate.
> - *Intention:* "I want to open email."
> - *Action specification:* Tap the Mail icon (direct manipulation, single gesture).
> - *Execution:* Physical tap with no ambiguity.
> - *Perception:* App zooms in with smooth animation (real-time status).
> - *Interpretation:* User instantly knows app is open.
> - *Evaluation:* App shows inbox — goal achieved in <2 seconds.
>
> **All 7 stages were bridged with zero Gulf.** No previous phone achieved this.

> **Nielsen Heuristic Compliance (3 marks):**
> - *Heuristic #2 (Match Real World):* Icons resemble real objects (envelope = email, phone receiver = calls).
> - *Heuristic #6 (Recognition over Recall):* All functions visible on home screen — no memorizing hidden menus.
> - *Heuristic #8 (Aesthetic & Minimalist):* One button, one screen at a time — radically simplified.

**(b) — Quantification & Design Principle:**

> **SUS Benchmark Comparison (3 marks):**
> - iPhone (2007): Estimated SUS ≈ **78** (above the 68 industry threshold).
> - BlackBerry Pearl (comparable era): Estimated SUS ≈ **52** (below acceptable).
> - The **26-point SUS gap** is one of the largest in consumer electronics history.

> **TCR Benchmark (2 marks):**
> - iPhone first-time task (make a call): TCR ≈ **95%** within 60 seconds.
> - Symbian S60 phones: TCR ≈ **60%** in same time.
>
> $$\Delta \text{TCR} = 95\% - 60\% = \mathbf{35\%} \text{ advantage}$$

> **Generalizable Design Principle (2 marks):**
> **"Direct manipulation with real-world metaphors, supported by immediate sensory feedback, collapses all 7 stages of Norman into a single fluid gesture — this is the foundation of intuitive interaction."**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- ✅ Case study = **empirical analysis** of a real system using HCI theory (not a story).
- ✅ Always classify case study into **Success / Failure / Iterative**.
- ✅ **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** — must be memorized verbatim and cited.
- ✅ **Norman's 7 Stages** — must know the **two gulfs** (Execution and Evaluation).
- ✅ Always **quantify** using TCR, ER, SUS, or Severity Rating (no qualitative-only answers).
- ✅ Close every case study answer with a **generalized design principle**.
- ✅ **5-User Rule:** With $p = 0.31$, coverage = $1 - (0.69)^5 \approx 85\%$.
- ✅ **SUS Formula:** $\text{SUS} = \left[\sum_{\text{odd}} (R_i - 1) + \sum_{\text{even}} (5 - R_i)\right] \times 2.5$.
- ✅ **TCR Formula:** $\frac{\text{Completed}}{\text{Attempted}} \times 100\%$, threshold = **78%**.
- ✅ Famous failure cases to remember: **Therac-25, Healthcare.gov, Boeing 737 MAX, London Ambulance, Windows Vista UAC**.
- ✅ Famous success cases to remember: **Apple iPhone, Google Search, ATMs, iPod Click Wheel**.
- ✅ **Pitfall:** Writing `\vert x \vert` with pipe characters breaks markdown tables — always use `\mid` inside tables.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Execution Based Code Testing: Test Cases and Test Suites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Execution Based Code Testing: Test Cases and Test Suites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Execution-Based Code Testing — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Execution-Based Code Testing (Dynamic Testing)** is a software verification technique in which the program code is **actually executed with a predefined set of input data** (test cases) and the **actual outputs are compared against the expected outputs** to discover defects, errors, or incorrect behavior.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus framing under the *Human Computer Interface (HCI)* evaluation pipeline, execution-based testing complements *inspection-based* and *model-based* testing, forming a critical pillar of the **V-Model** of interface validation.

Key Terminology — Authoritative Definitions:

| Term | Definition (KTU-Aligned) |
|---|---|
| **Test Case** | A triple $(D, S, O)$ where $D$ = input data, $S$ = execution state, $O$ = expected output. |
| **Test Suite** | A finite, ordered or unordered collection of test cases designed to be executed together under a common test harness. |
| **Test Oracle** | The mechanism (manual or automated) that decides whether a given program output is **correct**. |
| **Test Harness** | The supporting code (drivers + stubs) required to execute the System Under Test (SUT). |
| **Defect Density** | $\rho_D = \dfrac{\text{Number of defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ (defects per thousand lines of code). |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Hospital Medical Test Panel**
> Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. A single blood test reveals only one thing (e.g., sugar level). To fully assess health, the doctor orders a **panel** of tests — sugar, cholesterol, thyroid, kidney, etc. Each individual test is a **test case**, and the entire ordered panel is the **test suite**. The doctor's diagnosis logic (what's "normal" vs "abnormal") is the **test oracle**. Together, they execute against the patient (the **system under test**) and produce verdicts.

In HCI terms:
- The **HCI application** is the patient.
- Each test case probes one specific **interaction pathway** (e.g., login flow, error recovery, accessibility check).
- The **test suite** is the complete panel covering all critical usability and functional pathways.

### 1.3 Classification of Execution-Based Testing

Execution-based code testing divides into two broad categories:

1. **Functional (Black-Box) Testing** — based on requirements, ignores internal code.
2. **Structural (White-Box) Testing** — based on internal code structure (control flow, data flow).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 of PEPET861 emphasizes the **execution-based perspective** because HCI evaluation demands observable behavioral verification, not merely code review.

### 1.4 Test Case Anatomy — The `IEEE 829` Standard

A formal KTU-compliant test case contains the following fields:

- **Test Case ID** (e.g., `TC_LOGIN_001`)
- **Test Description** (intent)
- **Pre-conditions** (state required before execution)
- **Input Data** (the stimuli)
- **Execution Steps** (procedure)
- **Expected Result** (oracle output)
- **Actual Result** (recorded at runtime)
- **Post-conditions** (state after execution)
- **Pass/Fail Status**
- **Tester Identity & Date**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How Execution-Based Testing Works

The execution-based testing process follows a strict pipeline:

1. **Requirement Extraction:** Derive testable acceptance criteria from the HCI specification.
2. **Test Case Design:** Use techniques (equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis, decision tables, state-transition testing) to design test inputs.
3. **Test Suite Construction:** Group test cases logically — typically as *smoke*, *regression*, *performance*, and *usability* suites.
4. **Test Execution:** Run the test suite against the build using a test harness or test driver.
5. **Result Comparison:** Compare actual vs. expected output via the test oracle.
6. **Defect Logging:** If discrepancies exist, log them in a defect tracker.
7. **Coverage Analysis:** Compute structural metrics (statement, branch, path coverage).
8. **Re-test & Regression:** Re-run after fixes.

### 2.2 Why and How Each Step Matters

- **Why extract requirements first?** Without traceable requirements, tests cannot be validated as complete or correct.
- **Why group into suites?** Logical grouping enables *selective re-execution* (e.g., only run regression suite after a bug fix).
- **Why use an oracle?** A consistent oracle prevents subjective pass/fail decisions.
- **Why measure coverage?** Coverage exposes *unexecuted code*, which is statistically the highest-risk zone for latent defects.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **high-yield formulas** that KTU examiners frequently test under this topic. **Memorize the formula, the numerator, the denominator, and the typical answer range.**

| # | Metric | Formula | Typical KTU Range | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Statement Coverage** $C_s$ | $C_s = \dfrac{N_{es}}{N_{ts}} \times 100\%$ | 70% – 90% | Detects dead code |
| 2 | **Branch Coverage** $C_b$ | $C_b = \dfrac{N_{eb}}{N_{tb}} \times 100\%$ | 80% – 95% | Detects untested conditionals |
| 3 | **Path Coverage** $C_p$ | $C_p = \dfrac{N_{ep}}{N_{tp}} \times 100\%$ | 50% – 70% | Detects logic-flow defects |
| 4 | **Function Coverage** $C_f$ | $C_f = \dfrac{N_{cf}}{N_{tf}} \times 100\%$ | 90% – 100% | Detects unused functions |
| 5 | **Defect Detection Percentage (DDP)** | $\text{DDP} = \dfrac{D_{found}}{D_{total}} \times 100\%$ | 30% – 90% | Measures test effectiveness |
| 6 | **Mutation Score (MS)** | $\text{MS} = \dfrac{M_{killed}}{M_{total}} \times 100\%$ | 60% – 90% | Measures test sensitivity |
| 7 | **Defect Density** $\rho_D$ | $\rho_D = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | 0.5 – 5 / KLOC | Quality benchmarking |
| 8 | **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G)$ | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | 1 – 10 (low risk) | Lower bound on test paths |
| 9 | **Independent Paths** $P_i$ | $P_i = V(G)$ | equals $V(G)$ | Minimum test-suite size |
| 10 | **Test Effectiveness Ratio (TER)** | $\text{TER} = \dfrac{D_{caught}}{D_{released}}$ | > 0.95 ideal | QA gate metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Note:** All variables are **unitless percentages or pure ratios**, except $\rho_D$ (per KLOC). Use $\vert$ for absolute value where needed; NEVER use bare `|` inside markdown table cells.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In HCI product engineering (e.g., medical-device UIs, automotive infotainment, banking apps):

- **Statement Coverage** is mandated by **DO-178C** (avionics) and **IEC 62304** (medical software).
- **Mutation Testing** is used in safety-critical UI flows (e.g., infusion pumps) to detect weak test suites.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity** is integrated into IDEs (Visual Studio, SonarQube) for continuous quality monitoring.
- **Test Suites** are version-controlled alongside source code (CI/CD pipelines in Jenkins, GitHub Actions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A test suite is not a "one-time artifact." It is a **living document** that grows with the codebase. Industry rule of thumb: maintain test-to-code ratio ≈ 1:1 for critical systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lower Bound on Test Suite Size via Cyclomatic Complexity

**Theorem (McCabe, 1976):** The minimum number of **independent paths** through a program's control flow graph equals its **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G)$.

**Given:** A control flow graph $G = (N, E)$ with:
- $N$ = number of nodes (representing statements/blocks),
- $E$ = number of edges (representing control transfers),
- $P$ = number of connected components (typically $P = 1$ for a single program).

**To Derive:** The minimum test-suite size $T_{\min}$.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of Cyclomatic Complexity:**

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

**Step 2 — Explanation of terms:**

- Each **independent path** is a path that introduces at least one new edge (or node) not covered by previous paths.
- A set of independent paths forms a **basis** for the path space, meaning every other path is a linear combination of basis paths.
- To exercise **every edge** at least once, the test suite must contain at least as many test cases as there are independent paths.

**Step 3 — Equate independent paths to $V(G)$:**

$$P_i = V(G)$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the lower bound:**

$$T_{\min} = V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example:**

Consider the HCI login flow pseudo-code with two `if` statements:

```
1. read(username)
2. read(password)
3. if (username == "admin")
4.    if (password == "1234")
5.        grant_access()
6.    else
7.        deny("wrong password")
8. else
9.    deny("wrong username")
```

Construct the Control Flow Graph (CFG):

- Nodes: $N = 9$ (one per statement).
- Edges: $E = 10$ (including the two false branches).

Apply the formula:

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P = 10 - 9 + 2(1) = 3$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

At least **3 test cases** are required:
1. `username=admin`, `password=1234` → grant_access path.
2. `username=admin`, `password=wrong` → wrong-password path.
3. `username=guest`, `password=anything` → wrong-username path.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Test Coverage Computation (Multi-Criterion)

**Given:** A test suite $TS$ consisting of $n$ test cases executed against a program with:
- $S$ total executable statements,
- $B$ total branches (decision outcomes),
- $P$ total independent paths.

**Step 1 — Define raw coverage counts:**

Let $s_i$ = statements executed by test case $t_i$.

The total unique statements executed across the suite is:

$$S_{covered} = \vert \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} s_i \vert$$

**Step 2 — Statement coverage formula:**

$$C_s = \dfrac{S_{covered}}{S_{total}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Branch coverage formula:**

$$C_b = \dfrac{B_{covered}}{B_{total}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Path coverage formula:**

$$C_p = \dfrac{P_{covered}}{P_{total}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Combined coverage metric (KTU-favored):**

$$C_{combined} = w_s \cdot C_s + w_b \cdot C_b + w_p \cdot C_p$$

where $w_s + w_b + w_p = 1$ are weighted priorities.

**Step 6 — Worked Example (Numerical):**

A test suite of 4 test cases for an HCI form-validation module yields:
- $S_{covered} = 85$ out of $100$ total statements.
- $B_{covered} = 18$ out of $20$ total branches.
- $P_{covered} = 7$ out of $12$ total independent paths.

Compute each:

$$C_s = \frac{85}{100} \times 100\% = 85\%$$

$$C_b = \frac{18}{20} \times 100\% = 90\%$$

$$C_p = \frac{7}{12} \times 100\% = 58.33\%$$

With weights $w_s = 0.2$, $w_b = 0.5$, $w_p = 0.3$:

$$C_{combined} = (0.2)(85) + (0.5)(90) + (0.3)(58.33)$$

$$C_{combined} = 17 + 45 + 17.5 = 79.5\%$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation:**

A combined coverage of **79.5%** is below the **DO-178C Level B** threshold of **80%**, signaling the need for additional test cases targeting the **4 uncovered paths**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Defect Detection Percentage (DDP) in a Test Suite

**Step 1 — Define the universe of defects:**

$$D_{total} = D_{found} + D_{escaped}$$

where:
- $D_{found}$ = defects discovered by the test suite,
- $D_{escaped}$ = defects discovered by users after release.

**Step 2 — DDP formula:**

$$\text{DDP} = \frac{D_{found}}{D_{total}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example:**

A test suite found 42 defects. After release, customers reported 8 additional defects. Compute DDP:

$$D_{total} = 42 + 8 = 50$$

$$\text{DDP} = \frac{42}{50} \times 100\% = 84\%$$

**Step 4 — KTU Interpretation:**

A DDP of **84%** is **acceptable** for commercial software but **insufficient** for safety-critical HCI (target ≥ **95%** for medical devices).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Test Design Technique

| Technique | Best Suited For | Code Visibility | Coverage Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equivalence Partitioning | Reducing input space | Black-Box | High |
| Boundary Value Analysis | Numeric input edges | Black-Box | Very High |
| Decision Table Testing | Complex business rules | Black-Box | Very High |
| State Transition Testing | Event-driven UIs (HCI) | Black-Box | High |
| Statement Testing | Verifying all code lines | White-Box | Medium |
| Branch Testing | Verifying all decisions | White-Box | High |
| Path Testing | Verifying all logic flows | White-Box | Very High (costly) |
| Mutation Testing | Measuring test sensitivity | White-Box | Excellent (meta) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Execution-Based Testing Pipeline (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[REQ1: HCI Specification] --> B[REQ2: Extract Acceptance Criteria]
    B --> C[REQ3: Select Test Design Technique]
    C --> D[REQ4: Design Test Cases]
    D --> E[REQ5: Build Test Suite TS]
    E --> F[REQ6: Configure Test Harness]
    F --> G[REQ7: Execute Test Suite]
    G --> H{REQ8: Oracle Verdict}
    H -- PASS --> I[REQ9: Record Result]
    H -- FAIL --> J[REQ10: Log Defect]
    J --> K[REQ11: Developer Fix]
    K --> F
    I --> L[REQ12: Compute Coverage Metrics]
    L --> M{REQ13: Coverage Target Met?}
    M -- YES --> N[REQ14: Release Approval]
    M -- NO --> O[REQ15: Add New Test Cases]
    O --> D
```

### 4.2 Test Suite Architecture (Block-Level Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[REQ16: Master Test Suite] --> B[REQ17: Smoke Suite]
    A --> C[REQ18: Functional Suite]
    A --> D[REQ19: Regression Suite]
    A --> E[REQ20: Performance Suite]
    A --> F[REQ21: Usability Suite]
    B --> G[REQ22: Test Case TC001]
    B --> H[REQ23: Test Case TC002]
    C --> I[REQ24: Test Case TC003]
    C --> J[REQ25: Test Case TC004]
    D --> K[REQ26: Test Case TC005]
    E --> L[REQ27: Test Case TC006]
    F --> M[REQ28: Test Case TC007]
```

### 4.3 Test Case Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateDraft
    stateDraft --> stateReview: Peer Reviewed
    stateReview --> stateApproved: Approved
    stateReview --> stateDraft: Rejected
    stateApproved --> stateReady: Ready for Execution
    stateReady --> stateRunning: Executed
    stateRunning --> statePassed: Oracle Says PASS
    stateRunning --> stateFailed: Oracle Says FAIL
    stateFailed --> stateBlocked: Defect Logged
    stateBlocked --> stateReady: Fix Verified
    statePassed --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Coverage Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[REQ29: Test Suite TS] --> B[REQ30: Run Instrumented Code]
    B --> C[REQ31: Collect Probe Data]
    C --> D[REQ32: Aggregate Statements]
    C --> E[REQ33: Aggregate Branches]
    C --> F[REQ34: Aggregate Paths]
    D --> G[REQ35: Compute Cs]
    E --> H[REQ36: Compute Cb]
    F --> I[REQ37: Compute Cp]
    G --> J[REQ38: Coverage Report]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{REQ39: Target Met?}
    K -- YES --> L[REQ40: Sign Off]
    K -- NO --> M[REQ41: Backlog Gaps]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a test case and list any **four** essential components of a formally specified test case according to the IEEE 829 standard.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **test case** is a documented set of pre-conditions, inputs, actions, expected results, and post-conditions developed for a particular test objective.

**Four essential components** (1/2 mark each + 1 mark for the definition):
1. **Test Case ID** — Unique identifier (e.g., `TC_LOGIN_001`).
2. **Test Inputs** — The data values provided to the SUT.
3. **Expected Result** — The oracle-specified correct output.
4. **Actual Result** — The output observed during execution.
5. *(Optional 5th)* Pre/post conditions and execution steps.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a **test case** and a **test suite**. Why is grouping test cases into suites preferred over executing them individually?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Test Case | Test Suite |
|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Single input–output verification | Collection of test cases |
| **Scope** | Tests one scenario | Tests a module or feature |
| **Identity** | Unique ID per case | Group ID + member cases |

**Why grouping?** (1 mark)
- Enables **selective re-execution** (e.g., run only the regression suite after a bug fix).
- Facilitates **prioritized execution** (smoke suite first, then deep suite).
- Allows **parallel execution** across CI pipelines.
- Simplifies **reporting and traceability**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **execution-based code testing** with a suitable diagram. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** compared to inspection-based testing. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a given program snippet, construct the **control flow graph**, compute the **cyclomatic complexity** $V(G)$ using all three McCabe formulas, and determine the **minimum number of independent test cases** required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Execution-based code testing, also called **dynamic testing**, is the process of executing a program with a set of test inputs (test cases) and comparing the actual outputs against expected outputs to identify defects. It complements static review by exposing runtime defects.

**Diagram (2 marks):** Use the pipeline diagram from Section 4.1.

**Advantages vs Limitations (3 marks — distribute as 4 bullets: 3 advantages + 3 limitations, or as comparison table):**

| Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|
| Detects **runtime defects** (memory leaks, race conditions) | **Expensive** — requires execution environment |
| Validates **actual behavior**, not just code structure | **Incomplete** — exhaustive input space impossible |
| Measures **performance, usability, reliability** | Cannot detect **missing requirements** |
| Enables **automated regression** testing | **Time-consuming** for large codebases |

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — (7 marks)

**Given Program:**

```
1. function HCI_login(user, pwd):
2.    if (user == null)
3.        return "ERR_NO_USER"
4.    if (pwd == null)
5.        return "ERR_NO_PWD"
6.    if (user == "admin" AND pwd == "1234")
7.        return "GRANTED"
8.    else
9.        return "DENIED"
```

**Step 1 — Construct CFG (2 marks):**

Nodes: $N_1$ (entry), $N_2$ (user==null check), $N_3$ (pwd==null check), $N_4$ (admin check), $N_5$ (GRANTED), $N_6$ (DENIED), $N_7$ (ERR_NO_USER), $N_8$ (ERR_NO_PWD), $N_9$ (exit).
Total: $N = 9$.
Total edges (including conditional true/false branches and exit): $E = 11$.
Connected components: $P = 1$.

**Step 2 — Apply McCabe's three formulas (3 marks):**

**Formula 1 — $V(G) = E - N + 2P$:**

$$V(G) = 11 - 9 + 2(1) = 4$$

**Formula 2 — $V(G) = P + 1$ (where $P$ = number of predicate nodes):**

There are **3** predicate nodes (lines 2, 4, 6):

$$V(G) = 3 + 1 = 4$$

**Formula 3 — $V(G) = \text{Number of enclosed regions} + 1$:**

The flow graph has **3** enclosed regions (one per `if` decision):

$$V(G) = 3 + 1 = 4$$

All three methods agree: $V(G) = 4$.

**Step 3 — Minimum test cases (2 marks):**

$$T_{\min} = V(G) = 4$$

**Four required independent paths:**

1. `user=null` → returns `ERR_NO_USER`.
2. `user="x"`, `pwd=null` → returns `ERR_NO_PWD`.
3. `user="admin"`, `pwd="1234"` → returns `GRANTED`.
4. `user="admin"`, `pwd="wrong"` → returns `DENIED`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Define a **test suite**. Explain the different **categories of test suites** (smoke, regression, performance, usability) with examples in the HCI domain. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A test suite of 50 test cases is executed on an HCI module. Statement coverage achieved is **85%**, branch coverage is **78%**, and path coverage is **45%**. The program has 200 executable statements, 100 branches, and 40 independent paths. Compute the **absolute number of uncovered statements, branches, and paths**, and recommend the **next steps**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **test suite** is an ordered/unordered collection of test cases designed to be executed together under a common test harness, targeting a specific module, feature, or quality attribute.

**Categories (5 marks — 1.25 marks each):**

1. **Smoke Test Suite** — A *minimal* set of tests verifying that the build is stable. *HCI Example:* Open app → verify main screen renders → log out.

2. **Regression Test Suite** — Tests that ensure previously fixed bugs have not reappeared and existing functionality still works. *HCI Example:* Re-run all form-validation tests after UI redesign.

3. **Performance Test Suite** — Tests that measure response time, throughput, and resource usage. *HCI Example:* Measure login screen render time under 1000 concurrent users.

4. **Usability Test Suite** — Tests that validate user experience heuristics. *HCI Example:* Verify all interactive elements are keyboard-accessible; check color contrast ratios.

5. *(Bonus)* **Acceptance Test Suite** — Validates against business requirements; run pre-release. *HCI Example:* Confirm checkout flow matches wireframe.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $S_{total} = 200$, $C_s = 85\%$
- $B_{total} = 100$, $C_b = 78\%$
- $P_{total} = 40$, $C_p = 45\%$
- Test suite size: 50.

**Step 1 — Compute covered counts (3 marks):**

$$S_{covered} = 0.85 \times 200 = 170$$

$$B_{covered} = 0.78 \times 100 = 78$$

$$P_{covered} = 0.45 \times 40 = 18$$

**Step 2 — Compute uncovered counts (2 marks):**

$$S_{uncovered} = 200 - 170 = 30 \text{ statements}$$

$$B_{uncovered} = 100 - 78 = 22 \text{ branches}$$

$$P_{uncovered} = 40 - 18 = 22 \text{ paths}$$

**Step 3 — Recommendations (2 marks):**

| Gap | Severity | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| 22 uncovered paths | **High** | Apply **path testing**; prioritize error-handling and edge-case flows. |
| 22 uncovered branches | **High** | Add **boundary-value** and **decision-table** test cases. |
| 30 uncovered statements | **Medium** | Use code-coverage tool (e.g., JaCoCo, Istanbul) to identify and target. |

**Overall Recommendation:** Path coverage of **45%** is critically low (industry target ≈ 70%). **Add at least 15 more test cases** targeting the 22 uncovered independent paths before release.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply all three McCabe formulas.** Examiners award 1 mark per formula — missing two = lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $C_s$, $C_b$, $C_p$ variables.** Always state the numerator and denominator explicitly.
> 3. **Ignoring `2P` in $V(G)$.** For multi-component systems, $P > 1$; omitting it yields wrong complexity.
> 4. **Not listing pre/post-conditions.** Test cases without pre-conditions lose 1 mark in IEEE-829 style questions.
> 5. **Confusing DDP with defect density.** DDP uses **defects found vs total defects**; defect density uses **defects per KLOC**.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram.** Long-answer questions on this topic typically carry 2 marks for a labeled diagram.
> 7. **Writing "$|x|$" inside a markdown table.** KTU digital answer sheets parse `|` as a column separator — use `\vert` instead.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- [ ] **Execution-Based Testing** = **dynamic** testing — code is actually run.
- [ ] **Test Case** = triple $(D, S, O)$: input data, state, expected output.
- [ ] **Test Suite** = ordered/unordered collection of test cases sharing a common harness.
- [ ] **Test Oracle** = the judge of pass/fail; never subjective.
- [ ] **Test Harness** = drivers + stubs enabling SUT execution.
- [ ] **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ = minimum independent test paths.
- [ ] **Statement Coverage** $C_s$ targets lines executed.
- [ ] **Branch Coverage** $C_b$ targets decision outcomes (true/false).
- [ ] **Path Coverage** $C_p$ targets unique execution paths (most expensive).
- [ ] **DDP** measures test-suite effectiveness.
- [ ] **Mutation Score** measures test-suite sensitivity (advanced, KTU M.Tech level).
- [ ] **Test Suites are categorized**: smoke, regression, performance, usability, acceptance.
- [ ] **IEEE 829** standard defines 8–10 components of a formal test case.
- [ ] **Coverage ≥ 80%** is the typical KTU/civilian software release threshold.
- [ ] **Coverage ≥ 95%** is required for safety-critical HCI (medical, avionics).
- [ ] **Defect Density** $\rho_D$ uses **KLOC** as the denominator, never LOC.
- [ ] **Test suites must be version-controlled** and **continuously updated**.
- [ ] **Path testing** is impractical beyond $V(G) > 50$; switch to branch testing.
- [ ] **Boundary Value Analysis** is the most cost-effective black-box technique.
- [ ] **State-Transition Testing** is the most relevant black-box technique for event-driven HCI.

> **End of Notes — Module 3, Topic: Execution Based Code Testing: Test Cases and Test Suites**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Black-Box Testing - Equivalence Class Partitioning and Boundary Value Analysis Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Black-Box Testing - Equivalence Class Partitioning and Boundary Value Analysis Approaches # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Black-Box Testing — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Black-Box Testing** is a software testing methodology in which the tester evaluates the functionality of a system **without peering into its internal code structure, implementation details, or internal data pathways**. The system under test (SUT) is treated as an "opaque box," and tests are derived exclusively from the **requirements specification, functional design documents, and external behavioral contracts**. The focus is purely on **inputs supplied to the system** and the **outputs received in response**, with the implicit goal of validating that the program behaves correctly across a wide variety of legitimate and illegitimate conditions. ### Sub-Categories Relevant to Module 3 Within the broader umbrella of black-box testing, two specification-driven, systematic techniques dominate the KTU syllabus: - **Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)** — also called *Equivalence Partitioning*. - **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** — sometimes called *Boundary Testing* or *Edge-Case Testing*. > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme specifically classifies these two under the **functional / specification-based** branch of black-box testing. They are mutually complementary — ECP reduces the *number* of test cases, while BVA targets the *edges* where the highest defect density resides. ## 1.2 Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)** is a black-box test design technique that divides the entire input domain of a software module into mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive groups of data (called *equivalence classes* or *partitions*) from which **any single representative value is assumed to exercise all other values in the same class**. The underlying assumption is the **Equivalence Relation Hypothesis**: if one test case in a partition passes, the entire partition passes; if it fails, the entire partition fails. ## 1.3 Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** is a black-box test design technique that concentrates test execution on the values lying **at, immediately above, and immediately below the edges** of valid input partitions. It is founded on the empirical observation that programmers most frequently commit off-by-one logical errors at decision boundaries. ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a **vending machine** that accepts only coins in the range **₹1 to ₹10**. - **ECP Analogy:** Instead of feeding every coin (₹1, ₹2, ₹3 … ₹10) to verify that the machine returns the correct candy, you sample only a few representative coins. One ₹1 coin, one mid-range ₹5 coin, and one ₹10 coin — if all three work correctly, you confidently assume that ₹2, ₹3, ₹4, ₹6, ₹7, ₹8, ₹9 will *also* work. Conversely, you also throw in a ₹0.50 coin and a ₹20 note to see if the machine rejects them gracefully. - **BVA Analogy:** Now imagine the engineer who designed the machine almost made a *classic mistake* — they might have written `if amount > 10` instead of `if amount >= 10`. This kind of mistake shows up **right at the boundary** (₹10) and **just outside it** (₹11 or a coin worth slightly more). So you test the machine *very carefully* at exactly ₹9, ₹10, ₹10.01, and ₹11. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" behind BVA:** Decades of industrial defect logs (notably the IBM and Bell Labs studies cited by Beizer in *Software Testing Techniques*) confirm that **boundary conditions account for a disproportionately high percentage of software defects** — typically cited as a **2-to-1 ratio** (boundary defects vs. interior defects). ## 1.5 Core Metrics & Constants | Metric | Standard Value | Context | |---|---|---| | **BVA focus range** | **On, just-above, just-below** | 3 × 2 × N boundaries | | **ECP reduction ratio** | **~ 1 / N** | where N is class size | | **Industry defect density** | **2× higher at boundaries** | Empirical rule | > [!TIP] > A useful KTU one-liner to memorize: **"ECP divides; BVA targets the edges."** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Do We Need ECP and BVA? Exhaustive black-box testing of even a single integer input is impossible in practice. Consider an input that accepts any 32-bit integer — the possible input space is **2³² = 4,294,967,296 values**. Clearly, sampling all of them is computationally infeasible. ECP and BVA are **mathematically informed sampling strategies** that: - Reduce the test case count to a manageable size. - Maximize the probability of defect detection per test case. - Provide **systematic, repeatable, and documentable** test design logic. ## 2.2 Equivalence Class Partitioning — Operational Logic ECP operates through the following structured steps: 1. **Identify input conditions** from the specification. Each input field, button, or data parameter becomes a candidate. 2. **Partition each input condition** into at least two classes: - **Valid Equivalence Classes (VEC):** Sets of inputs that the specification *accepts* and that the program is *required* to handle correctly. - **Invalid Equivalence Classes (IEC):** Sets of inputs that the specification *rejects*, or that fall outside the documented contract. 3. **Select a representative** from each class — the chosen value should exercise the *typical* behavior of that class. 4. **Design test cases** by combining the representatives, often using pairwise or base-choice combination strategies. > [!NOTE] > **ECP Heuristic Rules for Class Identification** > - If a condition specifies a **range** (e.g., 1–100), define **one valid class** and **two invalid classes** (below and above). > - If a condition specifies a **discrete set of values** (e.g., {Yes, No, Maybe}), define **one valid class per member** and **one invalid class** (any other value). > - If a condition specifies a **"must-be" rule** (e.g., first character must be a letter), define **one valid class** and **one invalid class**. > - If a condition has **boolean nature**, treat true and false as two distinct classes. ## 2.3 Boundary Value Analysis — Operational Logic BVA is grounded in a tighter set of rules that focus exclusively on the *edges* of the equivalence classes identified by ECP: 1. For every input range identified, identify the **boundary points** (lower bound, upper bound). 2. Construct test cases at: - The **on-point** (the boundary value itself — *valid*). - The **off-point just inside** (one step away from the boundary but on the valid side). - The **off-point just outside** (one step across the boundary on the invalid side). 3. For discrete value sets, test the **first, last, and the value just before / just after** the set. 4. Apply the same logic to **output boundaries** as well — many testers forget that outputs have boundaries too (e.g., "report must list between 1 and 50 items"). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three classical BVA variants** that students must know for KTU 2024: > - **2-Value BVA (Traditional):** On-point + one off-point → 2n tests for n variables. > - **3-Value BVA:** On-point + off-point below + off-point above → 3n tests for n variables. > - **Robust BVA:** 3-Value BVA extended to include *exceptional / extreme* invalid values far beyond the boundary (e.g., MAXINT, empty string, null). ## 2.4 ECP vs. BVA — Comparative Matrix | Dimension | ECP | BVA | |---|---|---| | **Primary Goal** | Reduce test case count | Maximize defect detection at edges | | **Test Selection** | 1 representative per class | On-point + off-points per boundary | | **Best Used When** | Large input domains with no clear range | Inputs have explicit ranges or limits | | **Defect Target** | Logic errors throughout the domain | Off-by-one, comparison-operator errors | | **Mutual Relation** | Provides the **classes** | Operates on the **edges** of those classes | | **Typical Output** | Lower test volume | Higher defect yield per test | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Avionics (DO-178C compliance):** ECP/BVA documented test cases are a **regulatory requirement** for Level A and B software certification. - **Banking & FinTech:** Boundary testing of transaction limits, interest rate computations, and currency precision routines is **non-negotiable** due to financial liability. - **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** BVA is mandated for dosage, age, and weight input validation. - **Web-based HCI:** ECP/BVA drive the validation logic of every form, slider, dropdown, and date-picker in modern web applications. > [!TIP] > A KTU-viva-ready sentence: *"ECP and BVA are **specification-based**, **systematic**, and **defect-targeted** sampling techniques that fall under the black-box testing umbrella of HCI verification."* # 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Worked Examples To make the techniques fully concrete, this section works through **one canonical ECP example** and **one canonical BVA example**, both derived from a single specification commonly used in KTU model papers. ## 3.1 The Specification > **A university portal allows a student to enter their roll number for fetching results. The valid roll number range is from `1001` to `1500` (both inclusive), and the input must be a numeric string of exactly 4 digits.** ## 3.2 ECP — Step-by-Step Procedure ### Step 1: Identify Input Conditions The only input is the **roll-number text field**, but it has **two distinct sub-conditions**: - **Sub-condition A:** Numeric value of the roll number. - **Sub-condition B:** Format of the input (4-digit numeric string). ### Step 2: Partition Each Sub-condition #### Sub-condition A — Numeric Range Partitioning | Class ID | Class Type | Range Description | Representative | |---|---|---|---| | A1 | Valid | $1001 \leq \text{rollno} \leq 1500$ | `1250` | | A2 | Invalid | $\text{rollno} < 1001$ | `900` | | A3 | Invalid | $\text{rollno} > 1500$ | `1800` | #### Sub-condition B — Format Partitioning | Class ID | Class Type | Range Description | Representative | |---|---|---|---| | B1 | Valid | 4-digit numeric string | `"1250"` | | B2 | Invalid | Non-numeric characters | `"AB12"` | | B3 | Invalid | Wrong length (too short) | `"125"` | | B4 | Invalid | Wrong length (too long) | `"12500"` | | B5 | Invalid | Empty / null | `""` | ### Step 3: Select Representatives - From A1: `1250` - From A2: `900` - From A3: `1800` - From B1: `"1250"` *(also valid for A1 — combined)* - From B2: `"AB12"` - From B3: `"125"` - From B4: `"12500"` - From B5: `""` ### Step 4: Build the ECP Test Case Table | TC ID | Roll No. Input | Combines Classes | Expected Result | |---|---|---|---| | TC-ECP-01 | `1250` | A1, B1 | Result page displayed | | TC-ECP-02 | `900` | A2, B1 | Error: roll number below range | | TC-ECP-03 | `1800` | A3, B1 | Error: roll number above range | | TC-ECP-04 | `AB12` | A2 (numeric), B2 | Error: invalid format | | TC-ECP-05 | `125` | A2, B3 | Error: invalid length | | TC-ECP-06 | `12500` | A3, B4 | Error: invalid length | | TC-ECP-07 | `""` | A2, B5 | Error: input required | > [!NOTE] > **Reduction Achieved:** Without ECP, the tester might be tempted to type 500 values from 1001–1500. ECP reduced this to **7 test cases** — a reduction ratio of roughly **1.4%** while still covering all functional paths. ## 3.3 BVA — Step-by-Step Procedure (Same Specification) ### Step 1: Identify the Boundaries The numeric boundary points for the roll number range are: $$ \text{Lower boundary} = 1001, \quad \text{Upper boundary} = 1500 $$ ### Step 2: For Each Boundary, Compute On, Off-Below, Off-Above For the **lower boundary (1001)**: $$ \text{On-point} = 1001, \quad \text{Off-point below} = 1000, \quad \text{Off-point above} = 1002 $$ For the **upper boundary (1500)**: $$ \text{On-point} = 1500, \quad \text{Off-point below} = 1499, \quad \text{Off-point above} = 1501 $$ ### Step 3: Build the BVA Test Case Table (3-Value BVA) | TC ID | Roll No. | Boundary | Position | Expected Result | |---|---|---|---|---| | TC-BVA-01 | `1000` | Lower | Off-below | Error: below range | | TC-BVA-02 | `1001` | Lower | On-point (valid) | Result page displayed | | TC-BVA-03 | `1002` | Lower | Off-above (valid) | Result page displayed | | TC-BVA-04 | `1499` | Upper | Off-below (valid) | Result page displayed | | TC-BVA-05 | `1500` | Upper | On-point (valid) | Result page displayed | | TC-BVA-06 | `1501` | Upper | Off-above | Error: above range | ### Step 4 (Robust BVA Extension): Add Extreme Values | TC ID | Roll No. | Purpose | Expected Result | |---|---|---|---| | TC-BVA-07 | `0` | Robust lower extreme | Error: below range | | TC-BVA-08 | `99999` | Robust upper extreme | Error: above range | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Generalized BVA Formula (for KTU derivations):** > For an input variable with $n$ independent boundaries (e.g., lower bound, upper bound, distinct value sets), the number of BVA test cases required is: > $$\text{BVA Tests} = 4n \quad \text{(3-Value BVA)} \qquad \text{or} \qquad \text{BVA Tests} = 6n \quad \text{(Robust BVA)}$$ ## 3.4 Combining ECP and BVA — The Integrated Strategy The most powerful approach in industry — and a frequent **14-mark KTU question** — is to use **ECP to find the partitions** and then **BVA to test the edges of those partitions**. For our roll-number example, the integrated set would include: - All 7 ECP test cases (TC-ECP-01 to TC-ECP-07). - The 6 BVA test cases (TC-BVA-01 to TC-BVA-06), with TC-BVA-02 and TC-BVA-05 often overlapping with TC-ECP-01. > [!TIP] > When asked in a KTU 14-mark question, always present your answer in the **exact order**: (1) Identify inputs → (2) List valid & invalid classes → (3) Choose representatives → (4) Build ECP table → (5) Identify boundaries → (6) Build BVA table → (7) State the integrated result. ## 3.5 Worked Mini-Example: Date Input Field > **Specification:** A form field accepts a *day of the month* as an integer, valid values are **1 to 31** (inclusive). ### ECP Partitions - **V1:** $1 \leq \text{day} \leq 31$ → representative `15` - **I1:** $\text{day} < 1$ → representative `-3` - **I2:** $\text{day} > 31$ → representative `45` ### BVA Boundaries $$ \text{Lower on} = 1, \; \text{Lower off-below} = 0, \; \text{Lower off-above} = 2 $$ $$ \text{Upper on} = 31, \; \text{Upper off-below} = 30, \; \text{Upper off-above} = 32 $$ **6 BVA test cases** + 3 ECP test cases (with overlap) yield full coverage of the input domain. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — End-to-End ECP + BVA Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Read Specification] --> B[Identify all Input Conditions] B --> C{Input has a Numeric Range?} C -- Yes --> D[Define Valid EC and 2 Invalid ECs] C -- No --> E{Input is a Discrete Value Set?} E -- Yes --> F[Define one EC per value and one Invalid EC] E -- No --> G{Input is Boolean or Must-Be?} G -- Yes --> H[Define Valid and Invalid EC] G -- No --> I[Partition by Format or Type] D --> J[Select Representative per Class] F --> J H --> J I --> J J --> K[Build ECP Test Case Table] K --> L[For Each Range, Locate Boundary Points] L --> M[Generate On-Point and Off-Point Test Cases] M --> N[Build BVA Test Case Table] N --> O[Optional: Add Robust Extreme Values] O --> P[Merge ECP and BVA into Final Test Suite] P --> Q[Execute Tests and Record Results] Q --> R[End] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — ECP and BVA Relationship (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR spec[Specification Document] --> ecmain[ECP Engine] spec --> bvamain[BVA Engine] ecmain --> ec1[Valid Partition] ecmain --> ec2[Invalid Partition Low] ecmain --> ec3[Invalid Partition High] bvamain --> bv1[On-Point Test] bvamain --> bv2[Off-Point Below] bvamain --> bv3[Off-Point Above] bvamain --> bv4[Robust Extreme] ec1 --> testrep[Representative Test Cases] ec2 --> testrep ec3 --> testrep bv1 --> edgetest[Boundary Test Cases] bv2 --> edgetest bv3 --> edgetest bv4 --> edgetest testrep --> final[Final Combined Test Suite] edgetest --> final final --> report[Defect Report] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Visual Map — Boundaries of the Roll-Number Example ```mermaid flowchart LR P0["Below Lower: 0 to 1000
INVALID CLASS"] P1["Lower Boundary
1001 ON-POINT (Valid)"] P2["Interior Valid Range
1002 to 1499 (Valid)"] P3["Upper Boundary
1500 ON-POINT (Valid)"] P4["Above Upper: 1501 to MAX
INVALID CLASS"] P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 style P0 fill:#FFD2D2,stroke:#C00 style P1 fill:#D2FFD2,stroke:#0A0 style P2 fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#0A0 style P3 fill:#D2FFD2,stroke:#0A0 style P4 fill:#FFD2D2,stroke:#C00 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Green zones are valid partitions, red zones are invalid partitions, and the green-bold zones at `1001` and `1500` are the on-point boundary values that BVA targets with off-point neighbors on either side. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** > *Differentiate between Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP) and Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) as black-box testing techniques. Mention the *specific* defect type each one is most likely to uncover.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **ECP** divides the input domain into mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive classes of data, then selects **one representative per class**. It is most effective at uncovering **logic errors distributed throughout the input domain** and **missing-handler defects** (e.g., unhandled invalid inputs). - **BVA** focuses test cases on values **at, immediately above, and immediately below** the edges of valid input partitions. It is most effective at uncovering **off-by-one errors**, **incorrect comparison operators** (e.g., `>` instead of `>=`), and **boundary-handling defects**. - **Key difference:** ECP targets the *interior* of partitions, BVA targets the *edges*; the two are complementary and are typically applied together in industry practice. *(Award full 3 marks if the student explicitly states which defect type each technique targets.)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]** > *Define "Robust Boundary Value Analysis." How does it differ from the traditional 3-Value BVA approach?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **3-Value BVA** (traditional) generates test cases at three positions per boundary: the **on-point**, the **off-point below**, and the **off-point above** — totaling **3n tests** for *n* variables. - **Robust BVA** extends the 3-Value technique by adding test cases for **extreme / exceptional values** far outside the valid range, such as `MAXINT`, an **empty string**, a **null pointer**, or values at the **absolute limits of the data type**. - It is called *robust* because it guards against program crashes, exceptions, and undefined behaviors triggered by extreme inputs that 3-Value BVA would not test. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, ESE Model Paper]** > **(a)** Consider a software module that accepts a *temperature value* in Celsius from a user. The valid range is **`-40` °C to `125` °C** (both inclusive), and the value must be entered as a numeric string of **maximum 5 characters**. > - **(i)** Apply **Equivalence Class Partitioning** and list all valid and invalid classes with one representative each. **(7 Marks)** > - **(ii)** Apply **3-Value Boundary Value Analysis** on the numeric range and list all boundary test cases. **(7 Marks)** #### Part (a)(i) — ECP Solution (7 Marks) **Step 1: Identify Sub-conditions** - Sub-condition A: Numeric value (range `-40` to `125`). - Sub-condition B: Format (numeric string, maximum 5 characters). **Step 2: ECP Table** | Class ID | Sub-condition | Class Type | Range Description | Representative | |---|---|---|---|---| | A1 | Numeric | Valid | $-40 \leq \text{temp} \leq 125$ | `25` | | A2 | Numeric | Invalid | $\text{temp} < -40$ | `-100` | | A3 | Numeric | Invalid | $\text{temp} > 125$ | `200` | | B1 | Format | Valid | Numeric string $\leq 5$ chars | `"25"` | | B2 | Format | Invalid | Non-numeric characters | `"hot"` | | B3 | Format | Invalid | String > 5 characters | `"100000"` | | B4 | Format | Invalid | Empty input | `""` | **Step 3: Combined Test Cases** | TC ID | Input | Classes | Expected Output | |---|---|---|---| | TC-01 | `25` | A1, B1 | Accepted; converted and stored | | TC-02 | `-100` | A2, B1 | Error: below valid range | | TC-03 | `200` | A3, B1 | Error: above valid range | | TC-04 | `hot` | A1 (fails), B2 | Error: invalid format | | TC-05 | `100000` | A3, B3 | Error: too long / above range | | TC-06 | `""` | A2, B4 | Error: required field | *(7 marks: 2 marks for correct partitioning logic, 3 marks for the ECP table, 2 marks for the test cases.)* #### Part (a)(ii) — BVA Solution (7 Marks) **Step 1: Identify Boundaries** $$ \text{Lower boundary} = -40, \quad \text{Upper boundary} = 125 $$ **Step 2: 3-Value BVA Test Cases** | TC ID | Input | Boundary | Position | Expected Output | |---|---|---|---|---| | TC-BVA-01 | `-41` | Lower | Off-below (invalid) | Error: below range | | TC-BVA-02 | `-40` | Lower | On-point (valid) | Accepted | | TC-BVA-03 | `-39` | Lower | Off-above (valid) | Accepted | | TC-BVA-04 | `124` | Upper | Off-below (valid) | Accepted | | TC-BVA-05 | `125` | Upper | On-point (valid) | Accepted | | TC-BVA-06 | `126` | Upper | Off-above (invalid) | Error: above range | *(7 marks: 2 marks for boundary identification, 3 marks for correct on-point/off-point values, 2 marks for the final BVA table.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, ESE]** > **(a)** A library management module accepts a *book ID* that must be a **6-digit numeric string** in the range **`200000` to `299999`** (both inclusive). The first digit must always be `2`. > - **(i)** Identify **all equivalence classes** and provide **representative test data** for each. **(7 Marks)** > - **(ii)** Derive the **BVA test cases** and discuss why BVA complements ECP. **(7 Marks)** #### Part (a)(i) — ECP Solution (7 Marks) | Class ID | Class Type | Condition Description | Representative | |---|---|---|---| | C1 | Valid | 6-digit, starts with `2`, $200000 \leq \text{ID} \leq 299999$ | `250000` | | C2 | Invalid | Numeric ID below 200000 | `199999` | | C3 | Invalid | Numeric ID above 299999 | `300000` | | C4 | Invalid | First digit not `2` (e.g., starts with 1 or 3) | `350000` | | C5 | Invalid | Contains non-numeric characters | `2A0000` | | C6 | Invalid | Length is not exactly 6 digits | `25000`, `2500000` | | C7 | Invalid | Empty input | `""` | *(7 marks: 3 marks for correctly identifying the 7 classes, 2 marks for representative values, 2 marks for the test case set.)* #### Part (a)(ii) — BVA Solution (7 Marks) | TC ID | Book ID | Boundary | Position | Expected | |---|---|---|---|---| | BVA-01 | `199999` | Lower | Off-below | Reject: out of range | | BVA-02 | `200000` | Lower | On-point (valid) | Accept | | BVA-03 | `200001` | Lower | Off-above (valid) | Accept | | BVA-04 | `299998` | Upper | Off-below (valid) | Accept | | BVA-05 | `299999` | Upper | On-point (valid) | Accept | | BVA-06 | `300000` | Upper | Off-above | Reject: out of range | **Why BVA complements ECP (3 marks embedded in part (ii)):** - ECP **identifies the partitions** and ensures we have at least one test case per *class* — but it does not guarantee that the *boundary* itself is rigorously tested. - BVA **drills into the edges** of the partitions identified by ECP and verifies the on-point and off-point behavior, which is exactly where off-by-one defects typically manifest. - The two techniques are **not redundant**: ECP catches defects scattered through the domain (e.g., non-numeric, empty input), while BVA catches defects at the precise edges (e.g., `>=` vs `>`, off-by-one in loop counters). - An integrated test suite that combines both provides **maximum defect yield with minimum test case count** — the gold standard in specification-based testing. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students most commonly lose marks in ECP/BVA questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the "invalid" classes** — students list only the valid class and skip the "below" and "above" invalid classes. **Always** list at least 1 valid + 2 invalid classes for any range. > 2. **Confusing on-point vs. off-point** — the on-point is the boundary value itself (valid). Off-points are the values **just above and just below** the boundary. Writing `1000` as a valid test case is a sure way to lose 2 marks. > 3. **Omitting representatives** — KTU examiners expect **one concrete representative value per class**. Writing "any number in the range" is not acceptable. > 4. **Skipping the integrated justification** — in 14-mark questions, the final 2–3 marks are usually reserved for an **explicit discussion** of *why* ECP and BVA are combined in practice. Do not end your answer with just a table. > 5. **Forgetting output boundaries** — examiners occasionally give a specification with a **range on the output** (e.g., "the system must display between 1 and 100 results"). Many students ignore this. Always scan the specification for output constraints. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — ECP and BVA (Module 3)** - **Black-Box Testing** examines *functionality only*; no knowledge of internal code is required. - **ECP** = *partition* the input domain into valid and invalid classes; test **one representative per class**. - **BVA** = test values **at, just above, and just below** every boundary. - A **range** (e.g., 1–100) yields **1 valid EC + 2 invalid ECs** in ECP, and **6 BVA test cases** in 3-Value BVA. - A **discrete value set** (e.g., {A, B, C}) yields **N valid ECs + 1 invalid EC** in ECP, and tests focus on the **first, last, and the value just before/after** the set in BVA. - A **boolean condition** yields **2 ECs** (true, false) in ECP. - **3-Value BVA** generates `3n` tests for `n` boundary variables; **Robust BVA** extends this to `6n` by adding extreme invalid values. - **Off-by-one errors** are the most common defect type detected by BVA — remember the empirical **2-to-1 boundary-vs-interior defect ratio**. - Always **combine ECP + BVA** for an integrated, high-yield test suite. - Always **state the expected outcome** for every test case in your answer — never just list inputs. - **Output boundaries are real boundaries** — scan specifications for them. - ECP and BVA are **specification-based** (also called *behavioral* or *functional*) — they are distinct from white-box techniques like statement, branch, or path coverage. > [!TIP] > **One-line KTU Viva Answer:** *"ECP and BVA are specification-driven, black-box test design techniques where ECP minimizes test cases by partitioning the input domain, and BVA targets the on-point and off-point edges of those partitions to maximize defect detection at boundaries."*

topics.title: White-Box Testing - Control Flow Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** White-Box Testing - Control Flow Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **White-Box Testing (Structural / Glass-Box Testing):** A software testing methodology in which the internal structure, design, coding architecture, and control logic of the software under test (SUT) are known to and exercised by the tester. The test cases are derived from the *program source code*, *control flow*, and *data flow* paths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Flow Graph (CFG):** A directed graphical representation $\mathcal{G} = (N, E)$ where:
> - $N$ = set of **nodes** (representing program statements, basic blocks, or decision predicates)
> - $E$ = set of **directed edges** (representing the possible flow of control from one node to another)
> - Every edge connects exactly two nodes and indicates a transfer of program control.

A CFG abstracts away the syntactic details of source code (loops, branches, sequences) into a topological map of execution paths.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Metro Map of Code"**
> Imagine a city's metro/subway network map:
> - **Stations** = Program statements (nodes).
> - **Train tracks** = Direction of execution flow (edges).
> - **Junction stations** (where tracks split) = Decision points like `if`, `switch`, `while`, `for`.
> - **Loop lines** = Iterative constructs (`while`, `do-while`, `for`).
> - **Terminus stations** = `START` and `END` nodes.
>
> A **Control Flow Graph is the metro map of a program**. A white-box tester is like a metro inspector who, knowing every track and switch, plans routes (test cases) that cover *every junction at least once*, *every track at least once*, and ideally *every unique route*. White-box testing is therefore *path-aware*, whereas black-box testing is *blind to the tracks*.

## 1.3 Why CFG Matters in HCI Design

In Human–Computer Interaction engineering, the **controller logic** that drives a UI (event handlers, gesture interpreters, state machines) is software. Bugs in this controller logic cause:
- Buttons that never trigger.
- Gestures that freeze the UI.
- Accessibility flows that skip critical screen-reader announcements.

CFG-based white-box testing allows the HCI engineer to **mathematically guarantee** minimum coverage of interaction logic before usability testing even begins.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Node** | Statement / basic block in the program |
| **Edge** | Directed arrow showing control transfer |
| **Process Node** | Sequential statement (rectangular) |
| **Decision / Predicate Node** | Branching statement (diamond) |
| **Junction Node** | Convergence point of multiple edges |
| **Region** | Bounded area enclosed by edges and nodes |
| **Independent Path** | A path through the CFG introducing at least one new edge |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G)$** | Quantitative measure of logical complexity |
| **Basic Path Set** | Minimum set of independent paths that covers all edges |
| **McCabe Basis Set** | Set of $V(G)$ linearly independent paths |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction Rules for a Control Flow Graph

The conversion of source code into a CFG follows strict rules. The KTU examiner frequently asks these as 3-mark short-answer questions:

> [!NOTE]
> **CFG Construction Rules**
> 1. **Sequence of statements** → Chain of process nodes (single in-degree, single out-degree).
> 2. **`if-then`** → Predicate node with `True` and `False` outgoing edges.
> 3. **`if-then-else`** → Predicate node with two outgoing edges that **rejoin** at a junction node.
> 4. **`while` loop** → Predicate node with a backward edge forming a cycle.
> 5. **`for` loop** → Same as `while` after expansion of initialization/increment.
> 6. **`switch-case`** → Predicate node with $k$ outgoing edges (one per case).
> 7. **`break / continue`** → Unconditional jumps represented as single edges.
> 8. **Function calls** → Often abstracted into a single process node (intraprocedural CFG).

## 2.2 Logical Flow of White-Box Test Design

The end-to-end logic a tester follows when using CFGs:

- **Step 1 — Source Acquisition:** Obtain the source code/module under test.
- **Step 2 — CFG Drawing:** Convert source code to a CFG following the rules in §2.1.
- **Step 3 — Complexity Calculation:** Compute $V(G)$ using one of the three canonical formulas.
- **Step 4 — Independent Path Identification:** Enumerate $V(G)$ linearly independent paths (the **McCabe basis set**).
- **Step 5 — Test Case Generation:** Design at least one test input for each independent path.
- **Step 6 — Execution & Coverage Report:** Execute tests, verify expected vs actual outputs, and compute coverage metrics.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam ready**. Memorize the equivalence of the three forms.

| # | Formula Name | Expression | Components |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Edge–Node Formula** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components (always 1 for a single program) |
| 2 | **Predicate Formula** | $V(G) = \pi + 1$ | $\pi$ = number of predicate (decision) nodes |
| 3 | **Region Formula** | $V(G) = R$ | $R$ = number of bounded regions including the outer region |
| 4 | **Independent Paths** | $P_{\text{ind}} = V(G)$ | Minimum number of test cases for path coverage |
| 5 | **Statement Coverage** | $C_s = \dfrac{S_{\text{exec}}}{S_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Executed statements / total statements |
| 6 | **Branch Coverage** | $C_b = \dfrac{B_{\text{exec}}}{B_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Executed branches / total branches |
| 7 | **Path Coverage** | $C_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{exec}}}{P_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Executed paths / total paths |

> [!NOTE]
> **Coverage Hierarchy (Weakest → Strongest):**
> $$\text{Statement Coverage} \;\subset\; \text{Branch Coverage} \;\subset\; \text{Condition Coverage} \;\subset\; \text{Path Coverage}$$
> Achieving **100% path coverage** implies 100% branch and statement coverage, but it is generally **infeasible** for loops with unbounded iterations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics / Automotive (ISO 26262, DO-178C):** Mandatory $V(G) \le 10$ per function — CFG used as audit artifact.
- **Medical Devices (FDA IEC 62304):** White-box testing required for Software of Unknown Provenance (SOUP).
- **HCI Event Handlers:** CFG visualizes every UI state transition, ensuring *no dead states* (unreachable screens) and *no sink states* (trapped dialogs).
- **Compiler Design:** CFG is the foundation of every optimization (dead-code elimination, loop unrolling, register allocation).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Pseudocode to CFG

Consider the following Java-like method that validates a password for a login UI in an HCI application:

```text
1   public String validateLogin(String user, String pwd) {
2       int attempts = 0;
3       boolean valid = false;
4       while (attempts < 3 && !valid) {
5           if (user.equals("admin") && pwd.equals("1234")) {
6               valid = true;
7               System.out.println("Welcome");
8           } else {
9               attempts++;
10              System.out.println("Try again");
11          }
12      }
13      if (!valid) {
14          System.out.println("Account locked");
15      }
16      return valid ? "OK" : "FAIL";
17  }
```

### Step 3.1.1 — Node Decomposition

We assign a node number to every statement and predicate:

| Node | Type | Meaning |
|------|------|---------|
| 1 | Process | `int attempts = 0` |
| 2 | Process | `boolean valid = false` |
| 3 | Predicate | `attempts < 3 && !valid` (while) |
| 4 | Predicate | `user.equals("admin") && pwd.equals("1234")` (if) |
| 5 | Process | `valid = true` |
| 6 | Process | `System.out.println("Welcome")` |
| 7 | Process | `attempts++` |
| 8 | Process | `System.out.println("Try again")` |
| 9 | Predicate | `!valid` (if) |
| 10 | Process | `System.out.println("Account locked")` |
| 11 | Process | `return ...` |
| 12 | Process | END |

Total nodes $N = 12$.

### Step 3.1.2 — Edge Enumeration

List every directed edge (u, v):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E = \{ & (1,2),\;(2,3),\;(3,4),\;(3,9),\;(4,5),\;(4,7), \\
       & (5,6),\;(6,3),\;(7,8),\;(8,3),\;(9,10),\;(9,11), \\
       & (10,11),\;(11,12) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

Counting: $|E| = 14$ edges.

Connected components $P = 1$.

### Step 3.1.3 — Cyclomatic Complexity via Edge–Node Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\[4pt]
     &= 14 - 12 + 2(1) \\[4pt]
     &= 14 - 12 + 2 \\[4pt]
     &= \boxed{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3.1.4 — Cross-Verification using Predicate Formula

Predicate nodes are: 3 (while), 4 (if), 9 (if). So $\pi = 3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= \pi + 1 \\[4pt]
     &= 3 + 1 \\[4pt]
     &= \boxed{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both formulas give $V(G) = 4$, confirming our calculation. In the KTU exam, the examiner cross-checks; consistency is mandatory for full marks.

### Step 3.1.5 — Cross-Verification using Region Formula

Sketching the CFG (see Section 4) yields:
- **Region R1:** Inside the `while` loop body, on the `true` branch of the if-then-else.
- **Region R2:** Inside the `while` loop body, on the `false` branch.
- **Region R3:** Enclosed by the `while` loop's backward edge.
- **Region R4:** The unbounded outer region.

$$
R = 4 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V(G) = R = 4 \;\checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the McCabe Basis Set

Independent paths are obtained by traversing the CFG such that each new path introduces at least one **new edge** not previously traversed. For our example:

| Path # | Traversal Sequence | Description |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|
| **P1** | $1 \to 2 \to 3 \xrightarrow{F} 9 \xrightarrow{T} 10 \to 11 \to 12$ | Loop body never entered; account locked immediately |
| **P2** | $1 \to 2 \to 3 \xrightarrow{T} 4 \xrightarrow{T} 5 \to 6 \to 3 \xrightarrow{F} 9 \xrightarrow{F} 11 \to 12$ | First attempt succeeds; login OK |
| **P3** | $1 \to 2 \to 3 \xrightarrow{T} 4 \xrightarrow{F} 7 \to 8 \to 3 \xrightarrow{T} 4 \xrightarrow{T} 5 \to 6 \to 3 \xrightarrow{F} 9 \xrightarrow{F} 11 \to 12$ | One failure, then success on second attempt |
| **P4** | $1 \to 2 \to 3 \xrightarrow{T} 4 \xrightarrow{F} 7 \to 8 \to 3 \xrightarrow{T} 4 \xrightarrow{F} 7 \to 8 \to 3 \xrightarrow{T} 4 \xrightarrow{F} 7 \to 8 \to 3 \xrightarrow{F} 9 \xrightarrow{T} 10 \to 11 \to 12$ | Three consecutive failures; account locked |

Four paths ≡ $V(G) = 4$. This set is the **McCabe basis set** — minimum complete path coverage.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Path | Statement Coverage | Branch Coverage | New Edges Introduced |
|------|-------------------:|----------------:|--------------------:|
| P1 | 33% | 50% | 5 |
| P2 | 67% | 75% | 4 |
| P3 | 92% | 92% | 4 |
| P4 | 100% | 100% | 1 |
| **Cumulative** | **100%** | **100%** | **14 (= E)** |

After executing all four independent paths, every node and every edge of the CFG has been traversed at least once.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Trap — The "Compound Predicate" Pitfall

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A line like `if (a && b)` is **ONE predicate node** in the CFG, but it is **TWO logical conditions**. Branch coverage tests only `T/F` outcomes of the *combined* expression. To test the individual sub-conditions `a` and `b`, you need **condition coverage** or **modified condition/decision coverage (MC/DC)** — which is a stronger criterion not guaranteed by $V(G)$ alone.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control Flow Graph (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram renders the CFG derived in Section 3.1. Nodes prefixed with letters to comply with the alphanumeric identifier rule.

```mermaid
graph TD
    START([START]) --> n1[1 attempts = 0]
    n1 --> n2[2 valid = false]
    n2 --> n3{3 attempts less than 3 AND NOT valid}
    n3 -- TRUE --> n4{4 user equals admin AND pwd equals 1234}
    n3 -- FALSE --> n9{9 NOT valid}
    n4 -- TRUE --> n5[5 valid = TRUE]
    n4 -- FALSE --> n7[7 attempts increment]
    n5 --> n6[6 print Welcome]
    n6 --> n3
    n7 --> n8[8 print Try again]
    n8 --> n3
    n9 -- TRUE --> n10[10 print Account locked]
    n9 -- FALSE --> n11[11 return result]
    n10 --> n11
    n11 --> ENDNODE([12 END])
```

### Diagram Element Reference Table

| Mermaid Element | CFG Meaning |
|-----------------|-------------|
| `(...)`  | Terminator (START / END) — process node |
| `[...]`  | Process node (rectangle) |
| `{...}`  | Predicate / decision node (diamond) |
| `-- LABEL -->` | Directed edge with branch label |

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — White-Box Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Code Module] --> B[Lexical Analyzer]
    B --> C[CFG Constructor]
    C --> D[Metric Engine E N P]
    D --> E[Cyclomatic Complexity V of G]
    E --> F[Path Enumeration Module]
    F --> G[McCabe Basis Set]
    G --> H[Test Case Designer]
    H --> I[Test Harness Execution]
    I --> J[Coverage Analyzer]
    J --> K[Defect Report]
    J --> L[Coverage Report Statement Branch Path]
    L --> M{Meets Threshold}
    M -- NO --> C
    M -- YES --> N[Release Sign-off]
```

## 4.3 Coverage Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
graph BT
    P[Path Coverage 100 percent] --> C[Condition Coverage]
    C --> B[Branch Coverage]
    B --> S[Statement Coverage]
    S --> X[No Coverage]
    style P fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#c0c0c0,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#cd7f32,stroke:#333
    style S fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
    style X fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#333
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Control Flow Graph (CFG). With a neat diagram, illustrate the CFG representation of an `if-then-else` statement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** is a directed graphical model of a program where nodes represent statements/basic blocks and edges represent the flow of control between them.

For an `if-then-else` construct:

$$
\text{if (C) then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2
$$

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Process Before] --> D{C Predicate}
    D -- TRUE --> S1[Process S1 THEN branch]
    D -- FALSE --> S2[Process S2 ELSE branch]
    S1 --> J[Junction Node]
    S2 --> J
    J --> Q[Process After]
```

The predicate node `C` has two outgoing edges labelled `TRUE` and `FALSE` that rejoin at the junction node. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark diagram + 1 mark label explanation)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State any three formulas for computing McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Formulas (1 mark each):**
> 1. $V(G) = E - N + 2P$, where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components.
> 2. $V(G) = \pi + 1$, where $\pi$ = number of predicate (decision) nodes.
> 3. $V(G) = R$, where $R$ = number of regions enclosed by the graph.

These three expressions are mathematically equivalent for a single connected program.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU ESE, Module 3 contains at least one 14-mark question. Below are two alternative full-length questions; the examiner picks ONE.

---

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **step-by-step procedure** for deriving a Control Flow Graph from program source code. List the construction rules for `sequence`, `if-then-else`, `while-loop`, and `switch-case` constructs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G)$**. Compute $V(G)$ for the program segment shown below using **all three formulas** and verify consistency. Identify the **independent paths** (McCabe basis set) and design one test case per path. **(7 marks)**

```text
1  int x, y, z;
2  read(x, y);
3  if (x > 0 && y > 0) {
4      z = x + y;
5      print("Sum is positive");
6  } else {
7      if (x == 0) {
8          print("x is zero");
9      } else {
10         print("x is negative");
11     }
12 }
13 print(z);
```

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The CFG construction procedure is:

**Step 1 — Statement Tokenization:** Break the program into atomic statements and predicates. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Node Assignment:** Assign a unique numeric ID to each atomic element. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Edge Wiring:** Apply the rules in §2.1:

| Construct | CFG Pattern |
|-----------|-------------|
| Sequence | $n_1 \to n_2 \to n_3$ linear chain |
| `if-then-else` | Predicate node splits into TRUE/FALSE edges, rejoining at a junction |
| `while` | Predicate node with a backward edge to itself |
| `switch-case` | Predicate node with $k$ outgoing edges (one per case label) |

*(3 marks for the rule table + 1 mark for a worked example diagram)*

**Step 4 — Validation:** Ensure every node (except START/END) has at least one incoming and one outgoing edge; the graph must be connected.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Node enumeration:**

| Node | Type |
|------|------|
| 1 | `read(x,y)` |
| 2 | Predicate: `x>0 && y>0` |
| 3 | `z = x+y` |
| 4 | `print("Sum is positive")` |
| 5 | Predicate: `x==0` |
| 6 | `print("x is zero")` |
| 7 | `print("x is negative")` |
| 8 | `print(z)` |

So $N = 8$, $P = 1$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Edge enumeration:**

Edges: $(1,2),\;(2,3),\;(2,5),\;(3,4),\;(4,8),\;(5,6),\;(5,7),\;(6,8),\;(7,8)$.
Hence $E = 9$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Formula 1 (Edge–Node):**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P = 9 - 8 + 2 = \mathbf{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Formula 2 (Predicate):**

Predicate nodes: 2 and 5. So $\pi = 2$.

$$
V(G) = \pi + 1 = 2 + 1 = \mathbf{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Formula 3 (Regions):**

The graph contains: Region inside the outer `if-else`, Region inside the inner `if-else`, Outer region. So $R = 3$, and $V(G) = R = 3$. *(2 marks for all three consistent calculations)*

**Step 6 — Independent Paths:**

| Path | Sequence | Test Input |
|------|----------|------------|
| P1 | $1 \to 2_T \to 3 \to 4 \to 8$ | $x=5, y=7$ |
| P2 | $1 \to 2_F \to 5_T \to 6 \to 8$ | $x=0, y=1$ |
| P3 | $1 \to 2_F \to 5_F \to 7 \to 8$ | $x=-3, y=1$ |

*(2 marks for the McCabe basis set + 1 mark for sample test cases)*

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (14 Marks) — Alternative

**(a)** Differentiate between **White-Box Testing** and **Black-Box Testing**. List four white-box testing techniques. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the program given below, draw the CFG, compute $V(G)$, and determine the McCabe basis set of independent paths. **(7 marks)**

```text
1  int n = read();
2  int sum = 0;
3  int i = 1;
4  while (i <= n) {
5      if (i % 2 == 0) {
6          sum = sum + i;
7      }
8      i++;
9  }
10 print(sum);
```

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

| Aspect | White-Box | Black-Box |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Knowledge of internals | Required | Not required |
| Basis for test cases | Source code, CFG, data flow | Requirements, specifications |
| Performed by | Developers / white-box testers | Independent testers / end users |
| Detects | Logic errors, dead code, unreachable branches | Missing functionality, wrong outputs |
| Coverage metric | Statement, branch, path | Equivalence partitioning, boundary value |

*(4 marks for differentiation table)*

**Four white-box techniques:** *(3 marks)*
1. Statement coverage testing
2. Branch / decision coverage testing
3. Path coverage / basic path testing (McCabe)
4. Data flow testing (definition-use testing)

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Nodes (N = 9):**

| Node | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 1 | `int n = read()` |
| 2 | `int sum = 0` |
| 3 | `int i = 1` |
| 4 | Predicate: `i <= n` |
| 5 | Predicate: `i % 2 == 0` |
| 6 | `sum = sum + i` |
