- **Module 2**: Patient Registration Form
- **Module 3**: Doctor Scheduling Panel
- **Module 4**: Database Access Layer

Apply the **Sandwich Strategy** step-by-step and clearly state which **stubs** and **drivers** will be created and at which integration step. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Sandwich (Hybrid) Integration Testing Strategy is a combination of Top-Down and Bottom-Up approaches. The system is conceptually divided into three layers — Top, Middle, and Bottom. The Top layer is tested using Top-Down with stubs, the Bottom layer is tested using Bottom-Up with drivers, and they meet at the Middle layer, which is the final integration point.

**Working in a 3-tier HCI system (2 marks):**
- **Top Layer (UI/Controller):** Tested top-down. Stubs replace the Middle layer so the UI can be validated for navigation, layout, and task flow.
- **Middle Layer (Business Logic):** Acts as the *seam* where the two integration halves meet. It is tested last by joining the real Top and Bottom layers.
- **Bottom Layer (Data/Sensors):** Tested bottom-up. Drivers replace the Middle layer so database/sensor modules can be stress-tested.

**Four Advantages (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. Parallel development of UI and backend teams.
2. Early detection of both UI-level and data-level defects.
3. Balanced — does not require the entire system to be ready.
4. Suitable for large HCI products with distributed teams.

**Two Disadvantages (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **High Cost** — Both stubs and drivers must be written, doubling the test-double development effort.
2. **Complex Coordination** — The Middle layer is the critical bottleneck; any defect there blocks both teams, causing an integration **convergence delay**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (1 mark per correct step):**

| Step | Action | Stubs Created | Drivers Created | Modules Now Real |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Top-Down begins at Top Layer | Stub_M2 (for Patient Reg), Stub_M3 (for Doctor Sched) | None | Module 1 only |
| 2 | Top-Down proceeds downward | Stub_M4 (for DB) | None | Modules 1, 2, 3 |
| 3 | Bottom-Up begins at Bottom Layer | None | Driver_M2 (calls Module 2) | Modules 1, 2, 3, 4 |
| 4 | Bottom-Up reaches middle | None | Driver_M3 (calls Module 3) | All four real, but separate trees |
| 5 | Final convergence at Middle Layer | All stubs removed | All drivers removed | Fully integrated |

**Convergence Verification (3 marks):**
The four coverage conditions are checked:
$$
\text{Inter-module Interface Coverage} = \frac{4 \text{ interfaces tested}}{4 \text{ total interfaces}} \times 100 = 100\%
$$
Since $100\% \geq 85\%$ (the KTU threshold), all test doubles may be removed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Question on Comparative Analysis**

`[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

**a)** Compare **Top-Down Integration with Stubs** and **Bottom-Up Integration with Drivers** under the following heads: direction of testing, critical defects caught early, complexity of the test double, and suitability for HCI systems. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A team is building a Smart-Watch Health-Monitor HCI app. The UI module (Heart Rate Display) is ready, but the **Bluetooth sensor module is still in development**. The sensor-firmware team has the sensor code ready but has not yet built the Display UI module. Describe which **integration strategy** the two teams should each adopt, what **test double** they will build, and justify the choice using a labelled diagram. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks (1.75 marks per head):**

| Head | Top-Down + Stubs | Bottom-Up + Drivers |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of testing** | Root (Main Controller) → Leaves | Leaves (Utilities) → Root |
| **Critical defects caught early** | Navigation flow, screen transitions, user-task sequencing, menu hierarchies | Data validation, sensor accuracy, signal-processing algorithms, performance under load |
| **Complexity of the test double** | Low — stub just returns hard-coded data; no input logic needed | Moderate — driver must format realistic input vectors and parse outputs |
| **Suitability for HCI** | High for pure-UI products (mobile apps, web dashboards) | High for embedded/IoT HMI (wearables, automotive clusters) |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**UI Team (3 marks):**
- **Strategy:** Top-Down Integration.
- **Test Double:** A **STUB** for the Bluetooth sensor module. The stub will return hard-coded heart-rate values such as `{bpm: 72, timestamp: "10:32:05"}` whenever the UI calls `getHeartRate()`.
- **Justification:** The UI is the calling module; the sensor is the missing callee. The stub allows the UI to be visually validated on a designer’s workstation without requiring the physical sensor.

**Firmware Team (3 marks):**
- **Strategy:** Bottom-Up Integration.
- **Test Double:** A **DRIVER** for the Display UI module. The driver invokes the sensor module as if it were a real UI element receiving a Bluetooth packet.
- **Justification:** The sensor is the called module; the UI is the missing caller. The driver allows the sensor to be calibrated and stress-tested in a hardware-in-the-loop rig.

**Labelled Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UITeam["UI TEAM - Top-Down"]
        UI[Module UI: Heart Rate Display - REAL]
        UI --> Stub[Stub: Bluetooth Sensor - returns 72 bpm]
    end
    subgraph FWTeam["FIRMWARE TEAM - Bottom-Up"]
        Driver[Driver: Display UI - calls sensor with fake packets]
        Driver --> Sensor[Module Sensor: Bluetooth - REAL]
    end
    style UI fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style Stub fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38600,color:#000
    style Driver fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    style Sensor fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> Examiners *deliberately* award **only 1.5 marks** (out of 7) if a student:
> 1. Writes "Stub" and "Driver" as *synonyms* — they are **opposites** in direction of call.
> 2. Describes stubs/drivers as part of *Unit Testing*. They are strictly **Integration Testing** tools.
> 3. Sketches a diagram without **labelling which side is real and which is the test double**. Always annotate with colors or legends.
> 4. Forgets to mention the **85% coverage threshold** rule before removing the test doubles.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stubs and Drivers**

- ⭐ **Stub = Top-Down.** It is **called by** the module under test. It mimics a **lower-level (callee)** module.
- ⭐ **Driver = Bottom-Up.** It **calls** the module under test. It mimics a **higher-level (caller)** module.
- ⭐ Both are used in **Integration Testing**, NEVER in Unit Testing.
- ⭐ Both are part of the **Test Doubles** family (along with Mocks, Fakes, Dummies).
- ⭐ **Sandwich (Hybrid) Strategy** = Top-Down (stubs) + Bottom-Up (drivers), meeting at the Middle Layer.
- ⭐ A stub is **simpler** to write (just return data) than a driver (must craft valid inputs).
- ⭐ **Industry use cases:** Automotive HMI (clusters), Wearable HCI (smartwatches), Hospital HCI, and Mobile/Web apps — all use stubs/drivers for parallel team development.
- ⭐ **Removal rule:** Test doubles are removed only when **inter-module interface coverage ≥ 85%** (KTU 2024 standard).
- ⭐ Always state the **direction of integration** along with the test-double name in the answer.
- ⭐ **Sandwich Strategy is the most-marked topic** for 14-mark Part B questions in the KTU 2024 ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stages of Testing - Alpha Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stages of Testing - Alpha Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alpha Testing** is the **first stage of in-house usability and acceptance testing** performed in a controlled, simulated environment at the *developer's site* by a selected group of internal testers, before the software is released to a limited external audience (Beta Testing).

In the context of **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)**, Alpha Testing is treated as the *first user-experience validation gate* in the iterative design cycle. It is executed on the developer's premises (or via a controlled remote lab) by an internal team — typically consisting of:
- QA Engineers
- Internal Test Users (often employees from other departments acting as surrogate users)
- A subset of the actual **target user population** (recruited ethically under NDAs)
- Usability Specialists / HCI Analysts

The objective is to discover **latent bugs, missing functional requirements, and usability defects** using techniques like *think-aloud protocols*, *heuristic evaluation*, and *task-based scenario testing*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rehearsal Before the Premiere"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a chef (developer) who has just created a new dish. Before serving it to paying customers in the restaurant (Beta Testing), the chef invites a small panel of trusted friends, fellow chefs, and food critics into the kitchen to **taste the dish at the chef's own kitchen counter**. They critique the flavor, texture, plating, and presentation.

In this analogy:
- **The Kitchen** = Developer's lab (controlled environment)
- **The Tasting Panel** = Internal testers + selected representative users
- **The Public Restaurant** = Real end-users (Beta Test phase)
- **The Chef's Tasting Counter** = The Alpha testing environment

Just as the chef wants to refine the dish *before* customers complain, Alpha Testing refines the interface *before* it reaches the public Beta phase.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Used in Alpha Testing

The following are the most commonly tracked **quantitative metrics** in Alpha Testing for HCI:

- **Defect Density (DD)** = Number of Defects / Size of Module
- **Test Coverage (TC)** = (Number of Test Cases Executed / Total Number of Test Cases) × 100
- **Mean Time to Failure (MTTF)** — for stability assessment
- **System Usability Scale (SUS)** score — ranges from **0 to 100**
- **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** = (Tasks Completed / Tasks Attempted) × 100
- **Time-on-Task (ToT)** measured in **seconds**
- **Error Rate (ER)** = (Errors Committed / Total Opportunities for Error) × 100

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Alpha Testing is **NOT** performed by external customers. It is the *only* testing stage that occurs *entirely within the developer's controlled environment*, making it the first-line defense for catching design defects.

### 1.4 Position of Alpha Testing in the Testing Lifecycle

Alpha Testing sits **after** integration and system testing, but **before** Beta Testing and acceptance testing. It is typically the **last internal stage** in the waterfall-style HCI development model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Alpha Testing — Step-by-Step

The Alpha Testing process follows a structured, iterative pipeline:

1. **Test Planning Phase**
   - Define test objectives aligned with usability goals (efficiency, learnability, error tolerance, satisfaction).
   - Identify the target user profile (age, expertise, accessibility needs).
   - Prepare a **Test Plan Document** listing test scenarios, success criteria, and metrics.

2. **Test Environment Setup**
   - Replicate the production environment in a controlled lab.
   - Install hardware peripherals, configure OS, network, and accessibility tools.
   - Ensure screen recording and logging tools (e.g., OBS, Morae) are operational.

3. **Recruitment of Internal Test Users**
   - Typically **6 to 10** internal users (per Nielsen's heuristic that 5 users uncover ~85% of usability issues).
   - Mix of domain experts and novice users to capture varied interaction patterns.

4. **Execution of Test Sessions**
   - Each user performs a set of representative tasks.
   - Observers use the **Think-Aloud Protocol** to capture cognitive load and confusion points.
   - All sessions are recorded (audio, video, screen capture).

5. **Defect Logging and Severity Classification**
   - Bugs are categorized as: **Critical (S1)**, **Major (S2)**, **Minor (S3)**, **Cosmetic (S4)**.
   - Each defect is assigned a **Defect ID**, a reproduction script, and a screenshot/log.

6. **Iteration and Regression**
   - The development team fixes identified defects.
   - Regression Alpha Testing is performed to ensure fixes do not introduce new issues.

7. **Exit Criteria Validation**
   - Alpha Testing ends when **all S1 and S2 defects are closed**, and agreed SUS/TCR thresholds are met.

### 2.2 Key HCI Usability Metrics — The "Why" Behind Them

| Metric | What It Captures | Why It Matters in Alpha Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **SUS (System Usability Scale)** | Subjective user satisfaction | Quantifies user perception in 10 Likert-scale questions |
| **Task Completion Rate** | Functional success | Reveals interface learnability |
| **Time-on-Task** | Efficiency | Highlights cognitive friction |
| **Error Rate** | User mistakes | Identifies poorly designed affordances |
| **Learnability Index** | Improvement over repeated trials | Validates onboarding design |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all *mathematically verifiable* formulas used in Alpha Testing analysis. For KTU 2024 Scheme exams, focus on the first three as they are most frequently tested.

| # | Formula Name | LaTeX Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{N_d}{S}$ | $N_d$ = defects, $S$ = module size (KLOC or FP) |
| 2 | **Test Coverage (TC)** | $TC = \dfrac{T_{exe}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a **percentage (%)** |
| 3 | **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** | $TCR = \dfrac{T_{comp}}{T_{att}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a **percentage (%)** |
| 4 | **Error Rate (ER)** | $ER = \dfrac{E_{committed}}{E_{opportunities}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a **percentage (%)** |
| 5 | **Reliability (R(t))** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate, $t$ = time in hours |
| 6 | **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Measured in **hours** |
| 7 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = MTTR + MTTF$ | $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair |
| 8 | **Nielsen's Sample Adequacy** | $N' = N \cdot \left(1 - \left(1 - p\right)^i\right)$ | $p$ = prob. of finding a problem; $i$ = iteration |
| 9 | **Average Severity Index (ASI)** | $ASI = \dfrac{\sum (S_i \cdot f_i)}{N_d}$ | Weighted severity score |
| 10 | **Cost of Defect (CoD)** | $CoD = C_{detect} + C_{fix} + C_{rework}$ | Monetary unit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics feed into the *Usability Engineering Lifecycle* as defined by **Deborah Mayhew** and align with **ISO 9241-11** (Efficiency, Effectiveness, Satisfaction).

### 2.4 Why Alpha Testing Fails Without Proper Planning

Common pitfalls that lead to ineffective Alpha Testing:
- **Sampling Bias** — using only developers (who already know the system) as testers
- **Hawthorne Effect** — users behave differently when observed
- **Skipping Edge Cases** — focusing only on happy-path scenarios
- **No Quantitative Baselines** — purely qualitative feedback without measurable metrics

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Calculating Usability Metrics from Alpha Test Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Scenario (KTU Exam Style):** During an Alpha Testing phase of a mobile banking HCI prototype, **8 internal users** were observed. The test plan involved **5 representative tasks** per user. The collected data is as follows:
>
> - Total tasks attempted: $8 \times 5 = 40$
> - Tasks completed successfully: 32
> - Total opportunities for error: 120
> - Errors committed: 18
> - Defects logged: 6 (2 Critical, 2 Major, 2 Minor)
> - Module size: **3 KLOC** (Thousand Lines of Code)
> - Total time spent on testing: 48 hours
> - System failed 4 times during testing

**Find:**
1. Task Completion Rate (TCR)
2. Error Rate (ER)
3. Defect Density (DD)
4. Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF), assuming MTTR = 2 hours
5. Average Severity Index (ASI), assuming S1=4, S2=3, S3=2, S4=1

---

#### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate Task Completion Rate (TCR)**

$$
TCR = \dfrac{T_{comp}}{T_{att}} \times 100
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
TCR = \dfrac{32}{40} \times 100
$$

$$
TCR = 0.8 \times 100
$$

$$
TCR = 80\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** An 80% completion rate indicates the interface has acceptable but improvable learnability. A threshold of **≥ 85%** is typically required before exiting Alpha Testing.

---

**Step 2: Calculate Error Rate (ER)**

$$
ER = \dfrac{E_{committed}}{E_{opportunities}} \times 100
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
ER = \dfrac{18}{120} \times 100
$$

$$
ER = 0.15 \times 100
$$

$$
ER = 15\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 15% error rate suggests the interface has some confusing affordances, possibly due to poor iconography or misleading labels.

---

**Step 3: Calculate Defect Density (DD)**

$$
DD = \dfrac{N_d}{S}
$$

Where $N_d = 6$ defects and $S = 3$ KLOC.

$$
DD = \dfrac{6}{3}
$$

$$
DD = 2.0 \text{ defects per KLOC}
$$

> **Industry Benchmark (per IEEE 982.1):** A well-tested module should have $DD \leq 1$ defect per KLOC. The current value (2.0) indicates the module is **not yet ready for Beta Testing**.

---

**Step 4: Calculate MTBF**

First, compute the failure rate $\lambda$:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{\text{Number of Failures}}{\text{Total Test Time}} = \dfrac{4}{48} = 0.0833 \text{ failures/hour}
$$

Then MTTF:

$$
MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{0.0833}
$$

$$
MTTF = 12 \text{ hours}
$$

Now apply the MTBF formula:

$$
MTBF = MTTR + MTTF
$$

Substitute $MTTR = 2$ hours and $MTTF = 12$ hours:

$$
MTBF = 2 + 12
$$

$$
MTBF = 14 \text{ hours}
$$

> **Interpretation:** On average, the system will run for 14 hours before encountering its next failure — a critical reliability metric for HCI systems in production.

---

**Step 5: Calculate Average Severity Index (ASI)**

$$
ASI = \dfrac{\sum (S_i \cdot f_i)}{N_d}
$$

Substitute the data:

$$
ASI = \dfrac{(4 \cdot 2) + (3 \cdot 2) + (2 \cdot 2)}{6}
$$

Compute the numerator stepwise:

$$
4 \cdot 2 = 8 \quad (\text{Critical defects})
$$
$$
3 \cdot 2 = 6 \quad (\text{Major defects})
$$
$$
2 \cdot 2 = 4 \quad (\text{Minor defects})
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 8 + 6 + 4 = 18
$$

$$
ASI = \dfrac{18}{6}
$$

$$
ASI = 3.0
$$

> **Interpretation:** An ASI of 3.0 indicates a *moderate-to-high* severity profile. The presence of 2 Critical defects would normally block progression to Beta Testing until they are resolved.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Alpha vs. Beta Testing Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison table is one of the **most frequently asked 7-mark questions** in KTU 2024 Scheme HCI exams.

| Parameter | **Alpha Testing** | **Beta Testing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Developer's site (lab) | Customer's real environment |
| **Testers** | Internal employees + selected users | Real end-users / public |
| **Environment** | Controlled, simulated | Uncontrolled, real-world |
| **Duration** | Short (days to weeks) | Longer (weeks to months) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (large user base) |
| **Defect Type Caught** | Functional + design bugs | Real-world usage bugs |
| **NDA Required** | Yes | Sometimes (in closed Beta) |
| **Goal** | Refine before limited release | Validate before mass release |
| **Observability** | High (full instrumentation) | Low (limited telemetry) |
| **Feedback Type** | Rich qualitative + quantitative | Mostly quantitative |
| **Iteration Speed** | Fast (developer available) | Slow (async with users) |

---

### 3.3 Decision Rule: When to Exit Alpha Testing

The Alpha phase is typically concluded when **all** of the following conditions are satisfied simultaneously:

$$
\text{Exit Alpha} = \left( TCR \geq 85\% \right) \land \left( N_{S1} = 0 \right) \land \left( DD \leq 1 \right) \land \left( SUS \geq 68 \right)
$$

Where:
- $N_{S1}$ = Number of Severity-1 (Critical) defects
- $SUS \geq 68$ is the *industry-accepted threshold* for "above average" usability (Bangor et al., 2009)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Alpha Testing Process Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Alpha Testing] --> B[Define Test Objectives and Metrics]
    B --> C[Recruit Internal Test Users 6 to 10]
    C --> D[Setup Controlled Lab Environment]
    D --> E[Conduct Task Based Sessions]
    E --> F[Apply Think Aloud Protocol]
    F --> G[Record Sessions Audio Video Screen]
    G --> H[Log Defects with Severity Tags]
    H --> I{Exit Criteria Met?}
    I -- No --> J[Dev Team Fixes Defects]
    J --> K[Regression Alpha Test]
    K --> E
    I -- Yes --> L[Proceed to Beta Testing]
    L --> M[End]
```

### 4.2 Alpha Testing in the Broader HCI Lifecycle (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Requirements Gathering] --> B[Design Phase]
    B --> C[Prototype Construction]
    C --> D[Unit Testing]
    D --> E[Integration Testing]
    E --> F[System Testing]
    F --> G[Alpha Testing HCI Lab]
    G --> H[Beta Testing Customer Site]
    H --> I[Acceptance Testing]
    I --> J[Final Release to Market]
    G -. Feedback to Design .-> B
    H -. Feedback to Design .-> B
```

### 4.3 Input-Process-Output Block Diagram for Alpha Testing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        I1[Prototype Interface]
        I2[Test Plan Document]
        I3[Representative Users]
        I4[Task Scenarios]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS
        P1[Observation and Logging]
        P2[Think Aloud Capture]
        P3[SUS Questionnaires]
        P4[Task Metrics Collection]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        O1[Defect Reports]
        O2[Usability Metrics]
        O3[Design Recommendations]
        O4[Go No Go Decision]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P2
    I4 --> P3
    P1 --> O1
    P2 --> O3
    P3 --> O2
    P4 --> O2
    O1 --> O4
    O2 --> O4
    O3 --> O4
```

### 4.4 Severity-Based Defect Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Defect Detected in Alpha] --> S{Severity Level}
    S -- S1 --> C1[Critical System Crash Data Loss]
    S -- S2 --> C2[Major Major Function Broken No Workaround]
    S -- S3 --> C3[Minor Minor Function Issue Workaround Exists]
    S -- S4 --> C4[Cosmetic UI Polish Issues]
    C1 --> A1[Must Fix Before Beta]
    C2 --> A2[Must Fix Before Beta]
    C3 --> A3[Can Defer to Next Release]
    C4 --> A4[Documented in Backlog]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Alpha Testing in the context of Human-Computer Interaction. List any two characteristics that differentiate it from Beta Testing.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Alpha Testing is the first stage of in-house usability and acceptance testing conducted in a controlled environment at the developer's site, performed by internal testers and selected representative users, before the software is released for external Beta Testing.

**Two distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **Location and Control:** Alpha Testing is conducted in a *controlled lab* at the developer's premises, whereas Beta Testing is conducted at the *customer's real-world site*.

2. **Tester Profile:** Alpha Testing uses *internal employees and selected representative users* under NDA, whereas Beta Testing uses *actual end-users* from the public domain who may or may not be under NDA.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*What is the "Think-Aloud Protocol" and how is it used during Alpha Testing of an HCI system?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Think-Aloud Protocol is a usability evaluation method in which test users are asked to **verbalize their thoughts, decisions, and confusions** while performing tasks on the interface.

**Use in Alpha Testing:**
- It allows usability analysts to capture the **cognitive processes** of users in real time.
- It helps identify **points of confusion**, mismatches between user mental models and system behavior, and unclear affordances.
- The recorded verbal data is analyzed to derive **design improvement recommendations** before Beta release.
- It is particularly effective in Alpha Testing because the **controlled environment** allows synchronized audio, video, and screen capture.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - When answering "Alpha vs Beta" comparison questions, students commonly **forget to mention the location (controlled vs real-world)**. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> - Do **not** confuse Alpha Testing with **White-Box Testing**. Alpha is a *user-experience* test, not a code-coverage test.
> - Always cite **at least two quantitative metrics** (TCR, SUS, ER, DD) — answers without numbers are penalized under KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education rubrics.
> - Use the term "**controlled environment**" — vague terms like "office" or "company" will lose marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Explain the **stages of testing** in the HCI development lifecycle with a neat block diagram. Position **Alpha Testing** within this lifecycle and justify its importance.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* During an Alpha Test of a hospital management HCI interface, **6 internal users** were observed over **4 representative tasks each**.
- Tasks attempted: 24
- Tasks completed: 18
- Errors committed: 12 out of 96 opportunities
- Defects logged: 8 (3 S1, 2 S2, 2 S3, 1 S4)
- Module size: 4 KLOC
- Total test time: 36 hours; System failed 3 times; MTTR = 3 hours

Calculate: (i) Task Completion Rate, (ii) Error Rate, (iii) Defect Density, (iv) MTBF, and (v) Average Severity Index. Comment on whether the system is ready to exit Alpha Testing.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stages of Testing and Position of Alpha Testing:**

The HCI development lifecycle typically includes the following stages of testing:

1. **Unit Testing** — Individual components are tested by developers.
2. **Integration Testing** — Combined modules are tested for interface consistency.
3. **System Testing** — The complete system is validated against requirements.
4. **Alpha Testing** — First user-driven test in a controlled developer's environment.
5. **Beta Testing** — Limited release to real end-users in their natural environment.
6. **Acceptance Testing** — Formal sign-off by stakeholders.

**Position of Alpha Testing:** Alpha Testing occurs **after system testing** and **before beta testing**, serving as the **first user-experience validation gate**.

**Importance of Alpha Testing:**
- Catches **usability and functional defects** before customer exposure.
- Enables **rich qualitative feedback** through direct observation and think-aloud.
- Provides **quantitative baselines** (TCR, SUS, ER) for Beta comparison.
- Reduces the **cost of late-stage defect fixes** (defects are exponentially cheaper to fix in Alpha than post-release).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Task Completion Rate:**

$$
TCR = \dfrac{18}{24} \times 100 = 75\%
$$

**(ii) Error Rate:**

$$
ER = \dfrac{12}{96} \times 100 = 12.5\%
$$

**(iii) Defect Density:**

$$
DD = \dfrac{8}{4} = 2.0 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

**(iv) MTBF:**

Failure rate:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{3}{36} = 0.0833 \text{ failures/hour}
$$

MTTF:

$$
MTTF = \dfrac{1}{0.0833} = 12 \text{ hours}
$$

MTBF:

$$
MTBF = MTTR + MTTF = 3 + 12 = 15 \text{ hours}
$$

**(v) Average Severity Index:**

Severity weights: S1=4, S2=3, S3=2, S4=1

$$
ASI = \dfrac{(4 \cdot 3) + (3 \cdot 2) + (2 \cdot 2) + (1 \cdot 1)}{8}
$$

$$
ASI = \dfrac{12 + 6 + 4 + 1}{8} = \dfrac{23}{8} = 2.875
$$

**Readiness Comment:**

The system is **NOT ready** to exit Alpha Testing because:
- $TCR = 75\% < 85\%$ (below threshold)
- $DD = 2.0 > 1$ defect/KLOC (above threshold)
- 3 Critical (S1) defects remain open (violates exit rule $N_{S1} = 0$)

The development team must fix all S1 defects, improve task flows, and re-test before progressing to Beta.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Compare **Alpha Testing** and **Beta Testing** in detail with respect to **at least 8 parameters**. State **two metrics** used in Alpha Testing and explain their significance.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* Describe the **role of usability heuristics** and the **Think-Aloud Protocol** in Alpha Testing. How do they complement each other?

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table** (see Section 3.2 above — full 11-row table to be reproduced by student).

**Two Key Metrics:**
1. **System Usability Scale (SUS):** A 10-item questionnaire yielding a score from 0–100. A score ≥ 68 indicates above-average usability. It captures the *subjective satisfaction* dimension of ISO 9241-11.

2. **Task Completion Rate (TCR):** Measures the percentage of tasks users can complete without assistance. It captures the *effectiveness* dimension of ISO 9241-11. Industry standard threshold is **≥ 85%** for Alpha exit.

**Significance:** Together, SUS and TCR triangulate usability from both **subjective (perception)** and **objective (performance)** perspectives, giving the design team actionable data.

---

**(b) Role of Usability Heuristics and Think-Aloud Protocol:**

**Usability Heuristics (Nielsen's 10 Heuristics):**
- Used by **expert evaluators** to inspect the interface *without* users.
- Provide a **quick, low-cost** method to identify obvious usability violations.
- Particularly effective in early Alpha phases when prototypes are unstable.

**Think-Aloud Protocol:**
- Used **with** actual users performing tasks.
- Captures **real-time cognitive processes**, mental models, and confusions.
- Generates rich qualitative data that heuristics alone cannot provide.

**How They Complement Each Other:**

- Heuristics identify **what** is wrong (the defect).
- Think-Aloud reveals **why** it is wrong (the user's reasoning).
- Heuristics are *expert-driven*; Think-Aloud is *user-driven*.
- Combining both yields a **triangulated evaluation** — high in both internal validity (heuristics) and ecological validity (think-aloud).
- In Alpha Testing, this combination is the **gold standard** for iterative HCI refinement.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Alpha Testing**

- ⭐ **Definition:** First in-house user testing at the developer's site, in a **controlled environment**.
- ⭐ **Position:** Occurs **after System Testing** and **before Beta Testing**.
- ⭐ **Testers:** Internal QA + selected representative users (typically 6–10 per Nielsen).
- ⭐ **Environment:** Controlled lab, fully instrumented with audio/video/screen recording.
- ⭐ **Methods:** Think-Aloud Protocol, Heuristic Evaluation, Task-Based Scenarios.
- ⭐ **Key Metrics to Memorize:**
  - $TCR = \dfrac{T_{comp}}{T_{att}} \times 100$ (Threshold ≥ 85%)
  - $DD = \dfrac{N_d}{S}$ (Threshold ≤ 1 defect/KLOC)
  - $ER = \dfrac{E_{committed}}{E_{opportunities}} \times 100$
  - $MTBF = MTTR + MTTF$
  - $SUS \geq 68$ for above-average usability
- ⭐ **Exit Criteria:** All S1 defects closed + TCR ≥ 85% + DD ≤ 1 + SUS ≥ 68.
- ⭐ **Key Distinction:** Alpha is *not* White-Box testing; it is a *user-experience* test.
- ⭐ **Common Mistake:** Saying Alpha is done by "customers" — they participate in **Beta**, not Alpha.
- ⭐ **Standards Alignment:** ISO 9241-11 (Efficiency, Effectiveness, Satisfaction); Nielsen's 10 Heuristics.
- ⭐ **Cost Logic:** Defects fixed in Alpha are ~10× cheaper than in Beta and ~100× cheaper than post-release.
- ⭐ **Output Deliverables:** Defect Log, Usability Report, Design Recommendations, Go/No-Go Decision Document.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beta Testing and Acceptance Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Beta Testing and Acceptance Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Beta Testing — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Beta Testing** is a form of *external user acceptance testing* performed by a limited, real-world audience (the "beta testers") in a near-realistic environment **before** the software is released to the general market. It validates the system under genuine usage conditions and gathers qualitative + quantitative feedback on usability, stability, and overall user experience.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Beta Testing falls under the broader umbrella of **Usability Evaluation Methods (UEM)** as defined in Module 4 (Evaluation & Testing). It is categorized as a *Field Study / Field Test* in Dix, Finlay, Abowd & Beale's classification of evaluation methods.

### 1.2 Acceptance Testing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Testing** is the **formal, contractual evaluation** of a completed system against predetermined business, functional, and non-functional **acceptance criteria**. The objective is to certify whether the system is *fit for delivery* to the end user / customer. The test is typically **gated** — the product either *passes* (and goes live) or *fails* (and is sent back to the development team for rework).

Acceptance Testing has two principal variants:
1. **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** — performed by the *end users / client representatives*.
2. **Operational Acceptance Testing (OAT)** — performed by *operations / IT teams* (covering backup, disaster recovery, maintainability).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Beta Testing Analogy (The Restaurant Soft-Launch):**
> Imagine a chef invents a new dish. Before putting it on the regular menu, she invites **30 trusted regular customers** to taste it for free over a weekend. They eat under realistic conditions (real table, real cutlery, real hunger) and give honest opinions: *"Too salty," "Loved the crunch," "Confused by the sauce."* The chef fine-tunes the recipe based on this feedback before the **Grand Public Opening**. That invitation-only weekend tasting = **Beta Testing**.

> [!TIP]
> **Acceptance Testing Analogy (The Handing-Over of a New House):**
> Before you finalize the purchase of a builder's house, an inspector walks through every room with a checklist: *"Does every door open?" "Does every tap run?" "Are the promised tiles laid?"* Only when **every checklist item passes** do you sign the final handover document and accept the keys. That signed checklist = **Acceptance Testing**.

### 1.4 Why These Tests Matter in HCI / KTU Context

- They bridge the **gap between developer assumptions and real user behavior**.
- They uncover **usability defects** that lab-based evaluations (heuristic evaluation, controlled user testing) often miss.
- They are *legally and commercially binding* — without passing acceptance tests, payment / go-live is blocked.

---

### 1.5 Position in the KTU 2024 Evaluation Framework

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU 2024 HCI syllabus**, Beta Testing and Acceptance Testing are placed in **Module 4: Evaluation & Testing**, building on prior knowledge of:
> - **Module 2:** User models & mental models
> - **Module 3:** Usability heuristics & inspection methods
> - **Module 4 (earlier):** Controlled experiments & think-aloud protocols

They represent the **final gate-keeping activities** before product deployment.

| Property | Beta Testing | Acceptance Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Discover unforeseen defects in real conditions | Validate contract / business requirements |
| **Performed By** | External volunteer end users | Client / sponsor / end-user representatives |
| **Environment** | Real-world, naturalistic | Staging / pre-production (UAT) |
| **Stage in SDLC** | After alpha, before GA release | Final phase, before sign-off |
| **Pass/Fail Consequence** | Informs release readiness | Gates payment & go-live |
| **Strictness of Criteria** | Exploratory, flexible | Strictly against agreed criteria |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Beta Testing — Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Objectives (KTU-Mapped Learning Outcomes)
1. Validate **system stability** under heterogeneous hardware/software configurations.
2. Capture **real-world usability complaints** from representative users.
3. Gauge **user satisfaction / Net Promoter Score (NPS)** before mass release.
4. Provide **marketing pre-launch buzz** and gather testimonials.
5. Surface **defect leakage** that escaped internal QA.

#### 2.1.2 The Five Operational Phases of Beta Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners expect students to enumerate the **beta testing lifecycle** clearly. Memorize the following five phases:

1. **Planning Phase**
   - Define beta goals (e.g., "find bugs affecting > 5% of users").
   - Select beta test population (size, demographics, tech profile).
   - Decide **closed beta** vs **open beta**.
   - Author the **beta test plan** with timelines, scope, success metrics.

2. **Recruitment Phase**
   - Closed Beta: invite selected power-users, customers, internal staff.
   - Open Beta: publicly advertised, anyone may opt in.
   - Each tester signs a **Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA)** for confidential builds.

3. **Execution Phase**
   - Distribute software build (via download, pre-loaded device, TestFlight, Google Play Beta).
   - Testers use the software **in their natural environment** for a defined period (typically **2–8 weeks**).
   - Issues logged through bug-tracker (Jira, Bugzilla, GitHub Issues).

4. **Feedback & Triage Phase**
   - Tester reports classified by severity (P0, P1, P2, P3).
   - Defects routed to dev teams; **rapid patches** released as Beta-2, Beta-3 builds.
   - Quantitative metrics aggregated (crash rate, NPS, task completion).

5. **Closure Phase**
   - Final beta report produced: number of bugs found, severity distribution, user quotes, recommendation (**GO / NO-GO for General Availability**).

#### 2.1.3 Closed vs Open Beta — Comparative Matrix

> [!TIP]
> This comparison is a **favourite KTU Part A (3-mark) question**.

| Dimension | Closed Beta | Open Beta |
|---|---|---|
| Audience Size | **Small** (typically 50–500 users) | **Large** (1,000 to millions) |
| User Profile | Curated, NDA-bound | Voluntary, public |
| Feedback Quality | Deep, technical, structured | Volumetric, diverse, noisy |
| Risk Exposure | Low (confidential) | High (IP leakage possible) |
| Cost | Lower (fewer support tickets) | Higher (scaling issues surface) |
| Best Used When | Complex enterprise software | Consumer apps, games |

---

### 2.2 Acceptance Testing — Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Hierarchical Position of Acceptance Testing

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask students to *place* acceptance testing within the software testing pyramid. It sits at the **highest level** — performed last, on near-final code, by the customer.

Acceptance testing belongs to the family of **"black-box" functional tests** that operate on the fully integrated system. It is distinguished from:
- **Unit Testing** → developer runs, tests isolated functions.
- **Integration Testing** → tests combined modules.
- **System Testing** → tests complete system against specifications (internal QA).
- **Acceptance Testing** → tests system against **business / contractual requirements** (customer).

#### 2.2.2 Variants of Acceptance Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU requires knowledge of **at least three variants** — memorize with examples:

1. **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** — Verifies the system meets business workflows.
   - *Example:* A bank's loan officers test the new loan-management module with real customer scenarios.

2. **Operational Acceptance Testing (OAT)** — Verifies non-functional operational criteria.
   - *Example:* IT operations team confirms backup, restore, failover procedures work.

3. **Contract Acceptance Testing (CAT)** — Conducted against contractual SLAs.
   - *Example:* Vendor contract specifies 99.9% uptime; OAT verifies this is achieved.

4. **Regulatory / Compliance Acceptance Testing** — Ensures legal/industry standards are met.
   - *Example:* HIPAA compliance check for a hospital information system.

5. **Alpha Acceptance Testing** — Conducted at the developer's site by internal staff on behalf of the customer (sometimes considered a precursor).

#### 2.2.3 The Acceptance Test Process (Step-by-Step)

> [!TIP]
> Frequently asked as a **14-mark question in KTU ESE**.

1. **Requirements Capture** — Acceptance criteria are derived from the **Business Requirements Document (BRD)** or **System Requirements Specification (SRS)**.
2. **Acceptance Test Plan (ATP)** authored — defines scope, schedule, resources, entry/exit criteria.
3. **Acceptance Test Cases** designed — input, action, expected result, pass/fail rules.
4. **Test Environment Setup** — production-like staging environment with masked/anonymized data.
5. **Test Execution** — testers run the scripted scenarios.
6. **Defect Logging** — deviations logged into issue tracker.
7. **Test Sign-off / Rejection** — formal document stating *Accepted / Conditionally Accepted / Rejected*.

#### 2.2.4 Entry & Exit Criteria

| Stage | Entry Criteria | Exit Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| **Begin Acceptance Test** | All P0/P1 system test defects closed; UAT environment stable; test data prepared | All acceptance test cases executed |
| **Acceptance Pass** | ≥ 95% test cases passed; **zero P0 defects open**; no data-loss issues | Formal sign-off document signed by both parties |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Quantitative Metrics Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Beta and Acceptance Testing use **quantitative evaluation metrics**. KTU may ask you to compute these. Below are the high-yield formulae. **No pipe `|` symbol used in tables — it is replaced with `\vert`**.

| Metric | Formula | Meaning | Typical Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Defect Density** | $DD = \dfrac{N_{defects}}{KLOC}$ | Defects per **K**ilo-**L**ines-**O**f-**C**ode | Industry avg ≈ 1–5 |
| **Test Coverage** | $TC = \dfrac{T_{executed}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % of test cases actually run | ≥ 95% for acceptance |
| **Pass Rate** | $PR = \dfrac{T_{passed}}{T_{executed}} \times 100\%$ | % of executed tests that passed | ≥ 95% for sign-off |
| **Mean Time To Failure** | $MTTF = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N_{failures}}$ | Average uptime between crashes | Higher is better |
| **Crash-Free Sessions** | $CFS = \left(1 - \dfrac{N_{crashes}}{N_{sessions}}\right) \times 100\%$ | User-session stability metric | ≥ 99% target |
| **Net Promoter Score** | $NPS = \%P - \%D$ | Promoters (9–10) minus Detractors (0–6) | ≥ 30 is good |
| **Sample Size for Beta** | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$ | Statistical minimum beta user count | Depends on confidence Z |
| **System Usability Scale** | $SUS = 2.5 \times \sum_{i=1}^{10}(Score_i - 1)$ for odd items; $2.5 \times (5 - Score_i)$ for even items | 0–100 usability score | ≥ 68 = "good" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:**
> - $N_{defects}$ = total defects found
> - $KLOC$ = thousand lines of code
> - $T_{executed}$ = test cases executed; $T_{total}$ = total test cases; $T_{passed}$ = test cases passed
> - $Z$ = Z-score for confidence level (1.96 for 95%), $p$ = expected proportion, $e$ = margin of error
> - $t_i$ = duration of $i$-th uptime interval; $N_{failures}$ = total failure count
> - $N_{crashes}$ = crash count; $N_{sessions}$ = total sessions
> - $\%P$ = % of promoters; $\%D$ = % of detractors
> - $Score_i$ = raw score on SUS question $i$ (1–5 Likert)

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Consumer Software** (apps, games, OS): Beta testing surfaces device-fragmentation bugs and crashes.
- **Enterprise / B2B** (ERP, CRM, Banking): Acceptance testing is **contractually mandated**; without sign-off, no payment is released.
- **Safety-Critical Systems** (medical, avionics, nuclear): Acceptance testing includes **regulatory verification** (FDA, EASA).
- **HCI Research**: Beta testing and acceptance testing feed real-world validation data into **interaction design iterations**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners value **numerical worked examples** for the metrics derived in Section 2. The following worked problems train you to confidently compute sample size, SUS score, defect density, and pass rate during the ESE.

---

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Minimum Beta Test Sample Size

**Problem:** A product manager wants to run a beta test. She wants **95% confidence**, **5% margin of error**, and she conservatively assumes **p = 0.5** (maximum variance). Compute the minimum number of beta testers required.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

> Given: confidence = 95% $\Rightarrow Z = 1.96$, $p = 0.5$, $e = 0.05$.

**Step 1:** Substitute into the sample-size formula.

$$
n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
n = \dfrac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (0.5) \cdot (1 - 0.5)}{(0.05)^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute intermediate terms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z^2 &= (1.96)^2 = 3.8416 \\
p \cdot (1-p) &= 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 \\
e^2 &= (0.05)^2 = 0.0025
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Plug in and evaluate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{3.8416 \times 0.25}{0.0025} \\
&= \dfrac{0.9604}{0.0025} \\
&= 384.16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Always **round up** for sample size (cannot have 0.16 testers).

$$
\boxed{n \approx 385 \text{ testers}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation pitfall:** A common mistake is **not rounding up**, or using the wrong $Z$ value. KTU expects $Z = 1.96$ for 95% confidence — memorize this constant.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Computing System Usability Scale (SUS) Score

**Problem:** Ten users complete the SUS questionnaire (Likert 1–5). Their raw scores on items 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 (positive items) and items 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 (negative items) are tabulated below.

| User | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Q5 | Q6 | Q7 | Q8 | Q9 | Q10 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 2 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 2 | 5 | 1 |
| 2 | 4 | 2 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 2 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 2 |
| 3 | 4 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 1 |
| 4 | 5 | 2 | 5 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 2 |
| 5 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 2 | 4 | 1 |
| 6 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 1 |
| 7 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 2 |
| 8 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 1 |
| 9 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 2 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 |
| 10 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 4 | 2 |

Compute the **average SUS score**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the **sum for each user** using the SUS conversion rules.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SUS_{user} &= \sum_{i \text{ odd}} (Score_i - 1) + \sum_{i \text{ even}} (5 - Score_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

For User 1:
$$
SUS_1 = (5-1)+(4-1)+(5-1)+(4-1)+(5-1) + (5-1)+(5-2)+(5-1)+(5-2)+(5-1) = 20 + 20 = 40
$$

> Multiply by 2.5 to get the **per-user SUS score**: $40 \times 2.5 = 100$. *(This is the standard SUS convention.)*

**Step 2:** Repeat for each user (illustrating for User 2):

Positive items sum: $(4-1)+(5-1)+(4-1)+(5-1)+(4-1) = 16$  
Negative items sum: $(5-2)+(5-1)+(5-2)+(5-1)+(5-2) = 21$  
Total sum = $16 + 21 = 37$  
$SUS_2 = 37 \times 2.5 = 92.5$

**Step 3:** Compute every user's SUS (compact summary):

| User | Pos. Sum | Neg. Sum | Total | SUS Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 16 | 20 | 36 | 90.0 |
| 2 | 16 | 21 | 37 | 92.5 |
| 3 | 17 | 21 | 38 | 95.0 |
| 4 | 16 | 21 | 37 | 92.5 |
| 5 | 18 | 21 | 39 | 97.5 |
| 6 | 16 | 22 | 38 | 95.0 |
| 7 | 18 | 21 | 39 | 97.5 |
| 8 | 17 | 22 | 39 | 97.5 |
| 9 | 18 | 22 | 40 | 100.0 |
| 10 | 17 | 22 | 39 | 97.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction note (clarification):** A common convention is that the SUS raw contribution for each item is **either** $(Score_i - 1)$ for odd items **or** $(5 - Score_i)$ for even items, *without separately summing positive and negative contributions and adding them again*. The table above provides the per-user score consistent with the standard **Brook (1996) SUS formula**:
> $$SUS = 2.5 \times \sum_{i=1}^{10}\left[(Score_i - 1) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{i \text{ odd}} + (5 - Score_i) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{i \text{ even}}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **mean SUS**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{SUS} &= \dfrac{90.0 + 92.5 + 95.0 + 92.5 + 97.5 + 95.0 + 97.5 + 97.5 + 100.0 + 97.5}{10} \\
&= \dfrac{955.0}{10} \\
&= 95.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\overline{SUS} = 95.5}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret.

Since **95.5 > 68**, the interface is rated **"excellent"** on the SUS scale (≥ 85 = excellent).

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation pitfall:** KTU students often **forget the ×2.5 multiplier** when computing per-user SUS. Always remember: **raw item contributions sum to 0–40, multiplied by 2.5 gives 0–100.**

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Acceptance Test Pass-Rate and Sign-off Decision

**Problem:** A B2B CRM has **320 acceptance test cases**. After UAT execution, **288 passed**, **18 failed**, and **14 were blocked** (could not be executed due to environment issues). The contract states sign-off requires **≥ 95% pass rate on executed cases** and **zero P0 defects open**. All 18 failures are P2 severity (cosmetic / minor). Should the project manager sign off?

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the **pass rate on executed cases**.

$$
PR = \dfrac{T_{passed}}{T_{executed}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{288}{288 + 18} \times 100\% = \dfrac{288}{306} \times 100\% \approx 94.12\%
$$

**Step 2:** Check against the 95% criterion.

$$
94.12\% < 95\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Pass-rate criterion FAILED}
$$

**Step 3:** Check the P0 criterion.

Since all failures are P2, **P0 = 0** $\Rightarrow$ **P0 criterion PASSED.**

**Step 4:** Decision logic.

Because **two** independent criteria exist and the pass-rate failed, **sign-off is denied**. The 18 P2 defects must be fixed (or formally accepted with risk waiver) before re-testing.

> **Final Decision: NO SIGN-OFF** — return to development for defect remediation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often compute pass rate as $288/320 = 90\%$. KTU's strict definition is **pass rate = passed ÷ executed** (not ÷ total). Re-read the question!

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Crash-Free Sessions (Beta Stability Metric)

**Problem:** During a 30-day beta of a mobile app, the telemetry shows **12,500 sessions** with **75 reported crashes**. Compute the crash-free sessions percentage.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
CFS = \left(1 - \dfrac{N_{crashes}}{N_{sessions}}\right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
CFS = \left(1 - \dfrac{75}{12500}\right) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.006) \times 100\% = 99.4\%
$$

$$
\boxed{CFS = 99.4\%}
$$

Industry benchmark for consumer mobile apps is **≥ 99%**, so this beta result is acceptable.

---

### 3.5 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose Which Test

> [!TIP]
> KTU may ask you to **justify** the choice between Beta and Acceptance testing. Use this decision matrix in your answers.

| Scenario | Choose Beta Testing | Choose Acceptance Testing |
|---|---|---|
| You want to discover unknown real-world bugs | ✅ Best choice | ❌ Too late |
| You want to validate contractual SLAs | ❌ Inappropriate | ✅ Best choice |
| You want to gate payment / go-live | ❌ Insufficient authority | ✅ Legally binding |
| You want marketing pre-launch buzz | ✅ Ideal | ❌ Irrelevant |
| You want to test under heterogeneous devices | ✅ Realistic | ⚠️ Limited in UAT lab |
| You want to certify regulatory compliance | ❌ Informal | ✅ Formal sign-off required |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams illustrate the **workflows, decision logic, and comparative architecture** of Beta and Acceptance Testing. They are rendered with alphanumeric node identifiers and plain-text labels to satisfy the diagram-compilation safeguard.

---

### 4.1 Beta Testing — End-to-End Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Plan Beta Goals and KPIs"] --> nodeB["Recruit Testers NDA Signed"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Closed Beta Selected Users"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Open Beta Public Invite"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Distribute Build TestFlight APK"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["Users Use App 2 to 8 Weeks"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Log Issues in Tracker"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Triage by Severity P0 P1 P2"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Critical Bug Found?"}
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ["Patch and Release Beta 2"]
    nodeJ --> nodeF
    nodeI -- No --> nodeK["Collect NPS and SUS Metrics"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Generate Final Beta Report"]
    nodeL --> nodeM{"Go or No Go for GA?"}
    nodeM -- Go --> nodeN["Release to General Availability"]
    nodeM -- No Go --> nodeO["Continue Iteration"]
```

---

### 4.2 Acceptance Testing — Linear Gatekeeping Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["Receive Completed System"] --> nodeQ["Verify Entry Criteria Met"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR{"Entry Criteria OK?"}
    nodeR -- No --> nodeS["Reject Return to Dev"]
    nodeR -- Yes --> nodeT["Execute UAT Test Cases"]
    nodeT --> nodeU["Log Defects into Tracker"]
    nodeU --> nodeV{"All P0 Closed?"}
    nodeV -- No --> nodeS
    nodeV -- Yes --> nodeW{"Pass Rate greater 95 percent?"}
    nodeW -- No --> nodeS
    nodeW -- Yes --> nodeX["Formal Sign Off Document"]
    nodeX --> nodeY["Release Payment and Go Live"]
```

---

### 4.3 Beta vs Acceptance Testing — Comparative Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BD["BETA TESTING LAYER"]
        nodeBD1["External Real Users"]
        nodeBD2["Natural Environment"]
        nodeBD3["Exploratory Feedback"]
        nodeBD4["Bug Discovery Focus"]
    end
    subgraph AD["ACCEPTANCE TESTING LAYER"]
        nodeAD1["Client User Representatives"]
        nodeAD2["Staging UAT Lab"]
        nodeAD3["Scripted Test Cases"]
        nodeAD4["Contractual Criteria Focus"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["COMMON DOWNSTREAM"]
        nodeOut["Product Release Decision"]
    end
    nodeBD1 --> nodeBD2 --> nodeBD3 --> nodeBD4
    nodeAD1 --> nodeAD2 --> nodeAD3 --> nodeAD4
    nodeBD4 --> nodeOut
    nodeAD4 --> nodeOut
```

---

### 4.4 SUS Computation Block Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1["User completes 10 Likert items"] --> nodeS2["Odd items Score minus 1"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS3["Even items 5 minus Score"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS4["Sum contributions 0 to 40"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5["Multiply by 2.5"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6["Final SUS Score 0 to 100"]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!TIP]
> Practice the questions below under timed conditions. Each carries the **simulated KTU University Exam** tag, the **Part A / Part B classification** as per the 2024 scheme, and the **mark distribution**. Model answers follow each question.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Beta Testing. List any four objectives of beta testing.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Beta Testing** is a type of external user testing performed by a selected group of real users in their natural environment, just before the product is released to the general market. It aims to discover defects and usability issues that internal testing missed.

**Four objectives:**
1. Validate system stability on diverse hardware/OS configurations.
2. Capture real-world usability complaints and feature requests.
3. Measure user satisfaction (e.g., via SUS or NPS) before launch.
4. Generate marketing buzz and gather testimonials from early adopters.

*(Any four relevant points accepted. 3 marks = 1 for definition + 2 for objectives.)*

---

#### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between User Acceptance Testing (UAT) and Operational Acceptance Testing (OAT).**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | UAT | OAT |
|---|---|---|
| **Conducted by** | End users / business representatives | Operations / IT teams |
| **Focus** | Functional & business workflow correctness | Non-functional operations: backup, recovery, performance, maintainability |
| **Question Answered** | "Does the system do what the business needs?" | "Can the system be run, maintained, and supported in production?" |
| **Example** | Bank officers test loan workflow | Sysadmin verifies daily backup & disaster recovery |

*(Tabular answer with 4 distinctions = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Module 4 carries internal choice. The two options below are completely independent.

---

#### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — (14 Marks)

**a)** Explain in detail the **five phases of the Beta Testing lifecycle**, with one real-world example per phase. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A startup is launching a consumer mobile app and wants to determine the minimum number of beta testers required for 99% confidence with a 3% margin of error. Assume $p = 0.5$. Calculate the sample size and interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Part (a) — Model Answer

> **Beta Testing Lifecycle — Five Phases:**

**1. Planning Phase** — Define beta goals, KPIs (e.g., crash-free sessions ≥ 99%), success criteria, timeline, and budget.
   - *Example:* The startup decides "find all P0/P1 bugs before public launch in 8 weeks."

**2. Recruitment Phase** — Select testers matching the target persona. Sign NDAs. Decide closed vs open beta.
   - *Example:* Recruit 500 existing newsletter subscribers (closed beta) covering iOS and Android.

**3. Execution Phase** — Distribute the build via TestFlight / Play Beta. Testers use the app freely for 4–8 weeks in their natural environment. Issues logged via in-app feedback widget and Jira.
   - *Example:* Users log "crash when uploading photos larger than 5 MB on Samsung Galaxy A12."

**4. Feedback & Triage Phase** — Daily triage of incoming issues; rapid patches released as Beta-2, Beta-3 builds. Quantitative metrics (NPS, SUS, crash-free sessions) aggregated weekly.
   - *Example:* Photo-upload fix released in Beta-2 within 3 days; crash rate drops from 1.2% to 0.4%.

**5. Closure Phase** — Author the final beta report containing defect counts, severity distribution, user quotes, NPS/SUS scores, and a clear **GO / NO-GO recommendation** for general availability.
   - *Example:* Report states "Crash-free sessions 99.6%, NPS +42 — recommend GA launch on June 15."

*(1.5 marks per phase × 5 = 7.5 → rounded to 7 marks per KTU rubric.)*

---

##### Part (b) — Model Answer

**Given:** Confidence level = 99% $\Rightarrow Z = 2.576$ (from standard normal table for 99%); $p = 0.5$; $e = 0.03$.

**Step 1:** Write the formula.

$$
n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
n = \dfrac{(2.576)^2 \cdot (0.5) \cdot (1 - 0.5)}{(0.03)^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the intermediate terms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z^2 &= (2.576)^2 = 6.6358 \\
p(1-p) &= 0.25 \\
e^2 &= 0.0009
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate.

$$
n = \dfrac{6.6358 \times 0.25}{0.0009} = \dfrac{1.65895}{0.0009} \approx 1843.28
$$

**Step 5:** Round up.

$$
\boxed{n \approx 1844 \text{ testers}}
$$

**Interpretation:** With 99% confidence and a 3% margin of error, the startup must recruit **at least 1844 beta testers** to ensure the beta results are statistically representative of the broader market.

*(1 mark per step → 7 marks total. Examiner expects to see explicit substitution, intermediate computation, and final rounded-up integer.)*

---

#### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — (14 Marks)

**a)** Describe the **process of Acceptance Testing** in detail. Explain **entry criteria and exit criteria** for acceptance testing with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**b)** During UAT for a hospital management system, **240 test cases** were executed. **220 passed**, **15 failed**, and **5 were blocked**. The contract requires **≥ 95% pass rate** on executed cases and **zero P0 defects**. Of the 15 failures, **0 are P0, 12 are P1, 3 are P2**. Decide whether sign-off should be granted. Justify your decision with computations. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Part (a) — Model Answer

> **Acceptance Testing — Process:**

1. **Requirements Capture** — Extract acceptance criteria from the Business Requirements Document (BRD) and SRS.
2. **Authoring the Acceptance Test Plan (ATP)** — Define scope, resources, schedule, entry/exit criteria, tools, and responsibilities.
3. **Designing Acceptance Test Cases** — Each case specifies input data, action steps, expected results, and pass/fail logic.
4. **Setting up the UAT Environment** — Staging environment with anonymized production-like data.
5. **Test Execution** — Customer / end-user representatives execute test cases; deviations logged.
6. **Defect Management** — Issues tracked in the same tracker used during system testing; severity assigned.
7. **Test Closure & Sign-off** — Formal document with Pass / Conditional Pass / Fail status, signed by both sponsor and supplier.

> **Entry Criteria** (must be true before acceptance testing can begin):
> - All P0 and P1 system-test defects closed.
> - UAT environment stable and identical to production.
> - Test data prepared and anonymized.
> - Test cases reviewed and approved by the customer.

> **Exit Criteria** (must be true before sign-off):
> - 100% of planned acceptance test cases executed.
> - Pass rate on executed cases ≥ 95% (or contractually agreed threshold).
> - Zero P0 defects open.
> - Formal acceptance sign-off document signed by both parties.

*Example:* A retail ERP system — entry criteria include successful migration of last 6 months of order data; exit criteria require end-to-end "purchase order → invoice → payment" workflow to pass with no P0 defects.

*(Process steps = 4 marks; entry/exit criteria with example = 3 marks. Total = 7.)*

---

##### Part (b) — Model Answer

**Given:** $T_{executed} = 240$, $T_{passed} = 220$, $T_{failed} = 15$, $T_{blocked} = 5$.

**Step 1:** Compute the pass rate on **executed** cases.

$$
PR = \dfrac{T_{passed}}{T_{executed}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{220}{240} \times 100\% \approx 91.67\%
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the failure rate.

$$
FR = \dfrac{15}{240} \times 100\% \approx 6.25\%
$$

**Step 3:** Check both contract criteria.

| Criterion | Required | Actual | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pass rate on executed cases | ≥ 95% | 91.67% | ❌ FAIL |
| P0 defects open | 0 | 0 | ✅ PASS |

**Step 4:** Decision logic.

Both criteria must be satisfied. Since the **pass-rate criterion failed** (91.67% < 95%), the contract is **not satisfied**, and **sign-off must be DENIED.**

**Step 5:** Recommendation.

The development team must fix the 12 P1 defects (and optionally the 3 P2 ones), after which a re-test will be conducted. The 5 blocked test cases must also be unblocked and executed before re-evaluation.

> **Final Decision: NO SIGN-OFF — return to development for P1 defect remediation and re-test.**

*(Each step ~1 mark; decision justification with quantitative computation = 7 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas — Read Carefully Before Exam:**
> 1. **Conflating Beta and Acceptance Testing** — they are NOT the same. Beta is *exploratory*, Acceptance is *contractual*.
> 2. **Forgetting to round up sample sizes** — always round up, never truncate.
> 3. **Wrong $Z$ values** — 95% $\Rightarrow Z = 1.96$; 99% $\Rightarrow Z = 2.576$. Memorize.
> 4. **Wrong pass-rate formula** — use passed ÷ executed, NOT passed ÷ total.
> 5. **Forgetting the ×2.5 multiplier** in SUS calculation.
> 6. **Missing phases of beta testing lifecycle** — examiners award marks per phase; missing one = ~1.5 mark loss.
> 7. **Not stating entry & exit criteria explicitly** in acceptance-testing answers.
> 8. **Writing programs/code** for non-programming questions — KTU deducts marks for irrelevant content.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print and Memorize:**

- ☐ **Beta Testing** = external, real-world, exploratory, pre-GA, NDA-based.
- ☐ **Acceptance Testing** = internal-or-client, contractual, formal sign-off, gates go-live.
- ☐ Beta testing has **5 phases**: Plan, Recruit, Execute, Triage, Closure.
- ☐ Acceptance testing has **3+ variants**: UAT, OAT, CAT, Regulatory.
- ☐ Sample-size formula: $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p (1-p)}{e^2}$.
- ☐ SUS formula: per-item contributions summed and multiplied by 2.5; range 0–100; ≥ 68 = good; ≥ 85 = excellent.
- ☐ Pass rate = passed ÷ executed (×100%).
- ☐ Crash-free sessions ≥ 99% is industry target.
- ☐ Entry criteria for acceptance: P0/P1 system bugs closed; staging stable.
- ☐ Exit criteria for acceptance: ≥ 95% pass rate (contract-dependent); zero P0 open; signed sign-off.
- ☐ Closed beta = small, curated, NDA-bound; Open beta = large, voluntary, marketing-driven.
- ☐ Beta testing is **field study / field test** in Dix et al.'s evaluation taxonomy.
- ☐ Acceptance testing is the **topmost layer** of the test pyramid.
- ☐ Distinguish **black-box** (acceptance) from **white-box** (unit) testing.
- ☐ Always include **GO / NO-GO recommendation** in beta closure report.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** and user interface engineering, **Types of Testing** refers to the systematic classification of evaluation methodologies used to assess the quality, usability, accessibility, and acceptability of an interactive software system before, during, and after deployment. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, testing in HCI goes beyond functional verification—it measures how well a system supports **human users** in accomplishing their goals efficiently, effectively, and with satisfaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> HCI testing is the empirical investigation of an interactive system with **representative users** performing **realistic tasks**, under controlled or naturalistic conditions, to gather qualitative and quantitative data that inform iterative design decisions.

The five canonical pillars of HCI evaluation, recognized by KTU and aligned with **Nielsen's usability heuristics**, are:

| **Pillar** | **KTU Terminology** | **Core Question Answered** |
|---|---|---|
| Learnability | Ease of learning | How quickly can a first-time user accomplish basic tasks? |
| Efficiency | Speed of use | Once learned, how fast can tasks be performed? |
| Memorability | Recall over time | Can users re-establish proficiency after a period of non-use? |
| Error Rate | Error handling | How many errors do users make, and how do they recover? |
| Satisfaction | Subjective ease | How pleasant is the user experience? |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Inspection"**
>
> Imagine you are opening a restaurant. Before declaring it ready for the public, you test it in many ways:
>
> - **Tasting the food** → Functional testing: *Does the dish come out correctly?*
> - **Inviting friends to dine** → **Usability testing**: *Can real people easily eat here, read the menu, and find the restroom?*
> - **Letting a critic dine anonymously** → **Heuristic evaluation**: *An expert checks it against professional standards (cleanliness, ambience, service).*
> - **Running two menus side by side** → **A/B testing**: *Which menu layout gets more orders?*
> - **Soft opening in one branch** → **Pilot/Beta testing**: *Does it work in a real environment with real crowds?*
> - **Wheelchair ramp check** → **Accessibility testing**: *Can everyone, including people with disabilities, use it?*
>
> Just as a restaurant is evaluated from multiple angles before launch, an HCI interface must be **tested through multiple lenses** to ensure it truly serves its users.

### 1.3 Why Testing Types Matter in HCI

- **Different goals need different tests**: A test designed to find bugs differs from one designed to gauge satisfaction.
- **Cost-benefit trade-off**: Some tests are cheap and quick (heuristic evaluation); others are expensive but yield rich data (field studies).
- **Stage-specific application**: Early design stages favor discount methods; late stages favor empirical user testing.
- **Risk mitigation**: Catching usability problems after release can cost up to **100×** more than catching them during design.
- **Compliance and inclusion**: Accessibility testing ensures conformance with standards like **WCAG 2.1/2.2** (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:**
> When asked to "list types of testing," examiners expect you to **classify them** (e.g., user-based vs. expert-based, formative vs. summative, qualitative vs. quantitative). Always mention **at least 6 distinct types** with one-line purposes for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification Framework

Testing methodologies in HCI can be organized along **three orthogonal axes** (a key KTU theoretical framework):

#### Axis 1: Who Conducts the Test?
| **Approach** | **Conducted By** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **User-based (Empirical)** | Real end-users | Usability test, field study |
| **Expert-based (Discount)** | HCI professionals | Heuristic evaluation, cognitive walkthrough |
| **Model-based** | Software/computed | GOMS, Fitts' Law modeling |
| **Automated** | Tools/algorithms | Click-tracking analytics, eye-tracking software |

#### Axis 2: Stage of Design Lifecycle
| **Stage** | **Purpose** | **Test Type** |
|---|---|---|
| **Formative** (early/mid design) | Find problems to fix | Think-aloud, heuristic eval, prototyping tests |
| **Summative** (post-design) | Validate final product | Acceptance testing, A/B testing, benchmarking |

#### Axis 3: Setting
| **Setting** | **Environment** | **Test Type** |
|---|---|---|
| **Laboratory** | Controlled, observed | Lab usability test |
| **Field** | Real natural setting | Field study, diary study |

### 2.2 Detailed Types of Testing — KTU Core Reference

#### **Type 1: Usability Testing**
A structured, empirical evaluation where **representative users** perform **realistic tasks** with the system while observers collect data on performance, errors, and satisfaction.

- **Variants**: Moderated vs. unmoderated, remote vs. in-person, exploratory vs. comparative.
- **Sample Size**: Nielsen Norman Group recommends **5 ± 2 participants** per user group to uncover ~85% of usability issues.
- **Output metrics**: Task completion rate, time-on-task, error count, System Usability Scale (**SUS**) score.

#### **Type 2: Heuristic Evaluation (Discount Usability)**
A **cost-effective expert-based method** where 3–5 evaluators inspect the interface against a set of recognized usability principles (heuristics).

- **Invented by**: **Jakob Nielsen** (1994) — refined from Molich's 1990 work.
- **The 10 Nielsen Heuristics** are the KTU-favored standard (memorize all 10).
- **Advantages**: Cheap, fast, no users required, early in design cycle.
- **Disadvantage**: Misses real-user surprises; depends on evaluator expertise.

#### **Type 3: Cognitive Walkthrough**
An **expert-driven, theory-based method** focused on **learnability** for first-time users. Evaluators simulate a user's cognitive process step-by-step through a task.

- **Rooted in**: Norman's **Action Cycle** (Gulf of Execution / Gulf of Evaluation).
- **Four Key Questions** asked at each step (see Section 3 derivation).
- Best for **walk-up-and-use systems** like ATMs, kiosks, public information displays.

#### **Type 4: A/B Testing (Split Testing)**
A **comparative, summative method** where users are randomly assigned to **Version A** or **Version B** of an interface; behavioral metrics determine which version performs better.

- **Statistical core**: **Hypothesis testing** (typically a two-proportion z-test or t-test).
- **Common in**: Web design, mobile apps, email campaigns.
- **KTU relevance**: High for data-driven UX decisions.

#### **Type 5: Beta Testing (Field Testing)**
Releasing a **near-final version** to a limited, real-world audience to gather feedback in their natural environment.

- **Goal**: Identify unforeseen issues, gauge real-use acceptance, validate final design.
- **Difference from pilot**: Beta is closer to release; pilot is an early-stage dry run.

#### **Type 6: Accessibility Testing**
Evaluating whether the interface is usable by people with **disabilities** — visual, auditory, motor, cognitive.

- **Standards**: **WCAG 2.1/2.2** (Levels A, AA, AAA), **Section 508**.
- **Tools**: WAVE, axe DevTools, screen readers (JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver).
- **KTU 2024 focus area** under Universal Design principles.

#### **Type 7: Acceptance Testing (User Acceptance Testing - UAT)**
Final-stage validation where **end-users or clients** confirm the system meets business requirements and is "accepted" for production.

- **Sub-types**: Alpha testing (in-house), Beta testing (external).
- **Output**: Go / No-Go decision for release.

#### **Type 8: Compatibility / Cross-Platform Testing**
Ensuring the interface works consistently across **devices, operating systems, browsers, and assistive technologies**.

- Critical in modern multi-device environments.

#### **Type 9: Performance / Load Testing** *(mentioned for completeness)*
Measures system responsiveness under expected and peak load.

#### **Type 10: Eye-Tracking Testing**
Uses eye-tracking hardware/software to record **fixations, saccades, and gaze paths**, revealing attention patterns.

- Reveals what users notice vs. ignore.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Memorize These Standard Metrics:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Task Completion Rate (TCR)} &= \frac{\text{Number of users completing task}}{\text{Total number of users tested}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
\text{Mean Time on Task (MTOT)} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}{n} \quad \text{(seconds, lower is better)} \\[6pt]
\text{Error Rate (ER)} &= \frac{\text{Total errors committed}}{\text{Total task attempts}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
\text{SUS Score} &= \sum_{k=1}^{10} \left( (O_k - 1) \cdot \text{adj}_k \right) \times 2.5 \quad \text{(range: 0 to 100)} \\[6pt]
\text{Sample Size (Nielsen)} &\approx 5 \text{ users detects } \sim 85\% \text{ of issues}
\end{aligned}
$$

**SUS Scoring Rule (KTU must-know):**
- For **odd-numbered items** ($O_k$ = 1–5): contribution = $O_k - 1$
- For **even-numbered items** ($O_k$ = 1–5): contribution = $5 - O_k$
- Sum all 10, then **multiply by 2.5**.
- **SUS > 68** = above-average usability; **SUS < 50** = poor.

**A/B Testing Statistical Decision Rule:**

$$
Z = \frac{\hat{p}_A - \hat{p}_B}{\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})\left(\frac{1}{n_A} + \frac{1}{n_B}\right)}} \quad ; \quad \text{Reject } H_0 \text{ if } \vert Z \vert > 1.96 \text{ (at } \alpha = 0.05\text{)}
$$

**Heuristic Evaluation Yield (Nielsen's empirical formula):**

$$
\text{Issues Found} \approx N \cdot \left(1 - (1 - p)^{E}\right)
$$

Where $N$ = total usability problems in design, $E$ = number of evaluators, $p$ ≈ average detection rate per evaluator (~ 0.34 for Nielsen heuristics).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Heuristic Evaluation — Nielsen's Discovery Curve

Nielsen's classic 1993 study established that the **cumulative proportion of usability issues found** follows a diminishing-returns curve as evaluators are added.

#### Step 1: Single-Evaluator Detection Probability
Let $p$ be the probability an individual evaluator detects any specific usability issue. Empirically, Nielsen found $p \approx 0.34$ for his 10-heuristic method.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{issue missed by one evaluator}) &= 1 - p
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Probability All $E$ Evaluators Miss an Issue
For independent evaluators, the probability that **none** of $E$ evaluators detects an issue is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{missed by all } E) &= (1 - p)^{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Probability of Detection by At Least One Evaluator
Therefore, the probability the issue is detected by at least one evaluator is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{detected by } \geq 1 \text{ evaluator}) &= 1 - (1 - p)^{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Number of Issues Found out of $N$ Total
Expected number of issues discovered:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expected Issues Found} &= N \cdot \left[ 1 - (1 - p)^{E} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Numerical Illustration for KTU

Suppose a system has $N = 100$ latent usability issues and $p = 0.34$.

For $E = 1$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Found} &= 100 \times (1 - 0.66^{1}) = 100 \times 0.34 = 34 \text{ issues}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $E = 3$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Found} &= 100 \times (1 - 0.66^{3}) \\
&= 100 \times (1 - 0.2875) \\
&= 100 \times 0.7125 \\
&= 71.25 \approx 71 \text{ issues}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $E = 5$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Found} &= 100 \times (1 - 0.66^{5}) \\
&= 100 \times (1 - 0.1252) \\
&= 100 \times 0.8748 \\
&\approx 87 \text{ issues}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6: Diminishing Returns Interpretation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Marginal gain (E = 3 to E = 4)} &\approx 87.4 - 81.4 = 6.0 \text{ issues} \\
\text{Marginal gain (E = 4 to E = 5)} &\approx 87.4 - 81.4 = 6.0 \text{ issues (using } p=0.34 \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This justifies Nielsen's recommendation of **3–5 evaluators** — after 5, the cost of adding more evaluators outweighs the marginal benefit. **This is a classic KTU 14-mark derivation question.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Four Cognitive Walkthrough Questions

The Cognitive Walkthrough (CW) method, formalized by **Wharton, Rieman, Lewis, and Polson (1994)**, asks four questions at each step of a task to assess whether a first-time user can succeed.

For each **action** the user takes, the evaluator asks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &: \text{Will the user try to achieve the correct effect?} \\
Q_2 &: \text{Will the user notice that the correct action is available?} \\
Q_3 &: \text{Will the user associate the correct action with the effect they want?} \\
Q_4 &: \text{Will the user see that they are making progress toward the solution?}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Worked Example — ATM Withdrawal Task

**Task**: "Withdraw ₹5000 from an ATM."
**Step**: User sees the screen labeled "BALANCE INQUIRY / WITHDRAWAL."

| **Question** | **Analysis** | **Pass / Fail** |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_1$: Correct goal? | User wants cash → checking balance first is reasonable. | ✅ Pass |
| $Q_2$: Correct control visible? | "WITHDRAWAL" button is clearly visible. | ✅ Pass |
