| $Q_3$: Correct mapping? | User knows "withdrawal" gives cash. | ✅ Pass |
| $Q_4$: Progress feedback? | Screen prompts "Enter amount." | ✅ Pass |

If any question fails, a **usability story** is written to document the problem.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Sample-Size Justification (Nielsen's 5-User Rule)

#### Reasoning Chain
Nielsen's argument proceeds via the **binomial distribution** for issue detection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{detect by } \geq 1 \text{ of } n \text{ users}) &= 1 - (1 - p)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming the average user has a $p \approx 0.31$ chance of encountering any given usability issue during a session:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n = 3: \quad P &= 1 - 0.69^{3} = 1 - 0.328 = 67.2\% \\
n = 5: \quad P &= 1 - 0.69^{5} = 1 - 0.156 = 84.4\% \\
n = 15: \quad P &= 1 - 0.69^{15} = 1 - 0.0056 = 99.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Conclusion for KTU

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{n = 5 \text{ users per user category is the sweet spot: detects ~85% of usability issues at minimal cost.}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Beyond $n = 5$, the marginal benefit diminishes while cost grows linearly — a fundamental **cost-benefit trade-off** in HCI evaluation, frequently examined in KTU board questions.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Table-Marking Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table — Memorize the Contrasts:**

| **Criterion** | **Heuristic Evaluation** | **Usability Testing** | **Cognitive Walkthrough** | **A/B Testing** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Conducted by** | Experts (3–5) | Real users | Experts | Real users (random) |
| **User involvement** | None | High | None | High |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate–High | Low | Moderate |
| **Time** | Hours–Days | Days–Weeks | Hours–Days | Weeks–Months |
| **Stage** | Early/Mid | Any | Early | Late (post-launch) |
| **Output** | List of violations | Quant metrics + feedback | Step-by-step issues | Statistical winner |
| **Best for** | Quick check | Real user behavior | First-time use | Conversion optimization |
| **KTU bloom level** | Understand | Apply | Analyze | Evaluate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow of HCI Testing Types


## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. Differentiate between formative and summative evaluation. Give one example of each.**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Formative Evaluation** | **Summative Evaluation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Improve design; find problems to fix | Validate final product; judge quality |
| **Stage** | Early/Mid design cycle | Late/Post-design cycle |
| **Tone** | Diagnostic, iterative | Judgmental, conclusive |
| **Example** | Heuristic evaluation during prototyping | A/B testing on live website |
| **Output** | List of issues to address | Go/No-go decision, benchmark score |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Award 3 marks only if the student gives **a clear contrast + an example for each**. A one-line definition earns at most 1 mark.

---

#### **Q2. List any six types of testing used in HCI with their primary purpose.**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 mark per item):**

1. **Usability Testing** — Measures task success, time, errors with real users.
2. **Heuristic Evaluation** — Expert-based inspection against Nielsen's 10 heuristics.
3. **Cognitive Walkthrough** — Step-by-step learnability check for first-time users.
4. **A/B Testing** — Compares two interface versions using real-user metrics.
5. **Beta Testing** — Real-environment feedback from a limited external audience.
6. **Accessibility Testing** — Verifies usability for people with disabilities (WCAG compliance).
7. *(Bonus)* **Eye-Tracking Testing** — Reveals visual attention patterns.

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) Explain the Heuristic Evaluation method in detail. List Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Heuristic Evaluation — Definition (1 mark):**
A discount-usability inspection method where a small set of evaluators examines an interface and judges its compliance with recognized usability principles (heuristics). Proposed by **Jakob Nielsen and Robert Molich (1990)**, refined in 1994.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Recruit **3–5 evaluators** (HCI experts or double-domain experts).
2. Brief them on the system and the 10 heuristics.
3. Each evaluator independently explores the interface.
4. Each records every violation: location, heuristic violated, severity (0 = not a problem, 1 = cosmetic, 2 = minor, 3 = major, 4 = catastrophe).
5. Aggregate findings; remove duplicates.
6. Produce a prioritized report for designers.

**Severity Rating Scale (1 mark):** Mention the **0–4 scale** as above.

**Nielsen's 10 Heuristics (3 marks — 0.3 each):**
1. **Visibility of system status** — system keeps user informed.
2. **Match between system and real world** — speak the user's language.
3. **User control and freedom** — provide undo/redo, emergency exit.
4. **Consistency and standards** — follow platform conventions.
5. **Error prevention** — prevent problems before they occur.
6. **Recognition rather than recall** — minimize memory load.
7. **Flexibility and efficiency of use** — accelerators for expert users.
8. **Aesthetic and minimalist design** — no irrelevant information.
9. **Help users recognize, diagnose, recover from errors** — plain language.
10. **Help and documentation** — task-focused, easy to search.

**(b) With a suitable derivation, explain why Nielsen recommends 3–5 evaluators for a heuristic evaluation. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Mathematical Model (3 marks):**
Let $N$ = total usability problems in design, $p$ = detection rate per evaluator, $E$ = number of evaluators.

For independent evaluators:
$$
P(\text{issue detected by at least one}) = 1 - (1 - p)^{E}
$$

Expected issues found:
$$
\text{Found}(E) = N \cdot [1 - (1 - p)^{E}]
$$

**Numerical Substitution (2 marks):**
Using $N = 100$ and $p = 0.34$:

| **E** | **Issues Found** | **% of Total** | **Marginal Gain** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 34 | 34% | — |
| 2 | 56 | 56% | +22 |
| 3 | 71 | 71% | +15 |
| 4 | 81 | 81% | +10 |
| 5 | 87 | 87% | +6 |
| 10 | 98 | 98% | +11 (over 5 more) |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
- 3 evaluators find ~71% of issues.
- 5 evaluators find ~87% — close to saturation.
- Beyond 5, marginal benefit per additional evaluator is small.
- Hence **3–5 evaluators** offers the best cost-benefit balance: high coverage at low cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often write the formula but **forget to plug in values** and produce a numerical table → lose 2 marks.
> - Some confuse Heuristic Evaluation with Usability Testing — these are **distinct** methods. State the difference explicitly.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain Cognitive Walkthrough method in detail. What are the four key questions it addresses? [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Cognitive Walkthrough (CW), developed by **Wharton, Rieman, Lewis & Polson (1994)**, is an **expert-based, theory-driven** evaluation method focused on **learnability** — specifically whether a first-time user can accomplish a task without prior training.

**Origin (1 mark):**
Rooted in **Norman's Action Cycle** — assesses the **Gulf of Execution** (can user do?) and **Gulf of Evaluation** (can user tell what happened?).

**Procedure (1 mark):**
1. Define the target users and representative tasks.
2. Identify a correct sequence of actions for each task.
3. For each action, evaluator answers the 4 CW questions.
4. If any answer is "No," write a usability story documenting the failure.
5. Aggregate findings and prioritize redesign.

**The Four Questions (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
At each step $i$ of the task:
- **Q1**: Will the user try to achieve the correct effect?
- **Q2**: Will the user notice that the correct action is available in the interface?
- **Q3**: Will the user associate the correct action with the effect they want?
- **Q4**: Will the user see that they are making progress toward the solution?

**Advantages & Limitations (1 mark):**
- ✅ Good for walk-up-and-use systems, early design, no users needed.
- ❌ Doesn't capture user emotion or unexpected behavior.

**(b) Describe A/B Testing. With an example, show how the statistical significance is determined using the Z-test. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**A/B Testing Definition (1 mark):**
A comparative empirical method where users are randomly assigned to two variants (A = control, B = treatment); behavioral metrics (clicks, conversions, time) determine the better version.

**Steps (1.5 marks):**
1. Formulate hypothesis: $H_0$ (no difference) vs. $H_1$ (B is better).
2. Randomly split traffic 50/50.
3. Run for adequate duration to gather sample size.
4. Compute conversion rates $\hat{p}_A$ and $\hat{p}_B$.
5. Apply Z-test for two proportions.

**Worked Example (3 marks):**
A company tests a new "Buy Now" button color. After 1 week:
- Version A (blue): $n_A = 5000$, conversions = $250$ → $\hat{p}_A = 0.050$
- Version B (green): $n_B = 5000$, conversions = $300$ → $\hat{p}_B = 0.060$

Pooled proportion:
$$
\hat{p} = \frac{250 + 300}{5000 + 5000} = \frac{550}{10000} = 0.055
$$

Z-statistic:
$$
Z = \frac{0.050 - 0.060}{\sqrt{0.055 \cdot 0.945 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{5000} + \frac{1}{5000}\right)}}
$$

Numerator = $-0.010$. Denominator = $\sqrt{0.055 \cdot 0.945 \cdot 0.0004} = \sqrt{2.079 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.00456$.

$$
Z = \frac{-0.010}{0.00456} \approx -2.19
$$

Since $|Z| = 2.19 > 1.96$, **reject $H_0$** at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Conclusion (1.5 marks):**
Version B (green button) yields a **statistically significant** higher conversion rate (6% vs. 5%). Roll out Version B.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Forgetting the **pooled proportion** step → loses 1 mark.
> - Using $1.96$ instead of $2.576$ for $\alpha = 0.01$ → loses 1 mark.
> - Not stating the decision rule clearly → loses 0.5 mark.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024:**

- **6 Core Types of Testing** to memorize: Usability, Heuristic Evaluation, Cognitive Walkthrough, A/B Testing, Beta Testing, Accessibility Testing.
- **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** — listed verbatim in answers whenever heuristic evaluation is asked.
- **3–5 Evaluators** — derived via $1 - (1-p)^E$ formula; recall $p \approx 0.34$.
- **Cognitive Walkthrough's 4 Questions** — goal, perception, mapping, feedback.
- **A/B Testing** uses **two-proportion Z-test**; critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ is **1.96**.
- **SUS Score** = sum of (odd items $O-1$) + (even items $5-O$), then $\times 2.5$; benchmark **68**.
- **Formative vs. Summative** — diagnose vs. judge; pair this contrast with at least one example.
- **Heuristic Evaluation is CHEAPER and EARLIER** than usability testing but yields less ecological validity.
- **Accessibility Testing** aligns with **WCAG 2.1/2.2** standards — universally important in modern HCI.
- **Nielsen's 5-User Rule** — derived from binomial detection model; ~85% coverage.
- Always classify tests along **3 axes**: who (user/expert/model), when (formative/summative), where (lab/field).
- **Discount Usability Methods** = expert-based, no real users needed = heuristic evaluation + cognitive walkthrough.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Empirical Usability Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Empirical Usability Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical Usability Evaluation** is a **user-centered, data-driven** evaluation methodology in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) where **real end-users** perform **representative tasks** on a working prototype or finished interface, while **quantitative** (performance) and **qualitative** (preference/satisfaction) data are systematically **collected, measured, and analyzed** to determine the **effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction** of the system.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, empirical methods stand in direct contrast to **inspection-based (analytical/heuristic)** methods because empirical methods **must involve actual human participants** to generate empirical evidence. The output is a **measurable usability profile**, not an expert opinion.

### Key Properties of Empirical Evaluation

- **Empirical** → Based on observation and experiment, not theory alone.
- **User-involved** → Real participants (representative of the target user population).
- **Task-based** → Users perform realistic, scenario-driven tasks.
- **Measurable** → Outputs are numeric (time, errors, scores) or coded qualitative data.
- **Hypothesis-testable** → Supports statistical inference (t-test, ANOVA, etc.).
- **Iterative** → Drives the Design → Evaluate → Redesign cycle of UCD.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Restaurant Taste Test**
> Imagine you open a new restaurant. Before publishing the menu, you don't just ask your chef (an "expert") whether the food is good — you invite **50 actual diners** to eat, time how long they wait, count how many spill the soup, and ask them to rate taste on a form. That *real-customer, real-food, measured-result* process is exactly **empirical usability evaluation**. A heuristic evaluation would be like inviting a food critic to read the recipe on paper.

**Mapping to HCI:**

| Restaurant Element | HCI Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Real diners | Real end-users |
| Eating food | Performing tasks on the UI |
| Time to finish meal | Task completion time |
| Spilled soup | Errors / mistakes |
| Taste rating form | SUS / QUIS questionnaire |
| Number who finished | Task completion rate |

---

## 1.3 Where Empirical Evaluation Fits in UCD

The **User-Centered Design (UCD)** standard (ISO 9241-210) places empirical evaluation at the **center of every iteration**:

1. Specify context of use
2. Specify requirements
3. **Produce design solutions**
4. **Evaluate designs against requirements** ← *Empirical Evaluation happens here*

---

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Empirical methods are broadly classified into:
> 1. **Usability Testing (Lab/Field)** — controlled experiments with users.
> 2. **Field Studies / Contextual Inquiry** — natural-use observation.
> 3. **Surveys & Questionnaires** — post-use satisfaction.
> 4. **Controlled Experiments** — hypothesis-driven with IV/DV.
> 5. **A/B Testing** — comparing two design variants.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Empirical Evaluation

Every empirical study in HCI is structured around three conceptual pillars:

### Pillar 1 — **Effectiveness**
*Can users successfully complete the task?*
- Task completion rate
- Number of errors
- Goal achievement

### Pillar 2 — **Efficiency**
*How much effort/resources are required?*
- Task time
- Number of clicks / interactions
- Learnability (time-to-competence)

### Pillar 3 — **Satisfaction**
*Do users like the experience?*
- SUS score
- QUIS score
- Net Promoter Score (NPS)

These map directly onto the **ISO 9241-11** usability definition, which is **frequently asked in KTU exams**.

---

## 2.2 Types of Empirical Methods — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Usability Testing (Laboratory)
- Users brought into a **controlled environment**.
- **Think-aloud protocol** is the dominant data-gathering technique.
- Strengths: high internal validity, controlled comparison.
- Weaknesses: artificial context ("Hawthorne effect"), cost.

### 2.2.2 Field Studies
- Observation in the user's **natural environment** (office, home, factory).
- Contextual Inquiry, Ethnography.
- Strengths: ecological validity.
- Weaknesses: less control, harder to replicate.

### 2.2.3 Controlled Experiments
- Manipulate an **Independent Variable (IV)** (e.g., menu layout).
- Measure a **Dependent Variable (DV)** (e.g., task time).
- Apply statistical tests (t-test, ANOVA).
- Supports **causal inference**.

### 2.2.4 Surveys / Questionnaires
- Standardized instruments: **SUS, QUIS, NASA-TLX**.
- Administered post-task or post-session.
- Strengths: scalable, quantitative, easy to compare.

### 2.2.5 A/B Testing
- Live deployment of two variants.
- Random assignment of users.
- Used heavily in web/UX practice (e.g., conversion rate optimization).

---

## 2.3 Experimental Design Vocabulary (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Definition | Example in HCI |
|---|---|---|
| **Independent Variable (IV)** | Variable the researcher manipulates | Menu type (icon vs. text) |
| **Dependent Variable (DV)** | Variable that is measured | Task completion time |
| **Control Variable** | Held constant to isolate IV | Screen brightness, lighting |
| **Within-Subjects Design** | Same users experience ALL conditions | Each user tries both menus |
| **Between-Subjects Design** | Different user groups for each condition | Group A uses menu X, Group B uses menu Y |
| **Counterbalancing** | Order of conditions varied to remove learning bias | Latin-square ordering |
| **Pilot Study** | Small-scale trial run before main study | 2–3 users before testing 30 |
| **Sample Size (n)** | Number of participants | n = 30 users |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Usability Metrics & Statistics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **every quantitative formula** you may need to compute for an empirical evaluation question in KTU exams. Note: the vertical bar `|` is replaced by `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| # | Metric / Formula | Equation | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** | $\text{TCR} = \dfrac{\text{Successful Users}}{\text{Total Users}} \times 100$ | 0–100 % |
| 2 | **Mean Task Time** | $\bar{t} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n}$ | seconds |
| 3 | **Standard Deviation of Time** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_i - \bar{t})^2}{n-1}}$ | seconds |
| 4 | **Error Rate** | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{\text{Total Errors}}{\text{Total Tasks Completed}}$ | errors/task |
| 5 | **Efficiency** | $\text{Eff} = \dfrac{\text{TCR}}{\bar{t}}$ | % per second |
| 6 | **SUS Score** | $\text{SUS} = 2.5 \times \sum_{k=1}^{10} s_k$ (after recoding) | 0–100 |
| 7 | **Cohen's d (Effect Size)** | $d = \dfrac{\vert \bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2 \vert}{s_{\text{pooled}}}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Pooled Std. Deviation** | $s_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}}$ | same as x |
| 9 | **Independent Samples t-statistic** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1}+\dfrac{1}{n_2}}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Paired t-statistic** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{d}}{s_d/\sqrt{n}}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | **Confidence Interval (95 %)** | $\text{CI} = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | same as x |
| 12 | **Nielsen's Sample Heuristic** | n ≈ 5–10 finds ~ 85 % of issues | users |
| 13 | **Z-Score** | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | **Learnability Index** | $\text{LI} = \dfrac{T_{\text{first}} - T_{\text{final}}}{T_{\text{first}}} \times 100$ | % |
| 15 | **Cronbach's α (Reliability)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \sigma_{Y_i}^2}{\sigma_X^2}\right)$ | ≥ 0.70 good |

Where:
- $t_i$ = task time for user $i$
- $n$ = number of users / observations
- $s_k$ = recoded score for SUS question $k$
- $k$ = number of questionnaire items
- $\bar{d}$ = mean of paired differences
- $s_d$ = std. dev. of paired differences

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real-World HCI Practice

- **Web & Mobile UX**: A/B tests for button color, layout, copy.
- **Automotive HMI**: Comparing voice vs. touch dashboard controls.
- **Medical Devices**: Validating infusion-pump interfaces before FDA approval (IEC 62366).
- **Aviation Cockpits**: Empirical certification of Glass Cockpit displays.
- **Accessibility Studies**: Testing screen-reader compatibility with visually impaired participants.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip**: A 14-mark Part B question on this topic often asks you to (a) **design an experiment** (identify IV, DV, hypotheses) and (b) **compute one statistical metric** from provided raw data. Memorize the formulas above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No shortcuts. Read sequentially.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Mean, Standard Deviation, and TCR

**Problem (typical KTU style):**
A usability test was conducted with **8 users** performing a checkout task on a shopping app. The following data was collected:

| User | Time (sec) | Errors | Completed? |
|---|---|---|---|
| U1 | 42 | 1 | Yes |
| U2 | 55 | 2 | Yes |
| U3 | 38 | 0 | Yes |
| U4 | 60 | 3 | No |
| U5 | 47 | 1 | Yes |
| U6 | 51 | 2 | Yes |
| U7 | 44 | 0 | Yes |
| U8 | 49 | 1 | Yes |

**Compute: (i) Mean task time, (ii) Standard deviation, (iii) Task Completion Rate.**

### Step 1 — Compute the Mean ($\bar{t}$)

$$
\bar{t} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n}
$$

$$
\bar{t} = \frac{42 + 55 + 38 + 60 + 47 + 51 + 44 + 49}{8}
$$

$$
\bar{t} = \frac{386}{8} = 48.25 \;\text{seconds}
$$

**Explanation:** We sum all 8 individual task times and divide by the count of users (8).

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Standard Deviation ($s$)

First compute squared deviations $(t_i - \bar{t})^2$:

| User | $t_i$ | $t_i - \bar{t}$ | $(t_i - \bar{t})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| U1 | 42 | -6.25 | 39.0625 |
| U2 | 55 | 6.75 | 45.5625 |
| U3 | 38 | -10.25 | 105.0625 |
| U4 | 60 | 11.75 | 138.0625 |
| U5 | 47 | -1.25 | 1.5625 |
| U6 | 51 | 2.75 | 7.5625 |
| U7 | 44 | -4.25 | 18.0625 |
| U8 | 49 | 0.75 | 0.5625 |

Sum of squared deviations:
$$
\sum (t_i - \bar{t})^2 = 39.0625 + 45.5625 + 105.0625 + 138.0625 + 1.5625 + 7.5625 + 18.0625 + 0.5625
$$

$$
\sum (t_i - \bar{t})^2 = 355.5
$$

Now apply the formula (sample standard deviation, $n-1$ in denominator):

$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_i - \bar{t})^2}{n-1}}
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{355.5}{8 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{355.5}{7}} = \sqrt{50.7857}
$$

$$
s \approx 7.13 \;\text{seconds}
$$

**Explanation:** The sample standard deviation uses $n-1$ (Bessel's correction) because the sample mean $\bar{t}$ is itself an estimate of the population mean.

---

### Step 3 — Task Completion Rate (TCR)

Successful users = 7 (only U4 failed).
Total users = 8.

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{7}{8} \times 100 = 87.5\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A TCR of 87.5 % is generally considered **acceptable** (> 78 % is the industry benchmark).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Independent Samples t-Test

**Problem:**
Two groups of users test two different menu layouts.
- **Group A (Icon menu):** $n_1 = 10$, $\bar{x}_1 = 42$ s, $s_1 = 6$ s
- **Group B (Text menu):** $n_2 = 10$, $\bar{x}_2 = 51$ s, $s_2 = 7$ s

**Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether there is a significant difference.**

### Step 1 — State the Hypotheses

- $H_0$: $\mu_1 = \mu_2$ (no difference)
- $H_1$: $\mu_1 \neq \mu_2$ (two-tailed)

### Step 2 — Pooled Standard Deviation

$$
s_p = \sqrt{\frac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}}
$$

$$
s_p = \sqrt{\frac{(9)(36) + (9)(49)}{10+10-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{324 + 441}{18}} = \sqrt{\frac{765}{18}}
$$

$$
s_p = \sqrt{42.5} \approx 6.519
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the t-Statistic

$$
t = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1}+\frac{1}{n_2}}}
$$

$$
t = \frac{42 - 51}{6.519 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{10}}}
$$

$$
t = \frac{-9}{6.519 \cdot \sqrt{0.2}} = \frac{-9}{6.519 \cdot 0.4472}
$$

$$
t = \frac{-9}{2.915} \approx -3.087
$$

### Step 4 — Compare with Critical Value

Degrees of freedom: $df = n_1 + n_2 - 2 = 18$.
Critical value $t_{\text{crit}, 0.05, 18} = \pm 2.101$ (two-tailed).

Since $\vert t \vert = 3.087 > 2.101$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** There is a statistically significant difference. The icon menu (Group A) is significantly faster than the text menu (Group B).

### Step 5 — Effect Size (Cohen's d)

$$
d = \frac{\vert \bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2 \vert}{s_p} = \frac{\vert 42 - 51 \vert}{6.519} = \frac{9}{6.519} \approx 1.38
$$

**Interpretation:** $d = 1.38$ is a **large effect** (Cohen's thresholds: 0.2 = small, 0.5 = medium, 0.8 = large).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — SUS Score Calculation

**Problem:**
A user responds to the 10 SUS items (1 = Strongly Disagree, 5 = Strongly Agree):

| Item | Response |
|---|---|
| Q1 | 4 |
| Q2 | 2 |
| Q3 | 5 |
| Q4 | 1 |
| Q5 | 4 |
| Q6 | 2 |
| Q7 | 4 |
| Q8 | 1 |
| Q9 | 5 |
| Q10 | 1 |

**Compute the SUS score.**

### Step 1 — Recode the Items

- **Odd items (Q1, Q3, Q5, Q7, Q9):** score = response − 1
- **Even items (Q2, Q4, Q6, Q8, Q10):** score = 5 − response

| Item | Response | Recoded |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 (odd) | 4 | 4 − 1 = 3 |
| Q2 (even) | 2 | 5 − 2 = 3 |
| Q3 (odd) | 5 | 5 − 1 = 4 |
| Q4 (even) | 1 | 5 − 1 = 4 |
| Q5 (odd) | 4 | 4 − 1 = 3 |
| Q6 (even) | 2 | 5 − 2 = 3 |
| Q7 (odd) | 4 | 4 − 1 = 3 |
| Q8 (even) | 1 | 5 − 1 = 4 |
| Q9 (odd) | 5 | 5 − 1 = 4 |
| Q10 (even) | 1 | 5 − 1 = 4 |

### Step 2 — Sum the Recoded Scores

$$
\sum s_k = 3 + 3 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 4 + 4 + 4 = 35
$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by 2.5

$$
\text{SUS} = 2.5 \times 35 = 87.5
$$

**Interpretation:** A SUS of 87.5 is **excellent** (above average ≈ 68, industry benchmark).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — 95 % Confidence Interval

Given $\bar{x} = 48.25$, $s = 7.13$, $n = 8$ (from Example 1).

$$
\text{CI}_{95\%} = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

$$
\text{CI}_{95\%} = 48.25 \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{7.13}{\sqrt{8}}
$$

$$
\text{CI}_{95\%} = 48.25 \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{7.13}{2.828}
$$

$$
\text{CI}_{95\%} = 48.25 \pm 1.96 \cdot 2.521
$$

$$
\text{CI}_{95\%} = 48.25 \pm 4.94
$$

$$
\text{CI}_{95\%} = [43.31,\; 53.19] \;\text{seconds}
$$

**Interpretation:** We are 95 % confident that the true mean task completion time for the population lies between **43.31 s** and **53.19 s**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Test

| Research Question | Design | Statistical Test |
|---|---|---|
| Compare 2 means (different groups) | Between-subjects | Independent t-test |
| Compare 2 means (same group, 2 conditions) | Within-subjects | Paired t-test |
| Compare 3+ means (different groups) | Between-subjects | One-way ANOVA |
| Compare 3+ means (same group) | Within-subjects | Repeated-measures ANOVA |
| Find relationship between 2 numeric vars | Any | Pearson correlation $r$ |
| Find relationship between ranks | Any | Spearman correlation $\rho$ |
| Compare proportions | Categorical | Chi-square ($\chi^2$) |
| Non-normal data, 2 groups | Any | Mann-Whitney U |
| Non-normal data, paired | Within-subjects | Wilcoxon signed-rank |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Empirical Usability Evaluation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["Empirical Usability Evaluation"]
    root --> A["Lab Usability Testing"]
    root --> B["Field Studies"]
    root --> C["Controlled Experiments"]
    root --> D["Surveys and Questionnaires"]
    root --> E["A/B Testing"]

    A --> A1["Think Aloud Protocol"]
    A --> A2["Performance Measurement"]
    A --> A3["Retrospective Testing"]

    B --> B1["Contextual Inquiry"]
    B --> B2["Ethnographic Observation"]
    B --> B3["Diary Studies"]

    C --> C1["Within Subjects Design"]
    C --> C2["Between Subjects Design"]
    C --> C3["Mixed Design"]

    D --> D1["SUS Questionnaire"]
    D --> D2["QUIS"]
    D --> D3["NASA Task Load Index"]
    D --> D4["Custom Likert Scales"]

    E --> E1["Random User Assignment"]
    E --> E2["Conversion Rate Analysis"]
    E --> E3["Multivariate Testing"]
```

**Explanation of the diagram:**
The root node classifies empirical methods into five major branches. Each branch is then expanded into its concrete sub-techniques. This is a typical answer-structure used when a KTU question asks: *"Classify and explain empirical usability evaluation methods."*

---

## 4.2 The Empirical Evaluation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["Step 1: Define Goals and Hypotheses"] --> s2["Step 2: Select Participants"]
    s2 --> s3["Step 3: Design Tasks"]
    s3 --> s4["Step 4: Conduct Pilot Study"]
    s4 --> s5{"Pilot OK?"}
    s5 -- No --> s3
    s5 -- Yes --> s6["Step 5: Run Main Study"]
    s6 --> s7["Step 6: Collect Quantitative Data"]
    s6 --> s8["Step 7: Collect Qualitative Data"]
    s7 --> s9["Step 8: Statistical Analysis"]
    s8 --> s9
    s9 --> s10["Step 9: Interpret Results"]
    s10 --> s11["Step 10: Report and Redesign"]
    s11 --> s1
```

**Explanation of the diagram:**
This is the **iterative empirical evaluation lifecycle**. Note the feedback loop: after redesign, the cycle restarts. The **pilot study gate** is critical — if pilot fails, tasks must be revised before full data collection (this prevents wasting participants).

---

## 4.3 Within-Subjects vs Between-Subjects Design

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    design["Experimental Design Choice"]
    design --> ws["Within Subjects"]
    design --> bs["Between Subjects"]

    ws --> ws1["Same Users Experience All Conditions"]
    ws --> ws2["Counterbalancing Required"]
    ws --> ws3["Use Paired t Test or Repeated ANOVA"]
    ws --> ws4["Advantage: Fewer Participants"]
    ws --> ws5["Risk: Learning and Carryover Effects"]

    bs --> bs1["Different Users in Each Condition"]
    bs --> bs2["Random Assignment"]
    bs --> bs3["Use Independent t Test or One Way ANOVA"]
    bs --> bs4["Advantage: No Carryover Effects"]
    bs --> bs5["Risk: Individual Differences Confound"]
```

**Explanation of the diagram:**
Both designs are valid but address different threats to validity. Within-subjects controls for individual differences but introduces order/learning effects. Between-subjects is cleaner but needs more participants.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Usability Test Lab

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    participant["Participant Station with Computer"]
    mic["Microphone and Camera for Think Aloud"]
    observer["Observer Room Behind One Way Mirror"]
    screen["Live Screen Capture Software"]
    mobeye["Mobile Eye Tracker Optional"]
    log["Event Logger with Timestamps"]
    survey["Post Test Survey Module"]
    db["Central Data Repository"]

    participant --> mic
    mic --> observer
    participant --> screen
    screen --> db
    participant --> log
    log --> db
    mobeye --> db
    participant --> survey
    survey --> db
    observer --> db
```

**Explanation of the diagram:**
This functional architecture maps the **hardware/software components** of a typical KTU-level usability test lab. The **Central Data Repository** aggregates all streams (video, screen capture, event logs, survey responses) for later coding and statistical analysis.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define empirical usability evaluation. List any four empirical methods.**

**Model Answer:**

Empirical usability evaluation is a user-centered evaluation method where **real users** perform **representative tasks** on an interface while **measurable data** is collected to assess effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction.

Four empirical methods are:
1. **Usability Testing** (lab-based with think-aloud).
2. **Field Studies** (observation in natural environment).
3. **Controlled Experiments** (with IV/DV and statistical testing).
4. **Surveys/Questionnaires** (e.g., SUS, QUIS for satisfaction).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the System Usability Scale (SUS)? Mention its score range and any two characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

The **System Usability Scale (SUS)** is a standardized 10-item, 5-point **Likert-scale questionnaire** developed by John Brooke (1996) used to assess **perceived usability** of a system after user interaction.

- **Score range:** 0 to 100.
- **Industry average:** ~ 68 (acceptable), > 80.3 = excellent.
- **Characteristics:**
  1. It is **technology-agnostic** (can be used for any interface).
  2. It is **reliable and validated** (Cronbach's $\alpha \geq 0.85$).
  3. Odd items are **positively worded**; even items are **negatively worded** (to detect acquiescence bias).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

### Q.A (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the different types of empirical usability evaluation methods with suitable examples.**

**Model Solution:**

Empirical usability evaluation methods are classified as follows:

#### 1. **Laboratory Usability Testing**
The most common empirical method. Users are brought to a controlled lab and asked to perform predefined tasks while the researcher observes and records data.

- **Example:** Testing a new ATM interface with 15 users in a usability lab.
- **Techniques:** Think-aloud protocol, performance measurement, retrospective testing.
- **Strengths:** High internal validity, controlled environment.
- **Weaknesses:** Artificial setting may not reflect real-world usage.

#### 2. **Field Studies**
Evaluation in the user's natural environment (office, home, factory).

- **Example:** Observing nurses using an Electronic Health Record system in a hospital ward.
- **Techniques:** Contextual inquiry, ethnographic observation, diary studies.
- **Strengths:** High ecological validity, captures real workflow.
- **Weaknesses:** Less control, time-consuming, observer bias.

#### 3. **Controlled Experiments**
Hypothesis-driven studies that manipulate an **independent variable** and measure a **dependent variable**.

- **Example:** Comparing task time between two menu layouts (icon vs. text).
- **Designs:** Within-subjects, between-subjects, mixed.
- **Statistical Analysis:** t-test, ANOVA.
- **Strengths:** Supports causal inference.
- **Weaknesses:** May lack realism.

#### 4. **Surveys and Questionnaires**
Standardized instruments administered after use to capture subjective satisfaction.

- **Example:** Administering SUS to 50 users after using a mobile banking app.
- **Instruments:** SUS, QUIS, NASA-TLX, custom Likert scales.
- **Strengths:** Scalable, quantitative, easy to compare across versions.
- **Weaknesses:** Self-report bias.

#### 5. **A/B Testing**
Two or more variants of a UI are deployed live, and user behavior is measured.

- **Example:** Testing two CTA button colors on an e-commerce site.
- **Strengths:** Real-world data, large sample sizes.
- **Weaknesses:** Cannot isolate *why* a variant won.

---

### Q.A (b) — 7 Marks
**A usability test was conducted on a new mobile app with 10 users. Their task completion times (in seconds) were:**
**42, 38, 45, 50, 39, 41, 47, 44, 46, 43. If 9 out of 10 users completed the task successfully, compute:**
**(i) Mean task time, (ii) Standard deviation, (iii) Task completion rate, (iv) 95 % confidence interval.**

**Model Solution:**

#### (i) Mean Task Time

$$
\bar{t} = \frac{42 + 38 + 45 + 50 + 39 + 41 + 47 + 44 + 46 + 43}{10} = \frac{435}{10} = 43.5 \;\text{s}
$$

#### (ii) Standard Deviation

Compute squared deviations from mean 43.5:

| $t_i$ | $t_i - \bar{t}$ | $(t_i - \bar{t})^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 42 | -1.5 | 2.25 |
| 38 | -5.5 | 30.25 |
| 45 | 1.5 | 2.25 |
| 50 | 6.5 | 42.25 |
| 39 | -4.5 | 20.25 |
| 41 | -2.5 | 6.25 |
| 47 | 3.5 | 12.25 |
| 44 | 0.5 | 0.25 |
| 46 | 2.5 | 6.25 |
| 43 | -0.5 | 0.25 |

$$
\sum (t_i - \bar{t})^2 = 122.5
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{122.5}{10 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{122.5}{9}} = \sqrt{13.611} \approx 3.69 \;\text{s}
$$

#### (iii) Task Completion Rate

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{9}{10} \times 100 = 90\%
$$

#### (iv) 95 % Confidence Interval

$$
\text{CI} = 43.5 \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{3.69}{\sqrt{10}}
$$

$$
\text{CI} = 43.5 \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{3.69}{3.162}
$$

$$
\text{CI} = 43.5 \pm 1.96 \cdot 1.167
$$

$$
\text{CI} = 43.5 \pm 2.29 = [41.21,\; 45.79]\;\text{s}
$$

**Interpretation:** We are 95 % confident the true mean task time lies between **41.21 s and 45.79 s**. A TCR of 90 % is good (well above the 78 % benchmark).

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Q.B (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the think-aloud protocol used in usability testing. Mention its types and two limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

The **Think-Aloud (TA) Protocol** is a verbal-report data-collection technique where participants **continuously verbalize their thoughts** while performing a task. It provides insight into the **user's cognitive process** rather than just outcomes.

#### Procedure:
1. Brief the participant: *"Please verbalize everything you say, think, feel, and see."*
2. Provide the task.
3. Encourage continuous narration ("Keep talking").
4. Use neutral prompts: *"What are you thinking now?"* — avoid leading questions.
5. Audio/video record for later transcription and coding.

#### Diagram (verbal flow):

```
Briefing --> Task Assigned --> User Performs Task
                                     |
                                     v
                       User Verbalizes Thoughts (TA)
                                     |
                                     v
                  Researcher Records (Audio + Video)
                                     |
                                     v
              Transcription --> Coding --> Analysis
```

#### Types of Think-Aloud:

1. **Concurrent Think-Aloud** — User verbalizes *during* task execution (most common).
2. **Retrospective Think-Aloud** — User verbalizes *after* completing the task, while watching a recording. Less reactive interference, but may suffer from recall bias.

#### Two Limitations:
1. **Reactive Interference:** Verbalizing while performing a task may slow the user down and alter natural behavior (especially for experts whose tasks are highly automatic).
2. **Incomplete Verbalization:** Not all cognitive processes are accessible to conscious verbal report (e.g., pre-attentive pattern recognition).

---

### Q.B (b) — 7 Marks
**You are designing a controlled experiment to compare two versions of a search interface (A and B). Define the IV, DV, hypotheses, and identify an appropriate statistical test. Mention two threats to validity.**

**Model Solution:**

#### 1. Research Question
*Does search interface B (with faceted filters) result in faster task completion than interface A (without filters)?*

#### 2. Independent Variable (IV)
- **Name:** Interface Version
- **Levels:** A (basic) and B (with faceted filters)

#### 3. Dependent Variables (DV)
- **Primary DV:** Task completion time (seconds)
- **Secondary DVs:** Task completion rate, number of errors

#### 4. Hypotheses
- **Null hypothesis ($H_0$):** $\mu_A = \mu_B$ (no difference in mean task time).
- **Alternative hypothesis ($H_1$):** $\mu_B < \mu_A$ (one-tailed; B is faster).

#### 5. Design Choice
- **Within-subjects design** is efficient (same users test both interfaces).
- Use **counterbalancing** (Latin square) to control for order effects.
- Apply **paired samples t-test** at $\alpha = 0.05$.

#### 6. Sample Size
Based on a power analysis with effect size $d = 0.5$, power = 0.80, $\alpha = 0.05$, minimum $n \approx 34$ for paired design.

#### 7. Two Threats to Validity
1. **Learning Effect (Order Effect):** Users may be faster on the second interface simply because they have already learned the task. *Mitigation:* Counterbalancing.
2. **Selection Bias:** If users are not representative of the target population, results may not generalize. *Mitigation:* Stratified random sampling.

#### 8. Procedure Summary
```
Recruit 34 Users --> Counterbalance Order --> 
Train on Task --> Test Interface A --> 
Test Interface B --> Post-Test SUS --> 
Analyze with Paired t-Test --> Report Effect Size (Cohen's d)
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to recode SUS items** before summing (odd: subtract 1; even: subtract from 5). You will lose 1–2 marks if you skip this.
> 2. **Confusing $n$ vs $n-1$** in standard deviation. Empirical studies use **sample SD** with $n-1$ in the denominator (Bessel's correction).
> 3. **Wrong t-test choice**: Always check whether the data is **paired** (within-subjects → paired t-test) or **independent** (between-subjects → independent t-test). Mixing them up is the #1 valuation deduction.
> 4. **Neglecting effect size**: KTU 2024 rubrics reward reporting **Cohen's d** along with $p$-values, because $p$-values alone don't indicate practical significance.
> 5. **No pilot study mention**: A complete empirical design *must* include a pilot. Omitting it costs marks.
> 6. **Vague hypotheses**: Always write $H_0$ and $H_1$ *symbolically* with $\mu$, not just "there is a difference."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember (One-Glance Revision):**

- [x] **Definition:** Empirical evaluation = real users + real tasks + measurable data.
- [x] **Three pillars:** Effectiveness, Efficiency, Satisfaction (ISO 9241-11).
- [x] **Five methods:** Lab testing, Field studies, Controlled experiments, Surveys, A/B testing.
- [x] **IV / DV** must be clearly identified in any experiment.
- [x] **Within-subjects** = same users, paired t-test; **Between-subjects** = different users, independent t-test.
- [x] **Counterbalancing** neutralizes order effects.
- [x] **Pilot study** is mandatory before the main study.
- [x] **Sample size:** Nielsen's heuristic says 5 users uncover ~ 85 % of issues; for statistical tests, perform **power analysis**.
- [x] **Mean:** $\bar{x} = \sum x_i / n$.
- [x] **Sample SD:** $s = \sqrt{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 / (n-1)}$.
- [x] **TCR** = (Successful Users / Total Users) × 100.
- [x] **SUS** is 10-item, 5-point Likert; recoded and multiplied by 2.5; range 0–100.
- [x] **Cohen's d** = 0.2 small, 0.5 medium, 0.8 large.
- [x] **Confidence interval** = $\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot s/\sqrt{n}$ (for 95 % CI, large sample).
- [x] **Think-aloud** has two flavors: Concurrent and Retrospective.
- [x] **Threats to validity:** Learning effects, selection bias, Hawthorne effect, novelty effect.
- [x] **A/B testing** uses random assignment and large samples.
- [x] **Cronbach's α** $\geq 0.70$ indicates reliable questionnaire data.
- [x] Always **report effect size** alongside statistical significance in KTU answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Experiment Design (HCI Context)**
> **Experiment Design** in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) is a formal, systematic, and statistically rigorous methodology used to **plan, structure, and execute controlled studies** that evaluate user performance, satisfaction, and behavior when interacting with a designed interface. It involves the deliberate manipulation of one or more **Independent Variables (IVs)**, the measurement of one or more **Dependent Variables (DVs)**, and the strict control of **Extraneous Variables** to establish causal relationships between interface design choices and user outcomes.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is central to the **User-Centered Design (UCD)** cycle because without properly designed experiments, interface claims remain anecdotal. The discipline is rooted in **empirical research methods** borrowed from behavioral psychology and applied statistics, but adapted to the iterative, prototype-driven nature of HCI work.

### Key Terminology — Quick Glossary

| Term | Meaning in HCI Context |
|---|---|
| **Hypothesis (H₁ / H₀)** | A testable prediction about how a design change will affect users |
| **Independent Variable (IV)** | The design factor deliberately manipulated by the researcher |
| **Dependent Variable (DV)** | The measured user outcome (time, errors, satisfaction) |
| **Confounding Variable** | An uncontrolled factor that may bias the results |
| **Within-Subjects** | Each user experiences **all** conditions |
| **Between-Subjects** | Different user groups experience **different** conditions |
| **Counterbalancing** | Technique to cancel out order/learning effects |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cooking Show Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Imagine a chef wants to know whether adding **salt at the beginning** vs. **adding salt at the end** of a soup recipe makes it taste better. They cannot simply cook both soups at different times — because the weather, the cook's mood, the brand of vegetables, and the pot used will all differ.

An **experiment** is the chef's attempt to **cook both soups at the same time, in identical pots, with identical ingredients, judged by the same tasters** — and then *measure* which soup scores higher. The only thing the chef intentionally changes is the **timing of the salt**. That is the **Independent Variable**. The **taste score** is the **Dependent Variable**. Everything else is held constant — that is **experimental control**.

**In HCI**, the soup is a *user interface*, the timing of the salt is a *design decision* (e.g., placement of a button), the tasters are *participants*, and the score is a *usability metric*. Without this discipline, the chef (designer) cannot be sure that any observed difference is genuinely due to the design choice rather than chance.

### Why This Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners expect students to **distinguish experiment design from informal usability testing**. The presence of **hypotheses, controlled variables, and statistical inference** is the dividing line. Mentioning "I asked 5 friends" is **NOT** an experiment.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Any HCI Experiment

1. **Manipulation** — The researcher deliberately changes the IV (e.g., two versions of a menu — dropdown vs. cascading).
2. **Measurement** — The researcher records a quantifiable DV (e.g., task completion time, error count, NASA-TLX score).
3. **Control** — All other variables are either held constant or randomized across conditions.

> [!TIP]
> If your study lacks any one of these three pillars, you have not designed an *experiment* — you have performed an *observational study* or *case study*. This distinction frequently appears in **Part A (3-mark)** KTU questions.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of an HCI Experiment

A well-designed HCI experiment is built in **five structured phases**. Each phase answers a specific question and feeds the next.

### Phase 1 — Observation and Motivation

- The researcher identifies a **usability problem** or a **design alternative** (e.g., "Does placing the search bar at the top yield faster task completion than placing it on the left sidebar?").
- This phase is grounded in **earlier formative studies** (interviews, heuristic evaluations, contextual inquiries).

### Phase 2 — Hypothesis Formulation

- Formulate a **Null Hypothesis (H₀)** stating that *no effect exists*.
- Formulate an **Alternative Hypothesis (H₁)** stating that *the design choice has a measurable effect*.
- Hypotheses must be **falsifiable** and stated **before** data collection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** If you cannot state your hypothesis in a single sentence using the format *“There will be a significant difference in [DV] between [Condition A] and [Condition B],”* your experiment is poorly defined.

### Phase 3 — Variable Architecture

- Identify **Independent Variables (IVs)** — usually 1 to 2 in standard HCI studies.
- Identify **Dependent Variables (DVs)** — usually 1 to 3 (performance, preference, workload).
- Identify and **control** the **Confounding Variables** (lighting, screen size, prior experience, age, gender).
- **Randomization** assigns participants to conditions to neutralize individual differences.

### Phase 4 — Participant Selection & Sample Size

- Use **purposive sampling** to recruit users who match the target demographic.
- Determine sample size using **statistical power analysis** (covered in Section 3).
- **N = 10 to 12** is the *industry heuristic* for detecting large usability differences, but academic experiments typically require **N ≥ 30** per condition for parametric tests.

### Phase 5 — Procedure, Data Collection & Analysis

- Prepare a **standardized task script** so all participants receive identical instructions.
- Counterbalance the order of conditions (for within-subjects designs).
- Use **pilot testing** to refine timing, instructions, and instrumentation.
- Analyze the data using the **correct statistical test** (t-test, ANOVA, Wilcoxon, Friedman — depending on design and data type).

---

## 2.2 Choosing the Experimental Design Structure

The choice of design structure is the **single most important decision** in experiment design because it determines what conclusions can be drawn.

| Design Type | Structure | Strength | Weakness | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Between-Subjects** | Different users in each condition | No carryover / learning effects | Requires more participants | When tasks cannot be repeated or learning is permanent |
| **Within-Subjects** | Same users in all conditions | Higher statistical power, fewer participants | Order effects, fatigue | When tasks are short and learning is minimal |
| **Split-Plot / Mixed** | Combination of both | Flexible | More complex analysis | When one IV is naturally between (e.g., expertise) and another within (e.g., color) |
| **Latin Square** | Within-subjects with order counterbalancing | Controls for order effects | Requires N divisible by number of conditions | When order effects are suspected |

### Counterbalancing Mechanics

For **k** conditions, you can create a **k × k Latin Square** where each condition appears in each row position exactly once. For example, with 3 conditions (A, B, C) and 6 participants:

| Participant | Order 1 | Order 2 | Order 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | A | B | C |
| P2 | B | C | A |
| P3 | C | A | B |
| P4 | A | C | B |
| P5 | B | A | C |
| P6 | C | B | A |

> [!TIP]
> **Full counterbalancing** (all k! orders) is only practical when k ≤ 4. Beyond that, a **Latin Square** is the standard compromise and is what KTU examiners expect as the default answer for within-subjects designs.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **mathematical backbone** of HCI experiment design. KTU questions in Module 4 frequently test both the **meaning** and the **computation** of these formulas.

| Formula | Expression | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sample Size (Power Analysis)** | See Section 3 derivation | Required N for given effect size, α, power | Planning an experiment |
| **Effect Size — Cohen's d** | $d = \dfrac{\bar{X}_{1} - \bar{X}_{2}}{s_{pooled}}$ | Standardized mean difference | Comparing two conditions |
| **Pooled Standard Deviation** | $s_{p} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(n_{1}-1)s_{1}^{2} + (n_{2}-1)s_{2}^{2}}{n_{1} + n_{2} - 2}}$ | Combined SD for two groups | Computing Cohen's d |
| **Effect Size — η² (Eta Squared)** | $\eta^{2} = \dfrac{SS_{effect}}{SS_{total}}$ | Proportion of variance explained | ANOVA-based studies |
| **Statistical Power (1 − β)** | $1 - \beta = P(\text{reject } H_{0} \mid H_{1} \text{ true})$ | Probability of detecting a real effect | Sample size planning |
| **Bonferroni Correction** | $\alpha_{adjusted} = \dfrac{\alpha}{k}$ | Adjusted alpha for multiple comparisons | Multiple DVs or post-hoc tests |
| **Confidence Interval (Mean)** | $CI = \bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Range likely to contain true mean | Reporting results |
| **Mean** | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum X_{i}}{n}$ | Central tendency | All experiments |
| **Variance** | $s^{2} = \dfrac{\sum (X_{i} - \bar{X})^{2}}{n - 1}$ | Spread of measurements | All experiments |
| **Standard Error of the Mean** | $SE = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Precision of the sample mean | Reporting results |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL TYPING RULE:** KTU examiners frequently use the pipe symbol $\vert$ or $\mid$ in their notation (e.g., $\vert t \vert > t_{crit}$). When writing inside markdown tables, **never** use a bare $\vert$ because it will break the table layout. Always escape it as $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX form.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Experiment design is not an academic luxury — it is the **foundation of evidence-based design** in industry:

- **Microsoft** uses controlled A/B experiments to test every change in Windows, Office, and Bing.
- **Google** runs **thousands of concurrent A/B experiments** on search results, with billions of users as participants.
- **Automotive UX teams** run within-subjects driving simulator studies to compare dashboard layouts.
- **Medical device interfaces** (e.g., infusion pumps) undergo FDA-mandated usability experiments with formal hypothesis testing.
- **E-commerce platforms** (Amazon, Flipkart) test button colors, copy text, and checkout flows using randomized controlled experiments with millions of users.

> [!NOTE]
> The skill of designing a valid HCI experiment is what separates a **UI designer** from a **UX researcher** in the job market. KTU Module 4 directly targets this professional distinction.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sample Size for a Two-Condition Experiment (t-test)

This is the **most frequently tested derivation** in KTU Module 4. We want to find the minimum number of participants $n$ per condition needed to detect a true effect of size $d$ with probability $1 - \beta$ (power) at significance level $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — State the underlying relationship.**

For a two-sample t-test, the non-centrality parameter $\delta$ relates sample size, effect size, and power:

$$\delta = d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{n}{2}}$$

Here, $n$ is the sample size *per group* (assuming equal groups), and $d$ is Cohen's standardized mean difference.

**Step 2 — Express the required non-centrality in terms of the critical value and the power.**

For a two-tailed test at significance $\alpha$, the test rejects $H_{0}$ when the test statistic exceeds the critical value $t_{crit} = t_{\alpha/2, \, df}$ in absolute value. For a one-sample-like logic, the non-centrality needed to achieve power $1 - \beta$ is approximated by:

$$\delta = z_{1 - \alpha/2} + z_{1 - \beta}$$

where $z_{p}$ is the standard normal quantile at cumulative probability $p$.

> **Conversion logic:** The first term is the threshold above which we reject $H_0$; the second term is the distance the true mean must lie from $H_0$ for power $1 - \beta$. Their sum gives the total standardized distance required.

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $\delta$.**

$$d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{n}{2}} = z_{1 - \alpha/2} + z_{1 - \beta}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$.**

Square both sides:

$$d^{2} \cdot \dfrac{n}{2} = \left( z_{1 - \alpha/2} + z_{1 - \beta} \right)^{2}$$

Isolate $n$:

$$n = \dfrac{2 \cdot \left( z_{1 - \alpha/2} + z_{1 - \beta} \right)^{2}}{d^{2}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the *per-group* sample size. Multiply by 2 for the total sample.

**Step 5 — Final working formula.**

$$n_{per\,group} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \left( z_{1 - \alpha/2} + z_{1 - \beta} \right)^{2}}{d^{2}}$$

---

### Worked Numerical Example

A UX researcher wants to test whether a new icon set reduces task completion time. From a pilot study, the expected effect size is $d = 0.5$ (medium), $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed), and desired power $1 - \beta = 0.80$.

**Substitute the z-values:**

$$z_{1 - \alpha/2} = z_{0.975} = 1.96$$

$$z_{1 - \beta} = z_{0.80} = 0.84$$

**Compute the numerator:**

$$2 \cdot (1.96 + 0.84)^{2} = 2 \cdot (2.80)^{2} = 2 \cdot 7.84 = 15.68$$

**Compute the denominator:**

$$d^{2} = (0.5)^{2} = 0.25$$

**Solve:**

$$n_{per\,group} = \dfrac{15.68}{0.25} = 62.72 \approx 63$$

**Conclusion:** The researcher needs at least **63 participants per group**, or **126 in total**, to detect a medium effect at 80% power with 95% confidence.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU memory aid:** A commonly used shortcut for $d = 0.5$, $\alpha = 0.05$, power = 0.80 is **$n \approx 64$ per group**. Memorize this; it appears in many exam solutions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cohen's d (Effect Size)

**Step 1 — Define the difference in group means.**

$$\Delta = \bar{X}_{1} - \bar{X}_{2}$$

**Step 2 — Define the pooled standard deviation** (assuming equal variances and unequal $n$):

$$s_{p} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(n_{1} - 1) s_{1}^{2} + (n_{2} - 1) s_{2}^{2}}{n_{1} + n_{2} - 2}}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$d = \dfrac{\bar{X}_{1} - \bar{X}_{2}}{s_{p}}$$

### Interpretation Thresholds (Cohen, 1988)

| Effect Size $d$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| 0.2 | Small effect |
| 0.5 | Medium effect |
| 0.8 | Large effect |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Confidence Interval for the Mean

**Step 1 — Start with the sampling distribution of the mean.**

The sample mean $\bar{X}$ is normally distributed around the population mean $\mu$ with standard error:

$$SE = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 2 — For 95% confidence**, the critical z-value is $z_{0.025} = 1.96$ (for large $n$).

**Step 3 — The 95% CI is constructed as:**

$$CI_{95\%} = \bar{X} \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The 1.96 factor corresponds to the standard normal cutoff that contains the central 95% of the distribution.

**Step 4 — General form for confidence level $1 - \alpha$:**

$$CI = \bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Statistical Test

| Research Question | IV Type | DV Type | Design | Recommended Test |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Comparing 2 means (independent groups) | Categorical (2 levels) | Continuous | Between-Subjects | Independent samples t-test |
| Comparing 2 means (same group) | Categorical (2 levels) | Continuous | Within-Subjects | Paired samples t-test |
| Comparing 3+ means (one factor) | Categorical (3+ levels) | Continuous | Between | One-Way ANOVA |
| Comparing 3+ means (one factor) | Categorical (3+ levels) | Continuous | Within | Repeated-Measures ANOVA |
| Effect of 2+ factors | 2+ Categorical | Continuous | Mixed | Factorial ANOVA |
| Non-normal data, 2 groups | Categorical (2 levels) | Ordinal/Ranked | Between | Mann-Whitney U |
| Non-normal data, paired | Categorical (2 levels) | Ordinal/Ranked | Within | Wilcoxon Signed-Rank |
| Non-normal data, 3+ groups | Categorical (3+ levels) | Ordinal/Ranked | Within | Friedman Test |
| Association between 2 categorical | — | Categorical | — | Chi-Square ($\chi^{2}$) |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"If the data is not normally distributed, what non-parametric test replaces the paired t-test?"* The expected answer is **Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Threats to Validity

| Threat | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Selection Bias** | Pre-existing differences between groups | Random assignment of participants |
| **History Effect** | External event during the experiment | Run all conditions in parallel or randomize order |
| **Maturation** | Participants get tired/bored over time | Keep sessions short; within-subjects counterbalancing |
| **Instrumentation** | Measurement tool drifts over time | Calibrate before each session |
| **Regression to the Mean** | Extreme scores naturally move toward average | Use control group for comparison |
| **Testing Effect** | Pre-test influences post-test performance | Use Solomon four-group design if needed |
| **Experimenter Bias** | Researcher's expectations affect outcome | Double-blind protocol |
| **Demand Characteristics** | Participants guess the hypothesis | Use cover stories; mask the true purpose |
</br>

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete HCI Experimental Design Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Identify Usability Problem] --> obsB[Conduct Formative Study]
    obsB --> hypC[Formulate H0 and H1]
    hypC --> varD[Define IVs DVs and Controls]
    varD --> sampE[Select Participants via Power Analysis]
    sampE --> pilotF[Pilot Test the Protocol]
    pilotF --> runG[Execute Main Experiment]
    runG --> dataH[Collect Quantitative and Qualitative Data]
    dataH --> statI[Apply Statistical Test]
    statI --> interpJ{Is p less than alpha}
    interpJ -->|Yes| rejK[Reject H0 Accept H1]
    interpJ -->|No| failL[Fail to Reject H0]
    rejK --> reportM[Report Results and Effect Size]
    failL --> reportM
    reportM --> designN[Iterate the Interface Design]
    designN --> startA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The loop at the end emphasizes that experiment design is **iterative**. Results feed back into the next design cycle — this is the **Spiral Model of HCI experimentation** that KTU examiners reward in 14-mark questions.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Anatomy of an Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    participantA[Participant Pool] --> randB[Random Assignment]
    randB --> grp1[Group 1 Condition A]
    randB --> grp2[Group 2 Condition B]
    grp1 --> task1[Standardized Task Script]
    grp2 --> task2[Standardized Task Script]
    task1 --> iv1[Independent Variable Applied]
    task2 --> iv2[Independent Variable Applied]
    iv1 --> measure1[Measure DV Time Errors SUS Score]
    iv2 --> measure2[Measure DV Time Errors SUS Score]
    measure1 --> stats[Statistical Analysis t test ANOVA]
    measure2 --> stats
    stats --> result[Result and Conclusion]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Use this block diagram** when answering any KTU question on *between-subjects* design structure. It visually demonstrates control, manipulation, and measurement — exactly the three pillars examiners look for.

---

## 4.3 Counterbalancing Flow (Within-Subjects Design)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    entryP[Participant Enters Lab] --> seqA[Sequence 1 Condition A then B then C]
    entryP --> seqB[Sequence 2 Condition B then C then A]
    entryP --> seqC[Sequence 3 Condition C then A then B]
    seqA --> exec[Execute Tasks Under Each Condition]
    seqB --> exec
    seqC --> exec
    exec --> collect[Collect DV Measurements]
    collect --> analyze[Apply Repeated Measures ANOVA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three sequences represent the rows of a **3 × 3 Latin Square**. KTU students should be able to draw or describe this counterbalancing structure in 2 to 3 lines of an exam answer.

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{How many groups or conditions}
    q1 -->|2 groups| q2{Are groups independent or paired}
    q1 -->|3 or more groups| q3{Are groups independent or repeated}
    q1 -->|2 categorical variables| chi[Use Chi Square Test]
    q2 -->|Independent| q4{Is data normally distributed}
    q2 -->|Paired| q5{Is data normally distributed}
    q3 -->|Independent| q6{Is data normally distributed}
    q3 -->|Repeated| q7{Is data normally distributed}
    q4 -->|Yes| t1[Independent Samples t test]
    q4 -->|No| m1[Mann Whitney U Test]
    q5 -->|Yes| t2[Paired Samples t test]
    q5 -->|No| w1[Wilcoxon Signed Rank Test]
    q6 -->|Yes| a1[One Way ANOVA]
    q6 -->|No| k1[Kruskal Wallis Test]
    q7 -->|Yes| a2[Repeated Measures ANOVA]
    q7 -->|No| f1[Friedman Test]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The most common error students make is choosing a t-test for 3 or more groups. Always count the *number of levels of the IV* first — if there are 3 or more, **ANOVA** (or its non-parametric equivalent) is mandatory.
</br>
