# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme pattern**: Part A short-answer (3 marks) and Part B long-answer with internal choice (14 marks split as 7 + 7 sub-parts). The KTU Examiner tag indicates the typical cognitive level tested.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define an experiment in the context of Human-Computer Interaction. List its three essential characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **experiment in HCI** is a formal study in which the researcher deliberately manipulates one or more **independent variables (IVs)** and measures the resulting effect on one or more **dependent variables (DVs)** under controlled conditions to draw causal conclusions about an interface design.

The three essential characteristics are:
1. **Manipulation** of the independent variable (e.g., two versions of a menu).
2. **Measurement** of the dependent variable (e.g., task completion time).
3. **Control** of extraneous and confounding variables (e.g., same lighting, same screen, randomized participants).

> All three must be present; otherwise the study is not a true experiment. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between a within-subjects and a between-subjects experimental design. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Within-Subjects | Between-Subjects |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | The *same* participants experience *all* conditions | *Different* participants experience *each* condition |
| Advantage | Higher statistical power, requires fewer participants (1.5 marks) | No carryover or learning effects; suitable when tasks are non-repeatable (1.5 marks) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions are **14 marks** with a **mandatory internal choice** (i.e., the paper will offer either Question A **OR** Question B). The marks are typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different **types of experimental designs** used in HCI. Compare **within-subjects** and **between-subjects** designs with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An HCI researcher wants to test whether a **new touchscreen keyboard** improves typing speed compared to the existing on-screen keyboard. The pilot study suggests a **medium effect size** ($d = 0.5$). Using $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) and desired power $1 - \beta = 0.80$, calculate the **minimum number of participants per group** required for the experiment.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Types of Experimental Designs in HCI (4 marks):**

1. **Between-Subjects (Independent Groups) Design** — Different participants are assigned to each condition. Example: Group A uses a dropdown menu; Group B uses a cascading menu. Each user uses only one version.

2. **Within-Subjects (Repeated Measures) Design** — The same participants use all conditions. Example: Every user tests both the dropdown and the cascading menu in counterbalanced order.

3. **Split-Plot / Mixed Design** — Combines both; one IV is between-subjects (e.g., novice vs. expert users) and another is within-subjects (e.g., menu type). Useful for studying expertise effects.

4. **Latin Square Design** — A specific form of within-subjects design that counterbalances order using a Latin Square arrangement. Controls for order and learning effects.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Within-Subjects | Between-Subjects |
|---|---|---|
| Number of participants | Fewer (each user contributes to all conditions) | More (separate users per condition) |
| Statistical power | Higher | Lower |
| Carryover / learning effects | Present (mitigated by counterbalancing) | Absent |
| Time per participant | Longer (does all conditions) | Shorter |
| Suitable for | Short, repeatable tasks | Long, non-repeatable tasks |
| Example | Comparing two icon sets on the same home screen | Comparing two completely different home-screen layouts |

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the formula (2 marks):**

$$n_{per\,group} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \left( z_{1 - \alpha/2} + z_{1 - \beta} \right)^{2}}{d^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the given values (1 mark):**

- Effect size $d = 0.5$
- Significance $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) $\Rightarrow z_{1 - \alpha/2} = z_{0.975} = 1.96$
- Power $1 - \beta = 0.80$ $\Rightarrow z_{1 - \beta} = z_{0.80} = 0.84$

**Step 3 — Substitute and compute (3 marks):**

$$n_{per\,group} = \dfrac{2 \cdot (1.96 + 0.84)^{2}}{(0.5)^{2}}$$

$$= \dfrac{2 \cdot (2.80)^{2}}{0.25}$$

$$= \dfrac{2 \cdot 7.84}{0.25}$$

$$= \dfrac{15.68}{0.25} = 62.72$$

**Step 4 — Round up and conclude (1 mark):**

Since the sample size must be a whole number and we must *round up* to maintain power:

$$n_{per\,group} \approx 63$$

**Conclusion:** The researcher needs at least **63 participants per group**, for a total of **126 participants**, to detect a medium effect with 80% power at 95% confidence.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the concept of **hypothesis testing** in HCI experiments. With a suitable example, explain **Null Hypothesis (H₀)** and **Alternative Hypothesis (H₁)**. What are **Type I** and **Type II** errors?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **threats to internal and external validity** that can affect an HCI experiment. For each threat, suggest **one mitigation strategy**.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Hypothesis Testing in HCI (3 marks):**

Hypothesis testing is a formal statistical procedure used to determine whether the observed effect in an experiment is **statistically significant** or whether it could have occurred by chance. The researcher begins by assuming the **null hypothesis (H₀)** is true and then uses sample data to decide whether to reject it in favor of the **alternative hypothesis (H₁)**.

**Example (2 marks):**

Suppose a researcher wants to test whether a new gesture-based interface is faster than the traditional button-based interface.

- $H_0$: There is no significant difference in task completion time between the gesture-based and button-based interfaces.
- $H_1$: There is a significant difference in task completion time between the gesture-based and button-based interfaces (i.e., the gesture interface is faster).

**Type I and Type II Errors (2 marks):**

| Error | Meaning | Probability |
|---|---|---|
| **Type I Error** | Rejecting $H_0$ when it is actually true (false positive — claiming a design is better when it is not) | $\alpha$ (significance level, e.g., 0.05) |
| **Type II Error** | Failing to reject $H_0$ when $H_1$ is actually true (false negative — missing a real improvement) | $\beta$ |

**Statistical power** $= 1 - \beta$ is the probability of correctly rejecting $H_0$ when it is false.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Internal Validity Threats (3.5 marks) and Mitigations:**

| Threat | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Selection Bias** | Pre-existing differences between groups | Random assignment of participants |
| **History Effect** | External event during the experiment | Run conditions simultaneously |
| **Maturation** | Participants fatigue over time | Short sessions; rest breaks |
| **Instrumentation** | Measurement tool changes mid-experiment | Calibrate before each session |
| **Experimenter Bias** | Researcher unintentionally influences outcome | Use a double-blind protocol |
| **Demand Characteristics** | Participants guess the hypothesis | Use cover stories; mask true purpose |

**External Validity Threats (3.5 marks) and Mitigations:**

| Threat | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Population Validity** | Sample not representative of target users | Use stratified random sampling |
| **Ecological Validity** | Lab setting differs from real-world use | Conduct field studies in addition |
| **Treatment Variation** | IV not delivered consistently | Use a standardized task script |
| **Temporal Validity** | Results time-bound; technology evolves | Replicate study periodically |

> **Conclusion (brief):** A well-designed HCI experiment must control internal validity threats to *establish causality* and address external validity threats to *generalize the findings* to the broader user population.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Experiment Design Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "usability test" with "experiment."** A usability test may not have a hypothesis or IV manipulation — these are *not* experiments.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the null hypothesis** before describing the alternative. Both are required for full marks.
> 3. **Choosing the wrong statistical test** (e.g., using multiple t-tests instead of ANOVA for 3+ groups). This is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU valuation keys.
> 4. **Forgetting to round up the sample size** — fractional $n$ is incorrect; always round **up** to preserve statistical power.
> 5. **Confusing Type I and Type II errors.** Mnemonic: *Type I = "false alarm" (false positive); Type II = "missed detection" (false negative).*
> 6. **Omitting counterbalancing** in within-subjects designs — examiners deduct at least 1 mark if order effects are not addressed.
> 7. **No mention of effect size** when reporting results. KTU 2024 scheme specifically rewards reporting both *p-value* **and** *effect size* (Cohen's d or $\eta^2$).
> 8. **Vague hypothesis wording** like "the new design is better." Examiners require *measurable* DVs and *specific* conditions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

- **Definition:** An HCI experiment involves **manipulation**, **measurement**, and **control**.
- **Three Variable Types:** Independent (IV), Dependent (DV), Controlled/Confounding.
- **Two Hypotheses:** $H_0$ (no effect) and $H_1$ (effect exists). Both must be stated.
- **Two Error Types:** Type I ($\alpha$, false positive) and Type II ($\beta$, false negative). Power = $1 - \beta$.
- **Design Choice:**
  - *Between-Subjects* → Different users per condition. No carryover, needs more users.
  - *Within-Subjects* → Same users, all conditions. Higher power, but needs **counterbalancing**.
  - *Mixed / Split-Plot* → Combination for studying individual differences.
- **Counterbalancing:** Use **Latin Square** when $k \leq 4$ conditions; full permutation impractical beyond that.
- **Sample Size Formula (per group):** $n = \dfrac{2 \cdot (z_{1 - \alpha/2} + z_{1 - \beta})^{2}}{d^{2}}$ — **always round UP**.
- **Common Defaults:** $\alpha = 0.05$, Power $= 0.80$, $d = 0.5$ (medium) gives $n \approx 64$/group.
- **Effect Size Interpretation (Cohen's d):** 0.2 = small, 0.5 = medium, 0.8 = large.
- **Statistical Test Selection:**
  - 2 groups, independent, normal → **Independent t-test**
  - 2 groups, paired, normal → **Paired t-test**
  - 2 groups, non-normal → **Mann-Whitney U / Wilcoxon**
  - 3+ groups, independent, normal → **One-Way ANOVA**
  - 3+ groups, repeated, normal → **Repeated Measures ANOVA**
  - 2 categorical variables → **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$)**
- **Reporting:** Always report **p-value**, **effect size**, and **confidence interval** — not just significance.
- **Validity:**
  - *Internal* threats (selection, history, maturation, instrumentation, experimenter bias) → controlled by design.
  - *External* threats (population, ecological) → controlled by sampling and setting.
- **Pilot Study:** Always run a pilot with 3–5 users to refine the protocol *before* the main experiment.
- **KTU Golden Rule:** *"An experiment without a hypothesis is just an observation."* — State $H_0$ and $H_1$ *before* collecting data.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Empirical Data Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Empirical Data Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical Data Analysis (EDA)** in Human–Computer Interaction (HCI) is defined as the **systematic, evidence-based process of collecting, organizing, transforming, statistically examining, and interpreting data** that is gathered from real users interacting with real or prototype interfaces, in order to **validate or refute hypotheses**, draw objective conclusions about usability, performance, and user experience, and to inform interface design decisions.

The word *empirical* is critical — it distinguishes this analysis from *theoretical* or *speculative* design. Empirical data is **observable, measurable, and reproducible**. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, empirical data analysis sits at the heart of the **Evaluate** and **Test** phases of the Design Thinking / User-Centered Design (UCD) cycle.

The two macro-families of empirical data are:

1. **Quantitative Data** — Numerical, countable, measurable (e.g., task completion time in seconds, error counts, NASA-TLX scores on a 0–100 scale, clicks-per-task).
2. **Qualitative Data** — Descriptive, interpretive, non-numerical (e.g., interview transcripts, think-aloud utterances, open-ended survey responses, field notes).

Both families demand **different analytical pipelines**, and most HCI studies use a **mixed-methods** approach.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms Students Must Memorize for KTU Board Exam:**
> - **Variable** — Any measurable property that changes (independent, dependent, controlled, confounding).
> - **Hypothesis (H₁)** — A testable claim about the relationship between variables.
> - **Null Hypothesis (H₀)** — The default "no-effect" claim that we attempt to reject.
> - **Significance Level (α)** — Usually fixed at **0.05** in HCI studies.
> - **p-value** — Probability of observing data as extreme as ours *if* H₀ were true.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Recipe Judge"

Imagine you have developed two recipes for a new soft drink — **Recipe A (the old one)** and **Recipe B (your new experimental one)**. You invite 50 customers to taste both, blind, and rate them on a 1–10 scale.

- The **ratings** are your **quantitative empirical data**.
- The **comments** scribbled beside the score sheets ("too fizzy", "smooth aftertaste") are your **qualitative empirical data**.
- The act of **averaging, comparing, and deciding whether the difference is real or just a lucky random result** is *empirical data analysis*.

If Recipe B scores 7.8 and Recipe A scores 7.2, is that *real* superiority, or just random noise? **Statistical tests** in EDA are the formal courtroom where this question is judged.

> [!TIP]
> Think of EDA as a **courtroom trial**:
> - **Data** = physical evidence gathered at the scene.
> - **Descriptive statistics** = presenting the evidence to the jury (mean, median, charts).
> - **Inferential statistics** = the judge's verdict on whether the evidence proves the claim (t-test, ANOVA, etc.).
> - **p-value** = the strength of the prosecutor's argument against the null hypothesis.

## 1.3 Why EDA is Indispensable in HCI

| Reason | Real-world HCI Implication |
|---|---|
| **Objectivity** | Removes designer bias ("I *think* users will love it"). |
| **Reproducibility** | Other researchers can re-run your experiment. |
| **Generalizability** | Lets you claim findings apply to *all* users, not just 3 friends. |
| **Accountability** | Industry standards (ISO 9241-210) demand empirical validation. |
| **Iteration** | Each analysis loop feeds the next prototype, enabling UCD. |

The KTU 2024 scheme **Course Outcome (CO4)** explicitly maps empirical data analysis to *"Analyze user study data using appropriate statistical methods to draw valid usability conclusions."* Mastery of this topic is therefore a **direct, high-weight** exam requirement.

---

## 1.4 The Empirical Data Analysis Pipeline (Big-Picture)

The pipeline below is the canonical workflow examined in KTU theory papers. Memorize its **six stages**:

1. **Define the research question** (operationalize the variable).
2. **Design the study** (within-subjects, between-subjects, mixed).
3. **Collect the data** (logs, surveys, video, biometrics).
4. **Preprocess the data** (cleaning, missing-value handling, coding).
5. **Analyze** (descriptive → inferential).
6. **Interpret and report** (with effect size, confidence intervals, limitations).

A pictorial version of this pipeline will be drawn in SECTION_4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Empirical Data in HCI

### 2.1.1 By Measurement Scale

| Scale | Property | HCI Example | Allowed Operations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal** | Categories, no order | Click vs. Tap vs. Voice | Mode, chi-square |
| **Ordinal** | Ordered ranks | Likert 1–5 rating | Median, Mann–Whitney U |
| **Interval** | Equal gaps, no true zero | Temperature in °C (rare in HCI) | Mean, t-test |
| **Ratio** | Equal gaps + true zero | Reaction time in ms, error count | Mean, t-test, ANOVA, regression |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Applying a *t-test* on **ordinal** Likert data is a frequent error. Use **non-parametric tests** (Mann-Whitney, Wilcoxon, Kruskal-Wallis) for ordinal data.

### 2.1.2 By Study Design

- **Within-subjects (repeated measures):** Same user performs all conditions. Pro: controls individual differences. Con: learning, fatigue, order effects.
- **Between-subjects (independent groups):** Different users in each condition. Pro: no carryover. Con: noise from individual variability.
- **Mixed designs:** Both within and between factors.

## 2.2 Descriptive Statistics — The "Summarization Layer"

Before any hypothesis testing, the data is *summarized*. KTU frequently asks students to **calculate these by hand in Part A questions**.

### 2.2.1 Central Tendency

- **Mean (Arithmetic Average):** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$
- **Median:** Middle value of sorted data; robust to outliers.
- **Mode:** Most frequent value; used in nominal data.

### 2.2.2 Dispersion

- **Range:** $R = x_{max} - x_{min}$
- **Variance (Sample):** $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$
- **Standard Deviation:** $s = \sqrt{s^2}$
- **Standard Error of the Mean (SEM):** $SEM = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$

### 2.2.3 Distribution Shape

- **Skewness:** Measures asymmetry. Positive skew = tail on the right.
- **Kurtosis:** Measures tail-heaviness vs. a normal distribution.

## 2.3 The Logic of Inferential Statistics

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental inferential question in HCI is: **"Is the observed difference between conditions real, or just sampling noise?"**

The answer is computed using **probability distributions** parameterized by the **degrees of freedom (df)**. The three distributions that dominate HCI exams are:

1. **Normal (Z) distribution** — for large samples (n ≥ 30).
2. **t-distribution** — for small samples; fatter tails than Z.
3. **F-distribution** — used in ANOVA and regression.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table mentally — it is the single most exam-relevant reference for Module 4.**

| # | Test / Concept | Formula / Statistic | df | When to Use | HCI Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | One-sample t-test | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ | $n-1$ | Compare sample mean to a known value | Compare mean task time to industry benchmark 30s |
| 2 | Independent two-sample t-test | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}}}$ | $n_1 + n_2 - 2$ | Between-subjects, 2 conditions | Group A vs. Group B on a new menu UI |
| 3 | Paired t-test | $t = \dfrac{\bar{d}}{s_d / \sqrt{n}}$ | $n-1$ | Within-subjects, 2 conditions | Same users pre- and post-redesign |
| 4 | One-way ANOVA (F) | $F = \dfrac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}}$ where $MS = \dfrac{SS}{df}$ | $k-1$ and $N-k$ | Compare 3+ group means | Comparing 4 layout prototypes |
| 5 | Chi-square (χ²) | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O - E)^2}{E}$ | $(r-1)(c-1)$ | Categorical (nominal) data | Mouse vs. Touch vs. Voice counts |
| 6 | Pearson correlation (r) | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | $n-2$ | Linear relationship between 2 ratio vars | Age vs. typing speed |
| 7 | Spearman rank correlation (ρ) | Rank-based version of r | $n-2$ | Ordinal or non-normal | Likert scale vs. Likert scale |
| 8 | Confidence Interval (95%) | $CI_{95} = \bar{x} \pm t_{0.025,df} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | varies | Estimate population parameter | "Mean task time is 12.4 ± 1.8 s" |
| 9 | Effect size — Cohen's d | $d = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_{pooled}}$ | n/a | Standardized mean difference | Magnitude of UI improvement |
| 10 | Effect size — η² (eta squared) | $\eta^2 = \dfrac{SS_{between}}{SS_{total}}$ | n/a | Variance explained by factor | Layout prototype's effect on satisfaction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Markdown Rule Applied:** All absolute values in the table are rendered using `$\vert \cdot \vert$` style rather than the pipe `|` character, to preserve table syntax. Use $\lvert t_{0.025, df} \rvert$ if you must express absolute value inside a row.

## 2.5 Non-Parametric Alternatives (for non-normal data)

| Parametric Test | Non-Parametric Equivalent | Data Type |
|---|---|---|
| Independent t-test | **Mann-Whitney U** | Ordinal / non-normal |
| Paired t-test | **Wilcoxon Signed-Rank** | Ordinal / paired |
| One-way ANOVA | **Kruskal-Wallis H** | Ordinal / 3+ groups |
| Pearson r | **Spearman ρ** | Ordinal |

## 2.6 Qualitative Analysis — The Counterpart Track

While numbers are summarized by statistics, **words** are summarized by **coding**. Two dominant KTU-referenced methods are:

- **Thematic Analysis (Braun & Clarke, 2006):** Six-phase iterative coding of interview transcripts.
- **Grounded Theory (Glaser & Strauss):** Theory emerges inductively from constant comparison of codes.

A typical coding workflow is:
1. Familiarization with data.
2. Generating initial codes.
3. Searching for themes.
4. Reviewing themes.
5. Defining and naming themes.
6. Producing the report.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of EDA in HCI

EDA is the **bridge between research and product**. Industry roles that depend on it daily include **UX Researcher, Data Scientist, Human Factors Engineer, Accessibility Specialist**, and **Product Manager**. Tools widely used: **SPSS, R, Python (pandas, scipy, statsmodels), JASP, Excel**, and **NVivo / ATLAS.ti** for qualitative coding.

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark KTU questions**, always frame the answer as: **Question → Hypothesis → Design → Data → Statistical Test → Result (p-value, effect size) → Interpretation → Design Implication**. Examiners reward this *complete chain* heavily.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **fully worked numerical examples** mirroring the 14-mark Part-B question style of KTU ESE. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — no skips, no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Independent Two-Sample t-Test (HCI between-subjects)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A UX team evaluates a new **gesture-based interface (Condition A)** versus the **old button-based interface (Condition B)**. **15 users per condition** are timed (in seconds) while completing a standard drag-and-drop task. The team wants to know if the new interface is significantly faster. Compute the t-statistic, df, and interpret against **α = 0.05**.

**Condition A (Gesture):**
$$
17.2,\ 19.5,\ 16.8,\ 18.1,\ 20.3,\ 15.9,\ 21.0,\ 18.7,\ 17.5,\ 19.2,\ 16.4,\ 20.1,\ 18.9,\ 17.8,\ 19.6
$$

**Condition B (Button):**
$$
22.5,\ 20.8,\ 24.1,\ 23.7,\ 21.9,\ 25.3,\ 22.0,\ 24.6,\ 23.1,\ 21.4,\ 25.8,\ 22.7,\ 24.0,\ 23.5,\ 21.7
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute the Means

Sum of A:
$$
\sum_A = 17.2 + 19.5 + 16.8 + 18.1 + 20.3 + 15.9 + 21.0 + 18.7 + 17.5 + 19.2 + 16.4 + 20.1 + 18.9 + 17.8 + 19.6
$$

$$
\sum_A = 277.0
$$

$$
\bar{x}_A = \frac{277.0}{15} = 18.467 \text{ seconds}
$$

Sum of B:
$$
\sum_B = 22.5 + 20.8 + 24.1 + 23.7 + 21.9 + 25.3 + 22.0 + 24.6 + 23.1 + 21.4 + 25.8 + 22.7 + 24.0 + 23.5 + 21.7
$$

$$
\sum_B = 347.1
$$

$$
\bar{x}_B = \frac{347.1}{15} = 23.140 \text{ seconds}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute Sample Variances

Squared deviations for A (deviation $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}_A$):

| $x_i$ | $d_i$ | $d_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 17.2 | −1.267 | 1.605 |
| 19.5 | 1.033 | 1.067 |
| 16.8 | −1.667 | 2.779 |
| 18.1 | −0.367 | 0.135 |
| 20.3 | 1.833 | 3.360 |
| 15.9 | −2.567 | 6.589 |
| 21.0 | 2.533 | 6.416 |
| 18.7 | 0.233 | 0.054 |
| 17.5 | −0.967 | 0.935 |
| 19.2 | 0.733 | 0.537 |
| 16.4 | −2.067 | 4.272 |
| 20.1 | 1.633 | 2.667 |
| 18.9 | 0.433 | 0.187 |
| 17.8 | −0.667 | 0.445 |
| 19.6 | 1.133 | 1.284 |

Sum of squared deviations for A:
$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x}_A)^2 = 32.333
$$

Sample variance for A:
$$
s_A^2 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x}_A)^2}{n_A - 1} = \frac{32.333}{14} = 2.310
$$

> This $n - 1$ in the denominator is **Bessel's correction**, which uses an *unbiased estimator* of the population variance.

By a parallel (omitted for brevity) computation for B:
$$
s_B^2 = \frac{33.564}{14} = 2.397
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute Pooled Standard Error

The standard error of the difference of two independent means is:
$$
SE = \sqrt{\frac{s_A^2}{n_A} + \frac{s_B^2}{n_B}}
$$

$$
SE = \sqrt{\frac{2.310}{15} + \frac{2.397}{15}} = \sqrt{0.154 + 0.160} = \sqrt{0.314} = 0.560 \text{ s}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Compute the t-Statistic

$$
t = \frac{\bar{x}_A - \bar{x}_B}{SE} = \frac{18.467 - 23.140}{0.560} = \frac{-4.673}{0.560} = -8.345
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Compute Degrees of Freedom

$$
df = n_A + n_B - 2 = 15 + 15 - 2 = 28
$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Decision Against α = 0.05

Critical t-value for two-tailed test: $t_{critical} = \pm 2.048$ (from t-table at df = 28, α = 0.05).

Our computed $\vert t \vert = 8.345 \gg 2.048$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision:** Reject H₀. The mean task time with the gesture interface is **significantly lower** than with the button interface.

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Effect Size (Cohen's d)

Pooled standard deviation:
$$
s_p = \sqrt{\frac{(n_A - 1) s_A^2 + (n_B - 1) s_B^2}{n_A + n_B - 2}} = \sqrt{\frac{14(2.310) + 14(2.397)}{28}} = \sqrt{2.354} = 1.534
$$

$$
d = \frac{\bar{x}_A - \bar{x}_B}{s_p} = \frac{-4.673}{1.534} = -3.046
$$

By Cohen's conventions ($\vert d \vert \geq 0.8$ = large), this is a **very large effect** — the new interface is not just statistically faster, it is *substantively* faster.

### 3.1.9 Step 8 — 95 % Confidence Interval for the Mean Difference

$$
CI_{95} = (\bar{x}_A - \bar{x}_B) \pm t_{0.025, 28} \cdot SE = -4.673 \pm (2.048)(0.560)
$$

$$
CI_{95} = -4.673 \pm 1.147 = [-5.820,\ -3.526] \text{ seconds}
$$

> **Interpretation:** We are 95 % confident that the **true** mean time-savings of the gesture interface over the button interface lies between **3.5 and 5.8 seconds** — a strong, practical improvement.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — One-Way ANOVA (3 prototypes)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

Three smartphone keyboard prototypes (P1, P2, P3) are evaluated. **6 users per prototype** type a fixed phrase; WPM (words per minute) is recorded. Test if any prototype differs significantly.

| P1 | P2 | P3 |
|---|---|---|
| 32 | 38 | 41 |
| 30 | 40 | 45 |
| 31 | 36 | 43 |
| 29 | 39 | 44 |
| 33 | 37 | 42 |
| 28 | 35 | 46 |

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Group Sums and Means

$$
\sum P1 = 183, \quad \bar{x}_{P1} = 30.5
$$

$$
\sum P2 = 225, \quad \bar{x}_{P2} = 37.5
$$

$$
\sum P3 = 261, \quad \bar{x}_{P3} = 43.5
$$

Grand total:
$$
\sum_{total} = 183 + 225 + 261 = 669
$$

Grand mean:
$$
\bar{x}_{..} = \frac{669}{18} = 37.167
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Sum of Squares Between (SSB)

$$
SSB = \sum_{j=1}^{k} n_j (\bar{x}_j - \bar{x}_{..})^2
$$

$$
SSB = 6(30.5 - 37.167)^2 + 6(37.5 - 37.167)^2 + 6(43.5 - 37.167)^2
$$

$$
SSB = 6(44.444) + 6(0.111) + 6(40.111) = 266.667 + 0.667 + 240.667 = 508.000
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Sum of Squares Within (SSW)

For each group compute $\sum (x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j)^2$:

P1 deviations: $-1.5, -0.5, 0.5, -1.5, 2.5, -2.5$ → sum of squares = $2.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 6.25 + 6.25 = 17.50$.

P2 deviations: $0.5, 2.5, -1.5, 1.5, -0.5, -2.5$ → sum of squares = $0.25 + 6.25 + 2.25 + 2.25 + 0.25 + 6.25 = 17.50$.

P3 deviations: $-2.5, 1.5, -0.5, 0.5, -1.5, 2.5$ → sum of squares = $6.25 + 2.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 6.25 = 17.50$.

$$
SSW = 17.50 + 17.50 + 17.50 = 52.50
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Sum of Squares Total (SST)

$$
SST = SSB + SSW = 508.00 + 52.50 = 560.50
$$

(Equivalently, $SST = \sum (x_{ij} - \bar{x}_{..})^2$, which gives the same value.)

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Degrees of Freedom

$$
df_{between} = k - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2
$$

$$
df_{within} = N - k = 18 - 3 = 15
$$

$$
df_{total} = N - 1 = 17
$$

### 3.2.7 Step 6 — Mean Squares and F-Ratio

$$
MS_{between} = \frac{SSB}{df_{between}} = \frac{508.00}{2} = 254.00
$$

$$
MS_{within} = \frac{SSW}{df_{within}} = \frac{52.50}{15} = 3.50
$$

$$
F = \frac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}} = \frac{254.00}{3.50} = 72.571
$$

### 3.2.8 Step 7 — Decision Against α = 0.05

Critical $F_{2, 15, 0.05} = 3.68$.

Computed $F = 72.57 \gg 3.68$.

> **Decision:** Reject H₀. There is a statistically significant difference in WPM across the three prototypes.

### 3.2.9 Step 8 — Effect Size (η²)

$$
\eta^2 = \frac{SSB}{SST} = \frac{508.00}{560.50} = 0.906
$$

> **Interpretation:** **90.6 %** of the variance in WPM is explained by which prototype was used — a massive effect. Post-hoc tests (e.g., Tukey HSD) would then identify *which* pairs differ.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Chi-Square Test of Independence (input modality)

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

A research team records the **preferred input modality** (Mouse, Touch, Voice) among **two age groups** (Young ≤ 35, Older > 35). Observed frequencies:

| Age | Mouse | Touch | Voice | Row Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Young | 30 | 45 | 25 | 100 |
| Older | 40 | 20 | 40 | 100 |
| Column Total | 70 | 65 | 65 | 200 |

### 3.3.2 Step 1 — Hypotheses

- $H_0$: Input preference is **independent** of age.
- $H_1$: Input preference is **associated** with age.

### 3.3.3 Step 2 — Expected Frequencies

For cell $(i, j)$:
$$
E_{ij} = \frac{(\text{Row Total}_i)(\text{Column Total}_j)}{N}
$$

$$
E_{11} = \frac{100 \times 70}{200} = 35, \quad E_{12} = \frac{100 \times 65}{200} = 32.5, \quad E_{13} = 32.5
$$

$$
E_{21} = 35, \quad E_{22} = 32.5, \quad E_{23} = 32.5
$$

### 3.3.4 Step 3 — Chi-Square Statistic

$$
\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}
$$

Cell (1,1):
$$
\frac{(30 - 35)^2}{35} = \frac{25}{35} = 0.714
$$

Cell (1,2):
$$
\frac{(45 - 32.5)^2}{32.5} = \frac{156.25}{32.5} = 4.808
$$

Cell (1,3):
$$
\frac{(25 - 32.5)^2}{32.5} = \frac{56.25}{32.5} = 1.731
$$

Cell (2,1):
$$
\frac{(40 - 35)^2}{35} = 0.714
$$

Cell (2,2):
$$
\frac{(20 - 32.5)^2}{32.5} = 4.808
$$

Cell (2,3):
$$
\frac{(40 - 32.5)^2}{32.5} = 1.731
$$

Sum:
$$
\chi^2 = 0.714 + 4.808 + 1.731 + 0.714 + 4.808 + 1.731 = 14.506
$$

### 3.3.5 Step 4 — Degrees of Freedom

$$
df = (r - 1)(c - 1) = (2 - 1)(3 - 1) = 2
$$

### 3.3.6 Step 5 — Decision Against α = 0.05

Critical $\chi^2_{0.05, 2} = 5.991$.

Computed $\chi^2 = 14.506 > 5.991$.

> **Decision:** Reject H₀. The preferred input modality **is significantly associated with age** — younger users favor touch, older users are split between mouse and voice. This finding should drive a multi-modal interface design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Test

> Use this decision matrix during exam preparation. **Memorize it.**

| Question | Measurement Scale | # Groups | Paired? | Recommended Test |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compare one sample to a benchmark | Ratio / Interval | 1 | n/a | One-sample t-test |
| Compare 2 group means | Ratio / Interval | 2 | No | Independent t-test |
| Compare 2 group means | Ratio / Interval | 2 | Yes | Paired t-test |
| Compare 3+ group means | Ratio / Interval | 3+ | No | One-way ANOVA |
| Compare 3+ group means | Ratio / Interval | 3+ | Yes | Repeated-measures ANOVA |
| 2 categorical variables | Nominal | any | n/a | Chi-square |
| Relationship 2 continuous vars | Ratio / Interval | n/a | n/a | Pearson r |
| Relationship 2 ordinal vars | Ordinal | n/a | n/a | Spearman ρ |
| Predict Y from X | Ratio / Interval | n/a | n/a | Linear regression |
| 2 group means, non-normal | Ordinal | 2 | No | Mann-Whitney U |
| 2 group means, non-normal | Ordinal | 2 | Yes | Wilcoxon Signed-Rank |
| 3+ group means, non-normal | Ordinal | 3+ | No | Kruskal-Wallis H |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> All Mermaid diagrams below use the **alpha-prefixed node ID** rule and contain no markdown formatting inside node labels.

## 4.1 The End-to-End Empirical Data Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[STEP 0 FRAMING] --> A1[STEP 1 RESEARCH QUESTION AND HYPOTHESIS]
    A1 --> A2[STEP 2 STUDY DESIGN WITHIN BETWEEN OR MIXED]
    A2 --> A3[STEP 3 DATA COLLECTION LOGS SURVEY VIDEO BIOMETRICS]
    A3 --> A4[STEP 4 DATA PREPROCESSING CLEAN MISSING VALUES CODE]
    A4 --> A5[STEP 5 DESCRIPTIVE STATISTICS MEAN SD MEDIAN PLOTS]
    A5 --> A6[STEP 6 INFERENTIAL TESTING T TEST ANOVA CHI SQUARE]
    A6 --> A7[STEP 7 EFFECT SIZE AND CONFIDENCE INTERVAL]
    A7 --> A8[STEP 8 INTERPRETATION AND DESIGN RECOMMENDATION]
    A8 --> A9[STEP 9 PEER REVIEW AND PUBLICATION]
    A9 --> A2
```

## 4.2 Branching Logic for Test Selection (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[START YOUR DATA] --> B1{WHAT IS THE TYPE OF DATA}
    B1 -- NUMERICAL --> B2{HOW MANY GROUPS}
    B1 -- CATEGORICAL --> B3{ONE OR TWO VARIABLES}
    B2 -- ONE GROUP --> B4[ONE SAMPLE T TEST]
    B2 -- TWO GROUPS --> B5{PAIRED OR NOT}
    B5 -- PAIRED --> B6[PAIRED T TEST]
    B5 -- NOT PAIRED --> B7[INDEPENDENT T TEST]
    B2 -- THREE OR MORE --> B8{PAIRED OR NOT}
    B8 -- NOT PAIRED --> B9[ONE WAY ANOVA]
    B8 -- PAIRED --> BA[REPEATED MEASURES ANOVA]
    B3 -- ONE VARIABLE --> BB[CHI SQUARE GOODNESS OF FIT]
    B3 -- TWO VARIABLES --> BC[CHI SQUARE INDEPENDENCE]
    B4 --> BD{NORMALITY ASSUMPTION}
    B6 --> BD
    B7 --> BD
    B9 --> BD
    BA --> BD
    BD -- VIOLATED --> BE[NON PARAMETRIC ALTERNATIVE]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of a Mixed-Methods HCI Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[PARTICIPANTS RECRUITED N EQUALS 30] --> C1[COUNTER BALANCED ORDER]
    C1 --> C2[QUANTITATIVE ARM TASK TIME ERRORS NASA TLX]
    C1 --> C3[QUALITATIVE ARM THINK ALOUD AND INTERVIEW]
    C2 --> C4[NUMERIC DATABASE CSV]
    C3 --> C5[TRANSCRIPTS NVIVO]
    C4 --> C6[STATISTICAL ENGINE PYTHON OR R]
    C5 --> C7[CODING AND THEMATIC ENGINE]
    C6 --> C8[CONVERGENT EVIDENCE MATRIX]
    C7 --> C8
    C8 --> C9[DESIGN RECOMMENDATIONS TO PRODUCT TEAM]
```

## 4.4 Hypothesis-Testing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[STATE H0 AND H1] --> D1[CHOOSE ALPHA TYPICALLY ZERO POINT ZERO FIVE]
    D1 --> D2[COMPUTE TEST STATISTIC T OR F OR CHI SQUARE]
    D2 --> D3[COMPUTE P VALUE FROM SOFTWARE]
    D3 --> D4{P LESS THAN ALPHA}
    D4 -- YES --> D5[REJECT H0 STATISTICALLY SIGNIFICANT]
    D4 -- NO --> D6[FAIL TO REJECT H0 NO EVIDENCE OF EFFECT]
    D5 --> D7[REPORT EFFECT SIZE AND CONFIDENCE INTERVAL]
    D6 --> D7
    D7 --> D8[INTERPRET IN HCI DOMAIN CONTEXT]
```

## 4.5 Qualitative Coding Cycle (Thematic Analysis)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E0[TRANSCRIPTS] --> E1[STEP 1 DATA FAMILIARIZATION]
    E1 --> E2[STEP 2 GENERATE INITIAL CODES]
    E2 --> E3[STEP 3 SEARCH FOR THEMES]
    E3 --> E4[STEP 4 REVIEW THEMES]
    E4 --> E5[STEP 5 DEFINE AND NAME THEMES]
    E5 --> E6[STEP 6 PRODUCE REPORT]
    E6 --> E0
```

> These five diagrams collectively summarize the **structural backbone of Module 4**. They are commonly drawn in 7-mark sub-parts of KTU 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions are framed strictly in the KTU 2024 ESE pattern. Solutions follow the **valuation key style** used by KTU board examiners.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Differentiate between descriptive and inferential statistics with one HCI example each.**

**Model Answer (Valuation Key Style):**

| Aspect | Descriptive Statistics | Inferential Statistics |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Summarize the *observed* sample | Generalize from the *sample* to the *population* |
| **Output** | Means, medians, percentages, charts | p-values, confidence intervals, hypothesis decisions |
| **Risk of error** | None — purely descriptive | Type-I and Type-II errors possible |
| **HCI Example** | "Mean task completion time across 30 users was 18.4 s" | "The new gesture UI is *significantly* faster than the old one (t(28) = 8.34, p < 0.001, d = 3.05)" |
| **Tools** | Excel, simple plots | t-test, ANOVA, regression, χ² |

> A common student error is to confuse "descriptive" with "qualitative" — they are **not the same**. Qualitative is a *data type*; descriptive is an *analytical stage*.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**What is a p-value? Why is p = 0.05 commonly used as the cutoff in HCI?**

**Model Answer:**

A **p-value** is the probability of obtaining results *at least as extreme* as those observed, **assuming the null hypothesis H₀ is true**. It does **not** measure the probability that H₀ is true.

- A p-value of **0.03** means: *if there were truly no effect, we would see data this extreme only 3 % of the time by random chance.*
- The **0.05** threshold is a historical convention (set by R.A. Fisher in 1925) and balances Type-I (false positive) versus Type-II (false negative) errors.
- HCI traditionally adopts **α = 0.05** to balance scientific rigor with the practical need to detect usability differences in small samples (typically 10–30 users).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module-4 Internal Choice A]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **six stages of the empirical data analysis pipeline** in HCI, with reference to a specific usability study (e.g., comparing two mobile-app onboarding flows).

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the following dataset, perform an **independent two-sample t-test** to determine whether Interface X is significantly faster than Interface Y. Use α = 0.05. Also report the **95 % confidence interval** and **Cohen's d**.

Interface X (n = 10): 12, 14, 11, 13, 15, 12, 14, 13, 11, 15 (seconds)
Interface Y (n = 10): 16, 18, 15, 17, 19, 16, 18, 17, 15, 19 (seconds)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The six stages of the empirical data analysis pipeline, applied to a usability study comparing two mobile-app onboarding flows:

1. **Define the research question and operationalize the variable.**
   - *Question:* "Does the redesigned onboarding flow reduce average completion time compared to the legacy flow?"
   - *Variable:* Completion time in seconds (ratio scale).
   - *Hypotheses:* H₀: μ_new = μ_old ; H₁: μ_new < μ_old (one-tailed).

2. **Design the study.**
   - **Between-subjects design** is chosen to avoid learning effects; 30 participants randomly assigned, 15 per flow.
   - Counterbalancing and blinding recommended if between-subjects is not feasible.

3. **Collect the data.**
   - Capture screen-recorded sessions; extract task-completion timestamps; log errors; administer the System Usability Scale (SUS) and NASA-TLX.

4. **Preprocess the data.**
   - Filter incomplete sessions; impute or exclude outliers (>3 SD); normalize SUS scores.

5. **Analyze.**
   - *Descriptive:* mean, median, SD per condition; box-plots; SUS distributions.
   - *Inferential:* independent two-sample t-test (or Mann-Whitney U if normality fails, verified by Shapiro-Wilk).

6. **Interpret and report.**
   - Report t(df), p-value, mean difference, 95 % CI, Cohen's d, and **practical significance** ("new flow saves ~3.5 s on average — meaningful for a 20-second task").

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute means.**

$$
\bar{x}_X = \frac{12+14+11+13+15+12+14+13+11+15}{10} = \frac{130}{10} = 13.0
$$

$$
\bar{x}_Y = \frac{16+18+15+17+19+16+18+17+15+19}{10} = \frac{170}{10} = 17.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute variances.**

For X, deviations from 13.0: $-1, 1, -2, 0, 2, -1, 1, 0, -2, 2$
$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x}_X)^2 = 1+1+4+0+4+1+1+0+4+4 = 20
$$
$$
s_X^2 = \frac{20}{9} = 2.222
$$

For Y, deviations from 17.0: $-1, 1, -2, 0, 2, -1, 1, 0, -2, 2$
$$
\sum (y_i - \bar{x}_Y)^2 = 20
$$
$$
s_Y^2 = \frac{20}{9} = 2.222
$$

**Step 3 — Standard error of the difference.**

$$
SE = \sqrt{\frac{s_X^2}{n_X} + \frac{s_Y^2}{n_Y}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.222}{10} + \frac{2.222}{10}} = \sqrt{0.444} = 0.667 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — t-statistic.**

$$
t = \frac{\bar{x}_X - \bar{x}_Y}{SE} = \frac{13.0 - 17.0}{0.667} = -6.00
$$

**Step 5 — Degrees of freedom.**

$$
df = n_X + n_Y - 2 = 18
$$

**Step 6 — Critical value and decision.**

For two-tailed α = 0.05 and df = 18, $t_{critical} = \pm 2.101$.

$$
\vert t \vert = 6.00 > 2.101 \Rightarrow \text{Reject } H_0
$$

**Step 7 — p-value.**

From t-tables, t = 6.00 with df = 18 yields **p < 0.0001**.

**Step 8 — 95 % Confidence Interval.**

$$
CI_{95} = (\bar{x}_X - \bar{x}_Y) \pm t_{0.025, 18} \cdot SE = -4.0 \pm (2.101)(0.667) = -4.0 \pm 1.401
$$

$$
CI_{95} = [-5.40,\ -2.60] \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 9 — Cohen's d.**

$$
s_p = \sqrt{\frac{9(2.222) + 9(2.222)}{18}} = \sqrt{2.222} = 1.491
$$

$$
d = \frac{-4.0}{1.491} = -2.68 \quad (\text{very large effect})
$$

**Conclusion:** Interface X is **statistically significantly faster** than Interface Y. The 95 % CI excludes zero, the effect is very large (d = −2.68), and the mean saving of 4 s is highly *practically* significant for a typical ~15-second task. The redesign should be deployed, with continued A/B monitoring post-launch.

---

### Q.B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module-4 Internal Choice B]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, assumptions, and HCI use-cases of one-way ANOVA**. Under what conditions would you prefer a **Kruskal-Wallis H test** instead?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A study investigates whether the **type of error message** (technical / plain-language / icon-based) affects **user recovery time** in seconds. Eight participants are tested per condition. The data is given below. Perform an **appropriate statistical test** and report F (or H), df, p-value interpretation, and effect size.

| Technical | Plain-Language | Icon-Based |
|---|---|---|
| 45 | 30 | 35 |
| 50 | 28 | 32 |
| 48 | 32 | 38 |
| 47 | 31 | 36 |
| 52 | 29 | 34 |
| 46 | 33 | 37 |
| 49 | 30 | 33 |
| 51 | 31 | 35 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**One-Way ANOVA (Analysis of Variance)** tests whether the **means of three or more independent groups** differ significantly. It does so by partitioning the total variability in the data into *between-group* variability (MSB) and *within-group* variability (MSW), and computing:

$$
F = \frac{MSB}{MSW}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. **Independence** of observations.
2. **Normality** of residuals within each group (Shapiro-Wilk test).
3. **Homogeneity of variance** across groups (Levene's test).
4. The dependent variable is measured on an **interval or ratio scale**.

**HCI Use-Cases:**
- Comparing **3+ layout prototypes** on completion time.
- Comparing **4 icon sets** on recognition accuracy.
- Comparing **3 menu structures** on satisfaction scores.

**Kruskal-Wallis H test** is the **non-parametric alternative** and is preferred when:
- Sample size is small and/or data is **ordinal** (Likert).
- Normality assumption is **violated** (Shapiro-Wilk p < 0.05).
- Variances are **highly unequal** (Levene's p < 0.05) and cannot be remedied.
- The data contains **outliers** that cannot be legitimately removed.

It ranks all observations across groups and tests whether the mean ranks differ.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Verify assumptions.**

- Normality: With n = 8 per group, normality is *plausible* (Central Limit Theorem) but should be checked. Assume met for this KTU-style question.
- Homogeneity of variance: Visual inspection shows similar spread (28–52, 30–33, 32–38). Plausible.

**Step 2 — Compute group means and overall mean.**

$$
\bar{x}_{Tech} = \frac{45+50+48+47+52+46+49+51}{8} = \frac{388}{8} = 48.5
$$

$$
\bar{x}_{Plain} = \frac{30+28+32+31+29+33+30+31}{8} = \frac{244}{8} = 30.5
$$

$$
\bar{x}_{Icon} = \frac{35+32+38+36+34+37+33+35}{8} = \frac{280}{8} = 35.0
$$

Grand total = 388 + 244 + 280 = 912. Grand mean:
$$
\bar{x}_{..} = \frac{912}{24} = 38.0
$$

**Step 3 — SSB.**

$$
SSB = 8(48.5-38)^2 + 8(30.5-38)^2 + 8(35-38)^2
$$

$$
SSB = 8(110.25) + 8(56.25) + 8(9) = 882 + 450 + 72 = 1404
$$

**Step 4 — SSW.**

Tech deviations from 48.5: $-3.5, 1.5, -0.5, -1.5, 3.5, -2.5, 0.5, 2.5$
$$
\sum d^2 = 12.25 + 2.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 12.25 + 6.25 + 0.25 + 6.25 = 42.00
$$

Plain deviations from 30.5: $-0.5, -2.5, 1.5, 0.5, -1.5, 2.5, -0.5, 0.5$
$$
\sum d^2 = 0.25 + 6.25 + 2.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 6.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 18.00
$$

Icon deviations from 35.0: $0, -3, 3, 1, -1, 2, -2, 0$
$$
\sum d^2 = 0 + 9 + 9 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 4 + 0 = 28.00
$$

$$
SSW = 42.00 + 18.00 + 28.00 = 88.00
$$

**Step 5 — df, MS, F.**

$$
df_B = k - 1 = 2, \quad df_W = N - k = 21
$$

$$
MS_B = \frac{1404}{2} = 702.00, \quad MS_W = \frac{88}{21} = 4.190
$$

$$
F = \frac{702.00}{4.190} = 167.54
$$

**Step 6 — Critical F and decision.**

$F_{critical}(2, 21, 0.05) = 3.47$. Since $167.54 \gg 3.47$, **reject H₀**.

**Step 7 — Effect size η².**

$$
\eta^2 = \frac{SSB}{SST} = \frac{1404}{1492} = 0.941
$$

> **Interpretation:** The type of error message **strongly affects** user recovery time, explaining **94.1 %** of variance. **Plain-language** messages are fastest (mean 30.5 s), followed by **icon-based** (35.0 s), and **technical** messages are slowest (48.5 s). A post-hoc Tukey HSD would confirm all three pairwise differences are significant.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most often lose marks in Module 4 EDA questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state H₀ and H₁ explicitly** — *always* open the answer with both hypotheses. Examiners allocate 1 mark solely for this.
> 2. **Reporting "p = 0.04" without the test statistic and df** — always write `t(df) = value, p = 0.04` or `F(df₁, df₂) = value, p = 0.04`. APA-style is mandatory.
> 3. **Skipping the normality / assumption check** before picking a test. KTU examiners actively look for it (≈ 1 mark).
> 4. **Conflating statistical significance with practical significance** — always discuss **effect size** and **confidence interval**, not just the p-value.
> 5. **Wrong df** — most common: writing $df = n$ instead of $df = n - 1$. Penalty: full mark loss for the t-statistic step.
> 6. **Not mentioning qualitative analysis** in mixed-methods questions — Module 4 explicitly expects both arms.
> 7. **Misusing chi-square** on small expected frequencies (< 5 per cell) — Fisher's exact test should be used; examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 8. **Writing only the formula with no substituted values** — show the *substitution* line so the examiner can see numerical understanding.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this checklist to your revision wall — it covers ≥ 85 % of KTU Module 4 questions.**

- [x] Empirical data analysis = **observe → measure → summarize → infer → interpret → redesign**.
- [x] Two data families: **quantitative** (numbers) and **qualitative** (themes).
- [x] Four measurement scales: **nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio** — pick the test by scale.
- [x] **α = 0.05** is the default significance level in HCI.
- [x] Always state **H₀** and **H₁** before computing any test.
- [x] **Independent t-test** for between-subjects, 2 groups, ratio data.
- [x] **Paired t-test** for within-subjects, 2 conditions, ratio data.
- [x] **One-way ANOVA** for 3+ independent groups, ratio data, normal residuals.
- [x] **Repeated-measures ANOVA** for 3+ within-subjects conditions.
- [x] **Chi-square (χ²)** for two categorical variables; requires **expected ≥ 5** per cell.
- [x] **Pearson r** for linear relationship between two ratio variables.
- [x] **Spearman ρ** for ordinal data or non-normal ratio data.
- [x] Use **Mann-Whitney U, Wilcoxon, Kruskal-Wallis** when normality is violated.
- [x] **Effect size** (Cohen's d, η²) is *mandatory* alongside p-value reporting.
- [x] **Confidence intervals** convey precision — always report them.
- [x] **Type-I error** (α) = false positive; **Type-II error** (β) = false negative.
- [x] **Power = 1 − β**; small samples → low power → risk of Type-II.
- [x] **Qualitative coding** (Thematic Analysis) has **6 phases** (Braun & Clarke).
- [x] **Mixed-methods** designs combine the two arms in a *convergent* evidence matrix.
- [x] Always close your answer with a **design recommendation**, not just a statistical verdict.
- [x] Use **APA format** when reporting statistics: `t(28) = 8.34, p < .001, d = 3.05`.
- [x] APA uses `p < .001` (no zero), not `p = 0.000` — small but examiner-noticed detail.
- [x] **Outliers** (> 3 SD) must be reported and justified (kept / removed / transformed).
- [x] **Cleaning** (missing data) options: listwise deletion, mean imputation, multiple imputation.
- [x] Always visualize data (boxplot, scatter, histogram) **before** testing assumptions.

---

**End of Module 4 — Empirical Data Analysis Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PEPET861)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET861 - DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Management (HCI Context):** The structured discipline of planning, organizing, motivating, and controlling resources (human, financial, temporal) to achieve specific design goals in the development of interactive systems, while ensuring the delivered product meets user requirements, usability goals, and organizational constraints.

In KTU's HCI syllabus, **Project Management** encompasses:
- The **4 Pillars of Good Interactive System Design**
- **Project Organization & Communication**
- **Project Scheduling, Budgeting & Risk Analysis**
- **Legal & Professional Issues** (Accessibility, Privacy, Standards)
- **Design Patterns & Internationalization**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Orchestra Conductor"

Imagine you are **conducting an orchestra** to produce a symphony. You must:
- **Plan** the concert program (project scope & requirements)
- **Recruit & Coordinate** musicians (team roles & responsibilities)
- **Schedule** rehearsals and the final performance (Gantt charts, milestones)
- **Control the budget** for instruments, venue, and sheet music (financial planning)
- **Identify risks** like a sick violinist or power outage (risk analysis)
- **Ensure harmony** between the violin and the piano section (consistency in design)

**The conductor doesn't play every instrument**, but ensures the **ensemble performs beautifully together**. Similarly, the HCI project manager does not perform every design activity but orchestrates the multidisciplinary team (designers, developers, users, managers) to deliver a usable, accessible, and effective interactive system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In HCI, project management is **NOT just about on-time delivery** — it is equally about **delivering a product that is usable, accessible, ethical, and aligned with user needs**.

## 1.3 Why Project Management is Critical in HCI

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **User Involvement** | HCI projects require continuous user feedback; poor scheduling = missed iterations |
| **Cross-Disciplinary Teams** | Coordination across psychology, design, engineering, and management |
| **Usability Standards** | Compliance with WCAG, ISO 9241, legal frameworks (ADA, GDPR) |
| **Iterative Cycles** | Need structured iteration management to avoid scope creep |
| **Risk of User Rejection** | HCI failures cause abandonment, training costs, and safety issues |

## 1.4 Core Terminology (Must Remember)

- **Pillar:** A fundamental, non-negotiable principle of good interactive design (4 pillars).
- **Stakeholder:** Any person affected by the system (users, managers, developers, regulators).
- **Milestone:** A significant checkpoint marking completion of a project phase.
- **Gantt Chart:** A horizontal bar chart showing tasks, durations, and dependencies over time.
- **PERT:** Program Evaluation Review Technique — uses Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic time estimates.
- **Critical Path:** The longest sequence of dependent tasks that determines minimum project duration.
- **Design Pattern:** A reusable, proven solution to a recurring design problem.
- **Internationalization (i18n):** Designing the product to be adaptable to various languages/regions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 4 Pillars of Good Interactive System Design

According to KTU's HCI curriculum (per Dix et al., *Human-Computer Interaction*), good interactive systems are built upon **four fundamental pillars**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 Pillars:** **1.** User-Centered Design (UCD) & **2.** Iterative Design → guide the **process**; **3.** Prototyping & **4.** Evaluation → guide the **design**.

### Pillar 1: User-Centered Design (UCD)
- Focus on users and their tasks from the very beginning
- **Involve users actively** in the design process
- **Iterative design** based on user feedback
- **Evaluate designs** empirically
- Adopt **user-centered attitudes** throughout

### Pillar 2: Iterative Design
- Design is **NOT a linear waterfall**; it cycles through analysis → design → prototype → evaluate → refine
- Each iteration brings the system closer to the user's needs
- Allows **early detection of usability problems**

### Pillar 3: Prototyping
- Build **throwaway prototypes** to explore ideas before committing
- Types: **Low-fidelity** (sketches, paper), **High-fidelity** (functional code), **Wizard of Oz**
- Reduces risk of costly redesign later

### Pillar 4: Evaluation
- Empirical testing with users
- Goals: **Utility** (does it do what users need?) and **Usability** (how well can users do it?)
- Methods: **User testing**, **Heuristic evaluation**, **Field studies**

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Project Management (Management Perspective)

While the 4 pillars describe **WHAT to do**, the **3 pillars of project management** describe **HOW to manage**:

> [!NOTE]
> **3 Pillars of PM:** **P**eople + **P**roduct + **P**rocess (Popularized by McConnell & Brooks)

| Pillar | Focus | HCI Application |
|---|---|---|
| **People** | Team members, stakeholders, users | Designers, developers, end-users, managers |
| **Product** | Software solution & its requirements | Interactive system functionality, usability |
| **Process** | Methodology & workflow | Agile, Iterative, Waterfall, RAD |

## 2.3 Project Management Skills (KTU Focus)

The project manager in HCI must possess:

1. **Project Scheduling** — Defining tasks, durations, dependencies (Gantt, PERT)
2. **Project Budgeting** — Estimating costs (labor, hardware, software, training)
3. **Risk Management** — Identifying, assessing, mitigating project risks
4. **Project Communication** — Reports, meetings, documentation
5. **People Management** — Motivation, conflict resolution, team building
6. **Quality Management** — Ensuring usability, accessibility, performance standards

## 2.4 Project Management Methodologies for HCI

| Methodology | Description | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Waterfall** | Linear, sequential phases | Simple, well-defined projects |
| **Iterative Design** | Repeated cycles of design-test-refine | Most HCI projects (recommended) |
| **RAD (Rapid App. Dev.)** | Prototyping-centric, time-boxed | Tight deadlines, evolving requirements |
| **Agile/Scrum** | Sprints, daily standups, backlog | User-facing apps with changing needs |
| **XP (Extreme Programming)** | Pair programming, TDD, customer on-site | Quality-critical interactive systems |

## 2.5 Project Organization & Communication

### Organizational Structures

- **Chief Programmer Team:** A lead developer supported by specialists (architect, tester, backup programmer). Effective for small to medium projects.
- **Democratic Team:** No formal leader; consensus-driven. Good for R&D, but slow for large projects.
- **Hybrid Team:** Combines structure with democratic flexibility. **Most common in HCI**.

### Communication Channels
- **Face-to-face meetings** (highest bandwidth)
- **Electronic communication** (email, Slack, Teams)
- **Documentation** (design specs, requirement docs)
- **Status reports & reviews** (milestone reviews)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Expect a question on **"Briefly explain the 4 pillars of good interactive system design"** — this is a high-frequency **3-mark question**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Quantitative Project Management)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **critical for solving numerical problems** in Part A and Part B of KTU exams on Project Management.

### A. PERT Time Estimation

The expected duration of a task under uncertainty:

$$E(t) = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$$

Where:
- $O$ = **Optimistic** time (best-case scenario)
- $M$ = **Most Likely** time (normal working conditions)
- $P$ = **Pessimistic** time (worst-case scenario)
- $6$ = the **PERT constant** (not arbitrary — derived from statistical distribution assumptions)

### B. PERT Variance & Standard Deviation

$$\sigma^2 = \left( \frac{P - O}{6} \right)^2$$

$$\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6}$$

The **variance** measures uncertainty. Higher variance = more risky task.

### C. Project Duration (Critical Path Method — CPM)

The project duration equals the **longest path** through the network:

$$T_{project} = \max\left( \sum_{i \in \text{path}} E(t_i) \right)$$

### D. Project Completion Probability (Normal Distribution)

To find the probability of completing a project within a target time $T_s$:

$$Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_E}$$

Where:
- $T_E$ = expected project duration (sum of expected times along critical path)
- $\sigma_E$ = standard deviation of critical path = $\sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$
- $T_s$ = scheduled/target completion time
- $Z$ = standard normal variable

### E. Earned Value Management (EVM)

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **PV (Planned Value)** | Budget × % planned | What we expected to spend |
| **EV (Earned Value)** | Budget × % completed | What we actually accomplished |
| **AC (Actual Cost)** | Actual money spent | What we actually spent |
| **CV (Cost Variance)** | $EV - AC$ | Negative = over budget |
| **SV (Schedule Variance)** | $EV - PV$ | Negative = behind schedule |
| **CPI (Cost Performance Index)** | $EV / AC$ | $< 1$ = over budget |
| **SPI (Schedule Performance Index)** | $EV / PV$ | $< 1$ = behind schedule |
| **EAC (Estimate at Completion)** | $BAC / CPI$ | Forecasted total cost |
| **ETC (Estimate to Complete)** | $EAC - AC$ | Money needed to finish |

Where **BAC** = Budget at Completion (total approved budget).

### F. Risk Exposure

$$Risk\ Exposure = Probability \times Impact$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** PERT/CPM is used in **safety-critical HCI** (aviation cockpits, medical devices) to estimate development timelines with statistical confidence. EVM is used in **large enterprise software projects** for real-time tracking.

## 2.7 Design Patterns (Reusable Solutions)

A **design pattern** is a proven, reusable solution to a commonly occurring design problem. Categorized in HCI as:

| Category | Example | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Navigation** | Tabs, Breadcrumbs, Tiles | Help users move through content |
| **Layout** | Grid, Card, Stacked | Organize information visually |
| **Input** | Autocomplete, Calendar picker | Ease data entry |
| **Feedback** | Toast, Progress bar, Modal | Communicate system status |
| **Social** | Like, Share, Comment | Encourage user engagement |

## 2.8 Internationalization, Accessibility & Legal Issues

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legal Compliance is a KTU Favourite Topic.**

| Concern | Standard/Law | Key Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Accessibility** | **WCAG 2.1 / 2.2** (W3C) | Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust (POUR) |
| **Accessibility (US)** | **ADA** (Americans with Disabilities Act) | Public systems must be accessible |
| **Privacy (EU)** | **GDPR** | Informed consent, right to erasure, data minimization |
| **Privacy (India)** | **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023** | Consent, purpose limitation, data fiduciary duties |
| **Professional Standards** | **BCS, ACM, IEEE codes of ethics** | Honesty, competence, public welfare |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: PERT Analysis (Full Derivation)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A project has four activities A, B, C, D. The optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic times (in days) are:
>
> | Activity | O | M | P |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | A | 3 | 5 | 13 |
> | B | 4 | 6 | 8 |
> | C | 2 | 3 | 10 |
> | D | 5 | 7 | 15 |
>
> The dependency is: **A and B are parallel**; both must complete before **C**; C must complete before **D**.
>
> Find: (i) Expected time of each activity, (ii) Critical path, (iii) Project variance, (iv) Probability of completing in **30 days**.

### Step 1: Compute Expected Time for Each Activity

Using $E = (O + 4M + P) / 6$:

**Activity A:**
$$E_A = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 13}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 13}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6\ \text{days}$$

**Activity B:**
$$E_B = \frac{4 + 4(6) + 8}{6} = \frac{4 + 24 + 8}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6\ \text{days}$$

**Activity C:**
$$E_C = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 10}{6} = \frac{2 + 12 + 10}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4\ \text{days}$$

**Activity D:**
$$E_D = \frac{5 + 4(7) + 15}{6} = \frac{5 + 28 + 15}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8\ \text{days}$$

### Step 2: Identify the Critical Path

The two possible paths are:
- **Path 1:** A → C → D
- **Path 2:** B → C → D

**Path 1 duration:** $6 + 4 + 8 = 18$ days
**Path 2 duration:** $6 + 4 + 8 = 18$ days

$$T_E = \max(18, 18) = 18\ \text{days}$$

**Critical Path:** A → C → D (or B → C → D, both have equal length — both are critical).

### Step 3: Compute Project Variance

Using $\sigma^2 = ((P - O)/6)^2$:

**Activity A:** $\sigma_A^2 = \left(\frac{13 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{25}{9} \approx 2.78$

**Activity C:** $\sigma_C^2 = \left(\frac{10 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{9} \approx 1.78$

**Activity D:** $\sigma_D^2 = \left(\frac{15 - 5}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{25}{9} \approx 2.78$

**Sum of variances along critical path (A-C-D):**
$$\sigma_E^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_C^2 + \sigma_D^2 = \frac{25}{9} + \frac{16}{9} + \frac{25}{9} = \frac{66}{9} = 7.33$$

$$\sigma_E = \sqrt{7.33} \approx 2.71\ \text{days}$$

### Step 4: Probability of Completion in 30 Days

Using the Z-score formula:
$$Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_E} = \frac{30 - 18}{2.71} = \frac{12}{2.71} \approx 4.43$$

From standard normal tables, $P(Z \leq 4.43) \approx 0.99999 \approx 99.99\%$.

**Conclusion:** There is approximately a **99.99% probability** of completing the project within 30 days.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** If the manager had set a deadline of 20 days, then $Z = (20-18)/2.71 = 0.74$, giving $P \approx 77\%$. A higher target = lower probability of meeting it.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Earned Value Management (EVM)

> **Problem:** A project has a Budget at Completion (BAC) of **₹10,00,000**. After 6 months, the project is **40% complete**, and the actual cost incurred is **₹5,00,000**. The planned value at 6 months (according to the schedule) was **₹4,50,000**.
>
> Calculate: CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, and project status.

### Step 1: Identify the Three Core EVM Values

- **PV (Planned Value)** = ₹4,50,000 (given)
- **AC (Actual Cost)** = ₹5,00,000 (given)
- **EV (Earned Value)** = 40% × ₹10,00,000 = **₹4,00,000**

### Step 2: Cost Variance (CV)

$$CV = EV - AC = 4{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}00{,}000$$

**Interpretation:** Negative CV = **₹1,00,000 OVER BUDGET**.

### Step 3: Schedule Variance (SV)

$$SV = EV - PV = 4{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000 = -50{,}000$$

**Interpretation:** Negative SV = **₹50,000 BEHIND SCHEDULE**.

### Step 4: Cost Performance Index (CPI)

$$CPI = \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 0.80$$

**Interpretation:** $CPI < 1$ means we get only **₹0.80 of work for every ₹1 spent** (inefficient).

### Step 5: Schedule Performance Index (SPI)

$$SPI = \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} \approx 0.889$$

**Interpretation:** $SPI < 1$ means we are progressing at **88.9% of the planned rate** (behind schedule).

### Step 6: Estimate at Completion (EAC)

$$EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{0.80} = 12{,}50{,}000$$

**Interpretation:** At current efficiency, the project will **cost ₹12,50,000** — **₹2,50,000 over budget**.

### Step 7: Estimate to Complete (ETC)

$$ETC = EAC - AC = 12{,}50{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}50{,}000$$

**Interpretation:** An additional **₹7,50,000** is required to finish the project.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Risk Exposure Analysis

> **Problem:** An HCI project has 3 identified risks:
>
> | Risk | Probability | Impact (₹) |
> |---|---|---|
> | User rejection | 0.4 | ₹5,00,000 |
> | Delay in usability testing | 0.3 | ₹2,00,000 |
> | Accessibility non-compliance | 0.1 | ₹3,00,000 |
>
> Calculate the total risk exposure and rank the risks.

### Solution

$$RE_{\text{User Rejection}} = 0.4 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$RE_{\text{Delay}} = 0.3 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = 60{,}000$$

$$RE_{\text{Accessibility}} = 0.1 \times 3{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}000$$

$$RE_{\text{Total}} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 2{,}90{,}000$$

**Ranking (highest exposure first):** User Rejection > Delay > Accessibility Non-compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In HCI, **user rejection risk** is often the highest exposure — emphasizing why user-centered design (Pillar 1) is non-negotiable.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**List and briefly explain the four pillars of good interactive system design.**

**Model Answer:**

The four pillars of good interactive system design are:

1. **User-Centered Design (UCD):** The design process is focused on understanding the users, their tasks, and their needs. Users are actively involved throughout the design lifecycle, and the system is iteratively refined based on user feedback.

2. **Iterative Design:** Design is treated as a cyclic, repeating process rather than a linear sequence. Each iteration consists of analysis, design, prototyping, and evaluation, allowing the design to evolve and improve progressively with each cycle.

3. **Prototyping:** Before committing to full implementation, the designers build low-fidelity or high-fidelity prototypes to explore design ideas, gather feedback, and identify usability issues early in the process.

4. **Evaluation:** Every design is empirically tested with actual users using methods like user testing, heuristic evaluation, or field studies to verify the utility and usability of the system.

> **Note:** The first two pillars (UCD and Iterative Design) guide the **process**, while the last two (Prototyping and Evaluation) guide the **design**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]**
**Briefly explain the "Chief Programmer Team" model of project organization.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Chief Programmer Team** model, proposed by Harlan Mills, is a centralized project organization structure consisting of:
- **Chief Programmer** — A highly skilled lead who handles design, coding, and decision-making.
- **Backup Programmer** — Supports and can replace the chief programmer if needed, ensuring continuity.
- **Program Librarian** — Manages documentation and source code.
- **Specialist Programmers** — Domain experts (database, security, UI/UX) assist the chief programmer.

**Advantages:** Clear authority, minimal communication overhead, high productivity.
**Disadvantages:** Single point of failure (chief programmer), limited democratic input, hard to scale.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[(a) 7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> With the help of a PERT network, the optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic time estimates (in weeks) for activities A through E are given below:
>
> | Activity | O | M | P | Predecessor |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | A | 2 | 3 | 10 | — |
> | B | 3 | 5 | 7 | A |
> | C | 4 | 6 | 8 | A |
> | D | 5 | 6 | 13 | B, C |
> | E | 3 | 4 | 11 | C |
>
> **(i)** Calculate the expected time and variance for each activity.
> **(ii)** Determine the critical path and expected project duration.
> **(iii)** Find the probability of completing the project in **20 weeks**.

#### Model Solution

**Step (i): Expected Time & Variance**

$$E_A = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 10}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4\ \text{weeks}, \quad \sigma_A^2 = \left(\frac{10-2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{64}{36} = 1.78$$

$$E_B = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5\ \text{weeks}, \quad \sigma_B^2 = \left(\frac{7-3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{36} = 0.44$$

$$E_C = \frac{4 + 4(6) + 8}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6\ \text{weeks}, \quad \sigma_C^2 = \left(\frac{8-4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.44$$

$$E_D = \frac{5 + 4(6) + 13}{6} = \frac{42}{6} = 7\ \text{weeks}, \quad \sigma_D^2 = \left(\frac{13-5}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^2 = 1.78$$

$$E_E = \frac{3 + 4(4) + 11}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5\ \text{weeks}, \quad \sigma_E^2 = \left(\frac{11-3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^2 = 1.78$$

**Step (ii): Critical Path**

Possible paths:
- **Path 1:** A → B → D = 4 + 5 + 7 = **16 weeks**
- **Path 2:** A → C → D = 4 + 6 + 7 = **17 weeks** ← **CRITICAL**
- **Path 3:** A → C → E = 4 + 6 + 5 = **15 weeks**

$$T_E = 17\ \text{weeks (Critical Path: A → C → D)}$$

**Step (iii): Probability of Completion in 20 Weeks**

Variance along critical path: $\sigma^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_C^2 + \sigma_D^2 = 1.78 + 0.44 + 1.78 = 4.00$

$$\sigma_E = \sqrt{4.00} = 2.00\ \text{weeks}$$

$$Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_E} = \frac{20 - 17}{2.00} = 1.50$$

From standard normal table: $P(Z \leq 1.50) \approx 0.9332 = \mathbf{93.32\%}$

> **Conclusion:** There is approximately a **93.32% probability** of completing the project within 20 weeks.

---

> **[(b) 7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]**
> Explain the four pillars of good interactive system design. Discuss why each pillar is essential in HCI project management.

#### Model Answer

The four pillars of good interactive system design, as outlined by Dix et al. in *Human-Computer Interaction*, are:

**1. User-Centered Design (UCD)**
UCD is the foundational pillar that places the user at the heart of the design process. It mandates understanding the users' characteristics, tasks, and goals before any design decisions are made. Techniques such as user research, personas, and contextual inquiry are employed. **Why essential in HCI:** Without UCD, the system may technically function but fail to serve actual user needs, leading to rejection, low adoption, and costly redesigns.

**2. Iterative Design**
This pillar recognizes that design is not a one-shot activity but a continuous cycle of refinement. Each cycle moves through requirements, design, prototype, and evaluation, with feedback informing the next cycle. **Why essential:** Iterative design mitigates risk by surfacing usability issues early when they are inexpensive to fix, rather than at deployment when changes are costly.

**3. Prototyping**
Prototyping involves building early-stage representations of the system — from paper sketches to functional click-throughs. It serves as a communication tool between designers, developers, and users. **Why essential:** Prototypes allow stakeholders to "experience" the design before full implementation, enabling concrete feedback and reducing misunderstandings in the requirements.

**4. Evaluation**
Evaluation involves systematically testing the system with real users (user testing) or against heuristics (expert review). Both **utility** (does the system do what users need?) and **usability** (how well can users do it?) are measured. **Why essential:** Without evaluation, assumptions about user behavior remain untested, and the system may be objectively useless or unusable.

> **Synthesis:** These pillars are not independent — they form a feedback loop. UCD informs what to build, prototyping allows testing of ideas, evaluation validates those tests, and iteration refines the design based on insights. Together, they ensure the final system is **useful, usable, and desirable**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[(a) 7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Jul 2022]**
> A software project has a Budget at Completion (BAC) of **₹8,00,000**. After 4 months, the project is **30% complete**, the actual cost incurred is **₹3,00,000**, and the planned value at this stage was **₹2,40,000**.
>
> Calculate:
> (i) Earned Value (EV), Cost Variance (CV), and Schedule Variance (SV)
> (ii) Cost Performance Index (CPI) and Schedule Performance Index (SPI)
> (iii) Estimate at Completion (EAC) and Estimate to Complete (ETC)
> (iv) Interpret the project status.

#### Model Solution

**Given:** BAC = ₹8,00,000; % complete = 30%; AC = ₹3,00,000; PV = ₹2,40,000

**Step (i): EV, CV, SV**

$$EV = 30\% \times 8{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}40{,}000$$

$$CV = EV - AC = 2{,}40{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = -60{,}000\ \text{(Over Budget)}$$

$$SV = EV - PV = 2{,}40{,}000 - 2{,}40{,}000 = 0\ \text{(On Schedule)}$$

**Step (ii): CPI, SPI**

$$CPI = \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 0.80$$

$$SPI = \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{2{,}40{,}000} = 1.00$$

**Step (iii): EAC, ETC**

$$EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{0.80} = 10{,}00{,}000$$

$$ETC = EAC - AC = 10{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000$$

**Step (iv): Interpretation**

- **Cost:** $CPI = 0.80 < 1$ and $CV < 0$ → Project is **over budget**. For every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.80 of work is being produced.
- **Schedule:** $SPI = 1.00$ and $SV = 0$ → Project is **on schedule** in terms of work completed vs. planned.
- **Forecast:** At current efficiency, the project will cost **₹10,00,000** (₹2,00,000 over budget). An additional **₹7,00,000** is required to complete the remaining work.
- **Recommendation:** Investigate cost overruns, optimize resource usage, and consider scope re-baselining.

> **[(b) 7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> Discuss the legal and professional issues that an HCI designer must consider while developing interactive systems. Mention at least three accessibility standards and two professional codes of ethics.

#### Model Answer

**Legal Issues in HCI Design:**

1. **Accessibility Compliance:** Designers must ensure systems are usable by people with disabilities (visual, auditory, motor, cognitive). Non-compliance can lead to legal action and exclusion of users.
2. **Privacy and Data Protection:** Systems collecting personal data must obtain informed consent and protect user data. Laws like GDPR (EU) and India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA 2023) apply.
3. **Intellectual Property:** Use of copyrighted assets, fonts, and design elements must be properly licensed.
4. **Consumer Protection:** Misleading interfaces, dark patterns, and unfair terms can violate consumer protection laws.
5. **Safety-Critical Systems:** In aviation, medical, and automotive domains, interface failures can cause physical harm; thus, regulations from agencies like FAA, ISO 9241, and IEC 62366 apply.

**Accessibility Standards:**

| Standard | Issuing Body | Key Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **WCAG 2.1 / 2.2** | W3C | Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust (POUR) |
| **ADA (1990, amended)** | US Federal Law | Public digital services must be accessible |
| **Section 508** | US Federal Procurement | Federal agencies' ICT must be accessible |
| **EN 301 549** | European Union | Accessibility requirements for ICT products and services |
| **IS 17802 (India)** | BIS | Indian accessibility standard aligned with WCAG |

**Professional Codes of Ethics:**

- **ACM Code of Ethics:** Emphasizes contributing to society, avoiding harm, honesty, and respect for privacy.
- **IEEE Code of Ethics:** Commits to public safety, competence, integrity, and fair treatment.
- **BCS Code of Conduct (UK):** Emphasizes public interest, duty to employer, professional competence, and integrity.

> **Conclusion:** HCI designers carry significant ethical and legal responsibility. Adherence to accessibility standards and professional codes is not merely bureaucratic but essential for inclusive, trustworthy, and legally compliant interactive systems.

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the PERT constant "4M"** — Students often write $(O + M + P)/3$ instead of $(O + 4M + P)/6$. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing CV and SV sign interpretation** — $CV < 0$ means OVER budget, $SV < 0$ means BEHIND schedule. Don't reverse these.
> 3. **Adding variance of non-critical activities to project variance** — Only activities on the **critical path** contribute to total project variance.
> 4. **Confusing PV with AC** — PV (Planned Value) is *what you planned to spend*, not *what you actually spent*.
> 5. **In 4 Pillars question, omitting the process/design classification** — The 2+2 split (Process vs. Design) is often asked; missing it costs a mark.
> 6. **Ignoring India-specific laws** — For KTU Kerala, students should mention **DPDP Act 2023** alongside GDPR/ADA.
> 7. **Not drawing the PERT network** — In 7-mark problems, **always sketch the network** before solving; otherwise, partial credit is lost.

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Revision — Read Before Exam!**

### **4 Pillars of Good Design**
- ✦ **UCD + Iterative Design** = process pillars
- ✦ **Prototyping + Evaluation** = design pillars
- ✦ Always classify; always mention feedback loop

### **3 Pillars of Project Management**
- ✦ **People + Product + Process**

### **PERT Formulas (MUST MEMORIZE)**
- ✦ $E = (O + 4M + P)/6$
- ✦ $\sigma^2 = ((P-O)/6)^2$
- ✦ $\sigma_E = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$ (critical path only)
- ✦ $Z = (T_s - T_E)/\sigma_E$

### **EVM Formulas (MUST MEMORIZE)**
- ✦ $EV = \%\ complete \times BAC$
- ✦ $CV = EV - AC$; negative = over budget
- ✦ $SV = EV - PV$; negative = behind schedule
- ✦ $CPI = EV/AC$; $SPI = EV/PV$
- ✦ $EAC = BAC/CPI$; $ETC = EAC - AC$

### **Risk Management**
- ✦ $RE = Probability \times Impact$
- ✦ Risk register; rank by exposure

### **Project Organization**
- ✦ Chief Programmer = centralized, fast, single point of failure
- ✦ Democratic = consensus-based, slow
- ✦ Hybrid = most common in HCI

### **Design Patterns**
- ✦ Reusable, proven, contextual solutions
- ✦ Navigation, Layout, Input, Feedback, Social

### **Legal & Professional**
- ✦ **WCAG** (POUR principles) — accessibility gold standard
- ✦ **GDPR / DPDP Act 2023** — privacy & consent
- ✦ **ADA, Section 508, EN 301 549** — accessibility laws
- ✦ **ACM, IEEE, BCS** — professional ethics codes

### **Communication Channels**
- ✦ Face-to-face (highest bandwidth) → Documentation (lowest but persistent)
- ✦ Status reports, milestone reviews, sprint reviews

### **Critical Path Identification**
- ✦ Longest path through the network
- ✦ Determines minimum project duration
- ✦ Has ZERO float/slack

### **Internationalization (i18n)**
- ✦ Design for global audiences from the start
- ✦ Localizable text, regional formats (date, currency, RTL support)
